### [1] [A Cascaded Generative Approach for e-Commerce Recommendations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11118)
*Moein Hasani,Hamidreza Shahidi,Trace Levinson,Yuan Zhong,Guanghua Shu,Vinesh Gudla,Tejaswi Tenneti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cascaded merchandising framework for e-commerce storefronts that uses generative AI for theme and keyword creation, combined with teacher-student fine-tuning and hybrid ranking, achieving +2.7% lift in cart adds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional e-commerce storefront assembly from independent components is rigid, limits personalization and semantic cohesion across pages, and cannot effectively support dynamic objectives and merchandising requirements over time.

Method: Introduces a cascaded merchandising framework with two generative tasks: placement-level theme generation and constrained keyword generation per placement. Uses teacher-student fine-tuning for scalability under production constraints. Combines generative output with traditional ranking models in hybrid infrastructure, plus frameworks for AI-driven content evaluation and quality filtering.

Result: Fine-tuned model ablations approach closed-weight LLM performance. Online experiments show an estimated +2.7% lift in cart adds per page view over a strong baseline.

Conclusion: The generative framework successfully addresses limitations of traditional storefront assembly by enabling dynamic personalization and semantic cohesion while maintaining production scalability through teacher-student fine-tuning and hybrid infrastructure, demonstrating significant business value.

Abstract: Personalized storefronts in large e-commerce marketplaces are often assembled from many independent components: static themes per page section ("placement"), retrieval systems to fetch eligible products per placement, and pointwise rankers to order content. While effective in optimizing for aggregate preferences, this paradigm is rigid and can limit personalization and semantic cohesion across the page. This makes it poorly suited to support dynamic objectives and merchandising requirements over time. To address this, we introduce a cascaded merchandising framework that decomposes storefront construction into two generative tasks: (i) placement-level theme generation and (ii) constrained keyword generation per placement to power product retrieval. Teacher-student fine-tuning is leveraged to improve scalability of this framework under production latency and cost constraints. Fine-tuned model ablations are shown to approach closed-weight LLM performance. We further contribute frameworks for AI-driven content evaluation and quality filtering, enabling safe and automated deployment of dynamic content at scale. Generative output is fused with traditional ranking models to preserve hybrid infrastructure. In online experiments, this framework yields an estimated +2.7% lift in cart adds per page view over a strong baseline.

</details>


### [2] [EVOCHAMBER: Test-Time Co-evolution of Multi-Agent System at Individual, Team, and Population Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11136)
*Yaolun Zhang,Tianyi Xu,Shengyu Dai,Zhenwen Shao,Qingyun Wu,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EVOCHAMBER is a training-free multi-agent test-time evolution framework that enables asymmetric knowledge transfer between agents through collaborative reflection, preserving specialization while filling knowledge gaps, leading to emergent niche specialists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent test-time evolution differs fundamentally from single-agent evolution - it involves evolving collaboration structures and knowledge flow across agents, which can produce emergent specialization. Prior methods either isolate agent experiences (losing cross-agent learning) or broadcast symmetrically (erasing specialization), failing to capture the true potential of multi-agent systems.

Method: EVOCHAMBER implements test-time evolution at three levels: 1) Core CODREAM protocol triggered on failure/disagreement, where agents collaboratively reflect, distill insights, and route knowledge asymmetrically from strong to weak agents; 2) Team-level operators assemble niche-conditioned teams and select collaboration structures; 3) Population-level lifecycle operators fork, merge, prune, and seed agents under performance pressure.

Result: On heterogeneous task streams with Qwen3-8B, EVOCHAMBER achieves 63.9% on competition math, 75.7% on code, and 87.1% on multi-domain reasoning, outperforming best baseline by 32% relative on math. Ablations confirm asymmetric cross-agent transfer as the primary driver. Starting from identical agents, 4-5 stable niche specialists spontaneously emerge.

Conclusion: EVOCHAMBER demonstrates that multi-agent test-time evolution enables emergent specialization through asymmetric knowledge transfer, a phenomenon impossible for single-agent learners. The framework successfully evolves collaboration structures and knowledge flow patterns that improve performance while preserving valuable specialization.

Abstract: We argue that multi-agent test-time evolution is not single-agent evolution replicated N times. A single-agent learner can only evolve its own context and memory. A multi-agent system additionally evolves who collaborates, how they collaborate, and how knowledge flows across the population. These components have no single-agent counterpart and can produce phenomena such as emergent specialization. Yet prior test-time methods either confine experiences to individual agents, forfeiting cross-agent learning, or broadcast symmetrically to all agents, erasing the specialization that makes collaboration valuable. We present EVOCHAMBER, a training-free framework that instantiates test-time evolution at three levels over a coevolving agent pool. At its core is CODREAM (Collaborative Dreaming), a post-task protocol triggered on team failure or disagreement, in which agents collaboratively reflect, distill insights, and route them asymmetrically from strong to weak agents on the failed niche, preserving specialization while filling knowledge gaps. Team-level operators assemble niche-conditioned teams and select collaboration structures online. Population-level lifecycle operators fork, merge, prune, and seed agents under performance pressure. On three heterogeneous task streams with Qwen3-8B, EVOCHAMBER reaches 63.9% on competition math, 75.7% on code, and 87.1% on multi-domain reasoning, outperforming the best baseline by 32% relative on math and confirming asymmetric cross-agent transfer as the primary driver in ablation. Starting from several identically initialized agents, four to five stable niche specialists spontaneously emerge, a structural signature of multi-agent evolution that no single-agent learner can express. See our code at: https://github.com/Mercury7353/EvoChamber

</details>


### [3] [RankQ: Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning via Self-Supervised Action Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11151)
*Andrew Choi,Wei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RankQ improves offline-to-online RL by using a ranking loss rather than pessimistic penalties, enabling better policy improvement from suboptimal datasets through structured action ordering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing offline-to-online RL methods use pessimistic penalties on out-of-distribution actions, which act like behavior cloning anchors and hinder online policy improvement when dataset actions are suboptimal. The challenge is learning accurate critics with limited dataset coverage while avoiding value overestimation.

Method: RankQ is an offline-to-online Q-learning objective that combines temporal-difference learning with a self-supervised multi-term ranking loss. Instead of uniformly penalizing unseen actions, it enforces structured action ordering to learn relative action preferences, shaping the Q-function to direct action gradients toward higher-quality behaviors.

Result: On sparse reward D4RL benchmarks, RankQ matches or exceeds seven prior methods. In vision-based robot learning: 1) Low-data setting: 42.7% higher simulation success than next best method; 2) High-data setting: 13.7% simulation improvement over next best; 3) Real-world transfer: Cube stacking success increased from 43.1% to 84.7% relative to initial VLA model performance.

Conclusion: RankQ's ranking-based approach avoids the limitations of pessimistic OOD penalties, enabling effective offline-to-online fine-tuning by learning structured action preferences that guide policy improvement toward higher-quality behaviors, with strong empirical results across diverse domains including real-world robot manipulation.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning (RL) improves sample efficiency by leveraging pre-collected datasets prior to online interaction. A key challenge, however, is learning an accurate critic in large state--action spaces with limited dataset coverage. To mitigate harmful updates from value overestimation, prior methods impose pessimism by down-weighting out-of-distribution (OOD) actions relative to dataset actions. While effective, this essentially acts as a behavior cloning anchor and can hinder downstream online policy improvement when dataset actions are suboptimal. We propose RankQ, an offline-to-online Q-learning objective that augments temporal-difference learning with a self-supervised multi-term ranking loss to enforce structured action ordering. By learning relative action preferences rather than uniformly penalizing unseen actions, RankQ shapes the Q-function such that action gradients are directed toward higher-quality behaviors. Across sparse reward D4RL benchmarks, RankQ achieves performance competitive with or superior to seven prior methods. In vision-based robot learning, RankQ enables effective offline-to-online fine-tuning of a pretrained vision-language-action (VLA) model in a low-data regime, achieving on average a 42.7% higher simulation success rate than the next best method. In a high-data setting, RankQ improves simulation performance by 13.7% over the next best method and achieves strong sim-to-real transfer, increasing real-world cube stacking success from 43.1% to 84.7% relative to the VLA's initial performance.

</details>


### [4] [OLIVIA: Online Learning via Inference-time Action Adaptation for Decision Making in LLM ReAct Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11169)
*Sheldon Yu,Junda Wu,Xintong Li,Nikki Lijing Kuang,Sizhe Zhou,Tong Yu,Jiawei Han,Jingbo Shang,Julian McAuley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OLIVIA is an inference-time adaptation framework for ReAct-style LLM agents that models action selection as a contextual linear bandit, enabling direct action-level adaptation with uncertainty estimates during deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM agents accumulate small action-selection errors during repeated multi-step tasks, causing wasted tool calls and reduced reliability. Existing inference-time adaptation methods (prompting/retrieval) only indirectly influence behavior through context manipulation without exposing explicit decision layers for scoring actions, representing uncertainty, or enabling online updates from action-level feedback.

Method: Models the LLM's final action-selection layer as a contextual linear bandit over candidate actions, using frozen hidden states as decision contexts. This preserves reasoning processes while enabling direct action selection adaptation with upper-confidence-bound exploration for sample-efficient policy improvement with minimal computational overhead.

Result: OLIVIA consistently improves task performance over static ReAct and prompt-based inference-time baselines across four benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness for deployment-time adaptation.

Conclusion: Explicit online decision layers (like OLIVIA's contextual bandit approach) provide an effective alternative to purely prompt- or retrieval-based adaptation for LLM agents during deployment, enabling trackable, fine-grained, uncertainty-aware adaptation with lightweight online updates.

Abstract: Large language model agents interleave reasoning, action selection, and observation to solve sequential decision-making tasks. In deployed settings where agents repeatedly handle related multi-step tasks, small action-selection errors can accumulate into wasted tool calls, latency, and reduced reliability. Despite this need for deployment-time improvement, existing inference-time adaptation methods for LLM agents mainly rely on prompting or retrieval, which influence behavior indirectly through context manipulation. For ReAct-style agents, such approaches do not expose an explicit decision layer that can score candidate actions, represent uncertainty, or be updated online from action-level feedback. As a result, they provide limited support for trackable, fine-grained, and uncertainty-aware adaptation during deployment. We propose OLIVIA, an inference-time action adaptation framework for ReAct-style agents. OLIVIA models the LLM's final action-selection layer as a contextual linear bandit over candidate actions, with frozen hidden states as decision contexts. This choice is particularly suitable for deployment because it adapts behavior directly at the action-selection interface, preserves the underlying reasoning process, and provides explicit uncertainty estimates and lightweight online updates from action-level feedback. With upper-confidence-bound exploration, OLIVIA improves the policy sample-efficiently with minimal computational overhead. We instantiate OLIVIA on four benchmarks and show that it consistently improves task performance over static ReAct and prompt-based inference-time baselines. Our results suggest that explicit online decision layers provide an effective alternative to purely prompt- or retrieval-based adaptation for LLM agents during deployment.

</details>


### [5] [The Many Faces of On-Policy Distillation: Pitfalls, Mechanisms, and Fixes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11182)
*Siqi Zhu,Xuyan Ye,Hongyu Lu,Weiye Shi,Ge Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: On-policy distillation (OPD) and on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) for LLMs work effectively when privileged information represents shared latent rules (like system prompts), but fail when dealing with instance-specific privileged information or mathematical reasoning due to distribution mismatch and optimization issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to resolve the mixed results and inconsistent findings about the effectiveness of on-policy distillation methods for large language models, understanding when these techniques work, when they fail, and why they sometimes cause instability or degradation.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive empirical study analyzing OPD and OPSD, examining different scenarios including mathematical reasoning tasks, system prompt internalization, and knowledge transfer. They investigate sensitivity to teacher choice, loss formulations, and the role of privileged information.

Result: Key findings: (1) OPD on mathematical reasoning is highly sensitive to teacher choice and loss formulation; (2) OPSD fails when privileged information is instance-specific (absent at test time) but works when it represents shared latent rules like system prompts; (3) Three identified failure mechanisms include distribution mismatch, optimization instability from biased gradients, and OPSD's limitation of learning PI-free policies that aggregate PI-conditioned teachers.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that OPD/OPSD effectiveness depends critically on the nature of privileged information and implementation details. Proposed solutions include stop-gradient TopK objectives, RLVR-adapted teachers, and SFT-stabilized students to mitigate identified failure mechanisms.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) and on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) have emerged as promising post-training methods for large language models, offering dense token-level supervision on trajectories sampled from the model's own policy. However, existing results on their effectiveness remain mixed: while OP(S)D has shown promise in system prompt and knowledge internalization, recent studies also report instability and degradation. In this work, we present a comprehensive empirical study of when OPD and OPSD work, when they fail, and why. We find that OPD on mathematical reasoning is highly sensitive to teacher choice and loss formulation, whereas OPSD fails in our tested settings due to test-time absence of instance-specific privileged information (PI). In contrast, OPSD is effective when PI represents a shared latent rule, such as a system prompt or alignment preference. We identify three failure mechanisms: (1) distribution mismatch between teacher and student caused by conditioning on student-generated prefixes, (2) optimization instability from biased TopK reverse-KL gradients, and (3) an OPSD-specific limitation where the student learns a PI-free policy that aggregates PI-conditioned teachers, which is insufficient when PI is instance-specific. We further show that stop-gradient TopK objectives, RLVR-adapted teachers, and SFT-stabilized students mitigate these failures.

</details>


### [6] [PIVOT: Bridging Planning and Execution in LLM Agents via Trajectory Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11225)
*Tuo Zhang,Alin-Ionut Popa,Yan Xu,Rui Song,Dimitrios Dimitriadis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PIVOT is a self-supervised framework that refines LLM-generated plans through iterative execution and feedback loops, reducing plan-execution gaps in autonomous agents while being computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents often produce plans that seem coherent but fail during execution due to infeasible actions, constraint violations, and compounding errors over extended planning horizons. There's a significant misalignment between generated plans and their actual executability.

Method: PIVOT uses a four-stage framework: PLAN generates candidate trajectories; INSPECT executes them and computes structured losses with textual gradients encoding plan-execution discrepancies; EVOLVE applies these signals to produce improved trajectories; and VERIFY performs a final global check against task constraints. It features monotonic acceptance to ensure non-decreasing solution quality.

Result: Empirical evaluations on DeepPlanning and GAIA show state-of-the-art performance: with HITL feedback, up to 94% relative improvement in constraint satisfaction, and the autonomous variant retains substantial gains. It's computationally efficient, requiring 3x-5x fewer tokens than competing methods.

Conclusion: Feedback-based trajectory optimization (whether self-supervised or human-supervised) is a principled methodology for mitigating plan-execution gaps in autonomous agent systems, with PIVOT providing an effective implementation framework.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents frequently generate seemingly coherent plans that fail upon execution due to infeasible actions, constraint violations, and compounding errors over extended horizons. PIVOT (Plan-Inspect-eVOlve Trajectories) addresses this plan-execution misalignment through a self-supervised framework that treats trajectories as optimizable objects iteratively refined via environment interaction. The framework comprises four stages: PLAN generates candidate trajectories; INSPECT executes them and computes structured losses with textual gradients encoding plan-execution discrepancies; EVOLVE applies these signals to produce improved trajectories; and VERIFY performs a final global check against task constraints. A monotonic acceptance process ensures a non-decreasing solution quality. Empirical evaluations on DeepPlanning and GAIA demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: with human-in-the-loop (HITL) feedback, PIVOT establishes a strong upper bound up to 94% relative improvement in constraint satisfaction, while its fully autonomous variant retains substantial gains, showing that the core trajectory-refinement mechanism remains effective without external supervision. At the same time, PIVOT remains computationally efficient, requiring up to 3x to 5x fewer tokens than competing refinement methods. These findings establish that (self- or human-supervised) feedback-based trajectory optimization is a principled methodology for mitigating plan-execution gaps in autonomous agent systems.

</details>


### [7] [Don't Look at the Numbers: Visual Anchoring Bias and Layer-wise Representation in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11218)
*M. Shalankin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Embedded numeric anchors on images cause systematic bias in Vision-Language Model quality judgments, with anchor effects being 2.5x larger than severe image quality degradation. The study identifies dissociation between anchor classification and quality prediction layers, and reveals architecture-dependent fusion patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how embedded numeric anchors systematically bias Vision-Language Model quality judgments, establish that this bias is not reducible to visual changes, and understand the representational dynamics behind this susceptibility across different model architectures.

Method: Tested six Vision-Language Models from five architectural families with embedded numeric anchors on images. Used ANOVA to quantify bias effects. Employed layer-wise probing to analyze anchor classification versus quality prediction. Conducted fusion analysis to examine how different architectures integrate information. Compared anchor effects against severe image quality degradation.

Result: Significant anchor bias across all models (ANOVA eta^2 = 0.18-0.77, all p < 0.001). Anchor effects were 2.5x larger than severe image quality degradation. Layer-wise probing showed consistent dissociation: anchor classification saturates at layers L12-L34, while optimal quality prediction occurs deeper (R^2 = 0.69-0.91). Fusion analysis revealed architecture-dependent integration patterns: two models show instant fusion at L1-L2, while three others show partial or no fusion.

Conclusion: The study establishes a causal account of visual anchoring bias in Vision-Language Models, linking behavioral susceptibility to specific representation dynamics. Findings show systematic bias that persists across architectures, with distinct neural mechanisms explaining how anchors affect quality judgments differently from image quality itself.

Abstract: Embedded numeric anchors on images systematically bias Vision-Language Model quality judgments across six VLMs from five architectural families (ANOVA eta^2 = 0.18-0.77, all p < 0.001). Anchor effects are 2.5x larger than severe image quality degradation, confirming bias is not reducible to visual changes. Layer-wise probing reveals consistent dissociation: layers where anchor classification saturates (L12-L34) are suboptimal for quality prediction, with optimal layers deeper (R^2 = 0.69-0.91). Fusion analysis identifies architecture-dependent integration -- instant fusion at L1-L2 in two models versus partial or no fusion in three others. These results establish a causal account of visual anchoring bias, linking behavioral susceptibility to representation dynamics.

</details>


### [8] [Hierarchical LLM-Driven Control for HAPS-Assisted UAV Networks: Joint Optimization of Flight and Connectivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11509)
*Zijiang Yan,Hao Zhou,Wael Jaafar,Jianhua Pei,Ping Wang,Halim Yanikomeroglu,Hina Tabassum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hierarchical LLM-driven control framework for multi-UAV systems in integrated terrestrial/non-terrestrial networks that jointly optimizes motion control and connectivity, achieving significant improvements in transportation efficiency, telecommunication throughput, and collision avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-UAV systems face the fundamental challenge of jointly optimizing motion control and connectivity in complex networked environments, particularly in integrated terrestrial/non-terrestrial networks with dynamic, partially observable conditions where collision avoidance, traffic flow efficiency, and reliable communication must be balanced.

Method: The authors model the problem as a hierarchical multi-objective partially observable Markov decision process (H-MO-POMDP) and propose an LLM-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework: global LLM-based controller on HAPS for long-term planning and load balancing; local hybrid controllers combining slow-timescale LLMs for spatial reasoning with reinforcement learning agents for fast UAV-to-infrastructure communication and motion control.

Result: The framework achieves 14% increase in transportation efficiency, 25% improvement in telecommunication throughput, 23% reduction in physical collision rates, strong handover stability, and zero-shot generalization in dynamic scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical LLM-driven framework effectively addresses the joint optimization challenge of multi-UAV motion control and connectivity in integrated terrestrial/non-terrestrial networks, demonstrating superior performance across multiple metrics and showing promise for practical deployment in complex aerial scenarios.

Abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly deployed in complex networked environments, yet the joint optimization of multi-UAV motion control and connectivity remains a fundamental challenge. In this paper, we study a multi-UAV system operating in an integrated terrestrial and non-terrestrial network (ITNTN) comprising terrestrial base stations and high-altitude platform stations (HAPS). We consider a three-dimensional (3D) aerial highway scenario where UAVs must adapt their motion to ensure collision avoidance, efficient traffic flow, and reliable communication under dynamic and partially observable conditions. We first model the problem as a hierarchical multi-objective partially observable Markov decision process (H-MO-POMDP), capturing the coupling between control and communication objectives. Based on this formulation, we propose a large language model (LLM)-driven hierarchical multi-rate control framework. At the global level, an LLM-based controller on the HAPS performs long-term planning for load balancing and handover decisions. At the local level, each UAV employs a hybrid controller that integrates a slow-timescale LLM for high-level spatial reasoning with a reinforcement learning agent for faster UAV-to-infrastructure (U2I) communication and motion control. We further develop a high-fidelity 3D simulation platform by integrating the gym-pybullet-drones environment with 3GPP-compliant RF/THz channel models. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 14% increase in transportation efficiency and a 25% improvement in telecommunication throughput. Additionally, it achieves a 23% reduction in physical collision rates, demonstrating strong handover stability and zero-shot generalization in dynamic scenarios.

</details>


### [9] [Do Vision-Language-Models show human-like logical problem-solving capability in point and click puzzle games?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11223)
*Dominik Helfenstein,Marco Menner,Maximilian Triebel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VLATIM is a new benchmark for evaluating VLMs' logical problem-solving in physics puzzle games like The Incredible Machine 2, revealing gaps between reasoning and execution capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks often overlook complex physical reasoning in point-and-click puzzle games, and there's a gap between high-level logical reasoning and precise action execution in continuous spaces.

Method: Created VLATIM benchmark with five progressive parts testing capabilities from basic visual grounding to full puzzle solving, specifically designed for The Incredible Machine 2 physics puzzle game.

Result: Large proprietary models show superior planning but struggle with precise visual grounding, failing to achieve human-like problem-solving capabilities despite better reasoning abilities.

Conclusion: VLMs still lack the integration of reasoning and precise execution needed for human-like problem-solving in interactive physics puzzle environments, highlighting an important research direction.

Abstract: Vision-Language(-Action) Models (VLMs) are increasingly applied to interactive environments, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the complex physical reasoning required for point-and-click puzzle games. This paper introduces Vision-Language Against The Incredible Machine (VLATIM), a benchmark designed to evaluate human-like logical problem-solving capabilities within the classic physics puzzle game The Incredible Machine 2 (TIM). Unlike existing benchmarks, VLATIM specifically targets the critical gap between high-level logical reasoning and continuous action spaces requiring precise mouse interactions. This benchmark is structured into five progressive parts, assessing capabilities that range from basic visual grounding and domain understanding to multi-step manipulation and full puzzle solving. Our results reveal a significant disparity between reasoning and execution. While large proprietary models demonstrate superior planning abilities, they struggle with precise visual grounding. Consequently, they do not yet show human-like problem-solving capabilities.

</details>


### [10] [Rethinking LLMOps for Fraud and AML: Building a Compliance-Grade LLM Serving Stack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11232)
*Prathamesh Vasudeo Naik,Naresh Dintakurthi,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A specialized LLMOps stack for fraud/AML workloads achieves major performance gains through workload-aware optimizations like prefix caching, adaptive batching, and speculative decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud detection and AML compliance have unique serving requirements different from generic chat - they use prefix-heavy, schema-constrained prompts with reusable policy text and evidence, making systems concerns like prefix reuse, KV-cache efficiency, and output validation critical for performance.

Method: Developed a workload-aware LLMOps stack combining vLLM runtime tuning, PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, multi-adapter serving, adapter-aware batching, sleep/wake lifecycle management, speculative decoding, and optional prefill/decode disaggregation for self-hosted open-weight models like Llama and Qwen.

Result: The optimizations improved throughput from 612-650 to 3,600 requests/hour, reduced P99 latency from 31-38 seconds to 6.4-8.7 seconds, and increased GPU utilization from 12% to 78% on public-synthetic AML workloads.

Conclusion: Regulated LLM performance is primarily a workload-design, serving-optimization, and quality-gating problem rather than just a model-selection problem, demonstrating that specialized systems optimizations are essential for high-value compliance domains.

Abstract: Fraud detection and anti-money-laundering (AML) compliance are high-value domains for large language models (LLMs), but their serving requirements differ sharply from generic chat workloads. Compliance prompts are often prefix-heavy, schema-constrained, and evidence-rich, combining reusable policy instructions, risk taxonomies, transaction or document context, and short structured outputs such as JSON labels or risk factors. These properties make prefix reuse, KV-cache efficiency, runtime tuning, model orchestration, and output validation first-order systems concerns. This paper introduces a workload-aware LLMOps stack for fraud and AML workloads using self-hosted open-weight models such as Meta Llama and Alibaba Qwen. The stack combines vLLM-style runtime tuning, PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, multi-adapter serving, adapter and prompt-length-aware batching, sleep/wake lifecycle management, speculative decoding, and optional prefill/decode disaggregation. To avoid exposing institution-specific data, the reproducibility track converts public synthetic AML datasets, including IBM AML and SAML-D, into prefix-heavy compliance prompts with reusable policy text, transaction evidence, typology definitions, and schema-constrained outputs. We also incorporate an LLM-as-judge quality gate using deterministic compliance checks, reference metrics, expert-adjudicated calibration data where available, and multi-judge rubric scoring. Across public-synthetic AML workloads and controlled serving benchmarks, workload-aware tuning improved throughput from 612-650 to 3,600 requests/hour, reduced P99 latency from 31-38 seconds to 6.4-8.7 seconds, and increased GPU utilization from 12% to 78%. These results show that regulated LLM performance is a workload-design, serving-optimization, and quality-gating problem, not only a model-selection problem.

</details>


### [11] [Intermediate Artifacts as First-Class Citizens: A Data Model for Durable Intermediate Artifacts in Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12087)
*Josh Rosen,Seth Rosen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI systems should maintain durable, inspectable intermediate artifacts (not just final outputs) to make multi-step AI work more transparent, revisable, and maintainable over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems produce final artifacts while losing intermediate work, making multi-step AI work a "lossy projection" where the reasoning process and intermediate states remain ephemeral and inaccessible for inspection or revision.

Method: Proposes a systems-level data model for durable intermediate artifacts that are typed, structured, addressable, versioned, dependency-aware, authoritative, and consumable by downstream computation. The model formalizes update semantics and artifact lineage to support durable intermediate state across revisions.

Result: Introduces a framework distinguishing intermediate artifacts from chat transcripts, memory, chain-of-thought, and final answers, enabling explicit tracking of work products like evidence maps, claim structures, criteria, assumptions, plans, and partial products that can be inspected and revised.

Conclusion: Durable intermediate artifacts make AI-generated work more inspectable, revisable, and maintainable over time, shifting evaluation focus from final-output quality to maintained-state quality, without necessarily making models smarter but making their work more transparent and sustainable.

Abstract: Many AI systems are organized around loops in which models reason, call tools, observe results, and continue until a task is complete. These systems often produce final artifacts such as memos, plans, recommendations, and analyses, while the intermediate work that shaped those outputs remains ephemeral. For multi-step, revisable AI work, final artifacts are often lossy projections over upstream state.
  We argue that such systems should preserve durable, inspectable intermediate artifacts: typed, structured, addressable, versioned, dependency-aware, authoritative, and consumable by downstream computation. These artifacts are not the model's private chain-of-thought. They are maintained work products such as evidence maps, claim structures, criteria, assumptions, plans, transformation rules, synthesis procedures, unresolved tensions, and partial products that later humans and agents can inspect, revise, supersede, and improve.
  The contribution is a systems-level data model. We distinguish intermediate artifacts from chat transcripts, memory, hidden chain-of-thought, narration, thinking, and final answers; formalize additive and superseding update semantics with explicit current-state resolution; describe how artifact lineage supports durable intermediate state across revisions; and argue that evaluation must target maintained-state quality, not only final-output quality. The claim is not that artifacts make models smarter. It is that durable intermediate artifacts make AI-generated work more inspectable, revisable, and maintainable over time.

</details>


### [12] [The Semantic Training Gap: Ontology-Grounded Tool Architectures for Industrial AI Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11234)
*Grama Chethan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based AI agents in manufacturing lack operational semantics, causing incorrect outputs despite linguistic precision. This semantic gap is addressed by embedding manufacturing ontology directly into the AI tool layer, enforcing semantic constraints at runtime to eliminate domain hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based AI agents deployed in manufacturing environments demonstrate statistical fluency with domain terminology but lack grounded understanding of operational semantics - the relational structure connecting equipment identifiers, process parameters, failure codes, and regulatory constraints within specific production contexts. This semantic training gap causes operationally incorrect outputs even when model responses are linguistically precise.

Method: The paper presents an architecture that embeds manufacturing ontology directly into the AI tool layer as a typed relational configuration, enforcing semantic constraints at runtime rather than relying on model training. The architecture is formalized as a three-operation interface contract (resolve, contextualize, annotate) with invariants enforced by an AIOps orchestration layer.

Result: In controlled experiments across six industry configurations (72 tool invocations using Qwen3-32B), unconstrained tool parameters produced a 43% hallucination rate for domain identifiers. Ontology-grounded parameters reduced this to 0%. Validation through a digital twin analytics platform demonstrated that a single codebase with domain-specific ontology configurations eliminates tool-call hallucination and achieves cross-domain configurability without application code changes.

Conclusion: The semantic training gap in LLM-based manufacturing agents can be effectively addressed by embedding manufacturing ontology directly into the AI tool layer. This approach eliminates domain hallucinations and enables cross-domain configurability while maintaining linguistic precision, solving the compounding failure mode of semantic drift in multi-agent configurations.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based AI agents are increasingly deployed in manufacturing environments for analytics, quality management, and decision support. These agents demonstrate statistical fluency with domain terminology but lack grounded understanding of operational semantics -- the relational structure that connects equipment identifiers, process parameters, failure codes, and regulatory constraints within a specific production context. This paper identifies and formalizes the semantic training gap: a structural disconnect between how AI systems acquire domain vocabulary through training and how manufacturing operations define meaning through ontological relationships. We demonstrate that this gap causes operationally incorrect outputs even when model responses are linguistically precise, and that in multi-agent configurations it produces a compounding failure mode we term semantic drift. To close this gap, we present an architecture that embeds manufacturing ontology directly into the AI tool layer as a typed relational configuration, enforcing semantic constraints at runtime rather than relying on model training. The architecture is formalized as a three-operation interface contract -- resolve, contextualize, annotate -- with invariants enforced by an AIOps orchestration layer. In a controlled experiment across six industry configurations (72 tool invocations using Qwen3-32B), unconstrained tool parameters produced a 43% hallucination rate for domain identifiers; ontology-grounded parameters reduced this to 0%. We validate the approach through a digital twin analytics platform demonstrating that a single codebase with domain-specific ontology configurations eliminates tool-call hallucination and achieves cross-domain configurability without application code changes.

</details>


### [13] [Unlocking LLM Creativity in Science through Analogical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11258)
*Andrew Shen,Shaul Druckmann,James Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AR (Analogical Reasoning) is a new approach that uses cross-domain analogies to generate diverse, novel solutions for biomedical problems, significantly outperforming baseline methods on multiple metrics and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous science needs AI systems that can generate novel and diverse solutions to open-ended problems, but current LLMs suffer from mode collapse into low-diversity generations.

Method: Introduced analogical reasoning (AR) that generates analogies to cross-domain problems based on shared relational structure, then uses those analogies to search for novel solutions.

Result: AR improves solution diversity metrics by 90-173%, generates novel solutions over 50% of the time (vs. as little as 1.6% for baselines), and shows real-world feasibility across four biomedical problems with significant quantitative gains including 13-fold improvement on perturbation effect prediction and state-of-the-art performance on 2 oligonucleotide datasets.

Conclusion: AR effectively mitigates mode collapse in LLMs, produces novel and diverse solutions that can augment existing solution generation methods, and demonstrates practical value in biomedical applications through consistent performance improvements.

Abstract: Autonomous science promises to augment scientific discovery, particularly in complex fields like biomedicine. However, this requires AI systems that can consistently generate novel and diverse solutions to open-ended problems. We evaluate LLMs on the task of open-ended solution generation and quantify their tendency to mode collapse into low-diversity generations. To mitigate this mode collapse, we introduce analogical reasoning (AR) as a new approach to solution generation. AR generates analogies to cross-domain problems based on shared relational structure, then uses those analogies to search for novel solutions. Compared to baselines, AR discovers significantly more diverse generations (improving solution diversity metrics by 90-173%), generates novel solutions over 50% of the time (compared to as little as 1.6% for baselines), and produces high-quality analogies. To validate the real-world feasibility of AR, we implement AR-generated solutions across four biomedical problems, yielding consistent quantitative gains. AR-generated approaches achieve a nearly 13-fold improvement on distributional metrics for perturbation effect prediction, outperform all baselines on AUPRC when predicting cell-cell communication, infer brain region interactions with a high Spearman correlation ($ρ$=0.729) to published methods, and establish state-of-the-art performance on 2 datasets for oligonucleotide property prediction. The novel and diverse solutions produced by AR can be used to augment the search space of existing solution generation methods.

</details>


### [14] [Template-as-Ontology: Configurable Synthetic Data Infrastructure for Cross-Domain Manufacturing AI Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11259)
*Grama Chethan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework using a Template-as-Ontology principle generates synthetic manufacturing data with guaranteed structural alignment between simulation and AI analytics, enabling LLM-based AI validation without proprietary MES data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based AI agents in manufacturing need schema-correct data for validation, but real production data is proprietary, privacy-sensitive, and vendor-specific, creating a barrier for AI tool development and testing.

Method: Proposes Template-as-Ontology principle: a single Python configuration module serves both as manufacturing simulator specification and runtime domain schema for AI tools, ensuring alignment by construction. Implements a five-layer pipeline (simulation → PostgreSQL → CDC/Iceberg lakehouse → star schema → AI tools) with 12 parameterized AI tools generating causally coherent data across 66 entity types.

Result: Validated with six industry templates showing parametric controllability (KPIs within configured ranges). Ontology-constrained parameters eliminate tool-parameter fabrication (0% vs. 43% hallucination rate in unconstrained setting), providing architectural guarantee against hallucinations for any model.

Conclusion: The framework provides a reusable, vendor-agnostic data layer for discrete manufacturing AI validation, solving the proprietary data problem through synthetic data generation with guaranteed schema alignment and hallucination prevention.

Abstract: LLarge language model (LLM)-based AI agents deployed in manufacturing environments require populated, schema-correct data for validation, yet production MES data is proprietary, privacy-encumbered, and vendor-specific. This paper introduces the Template-as-Ontology principle: a single Python configuration module (700-770 lines, 45 validated exports) serves simultaneously as the specification for a time-stepped manufacturing simulator and as the runtime domain schema for AI analytics tools, producing alignment by construction rather than integration. We formally define the domain template as a typed relational configuration schema and prove that structural alignment between simulation and tool layers is guaranteed by single-source consumption. A five-layer pipeline--simulation, PostgreSQL, CDC/Iceberg lakehouse, star schema, and 12 parameterized AI tools--generates causally coherent, MES-shaped data spanning 66 entity types across four operational domains mapped to ISA-95/IEC 62264. We validate the architecture with six industry templates (aerospace, pharma, automotive, electronics, beverages, warehousing) running on identical framework code. Calibration experiments (60 runs, 10 seeds per template) confirm parametric controllability: observed KPIs fall within configured ranges across all templates. A controlled hallucination experiment (72 tool invocations, Qwen3-32B) demonstrates that ontology-constrained parameters eliminate tool-parameter fabrication (0% constrained vs. 43% unconstrained hallucination rate for the evaluated model, Fisher's exact test p < 10^-12); the 0% constrained rate is an architectural guarantee that holds for any model. The framework provides a reusable data layer for discrete manufacturing AI validation.

</details>


### [15] [LatentRouter: Can We Choose the Right Multimodal Model Before Seeing Its Answer?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11301)
*Xueqi Cheng,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LatentRouter is a novel MLLM routing system that uses latent communication between multimodal routing capsules and model capability tokens to predict counterfactual performance of candidate models, enabling intelligent model selection based on both performance and cost trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs have heterogeneous strengths across different capabilities (OCR, chart understanding, spatial reasoning, VQA, cost, latency), making effective routing challenging. Simple difficulty estimation isn't sufficient - routers need to match specific multimodal requirements of image-question inputs with each model's capabilities.

Method: Formulates MLLM routing as counterfactual multimodal utility prediction. Extracts learned multimodal routing capsules from image-question queries, represents each candidate MLLM with a model capability token, performs latent communication between these states to estimate how each model would perform if selected. Uses distributional outcome head for counterfactual quality prediction and bounded capsule correction for refining close decisions.

Result: Outperforms fixed-model, feature-level, and learned-router baselines on MMR-Bench and VL-RouterBench. Gains strongest on multimodal task groups where model choice depends on visual, layout-sensitive, or reasoning-oriented requirements. Latent communication identified as main contributor to improvement.

Conclusion: LatentRouter provides an effective utility-based routing policy that supports both performance-oriented and performance-cost routing, handles changing candidate pools through shared per-model scoring, and demonstrates superior performance through intelligent matching of multimodal requirements with model capabilities.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have heterogeneous strengths across OCR, chart understanding, spatial reasoning, visual question answering, cost, and latency. Effective MLLM routing therefore requires more than estimating query difficulty: a router must match the multimodal requirements of the current image-question input with the capabilities of each candidate model. We propose LatentRouter, a router that formulates MLLM routing as counterfactual multimodal utility prediction. Given an image-question query, LatentRouter extracts learned multimodal routing capsules, represents each candidate MLLM with a model capability token, and performs latent communication between these states to estimate how each model would perform if selected. A distributional outcome head predicts model-specific counterfactual quality, while a bounded capsule correction refines close decisions without allowing residual signals to dominate the prediction. The resulting utility-based policy supports performance-oriented and performance-cost routing, and handles changing candidate pools through shared per-model scoring with availability masking. Experiments on MMR-Bench and VL-RouterBench show that LatentRouter outperforms fixed-model, feature-level, and learned-router baselines. Additional analyses show that the gains are strongest on multimodal task groups where model choice depends on visual, layout-sensitive, or reasoning-oriented requirements, and that latent communication is the main contributor to the improvement. The code is available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/LatentRouter.

</details>


### [16] [Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization: Utilizing Data Attribution for Effective Data Pruning in Low-Data Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11312)
*Danilo Brajovic,David A. Kreplin,Marco F. Huber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CDVM method improves data pruning for low-data scenarios by optimizing data attributions with constraints, outperforming Shapley-based approaches when only limited data is retained.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Shapley-based data attribution methods are suboptimal for pruning low-value data when only limited data remains after pruning, creating a need for better approaches in low-data scenarios.

Method: Introduces Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization (CDVM), which frames data pruning as a constrained optimization problem that maximizes total influence while penalizing excessive per-test contributions for better pruning in low-data regimes.

Result: CDVM demonstrates strong performance on the OpenDataVal benchmark with competitive runtime, showing robustness when only a small fraction of training data is retained.

Conclusion: The CDVM approach effectively addresses limitations of Shapley-based data values for pruning in low-data scenarios, providing a more suitable method for data attribution and removal when limited data remains.

Abstract: Attributing model behavior to training data is an evolving research field. A common benchmark is data removal, which involves eliminating data instances with either low or high values, then assessing a model's performance trained on the modified dataset. Many existing studies leverage Shapley-based data values for this task. In this paper, we demonstrate that these data values are not optimally suited for pruning low-value data when only a limited amount of data remains. To address this limitation, we introduce the Constraint-Data-Value-Maximization (CDVM) approach, which effectively utilizes data attributions for pruning in low-data scenarios. By casting pruning as a constrained optimization that both maximizes total influence and penalizes excessive per-test contributions, CDVM delivers robust performance when only a small fraction of the data is retained. On the OpenDataVal benchmark, CDVM shows strong performance and competitive runtime.

</details>


### [17] [Rethinking Evaluation for LLM Hallucination Detection: A Desiderata, A New RAG-based Benchmark, New Insights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11330)
*Wenbo Chen,Veena Padmanabhan,Tootiya Giyahchi,Elaine Wong,Leman Akoglu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies gaps in existing hallucination detection benchmarks and introduces T RIVIA+, a new RAG-based benchmark with long contexts and realistic label noise, showing current detectors have room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detection benchmarks lack critical properties needed for effective evaluation: they lack RAG-based grounded benchmarks with long contexts (due to annotation challenges) and don't provide realistic label noise for stress-testing detectors, despite real-world applications facing noisy annotations.

Method: The authors first established desiderata for hallucination detection benchmarks, analyzed existing benchmarks against these criteria, identified major gaps, then built T RIVIA+ - a new RAG-based benchmark with rigorous human annotation featuring long contexts and four sets of noisy labels with different noise schemes (both sample-dependent and sample-independent).

Result: Experiments on RAG-based benchmarks including T RIVIA+ reveal: (1) significant room for improvement for current SOTA detectors, (2) the basic LLM-as-a-Judge baseline performs competitively, and (3) label noise significantly hinders detection performance.

Conclusion: The findings and the proposed T RIVIA+ benchmark will motivate and foster needed research on hallucination detection for RAG-based tasks, addressing critical gaps in current evaluation infrastructure.

Abstract: Hallucination, broadly referring to unfaithful, fabricated, or inconsistent content generated by LLMs, has wide-ranging implications. Therefore, a large body of effort has been devoted to detecting LLM hallucinations, as well as designing benchmark datasets for evaluating these detectors. In this work, we first establish a desiderata of properties for hallucination detection benchmarks (HDBs) to exhibit for effective evaluation. A critical look at existing HDBs through the lens of our desiderata reveals that none of them exhibits all the properties. We identify two largest gaps: (1) RAG-based grounded benchmarks with long context are severely lacking (partly because length impedes human annotation); and (2) Existing benchmarks do not make available realistic label noise for stress-testing detectors although real-world use-cases often grapple with label noise due to human or automated/weak annotation. To close these gaps, we build and open-source a new RAG-based HDB called T RIVIA+ that underwent a rigorous human annotation process. Notably, our benchmark exhibits all desirable properties including (1) T RIVIA+ contains samples with the longest context in the literature; and (2) we design and share four sets of noisy labels with different, both sample-dependent and sampleindependent, noise schemes. Finally, we perform experiments on RAG-based HDBs, including our T RIVIA+, using popular SOTA detectors that reveal new insights: (i) ample room remains for current detectors to reach the performance ceiling on RAG-based HDBs, (ii) the basic LLM-as-a-Judge baseline performs competitively, and (iii) label noise hinders detection performance. We expect that our findings, along with our proposed benchmark 1 , will motivate and foster needed research on hallucination detection for RAG-based tasks.

</details>


### [18] [CPEMH: An Agentic Framework for Prompt-Driven Behavior Evaluation and Assurance in Foundation-Model Systems for Mental Health Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11341)
*Giuliano Lorenzoni,Ivens Portugal,Paulo Alencar,Donald Cowan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CPEMH is an agentic framework for evaluating prompt-driven behavior in foundation models used for mental health screening, featuring autonomous orchestration of prompt design, evaluation, and selection to control behavioral variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the need for behavioral assurance in large-scale language systems operating on transcript-based datasets for mental health screening, particularly focusing on controlling behavioral variability and ensuring reliable performance in clinically sensitive domains.

Method: CPEMH uses a modular agentic design with three main components: orchestrator, inference, and evaluation agents. This orchestrated architecture autonomously performs the design, evaluation, and selection of prompt strategies throughout the prompting lifecycle.

Result: A case study on automated depression screening from interview transcripts demonstrates the framework's ability to stabilize and audit foundation-model behavior in conversational and clinically sensitive domains, showing improved control over behavioral variability.

Conclusion: The framework emphasizes modular orchestration for behavioral assurance, prioritizing stability over architectural complexity, and integrating F1, bias, and robustness as core acceptance criteria for reliable mental health screening systems.

Abstract: This paper presents CPEMH, an agentic framework designed to evaluate prompt-driven behavior in foundation-model systems operating on transcript-based datasets for mental-health screening. CPEMH serves as an engineering methodology for behavioral assurance in large-scale language systems, introducing an orchestrated architecture that autonomously performs the design, evaluation, and selection of prompt strategies, enabling systematic control of behavioral variability across contexts. Its modular agentic design, combining orchestrator, inference, and evaluation agents, ensures traceability, reproducibility, and robustness throughout the prompting lifecycle. A case study on automated depression screening from interview transcripts demonstrates the framework's capacity to stabilize and audit foundation-model behavior in conversational and clinically sensitive domains. Lessons learned emphasize the role of modular orchestration in behavioral assurance, the prioritization of stability over architectural complexity, and the integration of F1, bias, and robustness as core acceptance criteria.

</details>


### [19] [CVEvolve: Autonomous Algorithm Discovery for Unstructured Scientific Data Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11359)
*Ming Du,Xiangyu Yin,Yanqi Luo,Dishant Beniwal,Songyuan Tang,Hemant Sharma,Mathew J. Cherukara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CVEvolve is an autonomous AI agent system that enables domain scientists to discover scientific data processing algorithms through a zero-code interface, addressing expertise barriers in handling noisy, complex scientific data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific data processing requires specialized algorithms or AI models, creating barriers for domain scientists who lack computing/image-processing expertise, especially when dealing with noisy, high-dynamic-range, sparsely labeled, or loosely specified data.

Method: CVEvolve uses an autonomous agentic harness with multi-round search strategy, combining tools for code execution, evaluation implementation, history management, holdout testing, and optional data visualization. It employs lineage-aware stochastic candidate sampling to balance exploration and exploitation, alternating between discovery and improvement actions.

Result: CVEvolve successfully discovers algorithms that outperform baseline methods in x-ray fluorescence microscopy image registration, Bragg peak detection, and high-energy diffraction microscopy image segmentation. Holdout test tracking helps identify candidates with better generalization than over-optimized alternatives.

Conclusion: Zero-code, autonomous LLM-powered algorithm development can effectively help domain scientists transform unstructured scientific image data into practical algorithms and enable downstream scientific discoveries.

Abstract: Scientific data processing often requires task-specific algorithms or AI models, creating a barrier for domain scientists who need to analyze their data but may not have extensive computing or image-processing expertise. This barrier is especially pronounced when data are noisy, have a high dynamic range, are sparsely labeled, or are only loosely specified. We introduce CVEvolve, an autonomous agentic harness with a zero-code interface for scientific data-processing algorithm discovery. CVEvolve combines a multi-round search strategy with tools for code execution, evaluation implementation, history management, holdout testing, and optional inspection of scientific data and visual outputs. The search alternates between discovery and improvement actions, and uses lineage-aware stochastic candidate sampling to balance exploration and exploitation. We demonstrate CVEvolve on x-ray fluorescence microscopy image registration, Bragg peak detection, and high-energy diffraction microscopy image segmentation. Across these tasks, CVEvolve discovers algorithms that improve over baseline methods, while holdout test tracking helps identify candidates that generalize better than later over-optimized alternatives. These results show that zero-code, autonomous LLM-powered algorithm development can help domain scientists turn unstructured scientific image data into practical algorithms and downstream scientific discoveries.

</details>


### [20] [Causal Bias Detection in Generative Artifical Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11365)
*Drago Plecko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Causal fairness framework for generative AI that extends beyond standard ML fairness, enabling decomposition of bias along causal pathways and model mechanism replacements, with applications to race and gender bias in large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems in high-stakes domains raise fairness concerns, but existing causal fairness methods focus on standard ML settings where models learn a single predictive function. Generative AI is more complex as it constructs beliefs about all causal mechanisms, requiring new fairness methodologies.

Method: Formalizes causal fairness in generative AI and unifies it with standard ML under a common theoretical framework. Derives new causal decomposition results for quantifying fairness impacts along different causal pathways and mechanism replacements. Establishes identification conditions and introduces efficient estimators.

Result: Develops a comprehensive framework for analyzing fairness in generative AI models, enabling granular bias quantification. Demonstrates practical value by analyzing race and gender bias in large language models across different datasets.

Conclusion: Generative AI requires new causal fairness approaches due to its fundamental differences from standard ML, and the proposed framework provides principled tools for understanding and quantifying bias in these complex systems.

Abstract: Automated systems built on artificial intelligence (AI) are increasingly deployed across high-stakes domains, raising critical concerns about fairness and the perpetuation of demographic disparities that exist in the world. In this context, causal inference provides a principled framework for reasoning about fairness, as it links observed disparities to underlying mechanisms and aligns naturally with human intuition and legal notions of discrimination. Prior work on causal fairness primarily focuses on the standard machine learning setting, where a decision-maker constructs a single predictive mechanism $f_{\widehat Y}$ for an outcome variable $Y$, while inheriting the causal mechanisms of all other covariates from the real world. The generative AI setting, however, is markedly more complex: generative models can sample from arbitrary conditionals over any set of variables, implicitly constructing their own beliefs about all causal mechanisms rather than learning a single predictive function. This fundamental difference requires new developments in causal fairness methodology. We formalize the problem of causal fairness in generative AI and unify it with the standard ML setting under a common theoretical framework. We then derive new causal decomposition results that enable granular quantification of fairness impacts along both (a) different causal pathways and (b) the replacement of real-world mechanisms by the generative model's mechanisms. We establish identification conditions and introduce efficient estimators for causal quantities of interest, and demonstrate the value of our methodology by analyzing race and gender bias in large language models across different datasets.

</details>


### [21] [Causal Algorithmic Recourse: Foundations and Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11373)
*Drago Plecko,Collin Wang,Elias Bareinboim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal framework for algorithmic recourse that models it as a process over time with partial stability, introduces methods for inferring recourse effects from observational or paired data, and provides distribution-free learning when model assumptions fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing algorithmic recourse approaches treat recommendations as one-time counterfactuals for a fixed unit, ignoring real-world complexities where recourse involves repeated decisions on the same individual under varying latent conditions.

Method: Develops a causal framework modeling recourse as a process over pre- and post-intervention outcomes with partial stability. Introduces post-recourse stability conditions, a copula-based algorithm for observational data inference, methods for parameter estimation and goodness-of-fit testing with paired data, and a distribution-free algorithm for when copula models are rejected.

Result: Proposed methods demonstrate value on real and semi-synthetic datasets, providing ways to reason about recourse effects from both observational data alone and paired observations of the same individual before/after intervention.

Conclusion: The framework addresses limitations of existing recourse approaches by modeling the dynamic nature of real-world recourse processes, enabling more realistic inference of recourse effects under varying conditions.

Abstract: The trustworthiness of AI decision-making systems is increasingly important. A key feature of such systems is the ability to provide recommendations for how an individual may reverse a negative decision, a problem known as algorithmic recourse. Existing approaches treat recourse outcomes as counterfactuals of a fixed unit, ignoring that real-world recourse involves repeated decisions on the same individual under possibly different latent conditions. We develop a causal framework that models recourse as a process over pre- and post-intervention outcomes, allowing for partial stability and resampling of latent variables. We introduce post-recourse stability conditions that enable reasoning about recourse from observational data alone, and develop a copula-based algorithm for inferring the effects of recourse under these conditions. For settings where paired observations of the same individual before and after intervention are available (called recourse data), we develop methods for inferring copula parameters and performing goodness-of-fit testing. When the copula model is rejected, we provide a distribution-free algorithm for learning recourse effects directly from recourse data. We demonstrate the value of the proposed methods on real and semi-synthetic datasets.

</details>


### [22] [LLM-X: A Scalable Negotiation-Oriented Exchange for Communication Among Personal LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11376)
*Giuliano Lorenzoni,Paulo Alencar,Donald Cowan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-X is a scalable negotiation environment that enables direct LLM-to-LLM communication with policy enforcement, evaluated through large-scale multi-agent negotiations showing clear policy-performance tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing protocols focus on agent-API interactions rather than direct LLM-to-LLM coordination. There's a need for scalable negotiation environments with policy enforcement and schema validity guarantees for personal agents representing individual users.

Method: Proposed LLM-X architecture with federated gateways, topic-based routing, and policy enforcement. Introduced typed message protocol supporting capability negotiation and contract-net-style coordination. Conducted empirical evaluation with 5, 9, and 12 agents under different negotiation policies (Low, Medium, High) across both short-run and long-run (2h, 12h) load conditions.

Result: Clear policy-performance trade-offs observed: stricter policies improve robustness and fairness but increase latencies and message volume. System remained stable under sustained load with bounded latency drift in extended runs.

Conclusion: LLM-X provides a practical framework for scalable LLM-based multi-agent negotiation with policy enforcement, demonstrating that while stricter policies trade off performance for robustness, the system maintains stability under sustained operation.

Abstract: We propose a personal-LLM exchange (LLM-X), a scalable negotiation-oriented environment that enables direct, structured communication across populations of personal agents (LLMs), each representing an individual user. Unlike existing tool-centric protocols that focus on agent-API interaction, LLM-X introduces a message bus and routing substrate for LLM-to-LLM coordination with guarantees around schema validity and policy enforcement. We contribute: (1) an architecture for LLM-X comprising federated gateways, topic-based routing, and policy enforcement; (2) a typed message protocol supporting capability negotiation and contract-net-style coordination; and (3) the first empirical evaluation of LLM-based multi-agent negotiation at scale. Experiments span 5, 9, and 12 agents, under distinct negotiation policies (Low, Medium, High), and across both short-run (minutes) and long-run (2h, 12h) load conditions. Results highlight clear policy-performance trade-offs: stricter policies improve robustness and fairness but increase latencies and message volume. Extended runs confirm that LLM-X remains stable under sustained load, with bounded latency drift.

</details>


### [23] [Revisiting Privacy Preservation in Brain-Computer Interfaces: Conceptual Boundaries, Risk Pathways, and a Protection-Strength Grading Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11386)
*Lei Sun,Xiuqing Mao,Shuai Zhang,Qingyu Zeng,Min Zhao,Jiyuan Li,Wenle Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A review proposing a privacy protection framework for BCIs across data and model levels with strength grading and emphasis on neuroethical risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As BCIs move from labs to real-world applications, privacy risks extend beyond raw neural signal leakage to include derived data, models, and decoded outputs that can be re-associated with individuals or infer sensitive information beyond task requirements.

Method: The review proposes a three-dimensional framework (protection object, lifecycle stage, protection-strength level) to classify existing work into four protection strength levels, while treating mental privacy and neuroethical risks as open issues.

Result: A structured privacy protection classification system for BCIs that addresses data and model privacy across the entire lifecycle, emphasizing the need to disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information while preserving utility.

Conclusion: BCI privacy protection should not only obscure data but also actively disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information while maintaining downstream utility, with mental privacy and neuroethical considerations requiring ongoing attention as BCIs become more widespread.

Abstract: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) are moving rapidly from laboratory research into clinical, edge, and real-world settings. Under ISO/IEC 8663:2025, a BCI is a direct communication link between central nervous system activity and external software or hardware systems. This link expands privacy risk beyond raw neural-signal leakage: neural data, derived representations, model assets, and decoded outputs can be re-associated with individuals across collection, transmission, storage, training, inference, and feedback, or used to infer information beyond what a task requires. Starting from the general BCI paradigm, this review deffnes privacy-protection boundaries, protection objects, and the relationship between user data privacy and model privacy within a shared risk pathway. It then proposes a three-dimensional framework - protection object, lifecycle stage, and dominant protection-strength level - to classify existing work into four levels of protection strength. Finally, mental privacy and neuroethical risks are treated as open issues, emphasizing that BCI privacy protection should not only obscure data but also disentangle task-irrelevant sensitive information while preserving downstream utility. Keywords: Brain-computer interface, Neural data privacy, User data privacy, Model privacy, Disentanglement of task-irrelevant sensitive information, Protection-strength grading, Neuroethical risks

</details>


### [24] [Transformer Interpretability from Perspective of Attention and Gradient](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11392)
*Yongjin Cui,Xiaohui Fan,Huajun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method for Transformer interpretation by guiding gradient/attention direction to provide comprehensive feature region analysis, with applications showing security vulnerabilities in ViT models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers have focused more on Transformer performance while interpretation remains crucial. Gradient-based methods are widely used but need deeper exploration from attention and gradient perspectives.

Method: Proposes a Transformer interpretation method that guides gradient direction (specifically attention direction) to achieve comprehensive feature region interpretation and detailed analysis.

Result: The method enables comprehensive interpretation of feature regions, provides detailed analysis, and helps understand Transformer mechanisms. Demonstrates security vulnerabilities by altering image classes imperceptibly to humans using differences in ViT vs. human perception.

Conclusion: The gradient/attention direction guidance method advances Transformer interpretation capabilities. The identified class rewriting phenomenon in ViT models poses potential security risks in certain applications due to differences in visual perception between models and humans.

Abstract: Although researchers' attention is more focused on the performance of Transformer models, the interpretation of Transformer can never be ignored. Gradient is widely utilized in Transformer interpretation. From the perspective of attention and gradient, we conduct an in-depth study of Transformer interpretation and propose a method to achieve it by guiding the gradient direction, or more precisely, the attention direction. The method enables more comprehensive interpretation of feature regions, offers detail interpretation, and helps to better understand Transformer mechanism. Leveraging the difference in how Vision Transformer (ViT) and humans perceive images, we alter the class of an image in a way that is almost imperceptible to the human eye. This class rewriting phenomenon may potentially pose security risks in certain scenarios.

</details>


### [25] [AcuityBench: Evaluating Clinical Acuity Identification and Uncertainty Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11398)
*Robin Linzmayer,Georgianna Lin,Di Coneybeare,Jason Chu,Trudi Cloyd,Manish Garg,Miles Gordon,Elizabeth Hartofilis,Benjamin Hong,Ashraf Hussain,Eugene Y. Kim,Oluchi Iheagwara King,Ross McCormack,Erica Olsen,John K. Riggins,Mustafa N. Rasheed,Dana L. Sacco,Vinay Saggar,Osman R. Sayan,Amit Shembekar,Janice Shin-Kim,Wendy W. Sun,Bernard P. Chang,David Kessler,Noémie Elhadad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AcuityBench is a benchmark for evaluating language models' ability to identify appropriate medical urgency levels, harmonizing five datasets under a four-level acuity framework with 914 cases including ambiguous ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing health benchmarks focus on medical QA, broad health interactions, or narrow triage tasks, but lack unified evaluation of acuity identification across different settings.

Method: Harmonized five public datasets (user conversations, forum posts, clinical vignettes, patient portal messages) under a shared four-level acuity framework. Created 914 cases with 697 consensus cases and 217 physician-confirmed ambiguous cases. Supports two task formats: explicit four-way classification and free-form conversational responses with rubric-based evaluation.

Result: Substantial variation in acuity accuracy among 12 frontier models. Conversational responses reduce over-triage but increase under-triage compared to QA, especially in higher-acuity cases. No model matches physician judgment distribution in ambiguous cases; model predictions are more concentrated than expert clinical uncertainty.

Conclusion: Acuity identification is a distinct safety-critical capability. AcuityBench enables systematic comparison and stress-testing of models' ability to guide users to appropriate care levels in real-world health use.

Abstract: We introduce AcuityBench, a benchmark for evaluating whether language models identify the appropriate urgency of care from user medical presentations. Existing health benchmarks emphasize medical question answering, broad health interactions, or narrow workflow-specific triage tasks, but they do not offer a unified evaluation of acuity identification across these settings. AcuityBench addresses this gap by harmonizing five public datasets spanning user conversations, online forum posts, clinical vignettes, and patient portal messages under a shared four-level acuity framework ranging from home monitoring to immediate emergency care. The benchmark contains 914 cases, including 697 consensus cases for standard accuracy evaluation and 217 physician-confirmed ambiguous cases for uncertainty-aware evaluation. It supports two complementary task formats: explicit four-way classification in a QA setting, and free-form conversational responses evaluated with a rubric-based judge anchored to the same framework. Across 12 frontier proprietary and open-weight models, we find substantial variation in clear-case acuity accuracy and error direction. Comparing task formats reveals a systematic tradeoff: conversational responses reduce over-triage but increase under-triage relative to QA, especially in higher-acuity cases.
