In ambiguous cases, no model closely matches the distribution of physician judgments, and model predictions are more concentrated than expert clinical uncertainty. We also compare expert and model adjudication on a subset of maximally ambiguous cases, using those cases to examine the role of clinical uncertainty in label disagreement. Together, these results position acuity identification as a distinct safety-critical capability and show that AcuityBench enables systematic comparison and stress-testing of how well models guide users to the right level of care in real-world health use.

</details>


### [26] [Attributing Emergence in Million-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11404)
*Ling Tang,Jilin Mei,Qian Chen,Qihan Ren,Linfeng Zhang,Quanshi Zhang,Jing Shao,Xia Hu,Dongrui Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a scalable attribution method for LLM-powered multi-agent systems that addresses the gap between small-scale studies (N ~ 10^2) and real-world population scales (N ≥ 10^6), showing structural disagreements in attribution between these scales and proving these disagreements cannot be resolved by post-hoc scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing axiomatic attribution methods for multi-agent systems scale combinatorially and are limited to N ≲ 10^3 agents, while real-world social phenomena occur at N ≥ 10^6 agents. There's a critical gap between the scale of existing methods and the scale of phenomena they aim to explain.

Method: Adapted Aumann-Shapley path-integral attribution to LLM-powered multi-agent systems at million-agent scale. This method satisfies all four attribution axioms and runs 4-5 orders of magnitude faster than sampled Shapley on the same hardware.

Result: Empirical test on 14 days of Bluesky data (1,671,587 users) showed structural disagreement: full-scale attribution shows majority carried by long tail and middle tier, while biased small panel attributes almost everything to few high-follower accounts. Proved "Attribution Scaling Bias theorem" showing no global rescaling factor can reconcile small-scale and full-scale attribution for nonlinear macro indicators.

Conclusion: Full-scale attribution is not just a methodological choice but a theoretical requirement for any nonlinear macro indicator analysis in multi-agent systems, as small-scale convenience samples produce structurally biased attribution results that cannot be corrected.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can simulate human-like reasoning and decision-making in individual agents. LLM-powered multi-agent systems (MAS) combine such agents to simulate population-scale social phenomena such as polarization, information cascades, and market panics. Such studies require attributing macro emergence to individual agents, but existing axiomatic methods scale combinatorially in $N$ and have been confined to $N \lesssim 10^3$, while the phenomena they explain occur at $N \geq 10^6$. We address this gap by adapting Aumann--Shapley path-integral attribution to LLM-powered MAS at million-agent scale; the resulting method satisfies all four axioms, runs four to five orders of magnitude faster than sampled Shapley on the same hardware. We use this method to test the scale gap empirically: across 14 days of public Bluesky data ($1{,}671{,}587$ active users), we compute the attribution at both full scale and the visibility-biased $N = 10^2$ convenience sample used by small-scale studies, and the two disagree structurally. At full scale the long tail and middle tier jointly carry the majority; the biased small panel attributes almost everything to a few high-follower accounts. We then prove that under any nonlinear macro indicator the disagreement cannot be reduced by post-hoc rescaling: an Attribution Scaling Bias theorem shows that no global rescaling factor can reconcile small-scale and full-scale attribution. Full-scale attribution is therefore not a methodological choice but a theoretical requirement for any nonlinear macro indicator.

</details>


### [27] [What Do EEG Foundation Models Capture from Human Brain Signals?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11410)
*Ling Tang,Qian Chen,Jilin Mei,Houshi Xu,Quanshi Zhang,Jing Shao,Na Zou,Xia Hu,Dongrui Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EEG foundation models learn similar representations to traditional hand-crafted features, with 68.6% of features being representation-causal and frequency-domain features dominating across clinical tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether modern EEG foundation models that learn directly from raw signals via self-supervised pretraining align with traditional hand-crafted EEG feature catalogs, and to decompose this alignment into three sub-questions: what the model learns, what the model uses, and how much can be explained.

Method: Used layer-wise ridge probing, LEACE-style cross-covariance subspace erasure, and transparent classifier benchmarked against random-feature baseline. Audited three foundation models (CSBrain, CBraMod, LaBraM) on five clinical tasks (MDD, Stress, ISRUC-Sleep, TUSL, Siena) with a 6-family 63-feature lexicon.

Result: Of 945 (model, task, feature) units, 648 (68.6%) were representation-causal and 199 (21.1%) were encoded-only. Across tasks, 50 features qualified as universal candidates. Frequency-domain features dominated, but all six feature families contributed substantial causal mass. Confirmed features recovered an average of 79.3% of foundation models' advantage over random baseline, with task-dependent recovery rates from MDD (≈99%) down to Stress (≈56%).

Conclusion: EEG foundation models largely align with traditional feature catalogs, with frequency-domain features being most prominent. The method provides a framework for understanding what foundation models learn, with tasks near ceiling almost fully recoverable by traditional features while harder tasks leave residual knowledge that points to targets for future concept discovery.

Abstract: Clinical electroencephalogram (EEG) analysis rests on a hand-crafted feature catalog refined over decades, \emph{e.g.,} band power, connectivity, complexity, and more. Modern EEG foundation models bypass this catalog, learn directly from raw signals via self-supervised pretraining, and match or outperform feature-engineered baselines on most clinical benchmarks. Whether the two representations align is an open question, which we decompose into three sub-questions: \emph{what does the model learn}, \emph{what does the model use}, and \emph{how much can be explained}. We answer them with layer-wise ridge probing, LEACE-style cross-covariance subspace erasure, and a transparent classifier benchmarked against a random-feature baseline. The audit covers three foundation models (CSBrain, CBraMod, LaBraM), five clinical tasks (MDD, Stress, ISRUC-Sleep, TUSL, Siena), and a 6-family 63-feature lexicon. Of the $945$ (model, task, feature) units, $648$ ($68.6\%$) are representation-causal and $199$ ($21.1\%$) are encoded-only. Across tasks, $50$ features qualify as universal candidates with strong support (all three architectures RC) in two or more tasks. Frequency-domain features dominate, but the other five families each contribute substantial causal mass. Confirmed features recover, on average, $79.3\%$ of the foundation model's advantage over the random baseline, with a clean task gradient (MDD $\approx 0.99$ down to Stress $\approx 0.56$): tasks near ceiling are almost fully recovered by the lexicon, while harder tasks leave a non-trivial residual that pinpoints a concrete target for future concept discovery.

</details>


### [28] [Under the Hood of SKILL.md: Semantic Supply-chain Attacks on AI Agent Skill Registry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11418)
*Shoumik Saha,Kazem Faghih,Soheil Feizi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SKILL.md files in AI agent systems represent semantic supply-chain risk, where natural language metadata can be manipulated to affect skill discovery, selection, and governance, enabling adversarial control over agent capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous AI agents increasingly rely on modular Agent Skills with SKILL.md documentation, these natural-language files become operational text rather than passive documentation, creating semantic supply-chain vulnerabilities where attackers can manipulate how skills are discovered, selected, and governed.

Method: The study investigates SKILL.md-only attacks across three registry-facing stages: Discovery (using embedding-based retrieval manipulation), Selection (description-only framing biases), and Governance (semantic evasion strategies). Experiments use real ClawHub skills and realistic registry mechanisms.

Result: Discovery attacks achieved up to 86% pairwise win rate and 80% Top-10 placement; Selection attacks caused adversarial variants to be selected in 77.6% of trials; Governance evasion succeeded in 36.5%-100% of cases. SKILL.md significantly influences which third-party capabilities agents adopt.

Conclusion: SKILL.md files are not passive documentation but operational text that shapes agent capability adoption. The semantic supply-chain risk requires defenses against natural language manipulation in skill discovery, selection, and governance stages of AI agent ecosystems.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents increasingly extend their capabilities through Agent Skills: modular filesystem packages whose SKILL.md files describe when and how agents should use them. While this design enables scalable, on-demand capability expansion, it also introduces a semantic supply-chain risk in which natural-language metadata and instructions can affect which skills are admitted, surfaced, selected, and loaded. We study SKILL.md - only attacks across three registry-facing stages of the Agent Skill lifecycle, using real ClawHub skills and realistic registry mechanisms. In Discovery, short textual triggers can manipulate embedding-based retrieval and improve adversarial skill visibility, achieving up to 86% pairwise win rate and 80% Top-10 placement. In Selection, description-only framing biases agents toward functionally equivalent adversarial variants, which are selected in 77.6% of paired trials on average. In Governance, semantic evasion strategies cause malicious skills to avoid a blocking verdict in 36.5%-100% of cases. Overall, our results show that SKILL.md is not passive documentation but operational text that shapes which third-party capabilities agents find, trust, and use.

</details>


### [29] [A Mechanistic Investigation of Supervised Fine Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11426)
*Ruhaan Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SFT causes minimal geometric changes in LLM activations but significant divergence in sparse latent representations, revealed through pretrained Sparse Autoencoders as diagnostic tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite high cosine similarity between pre- and post-SFT hidden activations suggesting minimal geometric changes, there's a need to understand the underlying representational divergence that might be masked by this surface-level similarity.

Method: Introduced an investigative pipeline using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as high-resolution diagnostic tools to mechanistically examine representational divergence between base and SFT models. Projected activations through SAEs trained on base models to reveal underlying sparse latents.

Result: Discovered task-specific and layer-specific distributions of semantic features systematically altered during SFT. Identified layer-wise update profiles specific to safety alignment. Found that while activation geometry appears similar, sparse latent representations diverge significantly.

Conclusion: Pretrained SAEs serve as powerful diagnostic tools for understanding representational changes in LLMs during fine-tuning, revealing hidden divergence beneath superficial similarity and providing insights into task-specific and safety-alignment mechanisms.

Abstract: The cosine similarity between a large language model's hidden activations before and after Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) remains very high. This, at first glance, suggests that SFT leaves the model's activation geometry largely undisturbed. However, projecting both sets of activations through a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) pretrained on the base model reveals that the underlying sparse latents diverge significantly. We introduce a novel investigative pipeline which utilizes these pretrained SAEs as a high-resolution diagnostic tool to mechanistically investigate the drivers of this representational divergence. Through our analytical pipeline, we discover task-specific and layer-specific distributions of the precise semantic features that are systematically altered during supervised fine-tuning. We additionally identify a layer-wise update profile specific to safety alignment. All code, experimental scripts, and analysis files associated with this work are publicly available at: https://github.com/ruhzi/sae-investigation.

</details>


### [30] [Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11458)
*Zihao Han,Tiangang Zhang,Huaibin Wang,Yilun Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation (ATESD) introduces a learnable controller that adjusts how much reference reasoning the teacher sees during on-policy self-distillation, outperforming fixed-exposure methods by better matching teacher supervision to student competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies a teacher-side exposure mismatch problem in on-policy self-distillation: when teachers condition on reasoning far beyond students' current competence, the resulting token targets become too strong to absorb effectively. Fixed full exposure is not reliably optimal, and student-teacher mismatch grows monotonically with more privileged reasoning.

Method: ATESD treats teacher exposure as a learnable training-time control variable. It models the reveal ratio with a lightweight Beta-policy controller conditioned on compact training-state statistics, using one sampled exposure for short hold windows of student updates. The controller is optimized with a discounted learning-progress reward that scores decisions by their effect on future student improvement rather than immediate loss changes.

Result: Experiments on AIME 24, AIME 25, and HMMT 25 across Qwen3-{1.7B, 4B, 8B} show ATESD consistently outperforms competitive self-distillation and RL baselines, improving over OPSD by +0.95, +2.05, and +2.33 Average@12 points respectively.

Conclusion: Adaptive teacher exposure is established as an effective new axis for reasoning self-distillation, with ATESD demonstrating that dynamically adjusting how much reference reasoning teachers see significantly improves student learning outcomes.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation has become a strong recipe for LLM reasoning, where a privileged teacher supervises the student's own rollouts while conditioning on the reference solution. A design choice shared by nearly all such methods, however, has gone unquestioned: the teacher always sees the full reference reasoning. We argue that this default itself is part of the problem and identify a teacher-side exposure mismatch: when the teacher conditions on reasoning far beyond the student's current competence, the resulting token targets become too strong to absorb. A controlled fixed-exposure sweep makes this concrete on two fronts: 1) full exposure is not reliably the best choice, and 2) student-teacher mismatch grows monotonically as the teacher sees more privileged reasoning. This motivates treating teacher exposure not as a fixed hyperparameter but as a learnable training-time control variable. We therefore propose Adaptive Teacher Exposure for Self-Distillation (ATESD). ATESD models the reveal ratio with a lightweight Beta-policy controller conditioned on compact training-state statistics, and uses one sampled exposure for a short hold window of student updates. To make this exposure controller learnable, we optimize it with a discounted learning-progress reward that scores each held decision by its effect on the student's future improvement rather than its immediate loss change, addressing the delayed credit assignment induced by on-policy distillation. Experiments on AIME 24, AIME 25, and HMMT 25 across Qwen3-{1.7B, 4B, 8B} show that ATESD consistently outperforms competitive self-distillation and RL baselines, improving over OPSD by +0.95, +2.05, and +2.33 Average@12 points respectively, and establishing adaptive teacher exposure as an effective new axis for reasoning self-distillation.

</details>


### [31] [Breaking $\textit{Winner-Takes-All}$: Cooperative Policy Optimization Improves Diverse LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11461)
*Haoxuan Chen,Tianming Liang,Wei-Shi Zheng,Jian-Fang Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GCPO is a new reinforcement learning approach that shifts from individual competition to team cooperation, improving exploration and diversity in LLM reasoning by rewarding rollouts based on their contribution to team solution coverage rather than individual accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods like GRPO suffer from exploration collapse - models prematurely converge on narrow high-scoring patterns without exploring new solutions. Existing approaches (entropy regularization, diversity bonuses) don't address the fundamental "winner-takes-all" nature where rollouts compete individually rather than cooperate for global diversity.

Method: Group Cooperative Policy Optimization (GCPO) replaces independent rollout scoring with team-level credit assignment. A rollout is rewarded based on its contribution to the team's valid solution coverage, measured as a determinant volume over reward-weighted semantic embeddings. Only correct and non-redundant rollouts contribute. During advantage estimation, GCPO redistributes collective team reward to each rollout according to its average marginal contribution to the team.

Result: Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks show that GCPO significantly improves both reasoning accuracy and solution diversity over existing approaches.

Conclusion: GCPO successfully addresses exploration collapse in RLVR by shifting from rollout competition to team cooperation, optimizing toward non-redundant correct reasoning paths and improving both accuracy and diversity in LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiers (RLVR) has become a central paradigm for improving LLM reasoning, yet popular group-based optimization algorithms like GRPO often suffer from exploration collapse, where the models prematurely converge on a narrow set of high-scoring patterns, lacking the ability to explore new solutions. Recent efforts attempt to alleviate this by adding entropy regularization or diversity bonus. However, these approaches do not change the \textit{winner-takes-all} nature, where rollouts still compete for individual advantage rather than cooperating for maximizing global diversity. In this work, we propose Group Cooperative Policy Optimization (GCPO), which shifts the training paradigm from rollout competition to team cooperation. Specifically, GCPO replaces independent rollout scoring with team-level credit assignment: a rollout is rewarded by how much it contributes to the team's valid solution coverage, rather than its individual accuracy. This coverage is described as a determinant volume over reward-weighted semantic embeddings, where only correct and non-redundant rollouts contribute to this volume. During advantage estimation, GCPO redistributes the collective team reward to each single rollout according to its average marginal contribution to the team. This cooperative training paradigm routes optimization toward non-redundant correct reasoning paths. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GCPO significantly improves both reasoning accuracy and solution diversity over existing approaches. Code will be released at $\href{https://github.com/bradybuddiemarch/gcpo}{this}$.

</details>


### [32] [CAMPA: Efficient and Aligned Multimodal Graph Learning via Decoupled Propagation and Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11468)
*Daohan Su,Hao Liu,Xunkai Li,Yinlin Zhu,Xiong Yongfu,Yi Liu,Hongchao Qin,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAMPA introduces a cross-modal aligned propagation and aggregation framework for decoupled multimodal graph neural networks that addresses modal conflicts while maintaining efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal graph neural networks (MGNNs) using tightly coupled architectures suffer from prohibitive computational overhead, while decoupled approaches face critical bottlenecks from modal conflicts during both propagation and aggregation stages.

Method: CAMPA proposes a two-stage alignment mechanism: 1) cross-modal aligned propagation that injects cross-modal similarity priors into message passing to preserve semantic consistency, and 2) trajectory aligned aggregation using trajectory-level self-attention and cross-attention to capture and align long-range dependencies across modalities and hops.

Result: Extensive experiments on diverse benchmark datasets and tasks show that CAMPA consistently outperforms both coupled and decoupled baselines while preserving the efficiency advantages of the decoupled paradigm.

Conclusion: CAMPA effectively addresses the modal conflict bottleneck in decoupled multimodal graph learning through cross-modal alignment, achieving both superior performance and computational efficiency compared to existing approaches.

Abstract: Multimodal Graph Neural Networks (MGNNs) have shown strong potential for learning from multimodal attributed graphs, yet most existing approaches rely on tightly coupled architectures that suffer from prohibitive computational overhead. In this paper, we present a systematic empirical analysis showing that decoupled MGNNs are substantially more efficient and scalable for large-scale graph learning. However, we identify a critical bottleneck in existing decoupled pipelines, namely modal conflict, which arises in both the propagation and aggregation stages. Specifically, independent multi-hop diffusion causes cross-modal semantic divergence during propagation, while naive fusion fails to align multi-hop feature trajectories during aggregation, jointly limiting effective representation learning. To address this challenge, we propose CAMPA, a Cross-modal Aligned Multimodal Propagation & Aggregation framework for decoupled multimodal graph learning. Concretely, CAMPA introduces a two-stage alignment mechanism: (1) cross-modal aligned propagation, which injects cross-modal similarity priors into message passing to preserve semantic consistency without additional parameter overhead; (2) trajectory aligned aggregation, which leverages trajectory-level self-attention and cross-attention to capture and align long-range dependencies across modalities and hops. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmark datasets and tasks demonstrate that CAMPA consistently outperforms strong coupled and decoupled baselines while preserving the efficiency advantages of the decoupled paradigm.

</details>


### [33] [TOPPO: Rethinking PPO for Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Critic Balancing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11473)
*Yuanpeng Li,Gefei Lin,Annie Qu,Rui Miao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TOPPO addresses critic-side gradient conditioning issues in PPO for multi-task RL, achieving better performance than SAC baselines with fewer parameters and steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAC dominates multi-task RL due to off-policy efficiency, while PPO remains underexplored. The authors identify a previously overlooked problem: critic-side gradient ill-conditioning in PPO for MTRL, causing tail tasks to stall while easy tasks dominate updates.

Method: Propose TOPPO (Tail-Optimized PPO), a reformulation of PPO via "Critic Balancing" modules that improve gradient conditioning and balance learning dynamics across tasks. Unlike prior modular architectures or large models, TOPPO targets optimization bottlenecks within PPO itself.

Result: TOPPO achieves stronger mean and tail-task performance than published SAC-family and ARS-family baselines using substantially fewer parameters and environment steps on Meta-World+ benchmark. Matches or surpasses SAC baselines early in training and maintains superior performance at full budget.

Conclusion: With proper optimization, on-policy methods like PPO can rival or exceed off-policy approaches in multi-task RL, challenging the prevailing reliance on SAC. Highlights critic-side gradient conditioning as the central bottleneck in MTRL.

Abstract: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) and its variants dominate Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning (MTRL) due to their off-policy sample efficiency, while on-policy methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) remain underexplored. We diagnose that PPO in MTRL suffers from a previously overlooked issue: critic-side gradient ill-conditioning, which may cause tail tasks to stall while easy tasks dominate the value function's updates. To address this, we propose TOPPO (Tail-Optimized PPO), a reformulation of PPO via Critic Balancing -- a set of modules that improve gradient conditioning and balance learning dynamics across tasks. Unlike prior approaches that rely on modular architectures or large models, TOPPO targets the optimization bottleneck within PPO itself. Empirically, TOPPO achieves stronger mean and tail-task performance than published SAC-family and ARS-family baselines while using substantially fewer parameters and environment steps on Meta-World+ benchmark. Notably, TOPPO matches or surpasses strong SAC baselines early in training and maintains superior performance at full budget. Ablations confirm the effectiveness of each module in TOPPO and provide insights into their interactions. Our results demonstrate that, with proper optimization, on-policy methods can rival or exceed off-policy approaches in MTRL, challenging the prevailing reliance on SAC and highlighting critic-side gradient conditioning as the central bottleneck.

</details>


### [34] [FibQuant: Universal Vector Quantization for Random-Access KV-Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11478)
*Namyoon Lee,Yongjune Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FibQuant is a universal fixed-rate vector quantizer for KV cache compression that improves on scalar quantization by better matching the geometry of rotated attention activations, achieving 5-34x compression with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The KV cache in long-context LLM inference causes significant memory traffic issues due to its growth with context length, batch size, layers, and heads. Rotation-based scalar codecs help but discard important geometric structure created by normalization steps, leaving room for improvement.

Method: FibQuant replaces scalar tables in rotation-based codecs with a shared radial-angular codebook matched to spherical-Beta distributions. It combines Beta-quantile radii, Fibonacci/Roberts-Kronecker quasi-uniform directions for angular quantization, and multi-restart Lloyd-Max refinement. The method maintains the normalize-rotate-store interface while using vector quantization.

Result: Theoretically proven strict improvement over scalar quantization at matched rates. On GPT-2 KV caches: achieves 5x compression at 0.99 attention cosine similarity up to 34x at 0.95 similarity. On TinyLlama-1.1B: within 0.10 perplexity of fp16 at 4x compression, and 3.6x lower perplexity than scalar TurboQuant at 8x compression.

Conclusion: FibQuant demonstrates that vector quantization can effectively compress KV caches for long-context inference by better matching the geometric properties of rotated attention activations, outperforming scalar approaches while maintaining practical random-access properties.

Abstract: Long-context inference is increasingly a memory-traffic problem. The culprit is the key--value (KV) cache: it grows with context length, batch size, layers, and heads, and it is read at every decoding step. Rotation-based scalar codecs meet this systems constraint by storing a norm, applying a shared random rotation, and quantizing one coordinate at a time. They are universal and random-access, but they discard the geometry created by the normalization step. After a Haar rotation, a block of $k$ consecutive coordinates is not a product source; it is a spherical-Beta source on the unit ball. We introduce \textsc{FibQuant}, a universal fixed-rate vector quantizer that keeps the same normalize--rotate--store interface while replacing scalar tables by a shared radial--angular codebook matched to this canonical source. The codebook combines Beta-quantile radii, Fibonacci\,/\,Roberts--Kronecker quasi-uniform directions, and multi-restart Lloyd--Max refinement. We prove that the resulting vector code strictly improves on its scalar product specialization at matched rate, with a high-rate gain that separates into a cell-shaping factor and a density-matching factor. The same construction gives a dense rate axis, including fractional-bit and sub-one-bit operating points, without calibration or variable-length addresses. On GPT-2 small KV caches, \textsc{FibQuant} traces a memory--fidelity frontier from $5\times$ compression at $0.99$ attention cosine similarity to $34\times$ at $0.95$. End-to-end on TinyLlama-1.1B, it is within $0.10$ perplexity of fp16 at $4\times$ compression and has $3.6\times$ lower perplexity than scalar \textsc{TurboQuant} at $b = 2$ ($8\times$ compression), where scalar random-access quantization begins to fail.

</details>


### [35] [Engagement Process: Rethinking the Temporal Interface of Action and Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11484)
*Jialian Li,Yuchen Cao,Junhong Liu,Weiran Guo,Xutao Wang,Jiaming Song,Jiahao Zhang,Jie Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Engagement Process (EP), a new interaction formalism that explicitly models time in decision-making by decoupling actions and observations into separate event streams, addressing temporal complexities that step-based interfaces miss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current interaction models fail to capture complex temporal interactions in digital/physical environments where actions and observations unfold over different time scales rather than aligning with fixed steps. Step-based interfaces hide important timing behaviors and don't support realistic temporal issues like deliberation latency, delayed feedback, and persistent actions.

Method: The authors propose Engagement Process (EP), an interaction formalism that inherits POMDP's decision-theoretic structure but makes time explicit by representing actions and observations as decoupled event streams along a timeline, rather than as paired updates at fixed decision steps.

Result: EP successfully exposes temporal behaviors hidden by step-based interfaces across toy, LLM-agent, and learning experiments. It enables policies to adapt under explicit time costs and supports richer agent-side organization, multi-rate coordination, and compositional interaction among subsystems.

Conclusion: EP provides a more realistic interaction formalism for modeling complex temporal interactions in modern digital/physical environments by making time explicit in the action-observation interface, addressing limitations of traditional step-based models while maintaining decision-theoretic rigor.

Abstract: Task completion in digital and physical environments increasingly involves complex temporal interaction, where actions and observations unfold over different time scales rather than align with fixed observation--action steps. To model such interactions, we propose \emph{Engagement Process} (EP), an interaction formalism that inherits the decision-theoretic structure of POMDPs while making time explicit in the action--observation interface. EP represents actions and observations as decoupled event streams along time, rather than updates paired at fixed decision steps. This interface captures single-agent timing issues such as deliberation latency, delayed feedback, and persistent actions, while supporting richer agent-side organization, multi-rate coordination, and compositional interaction among subsystems. Across toy, LLM-agent, and learning experiments, EP exposes temporal behaviors hidden by step-based interfaces and enables policies to adapt under explicit time costs.

</details>


### [36] [The Evaluation Differential: When Frontier AI Models Recognise They Are Being Tested](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11496)
*Varad Vishwarupe,Nigel Shadbolt,Marina Jirotka,Ivan Flechais*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Evaluation Differential (ED) framework to address validity problems in AI safety evaluations when models recognize they're being evaluated and behave differently than in deployment, proposing TRACE audit protocol for more disciplined safety claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent evidence shows AI models can recognize when they're being evaluated and alter their behavior, creating validity problems for safety conclusions drawn from evaluations. Examples include Anthropic's BrowseComp incident, Natural Language Autoencoder findings, and OpenAI/Apollo anti-scheming work.

Method: 1. Introduces Evaluation Differential (ED) - a conditional divergence in behavior between recognized-evaluation and deployment contexts; 2. Defines normalized effect-size form (nED) for cross-property comparison; 3. Develops typology of safety claims based on ED status; 4. Proposes TRACE (Test-Recognition Audit for Claim Evaluation) protocol that wraps existing evaluation infrastructure.

Result: Developed a framework to categorize safety claims (ED-stable, ED-degraded, ED-inverted, ED-undetermined) and proposed TRACE protocol that produces restricted claims rather than capability scores. Applied framework retrospectively to three publicly documented evaluation incidents and discussed governance implications.

Conclusion: TRACE protocol disciplines safety claims by making explicit the conditions under which evaluation evidence was produced, addressing the evaluation differential problem. While it doesn't eliminate adversarial adaptation, it improves validity of safety conclusions from frontier evaluations for system cards, conformity assessment, and AI safety institutes.

Abstract: Recent published evidence from frontier laboratories shows that contemporary AI models can recognise evaluation contexts, latently represent them, and behave differently under those contexts than under deployment-continuous conditions. Anthropic's BrowseComp incident, the Natural Language Autoencoder findings on SWE-bench Verified and destructive-coding evaluations, and the OpenAI / Apollo anti-scheming work all document instances of this phenomenon. We argue that these findings create a claim-validity problem for safety conclusions drawn from frontier evaluations. We introduce the Evaluation Differential (ED), a conditional divergence in a target behavioural property between recognised-evaluation and deployment-continuous contexts, define a normalised effect-size form (nED) for cross-property comparison, and prove that marginal evaluation scores cannot identify ED. We develop a typology of safety claims (ED-stable, ED-degraded, ED-inverted, ED-undetermined) by their warrant-status under documented divergence, and specify TRACE (Test-Recognition Audit for Claim Evaluation), an audit protocol that wraps existing evaluation infrastructure and produces restricted claims rather than capability scores. We apply the framework retrospectively to three publicly documented evaluation incidents and discuss governance implications for system cards, conformity assessment, and the international network of AI safety and security institutes. TRACE does not eliminate adversarial adaptation; it disciplines the claims drawn from evaluation evidence by making explicit the conditions under which that evidence was produced.

</details>


### [37] [Selective Off-Policy Reference Tuning with Plan Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11505)
*Duc Anh Le,Tien-Phat Nguyen,Thien Huu Nguyen,Linh Ngo Van,Trung Le*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SORT adds a repair update to GRPO-style RL for reasoning tasks by deriving plans from reference solutions and using token predictability differences under plan conditioning to create selective learning signals from failed rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (like GRPO) helps reasoning but stalls on hard prompts where all sampled rollouts fail, creating a training bottleneck for challenging reasoning tasks.

Method: SORT adds a repair update that doesn't change rollout generation. It derives a plan from reference solution, compares token probabilities with and without that plan, and gives higher weight to tokens that become more predictable under plan conditioning, turning failed prompts into selective learning signals.

Result: Across three backbones and eight reasoning benchmarks, SORT improves over GRPO and guidance baselines, with largest gains on weaker models, demonstrating effective learning from previously unlearnable failed rollouts.

Conclusion: SORT effectively addresses the failure stall problem in GRPO-style RL by transforming all-wrong prompts into structured learning opportunities through plan-conditioned probability comparisons, enabling better learning from challenging reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards helps reasoning, but GRPO-style methods stall on hard prompts where all sampled rollouts fail. SORT adds a repair update for those failures without changing rollout generation: it derives a plan from the reference solution, compares token probabilities with and without that plan, and gives higher weight to tokens that become more predictable under plan conditioning. This turns all-wrong prompts into selective, structure-aware learning signals instead of uniform imitation. Across three backbones and eight reasoning benchmarks, SORT improves over GRPO and guidance baselines, with largest gains on weaker models.

</details>


### [38] [AutoLLMResearch: Training Research Agents for Automating LLM Experiment Configuration -- Learning from Cheap, Optimizing Expensive](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11518)
*Taicheng Guo,Nitesh V. Chawla,Olaf Wiest,Xiangliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoLLMResearch is an agentic framework that automates configuration of expensive LLM experiments by learning from low-fidelity experiments and extrapolating to high-cost settings, overcoming the limitation of traditional methods that rely on extensive trial and error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Configuring scalable LLM experiments (architecture design, hyperparameter tuning, etc.) is crucial but challenging because poor choices waste substantial computational resources. Prior automated methods work for low-cost settings with feasible trial and error, but scalable LLM experiments are too expensive for such extensive iteration. No existing work addresses automation of high-cost LLM experiment configurations, leaving this problem labor-intensive and dependent on expert intuition.

Method: Proposes AutoLLMResearch framework with two key components: 1) LLMConfig-Gym - a multi-fidelity environment encompassing four critical LLM experiment tasks, supported by over one million GPU hours of verifiable experiment outcomes; 2) A structured training pipeline that formulates configuration research as a long-horizon Markov Decision Process and incentivizes cross-fidelity extrapolation reasoning, enabling agents to learn from interaction with the multi-fidelity experimental environment.

Result: Extensive evaluation against diverse strong baselines on held-out experiments demonstrates the framework's effectiveness, generalization, and interpretability. The framework successfully automates identification of promising configurations in expensive LLM settings by learning from low-fidelity experiments and extrapolating to high-cost scenarios.

Conclusion: AutoLLMResearch presents a practical and general solution for scalable real-world LLM experiment automation, addressing the critical gap in automating high-cost LLM experiment configurations and reducing dependence on expert intuition while minimizing computational waste.

Abstract: Effectively configuring scalable large language model (LLM) experiments, spanning architecture design, hyperparameter tuning, and beyond, is crucial for advancing LLM research, as poor configuration choices can waste substantial computational resources and prevent models from realizing their full potential. Prior automated methods are designed for low-cost settings where repeated trial and error is feasible, but scalable LLM experiments are too expensive for such extensive iteration. To our knowledge, no work has addressed the automation of high-cost LLM experiment configurations, leaving this problem labor-intensive and dependent on expert intuition. Motivated by this gap, we propose AutoLLMResearch, an agentic framework that mimics how human researchers learn generalizable principles from low-fidelity experiments and extrapolate to efficiently identify promising configurations in expensive LLM settings. The core challenge is how to enable an agent to learn, through interaction with a multi-fidelity experimental environment that captures the structure of the LLM configuration landscape. To achieve this, we propose a systematic framework with two key components: 1) LLMConfig-Gym, a multi-fidelity environment encompassing four critical LLM experiment tasks, supported by over one million GPU hours of verifiable experiment outcomes; 2) A structured training pipeline that formulates configuration research as a long-horizon Markov Decision Process and accordingly incentivizes cross-fidelity extrapolation reasoning. Extensive evaluation against diverse strong baselines on held-out experiments demonstrates the effectiveness, generalization, and interpretability of our framework, supporting its potential as a practical and general solution for scalable real-world LLM experiment automation.

</details>


### [39] [Controllable User Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11519)
*Guy Tennenholtz,Ofer Meshi,Amir Globerson,Uri Shalit,Jihwan Jeong,Craig Boutilier*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Standard methods for training conversational AI simulators via supervised fine-tuning on trajectory data create structurally biased models due to look-ahead bias, causing controllability collapse where evaluation variance explodes. The paper proposes causally consistent training methods that eliminate this bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline evaluation of conversational agents fails to cover rare scenarios or test new policies, motivating controllable user simulators. However, current approaches using LLM prompting/fine-tuning suffer from structural biases that break causal consistency.

Method: Formalizes controllable simulation as causal inference problem, bridging natural language evaluation with off-policy evaluation methodology. Proposes three practical training mitigations: a priori controls, step-wise dynamic controls, and direct policy-conditioned learning to restore causal consistency.

Result: Standard global controls distort conversational distributions and collapse behavioral diversity, while the proposed causally grounded simulators eliminate look-ahead bias, preserve natural variance, and show robust zero-shot generalization to unseen agent behaviors.

Conclusion: Standard supervised fine-tuning on post-hoc trajectory labels creates structurally biased simulators due to look-ahead bias. Causal consistency is essential for accurate simulation, and the proposed methods successfully restore it while enabling robust evaluation.

Abstract: Using offline datasets to evaluate conversational agents often fails to cover rare scenarios or to support testing new policies. This has motivated the use of controllable user simulators for targeted, counterfactual evaluation, typically implemented by prompting or fine-tuning large language models. In this work, we formalize controllable simulation as a causal inference problem. By bridging natural language evaluation with off-policy evaluation methodology, we show that the standard practice of training simulators via supervised fine-tuning on post-hoc trajectory labels yields a structurally biased model. Specifically, these labels are inextricably coupled to the data-generating behavior policy, injecting a look-ahead bias that breaks causal consistency. Furthermore, we prove that under policy shift this failure causes the variance of evaluation metrics to explode geometrically, a phenomenon we term controllability collapse. To restore causal consistency, we establish theoretical conditions for accurate simulation and propose practical training mitigations: a priori controls, step-wise dynamic controls, and direct policy-conditioned learning. Empirical evaluation confirms that while standard global controls distort conversational distributions and collapse behavioral diversity, our causally grounded simulators eliminate look-ahead bias, preserve natural variance, and exhibit robust zero-shot generalization to unseen agent behaviors.

</details>


### [40] [Read, Grep, and Synthesize: Diagnosing Cross-Domain Seed Exposure for LLM Research Ideation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11532)
*Yunju Choi,Min Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PaperGym investigates whether LLM ideation systems benefit from cross-domain retrieval or just diverse exposure, finding that diverse seed exposure helps but systems don't yet exploit semantic reasoning behind retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether current LLM ideation systems benefit from targeted cross-domain retrieval or simply from exposure to diverse mechanisms, building on the observation that ML methodology discovery often involves technique migration across domains.

Method: PaperGym, a three-stage pipeline: (1) tool-augmented seed extraction via read, grep, and bash over isolated paper environment, (2) cross-domain seed retrieval via paraphrasing across seven ML domains, and (3) method synthesis from retrieved seeds scored by rubric-based judges.

Result: Tool-augmented extraction improves specificity, paraphrase-based retrieval broadens domain coverage. Cross-domain retrieval receives more pairwise novelty wins than no-retrieval and same-domain baselines, but shows no significant difference from random diverse-seed control.

Conclusion: LLM ideation systems benefit from diverse seed exposure, but do not yet reliably exploit the semantic reason particular seeds were retrieved, suggesting systems don't effectively leverage the reasoning behind cross-domain retrieval.

Abstract: The discovery of novel methodologies for emerging problems is a continuing cycle in ML, often driven by the migration of techniques across domains. Building on this observation, we ask whether current LLM ideation systems benefit from targeted cross-domain retrieval or simply from exposure to diverse mechanisms. We study this question through PaperGym, a three-stage pipeline: (1) tool-augmented seed extraction via read, grep, and bash over an isolated paper environment, (2) cross-domain seed retrieval via paraphrasing across seven ML domains, and (3) method synthesis from retrieved seeds, each scored by rubric-based judges. Tool-augmented extraction improves specificity, and paraphrase-based retrieval broadens domain coverage. In synthesis, cross-domain retrieval receives more pairwise novelty wins than no-retrieval and same-domain baselines, but shows no significant difference from a random diverse-seed control. These findings suggest LLM ideation systems benefit from diverse seed exposure, but do not yet reliably exploit the semantic reason particular seeds were retrieved. We release the seed library, rubric prompts, and run scripts at https://github.com/yunjoochoi/PaperGym

</details>


### [41] [Optimal LTLf Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11544)
*Yujian Cao,Sven Schewe,Qiyi Tang,Shufang Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces optimal LTLf synthesis that aims to realize as many objectives as possible when not all can be guaranteed, with three approaches: max-guarantee, max-observation, and incremental max-observation synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional strategy synthesis follows an all-or-nothing approach - it returns "unrealisable" when specifications cannot be fully guaranteed in uncertain environments. This is too restrictive for practical scenarios where partial satisfaction of multiple objectives is valuable.

Method: Three synthesis methods are introduced: 1) Max-guarantee synthesis - commits to maximal set of objectives that can be a priori guaranteed; 2) Max-observation synthesis - maximizes a posteriori realized objectives (may differ across executions); 3) Incremental max-observation synthesis - improves strategies by exploiting opportunities for stronger guarantees during execution.

Result: Experimental results show that all variations of optimal synthesis scale broadly equally well, solving a large fraction of benchmark instances within given timeouts, demonstrating practical feasibility of the approach.

Conclusion: The optimal LTLf synthesis framework provides a practical alternative to traditional all-or-nothing approaches, enabling partial satisfaction of specifications when full guarantees are impossible, with comparable scalability to existing methods.

Abstract: Strategy synthesis typically follows an all-or-nothing paradigm, returning unrealisable whenever a specification cannot be guaranteed in an uncertain environment. In this paper, we introduce optimal LTLf synthesis, where the goal is to realise as many objectives as possible from a given specification consisting of multiple objectives, especially for the case that they are not all jointly realisable. We first consider max-guarantee synthesis, which commits to a maximal set of objectives that we can a priori guarantee to realise. We then introduce max-observation synthesis, which maximises a posteriori realised objectives that may be incomparable on different executions. Finally, we present incremental max-observation synthesis, which further improves strategies by exploiting opportunities for stronger guarantees when they arise during an execution. Experimental results show that different variations of optimal synthesis scale broadly equally well, solving a large fraction of the benchmark instances within the given timeout, demonstrating the practical feasibility of the approach.

</details>


### [42] [Hindsight Hint Distillation: Scaffolded Reasoning for SWE Agents from CoT-free Answers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11556)
*Shengjie Wang,Guanghe Li,Zonghan Yang,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD) enables learning complex reasoning from question-answer pairs without chain-of-thought annotations by using model's failed rollouts to generate targeted hints that scaffold successful trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning complex long-horizon tasks requires strong planning and reasoning capabilities. While datasets with explicit chain-of-thought rationales are beneficial, they are costly to obtain. The paper addresses the challenge of learning from only easy-to-obtain question-answer pairs without CoT annotations.

Method: Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD): 1) Synthesizes hindsight hints from the model's own failed self-rollouts, 2) Uses these hints to scaffold on-policy rollouts that successfully complete tasks, 3) Self-distills these scaffolded trajectories, allowing generalization to new problems without hint guidance. Inspired by how human teachers use student mistakes to provide targeted guidance.

Result: HHD significantly outperforms iterative RFT and trajectory-synthesis baselines, achieving 8% absolute improvement on SWE-bench Verified (vs. ~2% for baselines). The reasoning strategies generalize effectively to out-of-distribution tasks, yielding largest gains on SWE-bench Multilingual despite no training on multilingual data.

Conclusion: HHD can effectively synthesize expert-like reasoning from CoT-free data and substantially improve long-horizon performance. The method demonstrates that targeted guidance from model's own failures enables effective learning of complex reasoning without expensive CoT annotations.

Abstract: Solving complex long-horizon tasks requires strong planning and reasoning capabilities. Although datasets with explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales can substantially benefit learning, they are costly to obtain. To address this challenge, we propose Hindsight Hint Distillation (HHD), which only requires easy-to-obtain question-answer pairs without CoT annotations. Inspired by how human teachers use student mistakes to provide targeted guidance, HHD synthesizes hindsight hints from the model's own failed self-rollouts and uses them to scaffold on-policy rollouts that successfully complete the tasks. The model then self-distills these scaffolded trajectories and generalizes to new problems without hint guidance. Experiments show that HHD significantly outperforms iterative RFT and trajectory-synthesis baselines, achieving an absolute improvement of 8\% on SWE-bench Verified, while all baselines improve by only around 2\%. Notably, the reasoning strategies induced by HHD generalize effectively to out-of-distribution tasks, yielding the largest gains on SWE-bench Multilingual despite no training on multilingual data. These results demonstrate that HHD can effectively synthesize expert-like reasoning from CoT-free data and substantially improve long-horizon performance.

</details>


### [43] [Dual-Temporal LSTM with Hybrid Attention for Airline Passenger Load Factor Forecasting: Integrating Intra-Flight and Inter-Flight Booking Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11569)
*ASM Nazrul Islam,Md. Hasanul Kabir,Md. Liakot Ali,Joydeb Kumar Sana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dual-stream LSTM with attention framework simultaneously processes horizontal (intra-flight booking accumulation) and vertical (inter-flight booking patterns) temporal sequences for airline demand forecasting, outperforming existing approaches and being adopted by Biman Bangladesh Airlines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing airline demand forecasting systems are inadequate because they treat booking data as a single temporal dimension (either accumulation for a specific flight or historical patterns for a route), discarding valuable information from the complementary temporal stream. Additionally, forecasting absolute passenger counts becomes operationally fragile when aircraft type changes alter seat capacity.

Method: Proposed a dual-stream Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) integrated with attention framework that simultaneously processes two complementary input sequences: 1) horizontal sequence capturing intra-flight booking accumulation over days preceding departure, and 2) vertical sequence capturing inter-flight booking patterns at fixed days-before-departure offsets across historical flights. Multiple architectural variants combining self-attention, cross-attention, and hybrid attention with concatenation, residual, and gated fusion strategies were developed.

Result: The proposed hybrid model achieved Mean Absolute Error of 2.8167 and R² of 0.9495 on real-world reservation data from Biman Bangladesh Airlines, outperforming single-stream baselines, tree-based models, and three prior dual-LSTM architectures. Model validation across four flight category pairs (domestic vs. international, direct vs. transit, high vs. low frequency, and short vs. mid vs. long haul) confirmed generalization across operationally diverse route types.

Conclusion: The dual-stream LSTM with attention framework successfully addresses the limitations of existing forecasting systems by leveraging both temporal dimensions of booking data, leading to superior predictive performance that generalizes across diverse flight types. The methodology has been officially integrated into Biman Bangladesh Airlines' operations, demonstrating practical value for airline revenue management.

Abstract: Accurate short-term demand forecasting is crucial to airline revenue management, yet most existing systems fail to meet this need because current models treat booking data as a single temporal dimension, either the accumulation of bookings for a specific flight or the historical booking profile of the same route. This unidimensional view discards information carried by the other temporal stream and forecasting absolute passenger counts introduces a further operational fragility when change in planned aircraft type alters total seat capacity. This study addresses both limitations. A dual-stream Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) integrated with attention framework is proposed that simultaneously processes two complementary input sequences: a horizontal sequence capturing intra-flight booking accumulation over the days preceding departure, and a vertical sequence capturing inter-flight booking patterns at fixed days-before-departure offsets across historical flights. Multiple dual-stream architectural variants, combining self-attention, cross-attention, and hybrid attention with concatenation, residual, and gated fusion strategies, are developed and evaluated. Experiments on real-world reservation data from the national airline of Bangladesh, Biman Bangladesh Airlines (BBA), demonstrate that the proposed hybrid model achieves a Mean Absolute Error of 2.8167 and a coefficient of determination ($R^{2}$) of 0.9495, outperforming single-stream baselines, tree-based models, and three prior dual-LSTM architectures applied to the same data. Validation across four flight category pairs; domestic versus international, direct versus transit, high versus low frequency, and short versus mid versus long haul confirms that the model generalizes across operationally diverse route types. Biman Bangladesh Airlines (BBA) has officially integrated this methodology into its operations.

</details>


### [44] [Native Explainability for Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Networks: A Framework for Trusted Brain-Like AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11595)
*Georgios Makridis,Georgios Fatouros,John Soldatos,George Katsis,Dimosthenis Kyriazis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a systematic XAI framework for Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Networks (BCPNN), addressing the EU AI Act's transparency requirements for edge-deployed AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The EU AI Act creates urgent demand for trustworthy, transparent AI on edge devices. BCPNNs offer state-of-the-art unsupervised learning and edge feasibility but lack systematic explanation frameworks, creating a critical gap for regulatory compliance and practical deployment.

Method: Four key contributions: 1) First XAI taxonomy for BCPNN mapping architectural components to explanation modalities, 2) Sixteen architecture-level explanation primitives with closed-form algorithms, 3) Five design-time Configuration-as-Explanation primitives, 4) Integration roadmap for industrial IoT with EU AI Act alignment.

Result: The paper establishes BCPNN as an inherently transparent model under Rudin's interpretable-by-design agenda, provides comprehensive explanation framework covering multiple XAI modalities, and demonstrates practical feasibility for edge deployment with regulatory compliance.

Conclusion: BCPNNs can serve as a credible transparent alternative to deep learning for edge AI, with the proposed XAI framework enabling compliance with EU AI Act requirements while maintaining edge deployment feasibility and supporting Industry 5.0 applications.

Abstract: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation 2024/1689), fully applicable to high-risk systems from August 2026, creates urgent demand for AI architectures that are simultaneously trustworthy, transparent, and feasible to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices. Brain-like neural networks built on the Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Network (BCPNN) formalism have re-emerged as a credible alternative to backpropagation-driven deep learning. They deliver state-of-the-art unsupervised representation learning, neuromorphic-friendly sparsity, and existing FPGA implementations that target edge deployment. Despite this momentum, no systematic framework exists for explaining BCPNN decisions -- a gap the present paper fills. We argue that BCPNN is, in the sense of Rudin's interpretable-by-design agenda, an inherently transparent model whose architectural primitives map directly onto established explainable-AI (XAI) families. We make four contributions. First, we propose the first XAI taxonomy for BCPNN. It maps weights, biases, hypercolumn posteriors, structural-plasticity usage scores, attractor dynamics, and input-reconstruction populations onto attribution, prototype, concept, counterfactual, and mechanistic explanation modalities. Second, we introduce sixteen architecture-level explanation primitives (P1--P16), several without analogue in standard ANNs. We provide closed-form algorithms for computing each from quantities the model already maintains. Third, we introduce five design-time Configuration-as-Explanation primitives (Config-P1 to Config-P5) that treat BCPNN hyperparameter choices as an auditable pre-deployment explanation artifact. Fourth, we sketch a roadmap for integration into industrial IoT deployments and discuss EU AI Act alignment, edge feasibility, and Industry 5.0 implications.

</details>


### [45] [GAR: Carbon-Aware Routing for LLM Inference via Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11603)
*Disha Sheshanarayana,Rajat Subhra Pal,Manjira Sinha,Tirthankar Dasgupta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Green-Aware Routing (GAR) is a framework that optimizes LLM routing to minimize CO2 emissions while maintaining accuracy and latency guarantees, addressing the gap in current methods that ignore sustainable energy considerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM routing methods fail to consider sustainable energy use and CO2 emissions as optimization objectives, despite significant variations in grid carbon intensity across time/regions and energy consumption differences between models.

Method: GAR is a constrained multi-objective optimization framework with adaptive constraint optimization via per-dataset floor tuning. It uses lightweight estimators for correctness, tail latency, and carbon emissions for real-time routing. GAR-PD is an online primal-dual routing algorithm for rolling carbon budgets with heuristic variants.

Result: Comprehensive experiments across NLP benchmarks with heterogeneous LLM pools (7B-70B) show GAR achieves substantial carbon reductions while maintaining competitive accuracy and p95 latency guarantees.

Conclusion: GAR provides a practical, theoretically grounded approach to sustainable LLM inference that balances environmental impact with quality-of-service requirements.

Abstract: The growing deployment of large language models (LLMs) makes per-request routing essential for balancing response quality and computational cost across heterogeneous model pools. Current routing methods rarely consider sustainable energy use and CO2 emissions as optimization objectives, despite grid carbon intensity varying by time and region, and models differing significantly in energy consumption. To address this gap, we introduce Green-Aware Routing (GAR), a constrained multi-objective optimization framework that minimizes per-request CO2 emissions subject to explicit accuracy floors and p95-latency service-level objectives (SLOs). GAR employs adaptive constraint optimization through per-dataset floor tuning and incorporates lightweight estimators for correctness, tail latency, and carbon emissions, enabling real-time routing decisions without additional inference passes. We present GAR-PD, a practical online primal-dual routing algorithm for rolling carbon budgets, alongside heuristic variants that achieve high feasibility coverage while limiting accuracy degradation. Comprehensive experiments across standard NLP benchmarks with heterogeneous LLM pools (7B-70B) demonstrate that GAR achieves substantial carbon reductions while maintaining competitive accuracy and p95 latency guarantees, providing a practical, theoretically grounded approach to sustainable LLM inference.

</details>


### [46] [CuSearch: Curriculum Rollout Sampling via Search Depth for Agentic RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11611)
*Jianghan Shen,Siqi Luo,Xinyu Cheng,Jing Xiong,Yue Li,Jiyao Liu,Jiashi Lin,Yirong Chen,Junjun He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CuSearch is a curriculum rollout sampling framework for RLVR-based agentic RAG training that reallocates update budget toward deeper-search trajectories based on search depth, improving performance over uniform sampling methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods use uniform rollout sampling, treating all trajectories as equally informative. However, trajectories differ substantially in search depth, with deeper ones containing more retrieval decision points and providing denser supervision. This heterogeneity grows during training but uniform sampling ignores it.

Method: Proposed CuSearch framework with Search-Depth Greedy Allocation (SDGA), a batch-level operator that reallocates fixed update budget toward deeper-search trajectories. Two variants: SDGA-Auto (implicit curriculum targeting deepest available trajectories) and SDGA-Phase (explicit curriculum with advancing thresholds as deeper trajectories become abundant).

Result: Experiments across model types and retrieval frameworks show CuSearch consistently improves performance, achieving up to 11.8 exact-match points over standard GRPO on ZeroSearch. Establishes search depth as reliable annotation-free proxy for retrieval supervision density.

Conclusion: Per-trajectory search depth serves as an effective, annotation-free proxy for retrieval supervision density in RLVR-based agentic RAG training. Curriculum-based rollout sampling focusing on deeper trajectories improves learning efficiency and performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for training agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems from outcome-only supervision. Most existing methods optimize policies from uniformly sampled rollouts, implicitly treating all trajectories as equally informative. However, trajectories differ substantially in search depth and are therefore not equally informative: deeper-search trajectories contain more retrieval decision points and provide denser direct supervision for the retrieval sub-policy. Moreover, this heterogeneity grows over training as the within-batch depth distribution shifts toward higher values, yet uniform rollout sampling remains blind to this shift. To address this, we propose CuSearch, a curriculum rollout sampling framework built on Search-Depth Greedy Allocation (SDGA), a batch-level operator that reallocates a fixed update budget toward deeper-search trajectories. SDGA-Auto always targets the deepest available trajectories in the current batch, yielding an implicit training-aligned curriculum as the depth distribution shifts upward. SDGA-Phase explicitly advances the curriculum threshold as deeper trajectories become sufficiently abundant. Experiments across model types and retrieval frameworks show that CuSearch consistently improves performance, achieving up to 11.8 exact-match points over standard GRPO on ZeroSearch. These results establish per-trajectory search depth as a reliable, annotation-free proxy for retrieval supervision density in RLVR-based agentic RAG training. The code is available at https://github.com/MrToser/CuSearch.

</details>


### [47] [Nice Fold or Hero Call: Learning Budget-Efficient Thinking for Adaptive Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11625)
*Zhaomeng Zhou,Lan Zhang,Junyang Wang,Mu Yuan,Junda Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BET (Budget-Efficient Thinking) is a two-stage framework that adaptively allocates compute budget based on expected solvability rather than just perceived difficulty, reducing reasoning tokens by ~55% while improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large reasoning models misallocate compute by spending large budgets on unsolvable queries while compressing hard-but-solvable ones that need deeper reasoning. Existing efficiency methods overlook solvability, focusing only on perceived difficulty or compressing reasoning traces.

Method: Formulates adaptive reasoning as computational investment under uncertainty. Proposes BET framework with two stages: behavioral cold-start combined with GRPO under an investment-cost-aware reward. Learns to align solve-or-fold decisions with rollout-derived solvability estimates.

Result: Across 7 benchmarks and 3 base models, BET reduces reasoning tokens by ~55% on average while achieving overall performance improvements. Demonstrates zero-shot transfer from mathematical reasoning to scientific QA and logical reasoning with comparable efficiency gains.

Conclusion: Budget allocation should follow expected return of reasoning rather than perceived difficulty alone. BET successfully learns three key behaviors (short solve, nice fold, hero call) that optimize compute allocation based on solvability, achieving significant efficiency improvements without performance loss.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve problem solving through extended reasoning, but often misallocate test-time compute. Existing efficiency methods reduce cost by compressing reasoning traces or conditioning budget on perceived difficulty, yet largely overlook solvability. As a result, they may spend large budgets on queries beyond the model's capability while compressing hard-but-solvable queries that require deeper reasoning. In this work, we formulate adaptive reasoning as a computational investment under uncertainty, where budget should follow the expected return of reasoning rather than perceived difficulty alone. To instantiate this principle, we propose Budget-Efficient Thinking (BET), a two-stage framework that combines behavioral cold-start with GRPO under an investment-cost-aware reward. By aligning solve-or-fold decisions with rollout-derived solvability, BET learns three behaviors: (1) short solve, answering easy queries concisely; (2) nice fold, abstaining early when continued reasoning has near-zero expected return; and (3) hero call, preserving sufficient compute for hard-but-solvable queries. Across seven benchmarks and three base models, BET reduces reasoning tokens by ~55% on average while achieving overall performance improvements, and transfers zero-shot from mathematical reasoning to scientific QA and logical reasoning with comparable efficiency gains.

</details>


### [48] [Can LLM Agents Respond to Disasters? Benchmarking Heterogeneous Geospatial Reasoning in Emergency Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11633)
*Junjue Wang,Weihao Xuan,Heli Qi,Pengyu Dai,Kunyi Liu,Hongruixuan Chen,Zhuo Zheng,Junshi Xia,Stefano Ermon,Naoto Yokoya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DORA is the first benchmark for end-to-end disaster response agents, featuring 515 expert-authored tasks across real-world disasters with 3,500 tool-call steps, revealing persistent challenges in disaster-domain grounding and compositional reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current disaster response work isolates remote-sensing perception or evaluates generic tool use, leaving end-to-end emergency operation workflows underexplored, despite real operational needs for integrated multi-sensor analysis, spatial reasoning, and actionable reporting.

Method: Created Disaster Operational Response Agent (DORA) benchmark with 515 expert tasks across 45 real disasters, 10 disaster types, using 108-tool MCP library over heterogeneous geospatial data (optical, SAR, multi-spectral imagery, elevation, social vector layers) with expert-verified replayable trajectories.

Result: Evaluation of 13 frontier LLMs revealed three key challenges: 1) unique disaster-domain failure modes, 2) double bottleneck of tool selection and argument grounding, 3) compositional fragility scaling with trajectory length (7-56% gap). Gold tool-order hints provide minimal improvements (1-4%).

Conclusion: DORA establishes a rigorous testbed for operationally reliable disaster-response agents, highlighting the gap between current AI capabilities and real-world operational needs for end-to-end disaster response workflows.

Abstract: Operational disaster response goes beyond damage assessment, requiring responders to integrate multi-sensor signals, reason over road networks, populations and key facilities, plan evacuations, and produce actionable reports. However, prior work largely isolates remote-sensing perception or evaluates generic tool use, leaving the end-to-end workflows of emergency operations underexplored. In this paper, we introduce Disaster Operational Response Agent benchmark (DORA), the first agentic benchmark for end-to-end disaster response: 515 expert-authored tasks across 45 real-world disaster events spanning 10 types, paired with expert-verified, replayable gold trajectories totaling 3,500 tool-call steps. Tasks span five dimensions that cover the operational disaster-response pipeline: disaster perception, spatial relational analysis, rescue and evacuation planning, temporal evolution reasoning, and multi-modal report synthesis. Agents compose calls from a 108-tool MCP library over heterogeneous geospatial data: optical, SAR, and multi-spectral imagery across single-, bi-, and multi-temporal sequences (0.015-10m GSD), complemented by elevation and social vector layers. We comprehensively evaluate 13 frontier LLMs on our benchmark, revealing three persistent challenges: 1) disaster-domain grounding exposes unique failure modes (damage-semantic grounding, sensor-modality mismatch, and disaster-pipeline composition); 2) agents are doubly bottlenecked by tool selection and argument grounding, where gold tool-order hints improve accuracy by only 1.08-4.40%, and alternative scaffolds yield at most a 3.24% gain; 3) compositional fragility scales with trajectory length, the agent-to-gold gap widening from 7% to 56% on long pipelines. DORA establishes a rigorous testbed for operationally reliable disaster-response agents.

</details>


### [49] [Seirênes: Adversarial Self-Play with Evolving Distractions for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11636)
*Chi Zhang,Haibo Qiu,Qiming Zhang,Yufei Xu,Xinbo Gao,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Seirênes is a self-play RL framework that turns contextual interference into a training signal, using adversarial self-play to create distracting contexts that expose reasoning blind spots, ultimately improving robustness across mathematical reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-trained models still exhibit fragility in non-idealized contexts with superfluous information, tangential instructions, or incidental correlations that differ from clean benchmark distributions. There's a need to make models more resilient to contextual interference beyond verifiable reward optimization.

Method: Seirênes employs a parameter-shared adversarial self-play loop where a single model both constructs plausible yet distracting contexts that expose its own reasoning blind spots, and solves problems by discerning essential tasks from these perturbations. This creates competing objectives that force the model beyond superficial pattern matching.

Result: Across seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 4B to 30B, Seirênes achieves average gains of +10.2, +9.1, and +7.2 points. Additionally, distracting contexts produced by the 4B Seirênes model reduce the accuracy of top-tier closed-source models (GPT and Gemini) by roughly 4-5 points.

Conclusion: Seirênes successfully transforms contextual interference from a failure mode into a productive training signal, enabling co-evolution of more resilient reasoners through adversarial self-play. The framework demonstrates strong generalization and reveals reasoning blind spots across different model types.

Abstract: We present Seirênes, a self-play RL framework that transforms contextual interference from a failure mode of LLM reasoning into an internal training signal for co-evolving more resilient reasoners. While RL with verifiable rewards has significantly advanced reasoning capabilities, models can still exhibit fragility when encountering non-idealized contexts: scenarios characterized by superfluous information, tangential instructions, or incidental correlations that differ from the clean distributions typical of standard benchmarks. Seirênes harnesses this vulnerability through a parameter-shared and adversarial self-play loop. Within this framework, a single model is trained to both construct plausible yet distracting contexts that expose its own reasoning blind spots, and solve problems by discerning the essential task from these perturbations to recover the core underlying logic. By pitting these competing objectives against each other, Seirênes compels the model to move beyond superficial pattern matching and anchors its capabilities in robust underlying reasoning. This continuous interaction sustains an informative co-evolutionary curriculum as the model improves. Across seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 4B to 30B, Seirênes achieves average gains of +10.2, +9.1, and +7.2 points. Besides, distracting contexts produced by the 4B Seirênes model reduce the accuracy of top-tier closed-source models (GPT and Gemini) by roughly 4--5 points, revealing Seirênes' general ability to uncover reasoning models' blind spots.

</details>


### [50] [A CAP-like Trilemma for Large Language Models: Correctness, Non-bias, and Utility under Semantic Underdetermination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11672)
*Vinu Ellampallil Venugopal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs face a trilemma similar to the CAP theorem: they cannot simultaneously guarantee strong correctness, strict non-bias, and high utility when dealing with semantically underdetermined prompts where premises don't determine a unique answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the CAP theorem in distributed systems, seeking to formulate a similar fundamental trade-off for LLMs. It addresses how LLMs must handle semantically underdetermined prompts where available information doesn't uniquely determine the correct answer.
