Method: The paper formalizes a correctness-non-bias-utility trilemma, develops theoretical examples to illustrate the trade-offs, and argues that certain LLM failures stem from the inherent structure of underdetermined decision requests rather than just model limitations.

Result: The analysis shows that when prompts are semantically underdetermined, LLMs face unavoidable trade-offs: they must either introduce unsupported preferences (creating bias), avoid such preferences (reducing utility through refusal/hedging), or risk correctness by making arbitrary choices.

Conclusion: Certain LLM failures are structural rather than technical limitations, arising from the fundamental impossibility of simultaneously guaranteeing correctness, non-bias, and utility in semantically underdetermined scenarios. This trilemma provides a framework for understanding and categorizing LLM behaviors and failures.

Abstract: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance under network partition. Inspired by this result, this paper formulates a CAP-like conjecture for Large Language Models (LLMs). The proposed trilemma states that, under semantic underdetermination, an LLM cannot always simultaneously guarantee strong correctness, strict non-bias, and high utility. A prompt is semantically underdetermined when the given premises do not determine a unique answer. In such cases, a useful and decisive response requires the model to introduce a selection criterion, preference, prior, or value ordering. If this criterion is not supplied by the user or justified by the available premises, the response becomes biased in a broad selection-theoretic sense. Conversely, if the model avoids unsupported preferences, it may preserve correctness and non-bias but may reduce utility through refusal, hedging, or clarification. The paper formalizes this correctness--non-bias--utility trilemma, develops examples, and argues that certain LLM failures arise not merely from model limitations but from the structure of underdetermined decision requests.

</details>


### [51] [OOM-Free Alpamayo via CPU-GPU Memory Swapping for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11678)
*Seungwoo Roh,Huiyeong Kim,Jong-Chan Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A system-level optimization framework that enables memory-efficient VLA model inference on VRAM-constrained GPUs without modifying models, achieving up to 3.55x speedup over existing offloading methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action models for autonomous driving require 20-60GB of GPU memory, exceeding the 12-16GB available on commodity GPUs, making them impractical for deployment on standard hardware.

Method: A three-stage optimization framework: 1) Sequential Demand Layering reduces VRAM usage to layer-level granularity; 2) Pipelined Demand Layering overlaps parameter transfer with layer execution; 3) GPU-Resident Layer Decision Policy eliminates residual transfer overhead. Also includes a performance prediction model for optimal configuration selection.

Result: Applied to NVIDIA's Alpamayo-R1-10B (21.52GB) on an RTX 5070Ti (16GB), achieves up to 3.55x speedup over Accelerate offloading while maintaining full BF16 precision, with less than 1.3% prediction error across configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables memory-efficient VLA inference on resource-constrained GPUs through system-level optimization alone, making high-performance autonomous driving models practical for deployment on commodity hardware.

Abstract: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving unify perception, reasoning, and control in a single neural network, achieving strong driving performance but requiring 20-60GB of GPU memory-far exceeding the 12-16GB available on commodity GPUs. We present a framework, which enables memory-efficient VLA inference on VRAM-constrained GPUs through system-level optimization alone, without model modification. Our work proceeds in three stages: (1) Sequential Demand Layering reduces VRAM usage from model-level to layer-level granularity; (2) Pipelined Demand Layering hides parameter transfer time within layer execution time via transfer--compute overlap; and (3) a GPU-Resident Layer Decision Policy, informed by per-module residency benefit analysis, eliminates the residual transfer overhead that pipelining cannot hide. We further propose a performance prediction model that determines the optimal configuration-both the number and placement of resident layers-from a single profiling run with less than 1.3% prediction error across all configurations. Applied to NVIDIA's Alpamayo-R1-10B (21.52GB) on an RTX 5070Ti (16GB), our work achieves up to 3.55x speedup over Accelerate offloading while maintaining full BF16 precision.

</details>


### [52] [Explaining and Breaking the Safety-Helpfulness Ceiling via Preference Dimensional Expansion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11679)
*ShiYing Huang,Liang Lin,Yuer Li,Kaiwen Luo,Zhenhong Zhou,An Zhang,Junhao Dong,Kun Wang,Zhigang Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MORA proposes a novel approach to multi-objective alignment in LLMs by rewriting prompts to incorporate multi-dimensional intents rather than just forcing compromises between competing objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-objective alignment for LLMs often involves zero-sum conflicts where optimizing one metric (helpfulness) harms another (harmlessness). Existing approaches force compromises along fixed Pareto frontiers without resolving inherent trade-offs.

Method: MORA (Multi-Objective Reward Assimilation) isolates single-reward prompts through pre-sampling, then rewrites original questions to incorporate multi-dimensional intents, expanding reward diversity instead of forcing compromises.

Result: In sequential alignment: 5-12.4% single-preference improvements with exceptional gains in harmlessness. In simultaneous alignment: 4.6% average overall reward improvement across helpful, harmless, and truthful dimensions.

Conclusion: The core conflict in multi-objective alignment stems from prompts inherently restricting achievable multi-dimensional rewards. MORA fundamentally addresses this by prompt rewriting rather than traditional compromise-based approaches.

Abstract: In the realm of multi-objective alignment for large language models, balancing disparate human preferences often manifests as a zero-sum conflict. Specifically, the intrinsic tension between competing goals dictates that aggressively optimizing for one metric (e.g., helpfulness) frequently incurs a substantial penalty on another (e.g., harmlessness). While prior work mainly focuses on data selection, parameter merging, or algorithmic balancing during training, these approaches merely force compromises between divergent preferences along a fixed Pareto frontier, failing to fundamentally resolve the inherent trade-off. In this work, we approach this problem from a novel perspective of multi-dimensional rewards. By scaling up the model's rollouts and analyzing the outputs across different reward dimensions, we arrive at a critical conclusion: the conflict among multiple objectives stems from the fact that the prompt itself inherently restricts the achievable multi-dimensional rewards. Based on this core observation, we propose MORA: Multi-Objective Reward Assimilation. Specifically, MORA isolates single-reward prompts through pre-sampling and expands their reward diversity by rewriting the original questions to incorporate multi-dimensional intents. Extensive experiments demonstrate that: (1) in sequential alignment, MORA achieves single-preference improvements ranging from 5% to 12.4%, with exceptional gains in harmlessness, after multiple-preference alignment across helpful, harmless, and truthful dimensions. (2) In simultaneous alignment, MORA achieves an average overall reward improvement of 4.6%. Our codes are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MORA-MPA.

</details>


### [53] [Persistent and Conversational Multi-Method Explainability for Trustworthy Financial AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11687)
*Georgios Makridis,Georgios Fatouros,John Soldatos,George Katsis,Dimosthenis Kyriazis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An architecture for human-centered explainable AI in financial sentiment analysis that treats XAI artifacts as persistent objects, enables multi-method explanation triangulation via RAG, and evaluates explanation faithfulness with automated checks, demonstrating improved performance in financial applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial institutions need AI explanations that are persistent, cross-validated across different methods, and accessible conversationally to human decision-makers, especially in regulated financial environments where trust and auditability are critical.

Method: Three main contributions: 1) Treat XAI artifacts (LIME, occlusion scores, saliency heatmaps) as persistent, searchable objects in S3-compatible storage with metadata and natural-language summaries; 2) Multi-method explanation triangulation using RAG assistant to compare/synthesize results from multiple XAI methods; 3) Evaluate faithfulness using automated checks for grounding completeness, hallucinated claims, and method-attribution behavior.

Result: Demonstrated on EXTRA-BRAIN financial sentiment analysis pipeline using FinBERT. Constrained prompting reduced hallucination rate by 36% and increased method-attribution citations by 73% compared to naive prompting. The system enables semantic retrieval over explanation history and automatic index reconstruction after failures.

Conclusion: The architecture addresses key requirements for trustworthy, human-centered AI services in regulated financial environments by providing persistent, cross-validated explanations accessible through natural-language dialogue, with demonstrated improvements in explanation faithfulness and reliability.

Abstract: Financial institutions increasingly require AI explanations that are persistent, cross-validated across methods, and conversationally accessible to human decision-makers. We present an architecture for human-centered explainable AI in financial sentiment analysis that combines three contributions. First, we treat XAI artifacts -- LIME feature attributions, occlusion-based word importance scores, and saliency heatmaps -- as persistent, searchable objects in distributed S3-compatible storage with structured metadata and natural-language summaries, enabling semantic retrieval over explanation history and automatic index reconstruction after system failures. Second, we enable multi-method explanation triangulation, where a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) assistant compares and synthesizes results from multiple XAI methods applied to the same prediction, allowing users to assess explanation robustness through natural-language dialogue. Third, we evaluate the faithfulness of generated explanations using automated checks over grounding completeness, hallucinated claims, and method-attribution behavior. We demonstrate the architecture on an EXTRA-BRAIN financial sentiment analysis pipeline using FinBERT predictions and present evaluation results showing that constrained prompting reduces hallucination rate by 36\% and increases method-attribution citations by 73\% compared to naive prompting. We discuss implications for trustworthy, human-centered AI services in regulated financial environments.

</details>


### [54] [Measuring What Matters Beyond Text: Evaluating Multimodal Summaries by Quality, Alignment, and Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11693)
*Abid Ali,Diego Molla-Aliod,Usman Naseem*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MM-Eval is a unified evaluation framework for multimodal summaries that integrates text quality, image-text alignment, and visual diversity assessment, using calibrated aggregation aligned with human preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of Multimodal Summarization with Multimodal Output (MSMO) is fragmented, with text quality, image-text alignment, and visual diversity assessed separately using unimodal metrics, making it difficult to capture whether modalities jointly support faithful and useful summaries.

Method: MM-Eval integrates three components: (1) text quality measured using OpenFActScore for factual consistency and G-Eval for coherence, fluency, and relevance; (2) image-text relevance evaluated via MLLM-as-a-judge approach; (3) image-set diversity quantified using Truncated CLIP Entropy. The framework is calibrated through a learned aggregation model trained on mLLM-EVAL benchmark to align component contributions with human preferences.

Result: The analysis reveals a text-dominant hierarchy where factual consistency is critical for overall quality, while visual relevance and diversity provide complementary signals. MM-Eval improves over heuristic aggregation baselines and provides an interpretable, reference-weak framework for comparative evaluation of multimodal summaries.

Conclusion: MM-Eval addresses the fragmentation in MSMO evaluation by providing a unified framework that captures how text and visuals jointly contribute to summary quality, offering better alignment with human preferences than isolated unimodal assessments.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have facilitated Multimodal Summarization with Multimodal Output (MSMO), wherein systems generate concise textual summaries accompanied by salient visuals from multimodal sources. However, current MSMO evaluation remains fragmented: text quality, image-text alignment, and visual diversity are typically assessed in isolation using unimodal metrics, making it difficult to capture whether the modalities jointly support a faithful and useful summary. To address this gap, we introduce MM-Eval, a unified evaluation framework that integrates assessments of textual quality, cross-modal alignment, and visual diversity. MM-Eval comprises three components: (1) text quality, measured using OpenFActScore for factual consistency and G-Eval for coherence, fluency, and relevance; (2) image-text relevance, evaluated via an MLLM-as-a-judge approach; and (3) image-set diversity, quantified using Truncated CLIP Entropy. We calibrate MM-Eval through a learned aggregation model trained on the mLLM-EVAL news benchmark, aligning component contributions with human preferences. Our analysis reveals a text-dominant hierarchy in this setting, where factual consistency acts as a critical determinant of perceived overall quality, while visual relevance and diversity provide complementary signals. MM-Eval improves over heuristic aggregation baselines and provides an interpretable, reference-weak framework for comparative evaluation of multimodal summaries.

</details>


### [55] [Toward Stable Value Alignment: Introducing Independent Modules for Consistent Value Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11712)
*Wenhao Chen,Sirui Sun,Shengyuan Bai,Guojie Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SVGT introduces an independent value module with separate value space and bridge tokens to safely align LLMs with human values without disrupting the main model's representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM alignment methods directly manipulate backbone parameters or representations, but the residual stream is highly dynamic and values exist as fragile, low-dimensional properties incompatible with stable value expression needed for consistent alignment.

Method: Proposes Stable Value Guidance Transformer (SVGT) with two key designs: (1) independent value modeling that maintains normative representations in a dedicated value space isolated from the backbone, and (2) explicit behavioral guidance that transduces stable signals into learnable latent Bridge Tokens which serve as dynamic value anchors to steer generative trajectories.

Result: Experiments across multiple backbones and safety benchmarks show that SVGT generally reduces harmful scores by over 70% while maintaining generation fluency.

Conclusion: SVGT demonstrates the efficacy of architecturally grounded value modeling for robustly aligning LLMs with human values across diverse contexts without disrupting the backbone's internal representations.

Abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values typically relies on post-training or inference-time steering that directly manipulates the backbone's parameters or representation space. However, a critical gap exists: the model's residual stream is highly dynamic, in which values exist as fragile, low-dimensional properties, inherently incompatible with the stability required for consistent value expression. In this paper, we propose the Stable Value Guidance Transformer (SVGT), which addresses this gap through an independent value module incorporating two key designs: (1) independent value modeling, maintaining normative representations in a dedicated value space isolated from the backbone, and (2) explicit behavioral guidance, transducing these stable signals into learnable latent Bridge Tokens. These tokens serve as dynamic value anchors to explicitly steer the generative trajectory, ensuring robust adherence across diverse contexts without disrupting the backbone's internal representations. Experiments across multiple backbones and safety benchmarks show that SVGT generally reduces harmful scores by over 70% while maintaining generation fluency, demonstrating the efficacy of architecturally grounded value modeling. Our code is available at https://github.com/Clervils/SVGT.git.

</details>


### [56] [SafeSteer: A Decoding-level Defense Mechanism for Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11716)
*Xinyi Zeng,Xue Yang,Jingyuan Zhang,Huanqian Yan,Xiang Chen,Kaiwen Wei,Hankun Kang,Yu Tian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeSteer is a decoding-level defense mechanism for Multimodal Large Language Models that detects and corrects harmful outputs during decoding without fine-tuning, improving safety by up to 33.40% while maintaining helpfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current defense methods for MLLMs rely on costly fine-tuning or inefficient post-hoc interventions, limiting their ability to address novel attacks and involving performance trade-offs. There's a need to explore inherent safety capabilities within MLLMs and develop more efficient defense mechanisms.

Method: Introduces SafeSteer with two key components: 1) Decoding-Probe - a lightweight probe for detecting and correcting harmful output during decoding, which iteratively steers the decoding process toward safety, and 2) Modal semantic alignment vector that transfers strong textual safety alignment to the vision modality.

Result: Experiments on multiple MLLMs demonstrate that SafeSteer can improve MLLMs' safety by up to 33.40% without fine-tuning. The method maintains the effectiveness of MLLMs, ensuring a balance between helpfulness and harmlessness.

Conclusion: SafeSteer provides an effective decoding-level defense mechanism that leverages MLLMs' inherent safety capabilities, addressing jailbreak attacks efficiently without performance trade-offs commonly associated with traditional defense methods.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are gaining increasing attention. Due to the heterogeneity of their input features, they face significant challenges in terms of jailbreak defenses. Current defense methods rely on costly fine-tuning or inefficient post-hoc interventions, limiting their ability to address novel attacks and involving performance trade-offs. To address the above issues, we explore the inherent safety capabilities within MLLMs and quantify their intrinsic ability to discern harmfulness at decoding stage. We observe that 1) MLLMs can distinguish the harmful and harmless inputs during decoding process, 2) Image-based attacks are more stealthy. Based on these insights, we introduce SafeSteer, a decoding-level defense mechanism for MLLMs. Specifically, it includes a Decoding-Probe, a lightweight probe for detecting and correcting harmful output during decoding, which iteratively steers the decoding process toward safety. Furthermore, a modal semantic alignment vector is integrated to transfer the strong textual safety alignment to the vision modality. Experiments on multiple MLLMs demonstrate that SafeSterr can improve MLLMs' safety by up to 33.40\% without fine-tuning. Notably, it can maintain the effectiveness of MLLMs, ensuring a balance between their helpfulness and harmlessness.

</details>


### [57] [Allegory of the Cave: Measurement-Grounded Vision-Language Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11727)
*Kepeng Xu,Li Xu,Gang He,Wenxin Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRISM-VL improves vision-language model grounding by using RAW sensor measurements instead of processed RGB images, reducing information loss and enhancing performance on challenging visual cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB rendering can clip, suppress, or quantize sensor evidence before inference, potentially losing important visual information that could improve model grounding and reasoning.

Method: PRISM-VL uses RAW-derived Meas.-XYZ inputs, camera-conditioned grounding, and Exposure-Bracketed Supervision Aggregation to transfer supervision from RGB proxies to measurement-domain observations.

Result: PRISM-VL-8B achieves 0.6120 BLEU, 0.4571 ROUGE-L, and 82.66% LLM-Judge accuracy, improving over RGB baseline Qwen3-VL-8B by +0.1074 BLEU, +0.1071 ROUGE-L, and +4.46 percentage points.

Conclusion: Part of VLM grounding error arises from information lost during RGB rendering, and preserving measurement-domain evidence can improve multimodal reasoning performance.

Abstract: Vision-language models typically reason over post-ISP RGB images, although RGB rendering can clip, suppress, or quantize sensor evidence before inference. We study whether grounding improves when the visual interface is moved closer to the underlying camera measurement. We formulate measurement-grounded vision-language learning and instantiate it as PRISM-VL, which combines RAW-derived Meas.-XYZ inputs, camera-conditioned grounding, and Exposure-Bracketed Supervision Aggregation for transferring supervision from RGB proxies to measurement-domain observations. Using a quality-controlled 150K instruction-tuning set and a held-out benchmark targeting low-light, HDR, visibility-sensitive, and hallucination-sensitive cases, PRISM-VL-8B reaches 0.6120 BLEU, 0.4571 ROUGE-L, and 82.66\% LLM-Judge accuracy, improving over the RGB Qwen3-VL-8B baseline by +0.1074 BLEU, +0.1071 ROUGE-L, and +4.46 percentage points. These results suggest that part of VLM grounding error arises from information lost during RGB rendering, and that preserving measurement-domain evidence can improve multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [58] [OptArgus: A Multi-Agent System to Detect Hallucinations in LLM-based Optimization Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11738)
*Zhong Li,Zihan Guo,Xiaohan Lu,Juntao Wang,Jie Song,Chao Shen,Jiageng Wu,Mingyang Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for detecting optimization-modeling hallucinations in LLM-generated mathematical formulations, using a multi-agent detector system called OptArgus with specialized auditing for structural consistency across problem description, symbolic model, and solver implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to translate natural-language optimization problems into mathematical formulations and solver code, but matching reference objective values isn't reliable for correctness detection. Artifacts can agree numerically while still changing the underlying optimization semantics, creating a need for structural consistency auditing.

Method: Developed a fine-grained hallucination taxonomy for optimization modeling spanning objective, variable, constraint, and implementation failures. Designed OptArgus, a multi-agent detector with conductor routing, specialist auditors, and evidence consolidation. Created a three-part benchmark suite with clean artifacts, controlled injected artifacts, and natural LLM-generated artifacts for evaluation.

Result: OptArgus outperforms a matched single-agent baseline: produces fewer false alarms on clean artifacts, more accurate top-ranked localization on controlled single-error cases, and stronger detection on natural LLM-generated artifacts. The modular, taxonomy-grounded auditing approach proves effective.

Conclusion: Turns optimization-modeling hallucination detection into a concrete empirical problem and demonstrates that modular, taxonomy-grounded auditing is a practical approach for more reliable optimization modeling with LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to translate natural-language optimization problems into mathematical formulations and solver code, but matching the reference objective value is not a reliable test of correctness: an artifact may agree numerically while still changing the underlying optimization semantics. We formulate this issue as \emph{optimization-modeling hallucination detection}, namely structural consistency auditing over the problem description, symbolic model, and solver implementation. We develop, to our knowledge, the first fine-grained hallucination taxonomy specifically for optimization modeling, spanning objective, variable, constraint, and implementation failures. We use this taxonomy to design OptArgus, a multi-agent detector with conductor routing, specialist auditors, and evidence consolidation. To evaluate this setting, we introduce a three-part benchmark suite with $484$ clean artifacts, $1266$ controlled injected artifacts, and $6292$ natural LLM-generated artifacts. Against a matched single-agent baseline, OptArgus produces fewer false alarms on clean artifacts, more accurate top-ranked localization on controlled single-error cases, and stronger detection on natural model outputs. Together, these contributions turn optimization-modeling hallucination detection into a concrete empirical problem and suggest that modular, taxonomy-grounded auditing is a practical route to more reliable optimization modeling.

</details>


### [59] [When Reasoning Traces Become Performative: Step-Level Evidence that Chain-of-Thought Is an Imperfect Oversight Channel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11746)
*Wenkai Li,Fan Yang,Ananya Hazarika,Shaunak A. Mehta,Koichi Onoue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought traces are often temporally misaligned with actual answer formation, with many reasoning steps being non-essential confabulations that continue after the answer has already been decided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the assumption that visible chain-of-thought traces accurately reflect when and how language models form their answers, given that CoT is increasingly used for both model improvement and behavior auditing.

Method: Developed a step-level Detect-Classify-Compare framework using answer-commitment proxies validated with Patchscopes, tuned-lens probes, and causal direction ablation. Applied across nine models and seven reasoning benchmarks, with additional architecture-matched Qwen2.5/DeepSeek-R1-Distill comparisons and paired truncation/donor-corruption tests.

Result: Only 61.9% of steps show alignment between latent commitment and explicit answer arrival on average. Dominant mismatch pattern is confabulated continuation (58% of mismatches), where reasoning continues after answer has stabilized. Architecture comparisons show reasoning pipeline changes failure composition more than aggregate alignment. Lower step-level alignment correlates with larger CoT utility, and post-commitment text is often not load-bearing.

Conclusion: Chain-of-thought reasoning can remain useful while being an unreliable report of when answers are actually formed, challenging assumptions about CoT's temporal faithfulness and its use for model auditing.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are increasingly used both to improve language model capability and to audit model behavior, implicitly assuming that the visible trace remains synchronized with the computation that determines the answer. We test this assumption with a step-level Detect-Classify-Compare framework built around an answer-commitment proxy that is cross-validated with Patchscopes, tuned-lens probes, and causal direction ablation. Across nine models and seven reasoning benchmarks, latent commitment and explicit answer arrival align on only 61.9% of steps on average. The dominant mismatch pattern is confabulated continuation: 58.0% of detected mismatch events occur after the answer-commitment proxy has already stabilized while the trace continues producing deliberative-looking text, and a vacuousness analysis shows that the committed answer does not change during these steps. In architecture-matched Qwen2.5/DeepSeek-R1-Distill comparisons, the reasoning pipeline changes failure composition more than aggregate alignment, most clearly at 32B where confabulated steps decrease as contradictory states increase. Lower step-level alignment is also associated with larger CoT utility, suggesting that the settings that benefit most from CoT are often the least temporally faithful. Paired truncation and a complementary donor-corruption test further indicate that much post-commitment text is not load-bearing for the final answer. These findings suggest that CoT can remain useful while still being an unreliable report of when the answer was formed.

</details>


### [60] [Towards Visually Grounded Multimodal Summarization via Cross-Modal Transformer and Gated Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11753)
*Abid Ali,Diego Molla-Aliod,Usman Naseem*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPeCTrA-Sum: A multimodal summarization framework that jointly generates text summaries and selects representative images through depth-aware visual-language fusion and principled image selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal summarization methods suffer from shallow visual feature injection into deep language models, leading to representational mismatches and weak cross-modal grounding. Current approaches fail to properly align visual and textual representations.

Method: Proposes SPeCTrA-Sum with two key innovations: 1) Deep Visual Processor (DVP) that aligns visual encoder with language model at corresponding depths for hierarchical, layer-wise fusion, and 2) lightweight Visual Relevance Predictor (VRP) that selects salient/diverse images by distilling soft labels from a Determinantal Point Processes (DPP) teacher. Uses multi-objective loss combining autoregressive summarization, cross-modal alignment, and DPP-based distillation.

Result: Experiments show the system produces more accurate, visually grounded summaries and selects more representative images compared to existing methods, demonstrating benefits of depth-aware fusion and principled image selection.

Conclusion: The proposed SPeCTrA-Sum framework successfully addresses limitations of previous multimodal summarization approaches by introducing depth-aware visual-language alignment and principled image selection, leading to improved performance in generating coherent summaries with representative visual content.

Abstract: Multimodal summarization requires models to jointly understand textual and visual inputs to generate concise, semantically coherent summaries. Existing methods often inject shallow visual features into deep language models, leading to representational mismatches and weak cross-modal grounding. We propose a unified framework that jointly performs text summarization and representative image selection. Our system, SPeCTrA-Sum (Sampler Perceiver with Cross-modal Transformer and gated Attention for Summarization), introduces two key innovations. First, a Deep Visual Processor (DVP) aligns the visual encoder with the language model at corresponding depths, enabling hierarchical, layer-wise fusion that preserves semantic consistency. Second, a lightweight Visual Relevance Predictor (VRP) selects salient and diverse images by distilling soft labels from a Determinantal Point Processes (DPP) teacher. SPeCTrA-Sum is trained using a multi-objective loss that combines autoregressive summarization, cross-modal alignment, and DPP-based distillation. Experiments show that our system produces more accurate, visually grounded summaries and selects more representative images, demonstrating the benefits of depth-aware fusion and principled image selection for multimodal summarization.

</details>


### [61] [Beyond Inefficiency: Systemic Costs of Incivility in Multi-Agent Monte Carlo Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11789)
*Alison Moldovan-Mauer,Benedikt Mangold*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based multi-agent systems simulate toxic debates, showing convergence latency increases with toxicity and smaller models, plus first-mover advantage in adversarial discussions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unconstructive debate and uncivil communication harm productivity but are hard to study experimentally due to ethical constraints, limited reproducibility, and unpredictability of human interactions.

Method: Use LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems as a controlled sociological sandbox with Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of 1-on-1 adversarial debates across varying toxicity conditions, measuring convergence time as efficiency metric.

Result: Confirmed previous 25% convergence latency from toxic behavior, found latency significantly larger for models with fewer parameters, and identified significant first-mover advantage where initiating agent wins above chance regardless of toxicity.

Conclusion: LLM-based multi-agent systems provide valuable controlled environments for studying communication dynamics, revealing how toxicity impacts debate efficiency and showing model size effects on interaction patterns.

Abstract: Unconstructive debate and uncivil communication carry well-documented costs for productivity and cohesion, yet isolating their effect on operational efficiency has proven difficult. Human subject research in this domain is constrained by ethical oversight, limited reproducibility, and the inherent unpredictability of naturalistic settings. We address this gap by leveraging Large Language Model (LLM) based Multi-Agent Systems as a controlled sociological sandbox, enabling systematic manipulation of communicative behavior at scale. Using a Monte Carlo simulation framework, we generate thousands of structured 1-on-1 adversarial debates across varying toxicity conditions, measuring convergence time, defined as the number of rounds required to reach a conclusion, as a proxy for interactional efficiency. Building on a prior study, we replicate and extend its findings across two additional LLM agents of varying parameter size, allowing us to assess whether the effects of toxic behavior on debate dynamics generalize across model scale. The convergence latency of 25% reported in the previous study was confirmed. It was found that this latency is significantly bigger for models with fewer parameters. We further identify a significant first-mover advantage, whereby the agent initiating the discussion wins significantly above chance regardless of toxicity condition.

</details>


### [62] [Why Users Go There: World Knowledge-Augmented Generative Next POI Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11807)
*Qiuyu Ding,Heng-Da Xu,Wei Zhang,Dongyi Lv,Changda Xia,Feng Xiong,Mu Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AWARE is an LLM-agent framework that enhances POI recommendation by dynamically generating personalized location-time narratives using external world knowledge, improving next POI prediction performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative POI recommendation models based on LLMs have fixed knowledge after training and cannot adapt to evolving real-world conditions like local events, cultural trends, and seasonal patterns that influence user mobility decisions.

Method: AWARE employs an LLM agent to generate location- and time-aware contextual narratives that capture regional cultural characteristics, seasonal trends, and ongoing events. It anchors these narratives in each user's behavioral context to ground external world knowledge in personalized spatial-temporal patterns.

Result: Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that AWARE consistently outperforms competitive baselines, achieving up to 12.4% relative improvement in POI recommendation performance.

Conclusion: AWARE successfully bridges the gap between fixed LLM knowledge and evolving real-world conditions by incorporating dynamic, personalized world knowledge through agent-generated narratives, significantly enhancing POI recommendation accuracy.

Abstract: Generative point-of-interest (POI) recommendation models based on large language models (LLMs) have shown promising results by formulating next POI prediction as a sequence generation task. However, the knowledge encoded in these models remains fixed after training, making them unable to perceive evolving real-world conditions that shape user mobility decisions, such as local events and cultural trends. To bridge this gap, we propose AWARE (Agent-based World knowledge Augmented REcommendation), which employs an LLM agent to generate location- and time-aware contextual narratives that capture regional cultural characteristics, seasonal trends, and ongoing events relevant to each user. Rather than introducing generic or noisy information, AWARE further anchors these narratives in each user's behavioral context, grounding external world knowledge in personalized spatial-temporal patterns. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that AWARE consistently outperforms competitive baselines, achieving up to 12.4% relative improvement.

</details>


### [63] [Beyond World-Frame Action Heads: Motion-Centric Action Frames for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11809)
*Huoren Yang,Jianchao Zhao,Hu Yusong,Qiguan Ou,Yuyang Gao,Wei Ke,Yuhang He,SongLin Dong,Zhiheng Ma,Yihong Gong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MCF-Proto: A lightweight action head for VLA models that introduces motion-centric local frames and prototype-based action parameterization to improve geometric structure and robustness in robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models use homogeneous action heads that directly predict actions in fixed world coordinates, lacking geometric structure and robust generalization despite advances in backbones and pre-training.

Method: Proposes MCF-Proto with Motion-Centric Action Frame (MCF) rotation prediction and prototype-based action parameterization. At each step, the policy predicts a rotation R_t ∈ SO(3), composes actions in local frame using prototypes, then maps back to world frame for end-to-end training using standard demonstrations.

Result: The learned local frames develop stable geometric structure with axes compatible with end-effector motion. Actions become more compact with variation captured by fewer dominant directions and better organized by shared prototypes. This translates to improved robustness, especially under geometric perturbations.

Conclusion: Adding lightweight geometric and compositional structure to action heads can significantly improve how VLA policies organize and generalize robotic manipulation behavior, suggesting promising directions for more structured action representations.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have advanced rapidly with stronger backbones, broader pre-training, and larger demonstration datasets, yet their action heads remain largely homogeneous: most directly predict action commands in a fixed world coordinate frame. We propose \textbf{MCF-Proto}, a lightweight action head that equips VLA policies with a Motion-Centric Action Frame (MCF) and a prototype-based action parameterization. At each step, the policy predicts a rotation $R_t \in SO(3)$, composes actions in the transformed local frame from a set of prototypes, and maps them back to the world frame for end-to-end training, using only standard demonstrations without auxiliary supervision. This simple design induces stable emergent structure. Without explicit directional labels, the learned local frames develop a stable geometric structure whose axes are strongly compatible with demonstrated end-effector motion. Meanwhile, actions in the learned representation become substantially more compact, with variation captured by fewer dominant directions and more regularly organized by shared prototypes. These structural properties translate into improved robustness, especially under geometric perturbations. Our results suggest that adding lightweight geometric and compositional structure to the action head can materially improve how VLA policies organize and generalize robotic manipulation behavior. An anonymized code repository is provided in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [64] [Automated Reformulation of Robust Optimization via Memory-Augmented Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11813)
*Jinbiao Chen,Shuang Jin,Guoyun Zhang,Junyu Zhang,Guanyi Wang,Hanzhang Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoREM is a tuning-free memory-augmented framework that uses LLMs to automatically reformulate robust optimization problems, with significant improvements over baseline methods across various datasets and base models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust optimization is difficult to use in practice because it requires manual reformulation of uncertain models into deterministic counterparts. While LLMs show promise for automating optimization formulation, RO reformulation remains particularly challenging due to its need for precise multi-step reasoning and mathematically consistent transformations.

Method: The authors propose AutoREM (Automated Reformulation with Experience Memory), a tuning-free memory-augmented framework that autonomously builds structured textual experience memory by reflecting on past failed trajectories through a tailored offline adaptation procedure. They also develop AutoRO-Bench, a benchmark with automated data generation pipeline for RO reformulation evaluation.

Result: Experimental results show that AutoREM consistently improves both accuracy and efficiency of RO reformulation across in-distribution datasets, out-of-distribution datasets, and diverse base LLMs. The framework requires no domain-specific expert knowledge or parameter updates, and the resulting memory transfers well across different LLMs.

Conclusion: AutoREM provides an effective solution for automating robust optimization reformulation using LLMs, overcoming the challenges of multi-step reasoning and mathematical consistency while being tuning-free and transferable across models. The AutoRO-Bench provides a valuable benchmark for future research in this area.

Abstract: Robust optimization (RO) provides a principled framework for decision-making under uncertainty, but its practical use is often limited by the need to manually reformulate uncertain optimization models into tractable deterministic counterparts. Recent large language models (LLMs) have been shown promising for automating optimization formulation, yet RO reformulation remains challenging because it requires precise multi-step reasoning and mathematically consistent transformations. To facilitate systematic evaluation of LLM-based reformulation, for which no dedicated benchmark currently exists, we develop AutoRO-Bench, a benchmark featuring an automated data generation pipeline for the core RO reformulation task and a curated dataset for the RO application task. To address the reformulation challenge, we propose Automated Reformulation with Experience Memory (AutoREM), a tuning-free memory-augmented framework that autonomously builds a structured textual experience memory by reflecting on past failed trajectories through a tailored offline adaptation procedure. AutoREM requires neither domain-specific expert knowledge nor parameter updates, and the resulting memory readily transfers across different base LLMs. Experimental results show that AutoREM consistently improves the accuracy and efficiency of RO reformulation across in-distribution datasets, out-of-distribution datasets, and diverse base LLMs.

</details>


### [65] [MedMemoryBench: Benchmarking Agent Memory in Personalized Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11814)
*Yihao Wang,Haoran Xu,Renjie Gu,Yixuan Ye,Xinyi Chen,Xinyu Mu,Yuan Gao,Chunxiao Guo,Peng Wei,Jinjie Gu,Huan Li,Ke Chen,Lidan Shou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MedMemoryBench is a novel benchmark for evaluating medical AI agents' memory capabilities, featuring realistic medical trajectories, a streaming assessment protocol, and focus on memory saturation effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for conversational agents focus on daily open-domain conversations but fail to capture the high-stakes complexity of real-world medical applications. With healthcare agents serving tens of millions of users, there's a critical need for memory mechanisms that are precise, safe, and capable of long-term clinical tracking.

Method: The researchers developed a human-agent collaborative pipeline to synthesize highly realistic, long-horizon medical trajectories based on clinically grounded, synthetic patient archetypes. They created a massive dataset of ~2,000 sessions and 16,000 interaction turns, and introduced an "evaluate-while-constructing" streaming assessment protocol that mimics dynamic memory accumulation in production environments.

Result: Comprehensive benchmarking revealed severe bottlenecks in mainstream AI architectures, particularly concerning complex medical reasoning and noise resilience. The study also formally investigated memory saturation - where sustained information influx degrades retrieval and reasoning robustness.

Conclusion: MedMemoryBench exposes fundamental flaws in current AI architectures for medical applications and establishes a vital foundation for developing robust, production-ready medical agents that can handle the stringent requirements of real-world healthcare deployments.

Abstract: The large-scale deployment of personalized healthcare agents demands memory mechanisms that are exceptionally precise, safe, and capable of long-term clinical tracking. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on daily open-domain conversations, failing to capture the high-stakes complexity of real-world medical applications. Motivated by the stringent production requirements of an industry-leading health management agent serving tens of millions of active users, we introduce MedMemoryBench. We develop a human-agent collaborative pipeline to synthesize highly realistic, long-horizon medical trajectories based on clinically grounded, synthetic patient archetypes. This process yields a massive, expertly validated dataset comprising approximately 2,000 sessions and 16,000 interaction turns. Crucially, MedMemoryBench departs from traditional static evaluations by pioneering an "evaluate-while-constructing" streaming assessment protocol, which precisely mirrors dynamic memory accumulation in production environments. Furthermore, we formalize and systematically investigate the critical phenomenon of memory saturation, where sustained information influx actively degrades retrieval and reasoning robustness. Comprehensive benchmarking reveals severe bottlenecks in mainstream architectures, particularly concerning complex medical reasoning and noise resilience. By exposing these fundamental flaws, MedMemoryBench establishes a vital foundation for developing robust, production-ready medical agents.

</details>


### [66] [On-Policy Self-Evolution via Failure Trajectories for Agentic Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11882)
*Bo Yin,Qi Li,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FATE is a self-evolving framework that transforms agent failure trajectories into repair supervision using verifier-scored repair candidates and Pareto-aware policy optimization, improving safety without sacrificing utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM agent safety approaches focus on response-level or off-policy signals, creating a safety-utility trade-off that limits real-world usability. Agent failures occur throughout trajectories (unsafe tool calls, following injected instructions, harmful compliance, over-refusal), but existing methods don't effectively leverage this trajectory-level information for continuous improvement.

Method: FATE uses an on-policy self-evolving framework where failures are transformed into repair supervision without expert demonstrations. For each failure, the same policy proposes repair candidates, which are re-scored by verifiers across security, utility, over-refusal control, and trajectory validity dimensions. The framework includes Pareto-Front Policy Optimization (PFPO) combining supervised warmup with Pareto-aware policy optimization to preserve safety-utility trade-offs.

Result: Experiments on AgentDojo, AgentHarm, and ATBench show FATE improves safety across different models and scales while preserving useful behavior. Compared to strong baselines, FATE reduces attack success rate by 33.5%, harmful compliance by 82.6%, and improves external trajectory-safety diagnosis by 6.5%.

Conclusion: Failed trajectories can provide structured repair supervision for safer self-evolving agents. The FATE framework demonstrates that on-policy self-evolution using verifier-scored repairs and Pareto-aware optimization can effectively improve agent safety without degrading task performance, addressing the fundamental safety-utility trade-off.

Abstract: Tool-using LLM agents fail through trajectories rather than only final responses, as they may execute unsafe tool calls, follow injected instructions, comply with harmful requests, or over-refuse benign tasks despite producing a seemingly safe answer. Existing safety-alignment signals are largely response-level or off-policy, and often incur a safety-utility trade-off: improving agent safety comes at the cost of degraded task performance. Such sparse and single-objective rewards severely limit real-world usability. To bridge this gap, we propose FATE, an on-policy self-evolving framework that transforms verifier-scored failures into repair supervision without expert demonstrations. For each failure, the same policy proposes repair candidates, which are then re-scored by verifiers and filtered across security, utility, over-refusal control, and trajectory validity. This dense trajectory-level information is then used as a supervision signal for agent self-evolution. During this process, we further introduce Pareto-Front Policy Optimization (PFPO), combining supervised warmup with Pareto-aware policy optimization to preserve safety-utility trade-offs. Experiments on AgentDojo, AgentHarm, and ATBench show that FATE improves safety across different models and scales while preserving useful behavior. Compared with strong baselines, FATE reduces attack success rate by 33.5%, harmful compliance by 82.6%, and improves external trajectory-safety diagnosis by 6.5%. These results suggest that failed trajectories can provide structured repair supervision for safer self-evolving agents.

</details>


### [67] [From Clever Hans to Scientific Discovery: Interpreting EEG Foundational Transformers with LRP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11885)
*Justus Meyer zu Bexten,Nico Scherf,Bogdan Franczyk,Simon M. Hofmann*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LRP-based attention attribution for EEG foundation models reveals biological insights and validates model decisions, despite heatmap interpretation challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models in EEG face adoption barriers due to their opaque nature, despite promising to overcome data scarcity in diagnostics and brain-computer interfaces.

Method: Attention-aware Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) is applied as a post-hoc attribution method for EEG foundation models, extending LRP from CNN-based EEG models to Transformer architectures.

Result: LRP uncovered 'Clever Hans' behavior in motor imagery (models using ocular signals instead of motor correlates) and identified a central electrode cluster signature for arousal in affect prediction. The method successfully verified EEG-FM decisions and generated biologically plausible hypotheses.

Conclusion: LRP serves as a valuable tool for both verification and exploration of EEG foundation models, with growing importance as these models mature, despite challenges in heatmap interpretation in this complex domain.

Abstract: Emerging foundation models (FMs) in electroencephalography (EEG) promise a path to scale deep learning in diagnostics and brain-computer interfaces despite data scarcity, yet their opaque nature remains a barrier to wider adoption. We investigate attention-aware Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) as a post-hoc attribution method for EEG-FMs, extending LRP's use on convolutional neural network (CNN)-based EEG models to the Transformer architectures that current FMs are based on. We find that LRP can both verify EEG-FM decisions and surface novel, biologically plausible hypotheses from them. In motor imagery, it unmasks 'Clever Hans' behavior where models prioritize task correlated ocular signals over the intended motor correlates. In a naturalistic paradigm for affect prediction, it reveals a recurring reliance on a central electrode cluster, suggesting a candidate sensorimotor signature of arousal. Though heatmap interpretation remains ambiguous in this complex domain, the results position LRP as a tool for both verification and exploration of EEG-FMs, a role that will grow in both importance and discovery potential as the underlying models mature.

</details>


### [68] [Toward Modeling Player-Specific Chess Behaviors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11893)
*Loris Sogliuzzo,Aloïs Rautureau,Eric Piette*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An AI chess model that captures individual champion styles using champion-specific embeddings with limited MCTS for tactical exploration, evaluated by novel behavioral similarity metrics rather than just move accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI chess models fail to reproduce the unique decision-making styles of specific historical champions and rely on move accuracy metrics that penalize natural human variance while ignoring long-term behavioral consistency.

Method: Adapts Maia-2 model with champion-specific embeddings, enhances with limited Monte Carlo Tree Search for tactical exploration, introduces novel behavioral evaluation using Jensen-Shannon divergence with compressed board representations via AutoEncoder and UMAP for move distribution comparison.

Result: MCTS integration decreases standard move accuracy but improves stylistic alignment according to the new metric, substantially reducing average Jensen-Shannon divergence. The metric successfully discriminates between individual players across 16 historical world champions.

Conclusion: The proposed approach and behavioral metric provide promising evidence for more comprehensive evaluations of behavioral alignment between human players and AI models, capturing individual stylistic characteristics beyond just skill level.

Abstract: While artificial intelligence has achieved superhuman performance in chess, developing models that accurately emulate the individualized decision-making styles of human players remains a significant challenge. Existing human-like chess models capture general population behaviors based on skill levels but fail to reproduce the behavioral characteristics of specific historical champions. Furthermore, the standard evaluation metric, move accuracy, inherently penalizes natural human variance and ignores long-term behavioral consistency, leading to an incomplete assessment of stylistic fidelity. To address these limitations, an architecture is proposed that adapts the unified Maia-2 model to champion-specific embeddings, further enhanced by the integration of a limited Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) process to enrich tactical exploration during move selection. To robustly evaluate this approach, a novel behavioral metric based on the Jensen-Shannon divergence is introduced. By compressing high-dimensional board representations into a latent space using an AutoEncoder and Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), move distributions are discretized on a common grid to compare behavioral similarities. Results across 16 historical world champions indicate that while integrating MCTS decreases standard move accuracy, it improves stylistic alignment according to the proposed metric, substantially reducing the average Jensen-Shannon divergence. Ultimately, the proposed metric successfully discriminates between individual players and provides promising evidence toward more comprehensive evaluations of behavioral alignment between players and AI models.

</details>


### [69] [Rethinking Supervision Granularity: Segment-Level Learning for LLM-Based Theorem Proving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11905)
*Shuo Xu,Jiakun Zhang,Junyu Lai,Chun Cao,Jingwei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Segment-level supervision for theorem proving with LLMs in Lean 4 bridges step-level tactics and whole-proof generation by extracting locally coherent proof segments, improving success rates and reducing inference costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to automated theorem proving with LLMs in Lean 4 have limitations: step-level tactic prediction provides dense local signals but fragments coherent proof processes, while whole-proof generation preserves global structure but requires complex end-to-end generation.

Method: Proposes segment-level supervision as a training data construction strategy that extracts locally coherent proof segments for training policy models. Also reuses this strategy at inference time for short rollouts with existing step-level models (goal-aware rollout).

Result: Segment-level supervision achieves proof success rates of 64.84%, 60.90%, and 66.31% on miniF2F across three datasets, consistently outperforming both step-level and whole-proof baselines. Goal-aware rollout improves existing provers: BFS-Prover-V2-7B from 68.77% to 70.74% and InternLM2.5-StepProver from 59.59% to 60.33%.

Conclusion: Appropriate supervision granularity better aligns model learning with proof structure and search, demonstrating that segment-level supervision bridges the gap between step-level tactics and whole-proof generation for more effective automated theorem proving.

Abstract: Automated theorem proving with large language models in Lean 4 is commonly approached through either step-level tactic prediction with tree search or whole-proof generation. These two paradigms represent opposite granularities for constructing supervised training data: the former provides dense local signals but may fragment coherent proof processes, while the latter preserves global structure but requires complex end-to-end generation. In this paper, we revisit supervision granularity as a training set construction problem over proof trajectories and propose segment-level supervision, a training data construction strategy that extracts locally coherent proof segments for training policy models. We further reuse the same strategy at inference time to trigger short rollouts for existing step-level models. When trained with segment-level supervision on STP, LeanWorkbook, and NuminaMath-LEAN, the resulting policy models achieve proof success rates of 64.84%, 60.90%, and 66.31% on miniF2F, respectively, consistently outperforming both step-level and whole-proof baselines. Goal-aware rollout further improves existing step-level provers while reducing inference costs. It increases the proof success rate of BFS-Prover-V2-7B from 68.77% to 70.74% and that of InternLM2.5-StepProver from 59.59% to 60.33%, showing that appropriate supervision granularity better aligns model learning with proof structure and search. Code and models are available at https://github.com/NJUDeepEngine/SEG-ATP.

</details>


### [70] [Rethinking Positional Encoding for Neural Vehicle Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11910)
*Chuanbo Hua,Federico Berto,Andre Hottung,Nayeli Gast Zepeda,Yining Ma,Zihan Ma,Paula Wong-Chung,Changhyun Kwon,Cathy Wu,Kevin Tierney,Jinkyoo Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes geometry-grounded positional encoding for routing-aware transformers that outperforms standard NLP positional encodings for vehicle routing problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard NLP positional encodings are inadequate for routing problems due to structural differences: NLP tokens are uniformly spaced on a line, but routing solutions have anisometric node distances, cyclic direction-aware topology, and hierarchical depot-anchored multi-route structure.

Method: Formalizes three structural properties for routing-aware PE and proposes hierarchical anisometric PE combining distance-indexed circular encoding for in-route structure with depot-anchored angular encoding for cross-route structure.

Result: Geometry-grounded PE consistently outperforms index-based alternatives across diverse VRP variants, with gains that transfer across problem variants, model architectures, and distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Positional encoding design is crucial for transformer-based combinatorial optimization of routing problems, and geometry-grounded PE provides significant performance improvements by respecting the unique structural properties of routing solutions.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have become the dominant paradigm for neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) of vehicle routing problems (VRPs), yet the role of positional encoding (PE) in these architectures remains largely unexplored. Unlike natural language, where tokens are uniformly spaced on a line, routing solutions exhibit several properties that render standard NLP positional encodings inadequate. In this work, we formalize three such structural properties that a routing-aware PE should respect, namely anisometric node distances, cyclic and direction-aware topology, and hierarchical depot-anchored global multi-route structure, combining them with a unifying design principle of geometric grounding. Guided by these criteria, we analyze and compare PE methods spanning NLP, graph-transformer, and routing-specific families, and propose a hierarchical anisometric PE that combines a distance-indexed, circularly consistent in-route encoding with a depot-anchored angular cross-route encoding. Extensive experiments across diverse VRP variants demonstrate that geometry-grounded PE consistently outperforms index-based alternatives, with gains that transfer across problem variants, model architectures, and distribution shifts.

</details>


### [71] [Domain Restriction via Multi SAE Layer Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11920)
*Elias Shaheen,Avi Mendelson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using sparse autoencoders to analyze internal layer transitions in LLMs helps detect out-of-domain text inputs by learning domain-specific signatures from the model's internal processing dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with domain-specific applications, often producing out-of-domain (OOD) interactions that undermine provider intent. Existing OOD detection methods treat LLMs as black boxes and ignore their internal processing mechanisms.

Method: The authors propose analyzing layer transitions within LLMs to extract domain-specific signatures. They use sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to encode internal dynamics and learn lightweight methods for distinguishing OOD texts based on these representations.

Result: The method demonstrates great capability in distinguishing OOD texts. By analyzing SAE representations of layer transitions, they gain interpretability into the LLM's internal processing evolution. Benchmarks with gemma-2 2B and 9B models show efficacy.

Conclusion: Internal layer transitions in LLMs provide a promising approach for OOD detection. The method effectively captures fine-grained input details and offers interpretability into LLM decision-making, addressing limitations of black-box approaches.

Abstract: The general-purpose nature of Large Language Models (LLMs) presents a significant challenge for domain-specific applications, often leading to out-of-domain (OOD) interactions that undermine the provider's intent. Existing methods for detecting such scenarios treat the LLM as an uninterpretable black box and overlook the internal processing of inputs. In this work we show that layer transitions provide a promising avenue for extracting domain-specific signature. Specifically, we present several lightweight ways of learning on internal dynamics encoded using a sparse autoencoder (SAE) that exhibit great capability in distinguishing OOD texts. Building on top of SAEs representation transitions enables us to better interpret the LLM internal evolution of input processing and shed light on its decisions. We provide a comprehensive analysis of the method and benchmark it with the gemma-2 2B and 9B models. Our results emphasize the efficacy of the internal process in capturing fine-grained input-related details.

</details>


### [72] [When Simulation Lies: A Sim-to-Real Benchmark and Domain-Randomized RL Recipe for Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11928)
*Xiaolin Zhou,Aojie Yuan,Zheng Luo,Zipeng Ling,Xixiao Pan,Yicheng Gao,Haiyue Zhang,Jiate Li,Shuli Jiang,Prince Zizhuang Wang,Zixuan Zhu,Jinbo Liu,Ryan A. Rossi,Hua Wei,Xiyang Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tool-use language agents fail in real deployments due to noisy inputs, timeouts, and duplicate tools, creating a sim-to-real gap. The paper introduces RobustBench-TC to evaluate robustness and ToolRL-DR, a domain-randomization RL method that improves robustness, especially against unseen runtime failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tool-use language agents are evaluated on clean benchmarks, but real deployments face issues like typos, timeouts, and duplicate tool names that cause failures. There's a significant gap between simulated testing environments and real-world noisy conditions.

Method: The authors introduce RobustBench-TC, a benchmark with 22 perturbation types organized by POMDP components (observation, action space, reward-relevant metadata, transition dynamics), each grounded in real GitHub issues. They then propose ToolRL-DR, a domain-randomization RL method that trains agents on perturbation-augmented trajectories across statically encodable POMDP components.

Result: Evaluation across 21 models (1.5B to 32B parameters) shows uneven robustness: observation perturbations reduce accuracy by <5%, while reward-relevant and transition perturbations reduce accuracy by ~40% and ~30%. ToolRL-DR on a 3B backbone retains ~75% of clean accuracy and achieves aggregate perturbed accuracy comparable to 14B baselines, while closing ~27% of the transition gap despite never seeing transition perturbations in training.

Conclusion: Real-world tool-use agents face significant robustness challenges, and scale alone doesn't solve them. Domain-randomization RL can effectively improve robustness, even transferring to unseen runtime failures. The benchmark and method provide valuable tools for developing more robust tool-use agents.

Abstract: Tool-use language agents are evaluated on benchmarks that assume clean inputs, unambiguous tool registries, and reliable APIs. Real deployments violate all these assumptions: user typos propagate into hallucinated tool names, a misconfigured request timeout can stall an agent indefinitely, and duplicate tool names across servers can freeze an SDK. We study these failures as a sim-to-real gap in the tool-use partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), where deployment noise enters through the observation, action space, reward-relevant metadata, or transition dynamics. We introduce RobustBench-TC, a benchmark with 22 perturbation types organized by these four POMDP components, each grounded in a verified GitHub issue or documented tool-calling failure. Across 21 models from 1.5B to 32B parameters (including the closed-source o4-mini), the robustness profile is sharply uneven: observation perturbations reduce accuracy by less than 5%, while reward-relevant and transition perturbations reduce accuracy by roughly 40% and 30%, respectively; scale alone does not close these gaps. We then propose ToolRL-DR, a domain-randomization reinforcement learning (RL) recipe that trains a tool-use agent on perturbation-augmented trajectories spanning the three statically encodable POMDP components. On a 3B backbone, ToolRL-DR-Full retains roughly three-quarters of clean accuracy and reaches an aggregate perturbed accuracy comparable to open-source 14B function-calling baselines while substantially narrowing the gap to o4-mini. It closes approximately 27% of the Transition gap despite never seeing transition perturbations in training, suggesting that RL on adversarial static tool-use inputs induces a more persistent retry policy that transfers to unseen runtime failures. The dataset, code and benchmark leaderboard are publicly available.

</details>


### [73] [From Noise to Diversity: Random Embedding Injection in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11936)
*Heejun Kim,Seungpil Lee,Jewon Yeom,Jaewon Sok,Seonghyeon Park,Jeongjae Park,Taesup Kim,Sundong Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Random Soft Prompts (RSPs) - using freshly drawn random embedding vectors as soft prompts - achieve comparable math reasoning performance to optimized prompts without training, demonstrating that much of soft prompt effectiveness comes from the injection mechanism itself rather than learned content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand whether the performance gains from soft prompts in LLM reasoning come from the learned content in trained prompt vectors or from the structural effect of injecting vectors itself. Previous research hasn't carefully separated these two factors.

Method: Proposes Random Soft Prompts (RSPs): sequences of random embedding vectors sampled from an isotropic Gaussian fitted to the pretrained embedding table statistics. These untrained prompts are appended to LLM inputs and tested on math reasoning benchmarks. The mechanism is analyzed through attention dynamics and token distribution changes during generation.

Result: RSPs achieve accuracy comparable to optimized soft prompts on math reasoning benchmarks despite having no learned content. The mechanism involves: 1) flattening early token distributions and branching reasoning trajectories due to attention on novel random positions, and 2) natural dilution of this influence as generation progresses. RSPs increase early-stage token diversity and improve Pass@N performance when combined with temperature sampling, with benefits extending to DAPO training.

Conclusion: RSPs provide the simplest form of soft prompts that isolate the structural effect of injection shared by all soft prompt variants. The work offers theoretical and empirical validation of the injection mechanism and extends the benefits from inference to training, showing that much of soft prompt effectiveness comes from the injection act itself rather than learned content.

Abstract: Recent soft prompt research has tried to improve reasoning by inserting trained vectors into LLM inputs, yet whether the gain comes from the learned content or from the act of injection itself has not been carefully separated. We study Random Soft Prompts (RSPs), which drop the training step entirely and append a freshly drawn sequence of random embedding vectors to the input. Each RSP vector is sampled from an isotropic Gaussian fitted to the entrywise mean and variance of the pretrained embedding table; the sequence carries no learned content, and yet reaches accuracy comparable to optimized soft prompts on math reasoning benchmarks in several settings. The mechanism unfolds in two stages: because attention has to absorb a never-seen-before random position, the distribution over the first few generated tokens flattens and reasoning trajectories branch, and as generation continues this influence dilutes naturally so the response commits to a single completion. We show that during inference RSPs lift early-stage token diversity and, combined with temperature sampling, widen Pass@N, the probability that at least one out of N attempts is correct. Beyond inference, we carry the same effect into DAPO training and demonstrate practical gains. Our contributions are: (i) RSP isolates the simplest form of soft prompt -- training-free, freshly resampled -- providing a unified lens for the structural effect of injection that variants otherwise differing in training and form all share; (ii) a theoretical and empirical validation of the underlying mechanism; and (iii) an extension from inference to training.

</details>


### [74] [Counterfactual Trace Auditing of LLM Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11946)
*Xiaolin Zhou,Jinbo Liu,Li Li,Ryan A. Rossi,Xiyang Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Counterfactual Trace Auditing (CTA) reveals that skills substantially reshape LLM agent behavior even when pass rates remain nearly unchanged, uncovering evaluation gaps in current skill assessment methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation methods for LLM agent skills are limited - they mainly report pass rates before and after skill attachment, treating skills as black boxes without revealing how they actually change agent behavior. There's a need for more granular behavioral analysis beyond just task outcomes.

Method: CTA pairs agent traces with and without skills on the same tasks, segments traces into goal-directed phases, aligns phases, and produces structured Skill Influence Pattern (SIP) annotations to describe behavioral effects rather than just task outcomes.

Result: While pass rates changed only +0.3 percentage points on average, CTA identified 522 SIP instances showing skills substantially reshape behavior. Found recurring effects invisible to pass rates: template copying, off-task artifacts, excess planning, task recovery. Identified patterns by baseline performance: surface anchoring on ceiling tasks, edge-case prompting on mid/floor tasks.

Conclusion: CTA reveals significant evaluation gaps in current skill assessment, turning informal failure mode observations into reproducible behavioral measurements, showing skills have substantial behavioral impacts even when pass rates remain stable.

Abstract: Large Language Model agents are increasingly augmented with agent skills. Current evaluation methods for skills remain limited. Most deployed benchmarks report only pass rate before and after a skill is attached, treating the skill as a black box change to agent behavior. We introduce Counterfactual Trace Auditing (CTA), a framework for measuring how a skill changes agent behavior. CTA pairs each with skill agent trace with a without skill counterpart on the same task, segments both traces into goal directed phases, aligns the phases, and emits structured Skill Influence Pattern (SIP) annotations. These annotations describe the behavioral effect of a skill rather than only its task outcome. We instantiate CTA on SWE-Skills-Bench with Claude across 49 software engineering tasks. The resulting audit reveals a clear evaluation gap. Pass rate changes by only +0.3 percentage points on average, suggesting little aggregate effect. Yet CTA identifies 522 SIP instances across the same paired traces, showing that the skills substantially reshape agent behavior even when pass rate is nearly unchanged. The audit also separates several recurring effects that pass rate cannot detect, including literal template copying, off task artifact creation, excess planning, and task recovery. Three findings emerge. First, high baseline tasks contain most of the observed skill effects, although their pass rate is already saturated and therefore cannot reflect those effects. Second, tasks with moderate baseline performance show the most recoverable gain, but often at substantially higher token cost. Third, the dominant SIP type can be identified by baseline bucket: surface anchoring is most common on ceiling tasks and edge-case prompting is most common on mid-range and floor tasks. These regularities turn informal failure mode observations into reproducible behavioral measurements.

</details>


### [75] [Assessing and Mitigating Miscalibration in LLM-Based Social Science Measurement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11954)
*Jinyuan Wang,Ningyuan Deng,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based social science measurement suffers from confidence miscalibration, which affects downstream analysis; a soft label distillation method can significantly improve calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as scalable measurement tools in social science, but measurement validity requires well-calibrated confidence that reflects true probability of correctness, not just high average accuracy. Current LLM confidence scores are miscalibrated, which can affect regression estimates in social science research.

Method: 1) Case study on FOMC showing how confidence filtering affects regression estimates when LLM confidence is miscalibrated; 2) Audit of calibration across 14 social science constructs using both proprietary (GPT-5-mini, DeepSeek-V3.2) and open-source models; 3) Proposed soft label distillation pipeline that converts LLM scores and verbalized confidence into soft target distributions to train smaller discriminative classifiers on encoder models.

Result: Across tasks and model families, LLM confidence is poorly aligned with tolerance-based correctness. The proposed calibration method reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by 43.2% and Brier score by 34.0% on average across datasets.

Conclusion: Calibration should be treated as an essential component of measurement validity in LLM-based social science pipelines, rather than as optional post-processing, and the proposed soft label distillation method provides an effective mitigation strategy.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in social science as scalable measurement tools for converting unstructured text into variables that can enter standard empirical designs. Measurement validity demands more than high average accuracy, which requires well calibrated confidence that faithfully reflects the empirical probability of each measurement being correct. This paper studies the model miscalibration in LLM-based social science measurement. We begin with a case study on FOMC and show that confidence based filtering can change downstream regression estimates when LLM confidence is miscalibrated.
