We then audit calibration across 14 social science constructs covering both proprietary models, including GPT-5-mini, DeepSeek-V3.2, and open source models. Across tasks and model families, reported confidence is poorly aligned with tolerance-based correctness. As a simple mitigation, we propose a soft label distillation pipeline for calibrating Bert with LLM. The method converts an LLM score and its verbalized confidence into a soft target distribution, then trains a smaller discriminative classifier on encoder models for these targets. Averaged across datasets, this approach reduces ECE by 43.2\% and Brier by 34.0\%. These results suggest that LLM-based social science pipelines should treat calibration as part of measurement validity, rather than as an optional post-processing concern.

</details>


### [76] [On the Limitations of Large Language Models for Conceptual Database Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11986)
*Arthur F. Siqueira,Carlos D. S. Nogueira,Eduarda Farias,Claudio E. C. Campelo,Júlia Menezes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show promise for generating ER diagrams from natural language requirements but struggle with complex scenarios due to inconsistencies and reliability issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether Large Language Models can reliably support conceptual modeling of relational databases by automatically generating Entity-Relationship diagrams from natural language requirements, potentially improving productivity in database design.

Method: Experimental evaluation using three different LLMs with three prompting techniques (Zero-Shot, Chain of Thought, and Chain of Thought + Verifier) on requirements scenarios with progressively increasing complexity. Generated diagrams were qualitatively analyzed by comparing with textual requirements for structural and semantic adherence.

Result: LLMs show reasonable performance in less complex scenarios but reliability decreases significantly as complexity increases, with rising inconsistencies, ambiguities, and failures in representing constraints. The cost of validation may offset productivity gains.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are not sufficiently mature for reliable use in complex database modeling scenarios. While they show promise for simpler cases, the validation overhead makes them impractical for complex requirements without further development and improvements.

Abstract: This article analyzes the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) as support for the conceptual modeling of relational databases through the automatic generation of Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams from natural language requirements. The approach combines different language models with prompt engineering techniques to evaluate their ability to identify entities, relationships, and attributes in a conceptually consistent manner. The experimental evaluation involved three LLMs, each subjected to three prompting techniques (Zero-Shot, Chain of Thought, and Chain of Thought + Verifier), applied to the same requirements scenario with progressively increasing complexity. The generated diagrams were qualitatively analyzed through direct comparison with the textual requirements, considering the structural and semantic adherence of the modeled elements. The results indicate that, although LLMs show reasonable performance in less complex scenarios, their reliability decreases as the complexity of the requirements increases, with a rise in inconsistencies, ambiguities, and failures in representing constraints. These findings reinforce that, in their current state, LLMs are not sufficiently mature for reliable use in complex scenarios, and the cost of validation may offset the apparent productivity gains.

</details>


### [77] [Random-Set Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11987)
*Tommy Woodley,Shireen Kudukkil Manchingal,Matteo Tolloso,Davide Bacciu,Fabio Cuzzolin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RS-GNN: A new framework using belief function theory to model epistemic uncertainty in Graph Neural Networks, enabling better uncertainty quantification with a random-set prediction head.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GNNs lack proper uncertainty quantification, especially for epistemic uncertainty arising from incomplete knowledge of graph topology and node features. This uncertainty affects model reliability in critical applications like autonomous driving.

Method: Proposes Random-Set Graph Neural Networks (RS-GNN) with a belief-function head that predicts a random set over class labels. Uses finite random set formalism to model node-level epistemic uncertainty, allowing extraction of both precise probability predictions and epistemic uncertainty measures.

Result: Extensive experiments on 9 graph learning datasets (including Nuscene and ROAD autonomous driving benchmarks) demonstrate RS-GNN's superior uncertainty quantification capabilities compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The RS-GNN framework effectively addresses epistemic uncertainty in GNNs through belief function theory, providing robust uncertainty quantification that is crucial for real-world applications like autonomous driving where model reliability is critical.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification has become an important factor in understanding the data representations produced by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Despite their predictive capabilities being ever useful across industrial workspaces, the inherent uncertainty induced by the nature of the data is a huge mitigating factor to GNN performance. While aleatoric uncertainty is the result of noisy and incomplete stochastic data such as missing edges or over-smoothing, epistemic uncertainty arises from lack of knowledge about a system or model (e.g., a graph's topology or node feature representation), which can be reduced by gathering more data and information. In this paper, we propose an original new framework in which node-level epistemic uncertainty is modelled in a belief function (finite random set) formalism. The resulting Random-Set Graph Neural Networks have a belief-function head predicting a random set over the list of classes, from which both a precise probability prediction and a measure of epistemic uncertainty can be obtained. Extensive experiments on 9 different graph learning datasets, including real-world autonomous driving benchmarks as such Nuscene and ROAD, demonstrate RS-GNN's superior uncertainty quantification capabilities

</details>


### [78] [BadSKP: Backdoor Attacks on Knowledge Graph-Enhanced LLMs with Soft Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11996)
*Xiaoting Lyu,Yufei Han,Hangwei Qian,Haoyuan Yu,Xiang Ao,Bin Wang,Chenxu Wang,Xiaobo Ma,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that KG-enhanced LLMs with soft-prompt graph channels are resistant to traditional text-only backdoor attacks but vulnerable to new graph-targeted backdoor attacks called BadSKP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent KG-enhanced LLMs use graph-conditioned channels alongside text interfaces, but existing backdoor attacks only target the textual channel. It's unclear how effective these attacks are against dual-channel architectures, and whether graph-based backdoor attacks could exploit this new vulnerability.

Method: The paper introduces BadSKP, a multi-stage optimization strategy that: 1) constructs adversarial target embeddings, 2) optimizes poisoned node embeddings to steer the induced soft prompt, and 3) approximates optimized representations with fluent adversarial node attributes.

Result: Experiments on two soft-prompt KG-enhanced LLMs across four datasets show BadSKP achieves high attack success under both frozen and trojaned settings, while text-only attacks remain unreliable even with perplexity-based defenses. The study reveals a robustness gap where text-channel attacks fail against soft-prompt systems.

Conclusion: KG-enhanced LLMs with graph channels create a robustness gap against text-only backdoor attacks due to semantic anchoring from graph-derived prompts. However, this architecture introduces new vulnerabilities to graph-targeted attacks like BadSKP, which can bypass current defenses and achieve high success rates.

Abstract: Recent knowledge graph (KG)-enhanced large language models (LLMs) move beyond purely textual knowledge augmentation by encoding retrieved subgraphs into continuous soft prompts via graph neural networks, introducing a graph-conditioned channel that operates alongside the standard text interface. However, existing backdoor attacks are largely designed for the textual channel, and their effectiveness against this dual-channel architecture remains unclear. We show that this architecture creates a robustness gap: text-channel backdoor attacks that readily compromise textual KG prompting systems become largely ineffective against soft-prompt-based counterparts. We interpret this gap through semantic anchoring, whereby graph-derived soft prompts bias the generation-driving hidden state toward query-consistent semantics and suppress surface-level malicious instructions. Because this anchoring effect is itself induced by the graph channel, an attacker who manipulates graph-level representations can in turn redirect it toward adversarial semantics. To demonstrate this risk, we propose BadSKP, a backdoor attack that targets the graph-to-prompt interface through a multi-stage optimization strategy: it constructs adversarial target embeddings, optimizes poisoned node embeddings to steer the induced soft prompt, and approximates the optimized representations with fluent adversarial node attributes. Experiments on two soft-prompt KG-enhanced LLMs across four datasets show that BadSKP achieves high attack success under both frozen and trojaned settings, while text-only attacks remain unreliable even under perplexity-based defenses.

</details>


### [79] [LegalCheck: Retrieval- and Context-Augmented Generation for Drafting Municipal Legal Advice Letters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12012)
*Virgill van der Meer,Julien Rossi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LegalCheck is an AI system combining RAG and CAG techniques to automate drafting of objection response letters for Dutch public-sector legal departments, reducing drafting time from hours to minutes while maintaining legal accuracy and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public-sector legal departments in the Netherlands face acute staff shortages, increased case volumes, and pressure for regulatory compliance. There's a need to improve efficiency while maintaining legal standards.

Method: Uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Context-Augmented Generation (CAG) with large language models alongside curated legal knowledge bases. Performs retrieval of relevant laws/precedents and uses controlled prompting to incorporate external knowledge and case-specific details. Includes expert-in-the-loop review for quality assurance.

Result: In real-world deployment in Amsterdam Municipality: reduced drafting time from hours to minutes; maintained high legal consistency and factual accuracy; outputs covered 80-100% of essential legal content; reduced workload while ensuring consistent application of legal standards; positive user acceptance among legal professionals.

Conclusion: LegalCheck demonstrates substantial efficiency gains and improved legal consistency in legal workflows. Shows how responsible AI can augment legal professionals by combining LLMs with domain knowledge and governance mechanisms, without replacing human judgment.

Abstract: Public-sector legal departments in the Netherlands face acute staff shortages, increased case volumes, and increased pressure to meet regulatory compliance. This paper presents LegalCheck, a novel system that addresses these challenges by automating the drafting of objection response letters through a combination of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Context-Augmented Generation (CAG). Using a large language model (LLM) alongside curated legal knowledge bases, LegalCheck performs retrieval of relevant laws and precedents, and uses controlled prompting to incorporate both external knowledge and case-specific details into a coherent draft. An expert-in-the-loop review ensures that each generated letter is legally sound and contextually appropriate. In a real-world deployment within the Municipality of Amsterdam, LegalCheck produced near-final advice letters in minutes rather than hours, while maintaining high legal consistency and factual accuracy. The output is based on actual regulations and prior cases, providing explainable outputs that captured the vast majority of required legal reasoning (often 80\% to 100\% of essential content). Legal professionals found that the system reduced their workload and ensured a consistent application of legal standards, without replacing human judgment. These results demonstrate substantial efficiency gains, improved legal consistency, and positive user acceptance. More broadly, this work illustrates how responsible AI can be deployed in the legal domain by augmenting LLMs with domain knowledge and governance mechanisms.

</details>


### [80] [LLMs and the ZPD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12016)
*Peter Wallis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large language models don't "think" with distributed representations but engage in "primitive thinking" through practices, similar to how children develop scientific thinking through social interaction in Vygotsky's Zones of Proximal Development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reframe our understanding of LLM cognition by drawing parallels with Vygotsky's theories of consciousness and development, challenging conventional views that LLMs "hallucinate" or require "guard rails."

Method: Applies Vygotsky's psychological framework (ZPD, scientific thinking development through adult-child interaction) to analyze LLM mechanisms as practicing "primitive thinking" rather than using distributed representations.

Result: LLMs don't hallucinate but rather "dream" through practice-based thinking, and interaction (not just understanding) is fundamental to human communication, suggesting we need cognitive tools rather than constraints.

Conclusion: The core insight is that interaction is central to human communication, not an add-on to understanding. We should investigate cognitive tools that enable commonsense-like behaviors in LLMs rather than imposing restrictive guard rails.

Abstract: One hundred years ago Vygotsky and his circle were exploring the nature of consciousness and defining what would become psychology in the Soviet Union. They concluded that children develop "scientific thinking" through interacting with enculturated adults in Zones of Proximal Development or ZPDs. The proposal is that, contrary to the claims of some, the LLM mechanism is not doing thinking with "distributed representations," but rather the completion model is doing "primitive thinking" in terms of *practices*. Viewed from this perspective, it would seem our large language models don't hallucinate, but rather dream, and that what is needed is not "guard rails" but an investigation of the set of cognitive tools that enable us to do things that look like common-sense. The proposal here is that *interaction* is core to human communication rather than just an add-on to "real" understanding.

</details>


### [81] [OmniRefine: Alignment-Aware Cooperative Compression for Efficient Omnimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12056)
*Yuchen Deng,Zidang Cai,Hai-Tao Zheng,Jie Wang,Feidiao Yang,Yuxing Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniRefine: A training-free two-stage framework for efficient audio-visual token compression in Omnimodal LLMs that preserves cross-modal alignment while reducing inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Omnimodal LLMs have strong audio-video understanding but face high inference costs due to long video streams and dense audio sequences. Existing compression methods use fixed/native units that disrupt cross-modal correspondence and complementary information needed for audio-video reasoning, making it hard to improve efficiency while maintaining performance.

Method: Two-stage training-free framework: 1) Correspondence-Preserving Chunk Refinement aligns native chunk boundaries into cross-modally aligned compression units using frame-audio similarity and dynamic programming; 2) Modality-Aware Cooperative Compression jointly compresses video and audio tokens within each refined unit to reduce redundancy while preserving critical evidence.

Result: OmniRefine achieves better efficiency-performance trade-off than strong baselines, maintains stable performance under lower compression ratios. On WorldSense, reaches 46.7% accuracy at 44% token retention ratio, nearly matching full-token baseline performance.

Conclusion: The proposed OmniRefine framework successfully addresses the efficiency limitations of Omnimodal LLMs by preserving cross-modal correspondence during compression, enabling practical deployment with minimal performance degradation while significantly reducing inference costs.

Abstract: Omnimodal large language models (Omni-LLMs) show strong capability in audio-video understanding, but their practical deployment remains limited by high inference cost of long video streams and dense audio sequences. Despite recent progress, existing compression methods for Omni-LLMs typically rely on fixed or native compression units, which can disrupt cross-modal correspondence and the complementary information required for audio-video reasoning, making it difficult to improve inference efficiency while stably preserving performance. To address this, we propose OmniRefine, a training-free two-stage framework for efficient audio-visual token compression in Omni-LLMs. First, Correspondence-Preserving Chunk Refinement refines native chunk boundaries into cross-modally aligned compression units through frame-audio similarity and dynamic programming. Second, Modality-Aware Cooperative Compression jointly compresses video and audio tokens within each refined unit to reduce redundancy while preserving critical evidence. Extensive experiments show that OmniRefine achieves a better efficiency-performance trade-off than strong baselines and maintains stable performance under lower compression ratios. On WorldSense, it still reaches 46.7% accuracy at a 44% token retention ratio, nearly matching the full-token baseline. The code and interface will be released to facilitate further research.

</details>


### [82] [SAGE: A Self-Evolving Agentic Graph-Memory Engine for Structure-Aware Associative Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12061)
*Juntong Wang,Haoyue Zhao,guanghui Pan,Xiyuan Wang,Yanbo Wang,Qiyan Deng,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAGE is a Self-evolving Agentic Graph-memory Engine that treats memory as a dynamic graph rather than static retrieval middleware, enabling better evidence chain recovery, structural role exploitation, and memory improvement through feedback loops between memory writer and reader components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG and GraphRAG systems treat memory graphs as static retrieval middleware, which limits their ability to: 1) recover complete evidence chains from partial cues, 2) exploit reusable graph-structural roles, and 3) improve memory through downstream feedback. Long-term memory is becoming a central bottleneck for language agents.

Method: SAGE couples two roles: a memory writer that incrementally constructs structured graph memory from interaction histories, and a Graph Foundation Model-based memory reader that performs retrieval and provides feedback to the memory writer. The framework includes rigorous theoretical analyses.

Result: SAGE improves evidence recovery, answer grounding, and retrieval efficiency across multiple benchmarks. After two self-evolution rounds, it achieves best average rank on multi-hop QA. In zero-shot open-domain transfer, reaches 82.5/91.6 Recall@2/5 on NQ. Shows improvements on LongMemEval and HaluMem for long-term memory and hallucination-diagnostic metrics.

Conclusion: Self-evolving, structure-aware graph memory is a promising foundation for robust long-horizon language agents, overcoming limitations of static memory approaches through the dynamic writer-reader feedback loop in SAGE.

Abstract: Long-term memory is becoming a central bottleneck for language agents. Exsting RAG and GraphRAG systems largely treat memory graphs as static retrieval middleware, which limits their ability to recover complete evidence chains from partial cues, exploit reusable graph-structrual roles, and improve the memory itself through downstream feedback. We introduce SAGE, a Self-evolving Agentic Graph-memory Engine that models graph memory as a dynamic long-term memory substrate. SAGE couples two roles: a memory writer that incrementally constucts structured graph memory from interaction histories, and a Graph Foundation Model-based memory reader to perform retrieval and provide feedback to the memory writer. We provide rigorooous theoretical annalyses supporting the framework. Across multi-hop QA, open-domain retireval, domain-specific review QA, and long-term agent-memory benchmarks, SAGE improves evidence recovery, answer grounding, and retrieval efficiency: after two self-evolution rounds, it achieves the best average rank on multi-hop QA; in zero-shot open-domain transfer, it reaches 82.5/91.6 Recall@2/5 on NQ. Further results on LongMemEval and HaluMem show that traning and reader-writer feedback improve multiple long-term memory and hallucination-diagnostic metrics, suggesting that self-evolving, structure-aware graph memory is a promising foundation for robust long-horizon language agents.

</details>


### [83] [Autonomy and Agency in Agentic AI: Architectural Tactics for Regulated Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12105)
*Damir Safin,Dian Balta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a two-dimensional design space for agentic AI systems that explicitly couples agency and autonomy with five operational levels each, providing six architectural tactics and five deployment parameters for principled, compliance-aware design in regulated contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches treat agency and autonomy independently in agentic AI systems, leaving practitioners without a principled basis for reasoning about oversight, action consequences, and error correction in regulated contexts where compliance requirements mandate human involvement based on action consequences.

Method: Proposes a two-dimensional design space with five operational levels for both agency (reasoning to committed writes) and autonomy (human-commanded to fully autonomous). Introduces six architectural tactics (checkpoints, escalation, multi-agent delegation, tool provisioning, tool fencing, write staging) and examines five deployment parameters (model capability, agent architecture, tool fidelity, workflow bottlenecks, evaluation) that shape achievable configurations.

Result: Provides a comprehensive framework that makes explicit the coupling between agency and autonomy, offering a shared vocabulary for principled design where responsibility, auditability, and reversibility are explicit design considerations rather than retrofitted properties, demonstrated through worked examples from public-sector contexts.

Conclusion: The design space, tactics, and deployment parameters collectively enable compliance-aware agentic AI design for regulated contexts, addressing the critical need for systems that properly balance agency and autonomy while ensuring appropriate human oversight, error correction, and compliance with regulatory requirements.

Abstract: Deploying agentic AI in regulated contexts requires principled reasoning about two design dimensions: agency (what the system can do) and autonomy (how much it acts without human involvement). Though often treated independently, they are coupled: at higher autonomy, human error correction is less available, so reliable operation requires constraining agency accordingly; compliance requirements reinforce this by mandating human involvement as action consequences grow. Yet no established approach addresses them jointly, leaving practitioners without a principled basis for reasoning about oversight, action consequences, and error correction.
  This work introduces a two-dimensional design space in which both dimensions are organised into five operational levels, making the coupling explicit and navigable. Autonomy ranges from human-commanded operation (L1) to fully autonomous monitoring (L5); agency ranges from reasoning over supplied context (L1) to committed writes to authoritative records (L5). Building on this space, we propose six architectural tactics--checkpoints, escalation, multi-agent delegation, tool provisioning, tool fencing, and write staging--for adjusting a deployment's position within it. The tactics are grounded in two worked examples from public-sector contexts, illustrating how they apply under realistic compliance constraints. We further examine five deployment parameters--model capability, agent architecture, tool fidelity, workflow bottlenecks, and evaluation--that shape what is achievable at any configuration independently of agency and autonomy. Together, the design space, tactics, and deployment parameters provide a shared vocabulary for principled, compliance-aware agentic AI design in which responsibility, auditability, and reversibility are explicit design considerations rather than properties that must be retrofitted after deployment.

</details>


### [84] [Large Language Models as Amortized Pareto-Front Generators for Constrained Bi-Objective Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12106)
*Peipei Xu,SiYuan Ma,Yaohua Liu,Yu Wu,Guanliang Liu,Yang Zhang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIPS uses fine-tuned LLMs as amortized generators to directly produce feasible Pareto fronts for constrained bi-objective convex optimization from textual problem descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for generating Pareto fronts in constrained bi-objective optimization require repeated optimization for each instance, which is computationally expensive. There's a need for efficient, amortized generators that can directly output Pareto fronts given problem descriptions.

Method: DIPS fine-tunes large language models as amortized Pareto-front generators using three key techniques: compact discretization scheme, Numerically Grounded Token Initialization for handling new numerical tokens, and Three-Phase Curriculum Optimization that progressively aligns structural validity, feasibility, and Pareto-front quality.

Result: The fine-tuned 7B-parameter model achieves normalized hypervolume ratios of 95.29% to 98.18% relative to reference fronts across five families of constrained bi-objective convex problems. With vLLM-accelerated inference, DIPS solves one instance in as little as 0.16 seconds and outperforms general-purpose and reasoning LLM baselines.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as effective amortized generators for continuous Pareto-front approximation, offering an end-to-end framework that bypasses the need for repeated optimization for each problem instance.

Abstract: Generating feasible Pareto fronts for constrained bi-objective continuous optimization is central to multi-criteria decision-making. Existing methods usually rely on iterative scalarization, evolutionary search, or problem-specific solvers, requiring repeated optimization for each instance. We introduce DIPS, an end-to-end framework that fine-tunes large language models as amortized Pareto-front generators for constrained bi-objective convex optimization. Given a textual problem description, DIPS directly outputs an ordered set of feasible continuous decision vectors approximating the Pareto front. To make continuous optimization compatible with autoregressive language modeling, DIPS combines a compact discretization scheme, Numerically Grounded Token Initialization for new numerical tokens, and Three-Phase Curriculum Optimization, which progressively aligns structural validity, feasibility, and Pareto-front quality. Across five families of constrained bi-objective convex problems, a fine-tuned 7B-parameter model achieves normalized hypervolume ratios of 95.29% to 98.18% relative to reference fronts. With vLLM-accelerated inference, DIPS solves one instance in as little as 0.16 seconds and outperforms general-purpose and reasoning LLM baselines under the evaluated setting. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as effective amortized generators for continuous Pareto-front approximation.

</details>


### [85] [Adaptive Multi-Round Allocation with Stochastic Arrivals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12111)
*Yuqi Pan,Davin Choo,Haichuan Wang,Milind Tambe,Alastair van Heerden,Cheryl Johnson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Efficient multi-round resource allocation for network recruitment using population-level surrogate value functions to overcome computational intractability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses adaptive network recruitment problems where limited resources must be allocated over multiple rounds to individuals with stochastic referral capacity, where successful referrals generate future decision opportunities but allocating more resources to an individual shows diminishing returns.

Method: 1. Single-round allocation solved exactly with greedy approach based on marginal survival probabilities. 2. For multi-round setting, introduced population-level surrogate value function depending only on remaining budget and frontier size. 3. Used truncated probability generating functions to enable exact dynamic program with polynomial complexity. 4. Analyzed robustness under model misspecification with error decomposition.

Result: Developed planning algorithm with polynomial complexity in total budget, proved multi-round error bound decomposing into single-round frontier error and population-level transition error, and evaluated method on real-world inspired recruitment scenarios.

Conclusion: The population-level surrogate value function successfully addresses computational intractability in multi-round resource allocation for network recruitment, providing efficient algorithms with provable error bounds and practical applicability to real-world scenarios.

Abstract: We study a sequential resource allocation problem motivated by adaptive network recruitment, in which a limited budget of identical resources must be allocated over multiple rounds to individuals with stochastic referral capacity. Successful referrals endogenously generate future decision opportunities while allocating additional resources to an individual exhibits diminishing returns. We first show that the single-round allocation problem admits an exact greedy solution based on marginal survival probabilities. In the multi-round setting, the resulting Bellman recursion is intractable due to the stochastic, high-dimensional evolution of the frontier. To address this, we introduce a population-level surrogate value function that depends only on the remaining budget and frontier size. This surrogate enables an exact dynamic program via truncated probability generating functions, yielding a planning algorithm with polynomial complexity in the total budget. We further analyze robustness under model misspecification, proving a multi-round error bound that decomposes into a tight single-round frontier error and a population-level transition error. Finally, we evaluate our method on real-world inspired recruitment scenarios.

</details>


### [86] [To Whom Do Language Models Align? Measuring Principal Hierarchies Under High-Stakes Competing Demands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12120)
*Fangyi Yu,Nabeel Seedat,Jonathan Richard Schwarz,Andrew M. Bean*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier language models frequently fail to uphold professional standards in medical and legal domains when user instructions conflict with those standards, showing unstable principal hierarchies across contexts and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models in professional settings face conflicting demands from users, institutional authorities, and professional norms. Understanding how models prioritize these competing stakeholders (principal hierarchy) is crucial for high-stakes deployments.

Method: Tested ten frontier models across 7,136 scenarios in legal and medical domains, examining how models respond when user instructions conflict with professional standards during task execution versus advisory contexts.

Result: Models frequently fail to adhere to professional standards during task execution when instructions conflict, despite upholding them in advisory contexts. Principal hierarchies are unstable across domains and inconsistent across model families. Primary failure mechanism is knowledge omission - models possess relevant knowledge but suppress it in outputs.

Conclusion: Current alignment methods, including published alignment hierarchies, are unlikely to be robust for high-stakes professional deployments due to inconsistent alignment across task framing, domain, and model families.

Abstract: Language models deployed in high-stakes professional settings face conflicting demands from users, institutional authorities, and professional norms. How models act when these demands conflict reveals a principal hierarchy -- an implicit ordering over competing stakeholders that determines, for instance, whether a medical AI receiving a cost-reduction directive from a hospital administrator complies at the expense of evidence-based care, or refuses because professional standards require it. Across 7,136 scenarios in legal and medical domains, we test ten frontier models and find that models frequently fail to adhere to professional standards during task execution, such as drafting, when user instructions conflict with those standards -- despite adequately upholding them when users seek advisory guidance. We further find that the hierarchies between user, authority, and professional standards exhibited by these models are unstable across medical and legal contexts and inconsistent across model families. When failing to follow professional standards, the primary failure mechanism is knowledge omission: models that demonstrably possess relevant knowledge produce harmful outputs without surfacing conflicting knowledge. In a particularly troubling instance, we find that a reasoning model recognizes the relevant knowledge in its reasoning trace -- e.g., that a drug has been withdrawn -- yet suppresses this in the user-facing answer and proceeds to recommend the drug under authority pressure anyway. Inconsistent alignment across task framing, domain, and model families suggests that current alignment methods, including published alignment hierarchies, are unlikely to be robust when models are deployed in high-stakes professional settings.

</details>


### [87] [Rollout Cards: A Reproducibility Standard for Agent Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12131)
*Charlie Masters,Ziyuan Liu,Stefano V. Albrecht*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces rollout cards to address reproducibility issues in agent research by preserving complete rollout records and reporting rules, showing how different scoring methods can significantly alter benchmark results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent research suffers from reproducibility problems similar to ML/RL - papers compare systems using reported scores while hiding the underlying rollout records, making it difficult to inspect how scores were computed. Different evaluation choices can lead to different scores for the same behavior.

Method: Conducted structured audit of 50 training/evaluation repositories; introduced rollout cards as publication bundles that preserve rollout records and declare views, reporting rules, and drops manifests; validated in two settings: analyzing four partial public releases and re-grading preserved benchmark outputs across various tasks.

Result: Found that none of 50 popular repositories report failure/error/skip rates alongside headline scores; documented 37 cases where reporting rules can change task-success rates, cost accounting, or timing; showed that changing only reporting rules can alter scores by 20.9 absolute percentage points and sometimes invert model rankings.

Conclusion: Rollout records, not reported scores, should be the unit of reproducibility for agent research. Rollout cards address this by preserving complete evidence, and the authors provide a reference implementation in Ergon with publicly available rollout-card exports for various benchmarks.

Abstract: Reproducibility problems that have long affected machine learning and reinforcement learning are now surfacing in agent research: papers compare systems by reported scores while leaving the rollout records behind those scores difficult to inspect. For agentic tasks, this matters because the same behaviour can receive different reported scores when evaluations select different parts of a rollout or apply different reporting rules. In a structured audit of 50 popular training and evaluation repositories, we find that none report how many runs failed, errored, or were skipped alongside headline scores. We also document 37 cases where reporting rules can change task-success rates, cost/token accounting, or timing measurements for fixed evidence, sometimes dramatically. We treat rollout records, not reported scores, as the unit of reproducibility for agent research. We introduce rollout cards: publication bundles that preserve the rollout record and declare the views, reporting rules, and drops manifests behind reported scores. We validate rollout cards in two settings. First, four partial public releases in tool safety, multi-agent systems, theorem proving, and search let us compute analyses their original reports did not include. Second, re-grading preserved benchmark outputs across short-answer, code-generation, and tool-use tasks shows that changing only the reporting rule can change reported scores by 20.9 absolute percentage points and, in some cases, invert rankings of frontier models. We release a reference implementation integrated into Ergon, an open-source reinforcement learning gym, and publicly publish Ergon-produced rollout-card exports for benchmarks spanning tool use, software engineering, web interaction, multi-agent coordination, safety, and search to support future research.

</details>


### [88] [BoolXLLM: LLM-Assisted Explainability for Boolean Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12139)
*Du Cheng,Serdar Kadioglu,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BoolXLLM is a hybrid framework that combines Large Language Models with Boolean rule-based classifiers (BoolXAI) to create more accessible and interpretable AI systems for non-technical users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While rule-based approaches like BoolXAI provide faithful and compact model representations, they still present practical challenges for non-technical stakeholders: 1) selecting semantically meaningful features, and 2) translating formal logical rules into accessible explanations.

Method: Integrates LLMs into BoolXAI's pipeline at three critical stages: 1) LLM-guided feature selection to identify domain-relevant variables, 2) LLM-proposed discretization strategies for numerical features, and 3) translation of Boolean rules into natural language explanations at both global and local levels.

Result: Early empirical results show that LLM-assisted pipelines improve interpretability while maintaining competitive predictive performance, creating a system that is both theoretically grounded and accessible to non-experts.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the promise of combining symbolic reasoning with language-based models for human-centered explainability, bridging formal explanations with human-understandable narratives.

Abstract: Interpretable machine learning aims to provide transparent models whose decision-making processes can be readily understood by humans. Recent advances in rule-based approaches, such as expressive Boolean formulas (BoolXAI), offer faithful and compact representations of model behavior. However, for non-technical stakeholders, main challenges remain in practice: (i) selecting semantically meaningful features and (ii) translating formal logical rules into accessible explanations.
  In this work, we propose BoolXLLM , as a hybrid framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) into the end-to-end pipeline of Boolean rule learning. We augment BoolXAI , an expressive Boolean rule-based classifier, with LLMs at three critical stages: (1) feature selection, where LLMs guide the identification of domain-relevant variables; (2) threshold recommendation, where LLMs propose semantically meaningful discretization strategies for numerical features; and (3) rule compression and interpretation, where Boolean rules are translated into natural language explanations at both global and local levels.
  This integration bridges formal, faithful explanations with human-understandable narratives. This allows build an explainable AI system that is both theoretically grounded and accessible to non-experts. Early empirical results demonstrate that LLM-assisted pipelines improve interpretability while maintaining competitive predictive performance. Our work highlights the promise of combining symbolic reasoning with language-based models for human-centered explainability.

</details>


### [89] [MM-OptBench: A Solver-Grounded Benchmark for Multimodal Optimization Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12154)
*Zhong Li,Qi Huang,Yuxuan Zhu,Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri,Niki van Stein,Thomas Bäck,Matthijs van Leeuwen,Zaiwen Wen,Lincen Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MM-OptBench introduces multimodal optimization modeling benchmarks requiring LLMs to generate optimization formulations and solver code from text+visual problem specifications, showing current MLLMs struggle (best models ~52% accuracy) and math-specialized models fail completely.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing optimization modeling benchmarks are text-only, but real-world optimization tasks often involve visual artifacts like tables, graphs, maps, and dashboards. Current language models can generate formulations from text, but struggle with multimodal inputs that combine text and visual information.

Method: Developed solver-grounded framework that generates structured optimization instances, verifies each with exact solver, builds both model-facing inputs and reference files. Instantiated as MM-OptBench with 780 solver-verified instances across 6 optimization families, 26 subcategories, and 3 difficulty levels.

Result: Best models achieve 52.1% and 51.3% pass@1; average across general-purpose MLLMs is 43.4% on easy and 15.9% on hard instances. All three math-specialized MLLMs solve 0/780 instances. Errors occur in both extracting data from text/visuals and converting to solver-correct code.

Conclusion: Multimodal optimization modeling remains far from solved, highlighting need for better multimodal reasoning in optimization contexts. MM-OptBench provides important testbed for advancing solver-grounded, decision-oriented multimodal intelligence.

Abstract: Optimization modeling translates real decision-making problems into mathematical optimization models and solver-executable implementations. Although language models are increasingly used to generate optimization formulations and solver code, existing benchmarks are almost entirely text-only. This omits many optimization-modeling tasks that arise in operational practice, where requirements are described in text but instance information is conveyed through visual artifacts such as tables, graphs, maps, schedules, and dashboards. We introduce multimodal optimization modeling, a benchmark setting in which models must construct both a mathematical formulation and executable solver code from a text-and-visual problem specification. To evaluate this setting, we develop a solver-grounded framework that generates structured optimization instances, verifies each with an exact solver, and builds both the model-facing inputs and hidden reference files from the same verified source. We instantiate the framework as MM-OptBench, a benchmark of 780 solver-verified instances spanning 6 optimization families, 26 subcategories, and 3 structural difficulty levels. We evaluate 9 multimodal large language models (MLLMs), including 6 frontier general-purpose models and 3 math-specialized models, with aggregate, family-level, difficulty-level, and failure-mode analyses. The results show that the task remains far from solved: the best two models reach 52.1% and 51.3% pass@1, while on average across the six general-purpose MLLMs, pass@1 is 43.4% on easy instances and 15.9% on hard instances. All three math-specialized MLLMs solve 0/780 instances. Failure attribution shows that errors arise both when extracting instance data from text and visuals and when turning extracted data into solver-correct formulations and code. MM-OptBench provides a testbed for solver-grounded, decision-oriented multimodal intelligence.

</details>


### [90] [ALGOGEN: Tool-Generated Verifiable Traces for Reliable Algorithm Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12159)
*Kunpeng Liao,Yuexiao Ma,Yisheng Lin,Hualin Zeng,Xiawu Zheng,Rongrong Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ALGOGEN proposes a new paradigm that decouples algorithm execution from rendering for algorithm visualization, using Visualization Trace Algebra (VTA) and Rendering Style Language (RSL) to reduce LLM hallucinations and improve success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based algorithm visualization systems (like CODE2VIDEO) suffer from hallucinations when trying to simultaneously handle algorithm simulation and video rendering constraints, leading to reduced execution success rates, element overlap, and inter-frame inconsistencies.

Method: ALGOGEN decouples algorithm execution from rendering: 1) Introduces Visualization Trace Algebra (VTA) as a monoid over algorithm states/operations, 2) LLM generates Python tracker to simulate algorithm flow and output VTA-JSON traces, 3) Defines Rendering Style Language (RSL) to templatize layouts, 4) Deterministic renderer compiles traces with RSL into Manim, LaTeX/TikZ, or Three.js outputs.

Result: On a LeetCode AV benchmark of 200 tasks, ALGOGEN achieves 99.8% success rate compared to 82.5% for end-to-end methods, representing a 17.3% improvement. This demonstrates effective mitigation of LLM hallucinations in complex AV tasks.

Conclusion: The decoupling paradigm effectively addresses LLM hallucinations in algorithm visualization tasks, providing a more reliable solution for automated generation of high-quality algorithm visualizations. The approach enables generation of consistent, overlap-free visualizations across multiple output formats.

Abstract: Algorithm Visualization (AV) helps students build mental models by animating algorithm execution states. Recent LLM-based systems such as CODE2VIDEO generate AV videos in an end-to-end manner. However, this paradigm requires the system to simultaneously simulate algorithm flow and satisfy video rendering constraints, such as element layout and color schemes. This complex task induces LLM hallucinations, resulting in reduced execution success rates, element overlap, and inter-frame inconsistencies.
  To address these challenges, we propose ALGOGEN, a novel paradigm that decouples algorithm execution from rendering. We first introduce Visualization Trace Algebra (VTA), a monoid over algorithm visual states and operations. The LLM then generates a Python tracker that simulates algorithm flow and outputs VTA-JSON traces, a JSON encoding of VTA. For rendering, we define a Rendering Style Language (RSL) to templatize algorithm layouts. A deterministic renderer then compiles algorithm traces with RSL into Manim, LaTeX/TikZ, or Three.js outputs.
  Evaluated on a LeetCode AV benchmark of 200 tasks, ALGOGEN achieves an average success rate improvement of 17.3% compared to end-to-end methods, with 99.8% versus 82.5%. These results demonstrate that our decoupling paradigm effectively mitigates LLM hallucinations in complex AV tasks, providing a more reliable solution for automated generation of high-quality algorithm visualizations. Demo videos and code are available in the project repository.

</details>


### [91] [Do Enterprise Systems Need Learned World Models? The Importance of Context to Infer Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12178)
*Jishnu Sethumadhavan Nair,Patrice Bechard,Rishabh Maheshwary,Surajit Dasgupta,Sravan Ramachandran,Aakash Bhagat,Shruthan Radhakrishna,Pulkit Pattnaik,Johan Obando-Ceron,Shiva Krishna Reddy Malay,Sagar Davasam,Seganrasan Subramanian,Vipul Mittal,Sridhar Krishna Nemala,Christopher Pal,Srinivas Sunkara,Sai Rajeswar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Enterprise discovery agents use runtime configuration reading instead of just learned world models to handle business logic changes in enterprise systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise systems have tenant-specific business logic that varies across deployments and evolves over time, making traditional world models trained on historical data brittle under deployment shifts. The paper questions whether agents need to learn rules when they can be read at inference time.

Method: Propose enterprise discovery agents that recover relevant transition dynamics at runtime by reading system configuration rather than relying solely on internalized representations. Introduce CascadeBench, a reasoning-focused benchmark for enterprise cascade prediction using diverse synthetic environments.

Result: Offline-trained world models perform well in-distribution but degrade as dynamics change, whereas discovery-based agents are more robust under shift by grounding predictions in the current instance.

Conclusion: In configurable enterprise environments, agents should incorporate mechanisms for discovering relevant transition logic at runtime rather than relying solely on fixed internalized dynamics.

Abstract: World models enable agents to anticipate the effects of their actions by internalizing environment dynamics. In enterprise systems, however, these dynamics are often defined by tenant-specific business logic that varies across deployments and evolves over time, making models trained on historical transitions brittle under deployment shift. We ask a question the world-models literature has not addressed: when the rules can be read at inference time, does an agent still need to learn them? We argue, and demonstrate empirically, that in settings where transition dynamics are configurable and readable, runtime discovery complements offline training by grounding predictions in the active system instance. We propose enterprise discovery agents, which recover relevant transition dynamics at runtime by reading the system's configuration rather than relying solely on internalized representations. We introduce CascadeBench, a reasoning-focused benchmark for enterprise cascade prediction that adopts the evaluation methodology of World of Workflows on diverse synthetic environments, and use it together with deployment-shift evaluation to show that offline-trained world models can perform well in-distribution but degrade as dynamics change, whereas discovery-based agents are more robust under shift by grounding their predictions in the current instance. Our findings suggest that, in configurable enterprise environments, agents should not rely solely on fixed internalized dynamics, but should incorporate mechanisms for discovering relevant transition logic at runtime.

</details>


### [92] [MolDeTox: Evaluating Language Model's Stepwise Fragment Editing for Molecular Detoxification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12181)
*Jueon Park,Wonjune Jang,Jiwoo Lee,Yein Park,Jaewoo Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MolDeTox is a novel benchmark for molecular detoxification that addresses limitations in existing toxicity repair benchmarks by enabling fine-grained evaluation of toxicity-aware molecular optimization across stepwise tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models and benchmarks overlook toxicity-related challenges in drug discovery, focusing on general property optimization without adequately addressing safety concerns. Existing toxicity repair benchmarks suffer from limited data diversity, low structural validity of generated molecules, and heavy reliance on proxy models for toxicity assessment.

Method: Proposed MolDeTox benchmark designed for fine-grained and reliable evaluation of toxicity-aware molecular optimization. Evaluated a wide range of general-purpose LLMs and VLMs under diverse settings, demonstrating that understanding and generating molecules at the fragment-level improves structural validity and enhances quality of generated molecules.

Result: Understanding and generating molecules at the fragment-level improves structural validity and enhances the quality of generated molecules. Through detailed task-level performance analysis, MolDeTox provides an interpretable benchmark that enables deeper understanding of the detoxification process.

Conclusion: MolDeTox addresses critical gaps in toxicity-aware molecular optimization benchmarks by providing a comprehensive, fine-grained evaluation framework that improves structural validity and enables interpretable analysis of detoxification processes in drug discovery.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) have recently shown promising capabilities in various scientific domain. In particular, these advances have opened new opportunities in drug discovery, where the ability to understand and modify molecular structures is critical for optimizing drug properties such as efficacy and toxicity. However, existing models and benchmarks often overlook toxicity-related challenges, focusing primarily on general property optimization without adequately addressing safety concerns. In addition, even existing toxicity repair benchmarks suffer from limited data diversity, low structural validity of generated molecules, and heavy reliance on proxy models for toxicity assessment. To address these limitations, we propose MolDeTox, a novel benchmark for molecular detoxification, designed to enable fine-grained and reliable evaluation of toxicity-aware molecular optimization across stepwise tasks. We evaluate a wide range of general-purpose LLMs and VLMs under diverse settings, and demonstrate that understanding and generating molecules at the fragment-level improves structural validity and enhances the quality of generated molecules. Moreover, through detailed task-level performance analysis, MolDeTox provides an interpretable benchmark that enables a deeper understanding of the detoxification process. Our dataset is available at : https://huggingface.co/datasets/MolDeTox/MolDeTox

</details>


### [93] [Goal-Oriented Reasoning for RAG-based Memory in Conversational Agentic LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12213)
*Jiazhou Liang,Armin Toroghi,Yifan Simon Liu,Faeze Moradi Kalarde,Liam Gallagher,Scott Sanner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Goal-Mem is a goal-oriented reasoning framework that uses backward chaining and Natural Language Logic to improve RAG-based conversational AI agents' ability to handle complex multi-hop and commonsense reasoning by decomposing goals into subgoals for targeted memory retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG-based conversational AI agents struggle with maintaining coherent behavior over long conversations. While RAG methods store interactions in external memory, they typically retrieve information based on semantic similarity to raw user utterances, which often leads to irrelevant or insufficient evidence for complex reasoning tasks like multi-hop and commonsense questions. This approach lacks explicit reasoning about missing intermediate facts.

Method: Goal-Mem introduces a goal-oriented reasoning framework that performs explicit backward chaining from the user's utterance as a goal. The method decomposes each goal into atomic subgoals, performs targeted memory retrieval to satisfy each subgoal, and iteratively identifies what information should be retrieved when intermediate goals cannot be resolved. This is formalized using Natural Language Logic, which combines the verifiability of First-Order Logic with natural language expressivity.

Result: Through extensive experiments on two datasets and comparisons with nine strong memory baselines, Goal-Mem consistently improves performance, particularly on tasks requiring multi-hop reasoning and implicit inference.

Conclusion: Goal-Mem demonstrates that explicit goal-oriented reasoning with backward chaining and Natural Language Logic significantly enhances RAG-based agentic memory systems, especially for challenging reasoning tasks that require connecting multiple pieces of information and making implicit inferences.

Abstract: LLM-based conversational AI agents struggle to maintain coherent behavior over long horizons due to limited context. While RAG-based approaches are increasingly adopted to overcome this limitation by storing interactions in external memory modules and performing retrieval from them, their effectiveness in answering challenging questions (e.g., multi-hop, commonsense) ultimately depends on the agent's ability to reason over the retrieved information. However, existing methods typically retrieve memory based on semantic similarity to the raw user utterance, which lacks explicit reasoning about missing intermediate facts and often returns evidence that is irrelevant or insufficient for grounded reasoning. In this work, we introduce Goal-Mem, a goal-oriented reasoning framework for RAG-based agentic memory that performs explicit backward chaining from the user's utterance as a goal. Rather than progressively expanding from retrieved context, Goal-Mem decomposes each goal into atomic subgoals, performs targeted memory retrieval to satisfy each subgoal, and iteratively identifies what information from memory should be retrieved when intermediate goals cannot be resolved. We formalize this process in Natural Language Logic, a logical system that combines the verifiability of reasoning provided by FOL with the expressivity of natural language. Through extensive experiments on two datasets and comparing to nine strong memory baselines, we show that Goal-Mem consistently improves performance, particularly on tasks requiring multi-hop reasoning and implicit inference.

</details>


### [94] [No Action Without a NOD: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Architecture for Reliable Service Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12240)
*Zixu Yang,Hang Zheng,Nan Jiang,Zhiyang Tang,Situo Zhang,Xiaobao Wu,Lu Chen,Kai Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NOD is a heterogeneous multi-agent architecture (Navigator-Operator-Director) that improves LLM agent reliability for long-horizon service tasks by externalizing task state tracking and adding selective external oversight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM service agents suffer from unreliability in long-horizon tasks, including policy violations, tool hallucinations, and misaligned actions, which prevents real-world deployment for complex services like flight booking.

Method: Proposes NOD: 1) Externalizes a structured Global State for explicit task state tracking rather than implicit dialogue context; 2) Uses a Navigator for consistent decision-making; 3) Introduces selective external oversight where a Director agent verifies critical actions before execution; 4) Employs a heterogeneous multi-agent architecture with Navigator-Operator-Director roles.

Result: Experiments on τ²-Bench show NOD achieves higher task success rates and critical action precision than baselines. It significantly reduces policy violations, tool hallucinations, and user-intent misalignment, improving overall service agent reliability.

Conclusion: NOD effectively addresses unreliability in long-horizon service tasks by combining explicit state tracking with selective external oversight, mitigating error propagation and unsafe behavior, making LLM agents more suitable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have increasingly advanced service applications, such as booking flight tickets. However, these service agents suffer from unreliability in long-horizon tasks, as they often produce policy violations, tool hallucinations, and misaligned actions, which greatly impedes their real-world deployment. To address these challenges, we propose NOD (Navigator-Operator-Director), a heterogeneous multi-agent architecture for service agents. Instead of maintaining task state implicitly in dialogue context as in prior work, we externalize a structured Global State to enable explicit task state tracking and consistent decision-making by the Navigator. Besides, we introduce selective external oversight before critical actions, allowing an independent Director agent to verify execution and intervene when necessary. As such, NOD effectively mitigates error propagation and unsafe behavior in long-horizon tasks. Experiments on $τ^2$-Bench demonstrate that NOD achieves higher task success rates and critical action precision over baselines. More importantly, NOD improves the reliability of service agents by reducing policy violations, tool hallucinations, and user-intent misalignment.

</details>


### [95] [Why Conclusions Diverge from the Same Observations: Formalizing World-Model Non-Identifiability via an Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12255)
*Toru Takahashi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that disagreements in inference are better explained by non-identifiability in learning systems rather than cognitive defects, showing how divergence occurs at two levels and projects onto fundamental bases due to learning constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the phenomenon where people sharing the same documents and observations reach different conclusions, often leading to accusations of cognitive defects, irrationality, or bad faith. The paper seeks to provide a better theoretical framework for understanding such disagreements.

Method: Introduces an inference profile θ = (R, E, S, D) consisting of Reference, Exploration, Stabilization, and Horizon components. Organizes divergence into θ-level non-identifiability (different conclusions under same world model W due to different inference settings) and W-level non-identifiability (learned world models W themselves diverge due to biased data exposure from repeated inference settings).

Result: Shows how outputs can split even for the same observation o and same world model W due to inference profile differences. Demonstrates why disagreements tend to project onto specific bases (abstract vs concrete, externalizability, order vs freedom) as consequences of computational, observational, and coordination constraints in learning systems.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unified explanation for divergent inferences, relates to deep representation learning concepts, and is illustrated through AI regulation debates. Suggests disagreements are inherent to inference and learning systems rather than cognitive failures.

Abstract: When people share the same documents and observations yet reach different conclusions, the disagreement often shifts into a judgment that the other party is cognitively defective, irrational, or acting in bad faith. This paper argues that such divergence is better described as a form of non-identifiability inherent in inference and learning, rather than as a defect of the other party. We organize the phenomenon into two levels: (i) $θ$-level non-identifiability, where conclusions diverge under the same world model $W$ because inference settings differ; and (ii) $W$-level non-identifiability, where repeated use of an inference setting $θ$ biases data exposure and update rules, causing the learned world model $W$ itself to diverge. We introduce an inference profile $θ= (R, E, S, D)$, consisting of Reference, Exploration, Stabilization, and Horizon, and show how outputs can split even for the same observation $o$ and the same $W$. We further explain why disagreements tend to project onto a small number of bases -- abstract versus concrete, externalizability, and order versus freedom -- as a consequence of general constraints on learning systems: computational, observational, and coordination constraints. Finally, we relate the framework to deep representation learning, including representation hierarchy, latent-state estimation, and regularization-exploration trade-offs, and illustrate the framework through a case study on AI regulation debates.

</details>


### [96] [Missingness-MDPs: Bridging the Theory of Missing Data and POMDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12262)
*Joshua Wendland,Markel Zubia,Roman Andriushchenko,Maris F. L. Galesloot,Milan Ceska,Henrik von Kleist,Thiago D. Simao,Maximilian Weininger,Nils Jansen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel subclass of POMDPs called missingness-MDPs (miss-MDPs) is introduced, which incorporates missing data theory. The paper provides PAC algorithms to learn missingness functions from data and guarantees epsilon-optimal policies in the true miss-MDP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning in partially observable environments where state features can be missing is challenging, especially when the missingness mechanism (which determines which features are unobserved) is unknown. General POMDP planning with unknown observation functions is infeasible.

Method: The authors introduce miss-MDPs as a structured subclass of POMDPs with missingness functions. They exploit structural properties of three missingness types (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) to derive PAC algorithms for learning the missingness function from action-observation trajectory data. The learned function yields an approximate miss-MDP that can be solved using standard planning methods.

Result: The algorithms provide theoretical guarantees that the resulting policies are epsilon-optimal in the true miss-MDP with high probability. Empirical results confirm the theory and show superior performance over two model-free POMDP methods.

Conclusion: By leveraging missing data theory and the structural properties of missingness types, the authors develop a principled framework for planning in environments with missing observations, providing both theoretical optimality guarantees and practical performance improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: We introduce missingness-MDPs (miss-MDPs), a novel subclass of partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) that incorporates the theory of missing data. A miss-MDP is a POMDP whose observation function is a missingness function, specifying the probability that individual state features are missing (i.e., unobserved) at a time step. The literature distinguishes three canonical missingness types: missing (1) completely at random (MCAR), (2) at random (MAR), and (3) not at random (MNAR). Our planning problem is to compute near-optimal policies for a miss-MDP with an unknown missingness function, given a dataset of action-observation trajectories. Achieving such optimality guarantees for policies requires learning the missingness function from data, which is infeasible for general POMDPs. To overcome this challenge, we exploit the structural properties of different missingness types to derive probably approximately correct (PAC) algorithms for learning the missingness function. These algorithms yield an approximate but fully specified miss-MDP that we solve using off-the-shelf planning methods. We prove that, with high probability, the resulting policies are epsilon-optimal in the true miss-MDP. Empirical results confirm the theory and demonstrate superior performance of our approach over two model-free POMDP methods.

</details>


### [97] [How Useful Is Cross-Domain Generalization for Training LLM Monitors?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12265)
*Sam Martin,Fabien Roger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning language models on multiple classification tasks with prompts improves generalization to new domains but fails when classification prompts change completely while domain stays the same. Mixing classification training with general instruction following mitigates this issue and surprisingly enables generalization to with-thinking classification and summarization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Using prompted language models as classifiers works in low-data domains but misses the robustness and performance benefits of fine-tuning. The paper investigates whether training on multiple classification tasks with their own prompts can improve performance on new domains with new classification prompts.

Method: The researchers study fine-tuning language models on multiple classification tasks, each with its own prompt, and examine generalization to new domains with new prompts. They investigate specific failure cases and explore mixing classification training with general instruction following training.

Result: Training on multiple classification tasks partially generalizes to adjacent domains, improving classification performance on unseen tasks. However, models fail when classification prompts change completely while data domain remains unchanged. Mixing classification with instruction following training retains classification benefits while mitigating generalization failures, and surprisingly enables generalization to with-thinking classification and summarization tasks.

Conclusion: Multi-task classification training with prompts enhances generalization but has limitations when prompts change dramatically. Combining classification training with instruction following creates more robust models that maintain classification benefits while improving generalization capabilities. No-thinking supervised classification training can be instrumentally useful for building other types of classifiers and monitoring systems through unexpected generalization to more complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Using prompted language models as classifiers enables classification in domains with limited training data, but misses some of the robustness and performance benefits that fine-tuning can bring. We study whether training on multiple classification tasks, each with its own prompt, improves performance on new domains with new classification prompts. We show that such training partially generalizes to adjacent domains, improving classification performance on tasks that are unseen during training. However, we identify specific edge cases where the fine-tuned models fail to follow prompts, such as when the classification prompt changes completely while the data domain remains the same as during training. We show that classification training can be mixed with general instruction following training, and that (when done well) such training keeps the benefits of classification training and mitigates its generalization failures. Surprisingly, we see that this no-thinking supervised classification training can generalize to with-thinking classification and summarization, suggesting that no-thinking classification training might be instrumentally useful in building other kinds of classifiers and monitoring systems.

</details>


### [98] [NARA: Anchor-Conditioned Relation-Aware Contextualization of Heterogeneous Geoentities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12276)
*Jina Kim,Gengchen Mai,Lingyi Zhao,Khurram Shafique,Yao-Yi Chiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NARA is a self-supervised framework for vector geospatial data that learns unified representations by jointly modeling semantics, geometry, and spatial relations across heterogeneous geoentities (points, polylines, polygons).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current geospatial foundation models focus mainly on raster data, while vector geospatial data representation learning remains fragmented, limited to specific geometry types or partial spatial relations, failing to capture unified spatial context across heterogeneous geoentities.

Method: NARA (Neural Anchor-conditioned Relation-Aware representation learning) is a self-supervised framework that learns context-dependent representations by jointly modeling semantics, geometry, and spatial relations within a unified framework, capturing relational spatial structure beyond proximity alone.

Result: Evaluation on three tasks (building function classification, traffic speed prediction, and next point-of-interest recommendation) shows consistent improvements over prior methods, demonstrating the benefit of unified relational modeling.

Conclusion: NARA successfully addresses the limitations of fragmented vector geospatial representation learning by providing a unified framework that captures rich contextualized representations across heterogeneous geoentities through joint modeling of semantics, geometry, and spatial relations.

Abstract: Geospatial foundation models have primarily focused on raster data such as satellite imagery, where self-supervised learning has been widely studied. Vector geospatial data instead represent the world as discrete geoentities with explicit geometry, semantics, and structured spatial relations, including metric proximity and topological relationships. These relations jointly determine how entities interact within space, yet existing representation learning methods remain fragmented, often restricted to specific geometry types or partial spatial relations, limiting their ability to capture unified spatial context across heterogeneous geoentities. We propose NARA (Neural Anchor-conditioned Relation-Aware representation learning), a self-supervised framework for vector geoentities. NARA learns context-dependent representations by jointly modeling semantics, geometry, and spatial relations within a unified framework and captures relational spatial structure beyond proximity alone, enabling rich contextualized representations across heterogeneous geoentities of points, polylines, and polygons. Evaluation on building function classification, traffic speed prediction, and next point-of-interest recommendation shows consistent improvements over prior methods, highlighting the benefit of unified relational modeling for vector geospatial data.

</details>


### [99] [Executable Agentic Memory for GUI Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12294)
*Zerui Qin,Sheng Yue,Xingyuan Hua,Yongjian Fu,Ju Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EAM introduces a structured Knowledge Graph approach for GUI automation that replaces step-wise LLM planning with retrieval-and-execution, achieving faster and more reliable performance through efficient memory construction and guided graph search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GUI agents use model-centric, step-wise interaction where LLMs must repeatedly re-interpret UI screens and decide actions at each step, which is fragile and inefficient for long-horizon tasks.

Method: Proposes Executable Agentic Memory (EAM), a structured Knowledge Graph that shifts GUI planning from free-form generation to retrieval-and-execution. Includes: 1) sample-efficient memory construction pipeline using state-aware DFS and action-group mining, 2) value-guided graph search where a lightweight Q-function model steers Monte Carlo Tree Search over the KG.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like UI-TARS-7B by up to 19.6% on AndroidWorld, reduces token costs 6× relative to GPT-4o, achieves 2.8s average latency, enabling reliable, quick, long-horizon GUI automation.

Conclusion: EAM represents a paradigm shift from model-centric to memory-centric GUI automation, demonstrating that structured knowledge graphs with efficient retrieval-and-execution mechanisms can significantly improve performance, efficiency, and reliability for complex GUI tasks.

Abstract: Modern GUI agents typically rely on a model-centric and step-wise interaction paradigm, where LLMs must re-interpret the UI and re-decide actions at every screen, which is fragile in long-horizon tasks. In this paper, we propose Executable Agentic Memory (EAM), a structured Knowledge Graph (KG) that shifts GUI planning from free-form generation to a robust retrieval-and-execution process. Our approach includes a sample-efficient memory construction pipeline using state-aware DFS and action-group mining to compress multi-step routines. To ensure efficient planning, we introduce a value-guided graph search where a lightweight Q-function model steers Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) over the KG. We theoretically establish bias-consistency for the Q-model and derive sample complexity bounds for path recovery. Empirically, EAM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like UI-TARS-7B by up to $19.6\%$ on AndroidWorld, while reducing token costs $6\times$ relative to GPT-4o. With a $2.8$s average latency, EAM enables reliable, quick, and long-horizon GUI automation.

</details>


### [100] [LISA: Cognitive Arbitration for Signal-Free Autonomous Intersection Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12321)
*Abderrahmane Lakas,Mohamed Amine Ferrag,Merouane Debbah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based intent-driven speed advisory system (LISA) enables signal-free autonomous intersection management with superior performance over traditional methods, reducing delays by 89.1% and fuel consumption by 48.8%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intersection management is challenging due to conflicting right-of-way demands, heterogeneous vehicle priorities, and real-time kinematic constraints. Existing approaches use LLMs as auxiliary components rather than primary decision-makers, and signal controllers remain vehicle-agnostic while reservation-based methods lack intent awareness. LLM inference latency also limits their use in sub-second control settings.

Method: LISA (LLM-Based Intent-Driven Speed Advisory) is a signal-free cognitive arbitration framework that uses an LLM to reason over declared vehicle intents, incorporating priority classes, queue pressure, and energy preferences for autonomous intersection management.

Result: LISA reduces mean control delay by up to 89.1%, maintains Level of Service C while baselines degrade to Level F, reduces mean waiting time by 93% and peak queue length by 60.6% under near-saturated demand, lowers fuel consumption by up to 48.8%, and achieves 86.2% intent satisfaction compared to 61.2% for the best non-LLM method.

Conclusion: LLM-based reasoning can enable real-time, signal-free intersection management, demonstrating that LLMs can serve as primary decision-makers rather than just auxiliary components in intelligent transportation systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) show strong potential for Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), particularly in tasks requiring situational reasoning and multi-agent coordination.
