TL;DR: A plug-and-play framework that projects ESM-2 protein language model representations onto contact graphs and uses SoftBlobGIN (a GNN with differentiable substructure pooling) to achieve strong performance on protein function prediction while providing directly interpretable structural explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein language models like ESM-2 achieve strong performance on function prediction but their learned features are difficult to interpret, as structural and evolutionary signals are encoded in dense latent spaces without clear structural explanations.

Method: Projects ESM-2 representations onto protein contact graphs and applies SoftBlobGIN, a lightweight Graph Isomorphism Network with differentiable Gumbel-softmax substructure pooling, to perform structure-aware message passing and learn coarse functional substructures for downstream tasks without retraining the language model.

Result: Achieves 92.8% accuracy and 0.898 macro-F1 on enzyme classification, improves binding-site detection AUROC from 0.885 (ESM-2 linear probe) to 0.983, and shows strong generalization with 0.733 F_max on Gene Ontology prediction. Provides auditable structural explanations including active-site residue recovery, spatial functional clusters, and catalytic contact patterns.

Conclusion: The framework serves as an interpretable structural companion to protein language models that makes their predictions more transparent and auditable, adding only ~1.1M parameters while requiring no retraining of the base language model.

Abstract: Protein language models such as ESM-2 learn rich residue representations that achieve strong performance on protein function prediction, but their features remain difficult to interpret as structural $\&$ evolutionary signals are encoded in dense latent spaces. We propose a plug-$\&$-play framework that projects ESM-2 representations onto protein contact graphs $\&$ applies $\textbf{SoftBlobGIN}$, a lightweight Graph Isomorphism Network with differentiable Gumbel-softmax substructure pooling, to perform structure-aware message passing $\&$ learn coarse functional substructures for downstream prediction tasks. Across enzyme classification, SoftBlobGIN achieves 92.8\% accuracy $\&$ 0.898 macro-F1. Unlike post hoc analysis of protein language models alone, our method produces directly auditable structural explanations: GNNExplainer recovers biologically meaningful active-site residues, spatially localized functional clusters, $\&$ catalytic contact patterns. On binding-site detection, SoftBlobGIN improves residue AUROC from $0.885$ using an ESM-2 linear probe to $0.983$, indicating that these structural explanations are not recoverable from language-model features alone. Learned blob partitions provide an additional layer of interpretability by automatically grouping residues into functional substructures, with blobs containing annotated active-site residues showing $1.85\times$ higher importance than other blobs ($ρ{=}0.339$, $p{=}0.009$), without any active-site supervision. Our framework requires no retraining of the language model, adds only $\sim$1.1M parameters, $\&$ generalises across ProteinShake tasks, achieving $F_{\max}$ of $0.733$ on Gene Ontology prediction $\&$ AUROC of $0.969$ on binding-site detection. We position this as an interpretable structural companion to protein language models that makes their predictions more transparent $\&$ auditable.

</details>


### [127] [Predicting Decisions of AI Agents from Limited Interaction through Text-Tabular Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12411)
*Eilam Shapira,Moshe Tennenholtz,Roi Reichart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers developed a method to predict AI agents' decisions in negotiations by combining game-state features with LLM-based text representations, including using an LLM as an observer to generate decision-oriented features, achieving better predictions than direct LLM prompting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents often negotiate with unfamiliar counterparts where the other agent's LLM, prompts, and logic are hidden, making it challenging to predict their decisions with potential monetary consequences. This creates a need for methods to predict an unfamiliar counterpart's decisions from limited interactions.

Method: Formulates the problem as target-adaptive text-tabular prediction where each decision point combines structured game state, offer history, and dialogue. Uses a tabular foundation model with game-state features and LLM-based text representations, plus adds LLM-as-Observer as an additional representation - a frozen LLM reads the decision-time state and dialogue, and its hidden state becomes a decision-oriented feature.

Result: The full model outperforms direct LLM-as-Predictor prompting and game+text features baselines. Observer features improve response-prediction AUC by about 4 points at K=16 across both bargaining and negotiation tasks, and reduce bargaining offer-prediction error by 14%.

Conclusion: Formulating counterpart prediction as a target-adaptive text-tabular task enables effective adaptation, and hidden LLM representations expose decision-relevant signals that direct prompting does not surface, making LLMs effective encoders rather than direct few-shot predictors.

Abstract: AI agents negotiate and transact in natural language with unfamiliar counterparts: a buyer bot facing an unknown seller, or a procurement assistant negotiating with a supplier. In such interactions, the counterpart's LLM, prompts, control logic, and rule-based fallbacks are hidden, while each decision can have monetary consequences. We ask whether an agent can predict an unfamiliar counterpart's next decision from a few interactions. To avoid real-world logging confounds, we study this problem in controlled bargaining and negotiation games, formulating it as target-adaptive text-tabular prediction: each decision point is a table row combining structured game state, offer history, and dialogue, while $K$ previous games of the same target agent, i.e., the counterpart being modeled, are provided in the prompt as labeled adaptation examples. Our model is built on a tabular foundation model that represents rows using game-state features and LLM-based text representations, and adds LLM-as-Observer as an additional representation: a small frozen LLM reads the decision-time state and dialogue; its answer is discarded, and its hidden state becomes a decision-oriented feature, making the LLM an encoder rather than a direct few-shot predictor. Training on 13 frontier-LLM agents and testing on 91 held-out scaffolded agents, the full model outperforms direct LLM-as-Predictor prompting and game+text features baselines. Within this tabular model, Observer features contribute beyond the other feature schemes: at $K=16$, they improve response-prediction AUC by about 4 points across both tasks and reduce bargaining offer-prediction error by 14%. These results show that formulating counterpart prediction as a target-adaptive text-tabular task enables effective adaptation, and that hidden LLM representations expose decision-relevant signals that direct prompting does not surface.

</details>


### [128] [AESOP: Adversarial Execution-path Selection to Overload Deep Learning Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10987)
*Tingxi Li,Mingfang Ji,Ravishka Shemal Rathnasuriya,Simin Chen,Yitao Hu,Wei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AESOP framework demonstrates how attackers can exploit ML inference pipelines by strategically selecting vulnerable paths, achieving massive efficiency degradation (up to 2,407× FLOPs inflation) that single-model attacks cannot achieve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ML deployments use dynamic inference pipelines where upstream components determine downstream workloads, creating an efficiency-attack surface that single-model attacks cannot exploit. These pipelines run under hard real-time constraints, making efficiency critical for system availability.

Method: AESOP framework combines vulnerability-guided path ranking with adaptive loss weighting to solve the adversarial path-selection problem. It targets where to attack in pipelines rather than just how to attack individual models.

Result: AESOP achieves up to 2,407× FLOPs and 419× latency inflation in white-box settings, and 58× FLOPs / 17× latency in gray-box settings. Even with system-level defenses (batching, buffering, confidence thresholds), attacks force pipelines to choose between throughput collapse (0.006 input/s from 0.578) or 96.7% data loss.

Conclusion: ML inference pipelines create new efficiency-attack surfaces requiring path-aware defenses. Existing single-model attack methods are insufficient, as demonstrated by the 20× performance gap between path-aware and single-model attacks.

Abstract: Modern machine learning deployments increasingly compose specialized models into dynamic inference pipelines, where upstream components produce intermediate predictions that determine the workload and inputs of downstream components. The cost of processing an input is therefore not determined by any single model, but by two coupled factors: the per-inference cost of each invoked component and its workload volume. Because these pipelines run under hard real-time constraints, efficiency is a fundamental requirement for system availability. We show that this structure creates an efficiency-attack surface that existing methods targeting single models cannot exploit: on identical inputs and budgets, path-aware targeting inflates FLOPs by $2,407\times$ while the strongest single-model baseline achieves $117\times$ -- a $20\times$ gap attributable entirely to where the attack is directed. We formalize this as the adversarial path-selection problem and present AESOP, a framework combining vulnerability-guided path ranking with adaptive loss weighting. We evaluate AESOP on five pipelines plus a production-realistic deployment variant with batching, bounded buffering, and confidence-threshold defenses. AESOP achieves up to $2,407\times$ FLOPs and $419\times$ latency inflation in white-box setting and 58$\times$ FLOPs / 17$\times$ latency in gray-box settings. Under system-level defenses, the attack is not neutralized but redirected: pipelines are forced to choose between throughput collapse ($0.578 \to 0.006$ input/s) and $96.7\%$ data loss to sustain throughput.

</details>


### [129] [Seeing the Needle in the Haystack: Towards Weakly-Supervised Log Instance Anomaly Localization via Counterfactual Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10988)
*Yutszyuk Wong,Wentai Wu,Yuen-Ying Yeung,Weiwei Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LogMILP is a weakly supervised framework that performs both bag-level anomaly detection and instance-level anomaly localization in log data using only bag-level labels, leveraging prototype-guided modeling and perturbation consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale networked systems, log data is massive but fine-grained instance-level annotations are too expensive, making traditional anomaly detection methods difficult to apply for precise anomaly localization.

Method: Proposes LogMILP: a multi-instance learning approach enhanced by prototypes and perturbation. Uses prototype-guided structural modeling with counterfactual perturbation consistency regularization to identify critical log entries.

Result: Experimental results on three public datasets show LogMILP achieves competitive detection performance while producing significantly more reliable instance-level localization compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: LogMILP successfully addresses the weakly supervised log anomaly localization challenge, providing both effective detection and interpretable instance-level localization using only coarse-grained supervision.

Abstract: Log anomaly detection is a critical task for system operations and security assurance. However, in networked systems at scale, log data are generated at massive scale while instance-level annotations are prohibitively expensive, posing great difficulties to fine-grained anomaly localization. To address this challenge, we propose LogMILP (Log anomaly localization based on Multi-Instance Learning enhanced by prototypes and Perturbation), a weakly supervised framework that enables both bag-level anomaly detection and instance-level anomaly localization using only bag-level labels. Our method guides the model to pinpoint the critical log entries using prototype-guided structural modeling with counterfactual perturbation consistency regularization, thereby improving localization reliability and interpretability under coarse-grained supervision. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that LogMILP achieves competitive detection performance while yielding significantly more reliable instance-level localization. Our code is open-sourced at https://github.com/YUK1207/LogMILP.

</details>


### [130] [SURGE: Surrogate Gradient Adaptation in Binary Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10989)
*Haoyu Huang,Boyu Liu,Linlin Yang,Yanjing Li,Yuguang Yang,Xuhui Liu,Canyu Chen,Zhongqian Fu,Baochang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SURGE proposes a learnable gradient compensation framework for Binary Neural Networks that mitigates gradient mismatch issues through dual-path architecture and adaptive scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for training BNNs (like STE and variants) suffer from gradient mismatch and information loss due to fixed-range gradient clipping, relying on hand-crafted designs that limit performance.

Method: Proposes SURGE framework with Dual-Path Gradient Compensator (DPGC) that creates parallel full-precision auxiliary branches for binarized layers, and Adaptive Gradient Scaler (AGS) that dynamically balances gradient contributions between branches via norm-based scaling.

Result: Experiments on image classification, object detection, and language understanding tasks show SURGE outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SURGE provides a theoretically grounded, learnable gradient compensation framework that effectively addresses gradient mismatch in BNN training, offering superior performance across multiple tasks.

Abstract: The training of Binary Neural Networks (BNNs) is fundamentally based on gradient approximation for non-differentiable binarization operations (e.g., sign function). However, prevailing methods including the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) and its improved variants, rely on hand-crafted designs that suffer from gradient mismatch problem and information loss induced by fixed-range gradient clipping. To address this, we propose SURrogate GradiEnt Adaptation (SURGE), a novel learnable gradient compensation framework with theoretical grounding. SURGE mitigates gradient mismatch through auxiliary backpropagation. Specifically, we design a Dual-Path Gradient Compensator (DPGC) that constructs a parallel full-precision auxiliary branch for each binarized layer, decoupling gradient flow via output decomposition during backpropagation. DPGC enables bias-reduced gradient estimation by leveraging the full-precision branch to estimate components beyond STE's first-order approximation. To further enhance training stability, we introduce an Adaptive Gradient Scaler (AGS) based on an optimal scale factor to dynamically balance inter-branch gradient contributions via norm-based scaling. Experiments on image classification, object detection, and language understanding tasks demonstrate that SURGE performs best over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [131] [Test-Time Personalization: A Diagnostic Framework and Probabilistic Fix for Scaling Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10991)
*Linhai Zhang,Yulan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for Test-Time Personalization (TTP) that scales inference-time computation by sampling multiple candidates and selecting the best using personalized reward models, with a unified scaling law to diagnose and fix reward model failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM personalization approaches focus on building better personalized models or inputs but treat inference as single-shot, missing opportunities to scale computation at test time for improved personalization.

Method: Proposed Test-Time Personalization (TTP) with sampling N candidates from a personalized policy model, selecting the best using personalized reward models; developed a unified scaling law to decompose reward model performance and diagnose failures; proposed probabilistic personalized reward models to address identified failure modes.

Result: Theoretical proof that oracle selection yields logarithmic utility growth with N; identified two reward model failure modes (user-level collapse and query-level reward hacking); proposed solution effectively mitigates these failures; experimental confirmation of consistent TTP scaling across multiple policy models and tasks, and accurate scaling law predictions.

Conclusion: Test-Time Personalization with scaled inference computation is a promising approach, but requires addressing reward model limitations; the proposed unified scaling law and probabilistic reward models successfully enable practical TTP scaling across diverse personalization tasks.

Abstract: Existing approaches to LLM personalization focus on constructing better personalized models or inputs, while treating inference as a single-shot process. In this work, we study Test-Time Personalization (TTP) along an unexplored axis: scaling inference-time computation by sampling N candidates from a personalized policy model and selecting the best with a personalized reward model. We prove that oracle selection yields expected utility growing logarithmically with the number of sampled candidates, establishing a theoretical ceiling for test-time scaling. However, standard reward models fail to realize this potential. To diagnose why, we derive a unified scaling law that decomposes any reward model's Best-of-N curve into four measurable quantities and reveals two failure modes, user-level collapse (near-constant prediction for some users) and query-level reward hacking (negative correlation with true quality for some queries). Guided by this law, we propose a probabilistic personalized reward model whose learned variance effectively mitigates both failure modes. Experiments confirm both elements of our framework: TTP delivers consistent scaling across multiple policy models and personalized text generation tasks, and our scaling law closely matches observed scaling curves across reward-model variants.

</details>


### [132] [Finite Volume-Informed Neural Network Framework for 2D Shallow Water Equations: Rugged Loss Landscapes and the Importance of Data Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11001)
*Xiaofeng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics-only FVM-PINNs often fail on realistic 2D shallow water equations, collapsing to trivial low-momentum states, but adding sparse data dramatically improves performance by breaking loss landscape degeneracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINNs struggle with shallow water equations due to inability to enforce local conservation, handle discontinuities, or leverage unstructured meshes used in real-world applications. There's a need for a framework that works with practical SWE simulations.

Method: Data-Guided FVM-PINN framework replaces strong-form residual with differentiable, well-balanced Roe Riemann-solver finite-volume (FVM) loss on unstructured meshes. Combines physics-based FVM loss with sparse data measurements and uses time-window decomposition with progressive initial-condition handoff.

Result: Physics-only FVM-PINN training often fails on realistic 2D problems, but adding sparse data dramatically improves performance: 200 random velocity measurements reduced velocity-field error by 22× vs physics-only. FVM-PINN loss reduces error by ~23% in sparse-data regime. Framework successfully applied to real-world Savannah River reach with SRH-2D anchor data.

Conclusion: The Data-Guided FVM-PINN framework effectively addresses limitations of standard PINNs for SWE, demonstrating that sparse data is crucial to break loss landscape degeneracy and enable accurate surrogate modeling for real-world applications with unstructured meshes.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are a simple surrogate-modelling paradigm for partial differential equations, but their standard strong-form residual formulation is ill suited to the shallow water equations (SWE). It cannot enforce local conservation, handle discontinuities, or leverage the boundary-conforming unstructured meshes used in real-world applications. We introduce ``Data-Guided FVM-PINN'', a framework that replaces the strong-form residual with a differentiable, well-balanced Roe Riemann-solver finite-volume (FVM) loss evaluated on unstructured meshes. The major finding is that physics-only FVM-PINN training often fails on realistic 2D problems: the network collapses to a trivial low-momentum state that nearly satisfies the FVM-PINN residual but bears no resemblance to the true flow. A loss-landscape diagnostic shows that the FVM-PINN loss at zero momentum is only about $7\times$ larger than at the trained solution, a shallow basin that an ordinary optimizer falls into; adding even sparse data turns this into a $310\times$ separation, breaking the degeneracy. On a 2D block-in-channel benchmark, just $200$ random velocity measurements drop the velocity-field $L_2$ error by $22\times$ versus physics-only; $50$ measurements still deliver a $7\times$ reduction. A controlled ablation isolates the contribution of the FVM-PINN loss: it reduces velocity-field $L_2$ by $\sim$$23\%$ in the sparse-data regime and is essentially neutral when dense reference data is available. On a real-world Savannah River reach ($1306$ cells, $3600$~s simulation, five Manning zones), the framework constructs an accurate surrogate from SRH-2D anchor data, with time-window decomposition reducing error monotonically via progressive initial-condition handoff.

</details>


### [133] [DisagMoE: Computation-Communication overlapped MoE Training via Disaggregated AF-Pipe Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11005)
*Zhichen Zeng,Chi-Chih Chang,Jiayi Wang,Zezhou Wang,Ningxin Zheng,Zheng Zhong,Cesar A. Stuardo,Dongyang Wang,Mohamed S. Abdelfattah,Haibin Lin,Banghua Zhu,Ang Li,Ziheng Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DisagMoE is a disaggregated MoE training system that optimizes model placement and scheduling to overcome communication bottlenecks in expert parallelism, achieving up to 1.8x speedup by separating attention and FFN layers into disjoint GPU groups and using multi-stage pipelines with optimized communication patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures enable trillion-parameter LLMs, but expert parallelism suffers from severe all-to-all communication bottlenecks, especially as models grow larger requiring experts distributed across nodes. Prior work tried overlapping communications with computations, but leaves residual stalls due to imbalance in attention and FFN layers' computation-communication ratios.

Method: 1. Separates attention and FFN layers into disjoint GPU groups
2. Introduces multi-stage pipeline with uni-directional, many-to-many communications
3. Employs computation-communication roofline model to balance GPU and network bandwidth allocation between attention and FFN groups
4. Jointly optimizes model placement and scheduling for maximal efficiency

Result: Implemented on Megatron-LM and evaluated on multiple MoE models, showing training efficiency improvements with up to 1.8x speedup on 16-node 8xH800 clusters.

Conclusion: DisagMoE effectively addresses communication bottlenecks in MoE training through disaggregated architecture and optimized scheduling, significantly improving training efficiency for large-scale MoE models.

Abstract: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures enable trillion-parameter LLMs with sparsely activated experts. Expert parallelism (EP) is a widely adopted MoE training strategy, but it suffers from severe all-to-all communication bottlenecks, which is exaggerated by the limited inter-node network bandwidth as the growing model size requires distributing experts across GPU nodes. Prior work focused on overlapping these all-to-all communications with feed-forward network (FFN) and self-attention computations, which often leaves residual network-bound stalls due to inherent imbalance in attention and FFN layers' computation-communication ratios. We present DisagMoE, a disaggregated MoE training system that jointly optimizes model placement and scheduling for maximal efficiency. DisagMoE separates attention and FFN layers into disjoint GPU groups, introduces a multi-stage pipeline with uni-directional, many-to-many communications, and employs a computation-communication roofline model to balance GPU and network bandwidth allocation among the attention and FFN groups. DisagMoE is implemented on Megatron-LM, and evaluation shows that DisagMoE improves training efficiency across multiple MoE models with up to 1.8x speedup on 16-node 8xH800 clusters.

</details>


### [134] [RT-Transformer: The Transformer Block as a Spherical State Estimator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11007)
*Peter Racioppo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers emerge naturally from a geometric estimation problem on the hypersphere, with attention, residuals, and normalization arising as components of directional inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to provide a unified geometric foundation for Transformer architecture components rather than treating them as separate design choices. It seeks to explain why attention, residual connections, and normalization appear together in Transformers.

Method: The authors model the latent state as a direction on a hypersphere, with noise defined in the tangent plane. They show that attention emerges as evidence aggregation, residual connections as incremental state updates, and normalization as retraction back onto the hypersphere through precision-weighted directional inference.

Result: The core Transformer components (attention, residual connections, normalization) are derived mathematically from a single geometric estimation problem rather than being independently designed architectural elements.

Conclusion: Transformers have a natural geometric interpretation where their fundamental building blocks emerge coherently from directional inference on the hypersphere, providing a principled foundation for their architecture.

Abstract: We show that the core components of the Transformer block -- attention, residual connections, and normalization -- arise naturally from a single geometric estimation problem. Modeling the latent state as a direction on the hypersphere, with noise defined in the tangent plane at the current estimate, yields a precision-weighted directional inference procedure in which attention aggregates evidence, residual connections implement incremental state updates, and normalization retracts the updated state back onto the hypersphere. Together, these components follow from the geometry of the estimation problem rather than being introduced as independent architectural choices.

</details>


### [135] [When and How to Canonize: A Generalization Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11008)
*Yonatan Sverdlov,Benjamin Friedman,Snir Hordan,Nadav Dym*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a theoretical framework to analyze generalization error of invariant methods for symmetric data processing, establishing that canonization's effectiveness depends on its regularity, with Hilbert curve serialization outperforming lexicographical sorting for point clouds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is growing interest in achieving invariance through group averaging or canonization rather than invariant architectures, but the theoretical generalization properties of these alternative strategies remain poorly understood and lack formal analysis.

Method: The authors introduce a theoretical framework analyzing generalization error by bounding covering numbers of different approaches. They establish a generalization hierarchy comparing structurally invariant, group-averaged, and canonized models. They analyze permutation groups in point cloud processing, specifically comparing lexicographical sorting and Hilbert curve canonization.

Result: The paper proves that canonized models have error bounds at best equal to structurally invariant/group-averaged models and at worst equal to non-invariant baselines. For point clouds, lexicographical sorting's covering number grows exponentially with dimension while Hilbert curve canonization guarantees polynomial growth. Experiments support these theoretical claims.

Conclusion: The regularity of canonization determines its effectiveness, with optimal canonizations achieving best error bounds and poor ones matching non-invariant baselines. Hilbert curve serialization provides formal theoretical justification for its empirical success in point cloud architectures, offering a practical and theoretically sound alternative to invariant architectures.

Abstract: While invariant architectures are standard for processing symmetric data, there is growing interest in achieving invariance by applying group averaging or canonization to non-invariant backbones. However, the theoretical generalization properties of these alternative strategies remain poorly understood. We introduce a theoretical framework to analyze the generalization error of these methods by bounding their covering numbers. We establish a rigorous generalization hierarchy: the error bounds of canonized models are at best equal to the error bounds of structurally invariant and group-averaged models, and at worst equal to the bounds of non-invariant baselines. Furthermore, we show that there exist optimal canonizations which attain the optimal error bounds, and poor canonizations which attain the non-invariant error bounds, and that this depends on the regularity of the canonization. Finally, applying this framework to permutation groups in point cloud processing, we rigorously prove that the covering number of lexicographical sorting grows exponentially with point cloud dimension, whereas Hilbert curve canonization guarantees polynomial growth. This provides the first formal theoretical justification for the empirical success of Hilbert curve serialization in state-of-the-art point cloud architectures. We conclude with experiments that support our theoretical claims.
  Code is available at https://github.com/yonatansverdlov/Canonization

</details>


### [136] [ACSAC: Adaptive Chunk Size Actor-Critic with Causal Transformer Q-Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11009)
*Qian Chen,Junqiao Zhao,Hongtu Zhou,Hang Yu,Yanping Zhao,Chen Ye,Guang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ACSAC introduces an adaptive chunk size actor-critic method for long-horizon sparse-reward tasks, using a causal Transformer critic to dynamically select optimal action chunk sizes without task-specific tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon sparse-reward tasks challenge RL due to bootstrapping error accumulation from TD learning. Fixed chunk size methods suffer from a trade-off: large chunks reduce responsiveness to new observations, while small chunks produce incoherent motions, requiring task-specific tuning.

Method: ACSAC uses a causal Transformer critic to evaluate expected returns for action chunks of different sizes. At each chunk boundary, it adaptively selects the chunk size that maximizes expected return, enabling flexible, state-dependent chunk sizes without manual tuning.

Result: Experiments on OGBench show ACSAC achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon, sparse-reward manipulation tasks in both offline RL and offline-to-online RL settings.

Conclusion: ACSAC successfully addresses the limitations of fixed chunk size methods by enabling adaptive action chunking, proving convergence guarantees while achieving superior performance on challenging long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: Long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks pose a fundamental challenge for reinforcement learning, since single-step TD learning suffers from bootstrapping error accumulation across successive Bellman updates. Actor-critic methods with action chunking address this by operating over temporally extended actions, which reduce the effective horizon, enable fast value backups, and support temporally consistent exploration. However, existing methods rely on a fixed chunk size and therefore cannot adaptively balance reactivity against temporal consistency. A large fixed chunk size reduces responsiveness to new observations, while a small one produces incoherent motions, forcing task-specific tuning of the chunk size. To address this limitation, we propose Adaptive Chunk Size Actor-Critic (ACSAC). ACSAC leverages a causal Transformer critic to evaluate expected returns for action chunks of different sizes. At each chunk boundary, it adaptively selects the chunk size that maximizes the expected return, supporting flexible, state-dependent chunk sizes without task-specific tuning. We prove that the ACSAC Bellman operator is a contraction whose unique fixed point is the action-value function of the adaptive policy. Experiments on OGBench demonstrate that ACSAC achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon, sparse-reward manipulation tasks across both offline RL and offline-to-online RL settings.

</details>


### [137] [A Comparative Study of Federated Learning Aggregation Strategies under Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Data Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11010)
*Antonios Makris,Christos Dousis,Emmanouil Kritharakis,Stavros Bouras,Konstantinos Tserpes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Experimental comparison of federated learning aggregation strategies showing their performance varies with data heterogeneity and dataset characteristics


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated Learning performance is strongly influenced by aggregation strategies, which affect learning performance, robustness, and system behavior, but there's a need for comprehensive comparison under different data distribution scenarios.

Method: Conducted experimental comparison of widely used federated aggregation strategies under both homogeneous and heterogeneous data distributions using benchmark image classification datasets. Analyzed how different aggregation mechanisms respond to varying degrees of data heterogeneity, examining impact on centralized accuracy and loss, and system-level efficiency metrics.

Result: Aggregation strategies exhibit distinct trade-offs across datasets and data distributions, with their effectiveness varying according to dataset characteristics and operating conditions. Different strategies perform differently in terms of accuracy, loss, and system efficiency metrics.

Conclusion: The choice of federated aggregation strategy should be carefully considered based on data distribution characteristics and specific operational requirements, as no single strategy is universally optimal across all scenarios.

Abstract: Federated Learning has emerged as a transformative paradigm for collaborative machine learning across distributed environments. However, its performance is strongly influenced by the aggregation strategy used to combine local model updates at the server, which directly affects learning performance, robustness, and system behavior. This work presents a comprehensive experimental comparison of widely used federated aggregation strategies under both homogeneous and heterogeneous data distributions. Using benchmark image classification datasets, we analyze how different aggregation mechanisms respond to varying degrees of data heterogeneity, examining their impact on centralized accuracy and loss, and system-level efficiency metrics, including aggregation, training, and communication time. The results demonstrate that aggregation strategies exhibit distinct trade-offs across datasets and data distributions, with their effectiveness varying according to dataset characteristics and operating conditions.

</details>


### [138] [LoopUS: Recasting Pretrained LLMs into Looped Latent Refinement Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11011)
*Taekhyun Park,Yongjae Lee,Dohee Kim,Hyerim Bae*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopUS transforms pretrained LLMs into looped architectures post-training to improve reasoning performance without training recurrent models from scratch or disrupting pretrained capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for looped computation require either training recurrent models from scratch or applying disruptive retrofits, which are computationally expensive and may compromise pretrained capabilities. There's a need for a method that can convert standard LLMs into looped architectures post-training.

Method: LoopUS recasts pretrained LLMs into encoder-loop-reasoning-block-decoder architecture using four components: 1) block decomposition guided by representation dynamics, 2) input-dependent selective gate to prevent hidden-state drift, 3) random deep supervision for memory-efficient learning, and 4) confidence head for adaptive early exiting.

Result: The method transforms standard non-looped models into looped forms while stabilizing against computational bottlenecks and representation collapse. It enables stable latent looping to improve reasoning performance.

Conclusion: LoopUS provides an effective post-training framework for converting standard LLMs into looped architectures, improving reasoning performance without extending generated traces or requiring recurrent training from scratch.

Abstract: Looped computation shows promise in improving the reasoning-oriented performance of LLMs by scaling test-time compute. However, existing approaches typically require either training recurrent models from scratch or applying disruptive retrofits, which involve substantial computational costs and may compromise pretrained capabilities. To address these limitations, we introduce \textbf{Looped Depth Up-Scaling} (LoopUS), a post-training framework that converts a standard pretrained LLM into a looped architecture. As a key technical contribution, LoopUS recasts the pretrained LLM into an encoder, a looped reasoning block, and a decoder. It operationalizes this latent-refinement architecture through four core components: (1) block decomposition, guided by staged representation dynamics; (2) an input-dependent selective gate to mitigate hidden-state drift; (3) random deep supervision for memory-efficient learning over long recursive horizons; and (4) a confidence head for adaptive early exiting. Collectively, these mechanisms transform a standard non-looped model into a looped form while stabilizing it against both computational bottlenecks and representation collapse. Through stable latent looping, LoopUS improves reasoning-oriented performance without extending the generated traces or requiring recurrent training from scratch. For more details, see https://thrillcrazyer.github.io/LoopUS

</details>


### [139] [Backbone-Equated Diffusion OOD via Sparse Internal Snapshots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11014)
*Yadang Alexis Rouzoumka,Jean Pinsolle,Eugénie Terreaux,Christèle Morisseau,Jean-Philippe Ovarlez,Chengfang Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFS is a family of OOD detectors that uses minimal internal activations from frozen diffusion backbones at low noise levels, achieving strong performance with very few forward passes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fair comparison of diffusion-based OOD detectors is difficult due to variations in backbone choice, corruption parameterization, and test-time budgets. Existing approaches lack standardized evaluation protocols.

Method: Introduces Mutualized Backbone-Equated (MBE) protocol for fair comparisons, and Canonical Feature Snapshots (CFS) - detectors that probe frozen diffusion backbones using only a tiny number of internal activations at canonical low-noise levels.

Result: On CIFAR-scale benchmarks, CFS(1x2) performs best among single-forward variants, while decoder-only variants remain competitive. Shows OOD signals are concentrated in sparse internal states rather than requiring full denoising trajectories.

Conclusion: Frozen diffusion backbones contain concentrated OOD signals in sparse internal states at low noise levels, enabling efficient detection without full denoising or complex downstream heads, with theoretical support for these observations.

Abstract: Fair comparison between diffusion-based OOD detectors is challenging, as conclusions can vary with backbone choice, corruption parameterization, and test-time budget. We address this issue through a Mutualized Backbone-Equated (MBE) protocol that aligns canonical corruption levels and logical test-time cost across diffusion backbones. Within this setting, we introduce Canonical Feature Snapshots (CFS), a family of detectors that probes a frozen diffusion backbone using only a tiny number of native internal activations at canonical low-noise levels. On a controlled CIFAR-scale benchmark, the strongest one-forward CFS variant is CFS(1x2), while an even smaller decoder-only variant remains highly competitive. This shows that much of the relative-OOD signal exposed by frozen diffusion backbones is concentrated in a small number of sparse internal states, rather than requiring full denoising trajectories or high-capacity downstream heads. We further provide a local diagnostic theory explaining these observations through conditional encoder-decoder complementarity, diagonal-score separation, and low-noise corruption stability. The official implementation is available at https://github.com/RouzAY/cfs-diffusion-ood/.

</details>


### [140] [Simpson's Paradox in Behavioral Curves: How Aggregation Distorts Parametric Models of User Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11017)
*Chao Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analysis reveals Simpson's paradox in behavioral curve modeling where aggregate curves systematically distort individual-level patterns due to survival bias, with individual users peaking at ~11 exposures while aggregates peak at ~34 (3x gap).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral curve modeling is standard practice in recommendation, advertising, and clinical dosing, but there's concern that aggregating individual curves may introduce systematic distortions when estimating individual behavioral parameters from aggregate data.

Method: Analyzed three datasets: Goodreads (3.3M users, 9 genres), Amazon Electronics (18M reviews), and MovieLens-25M as negative control. Compared individual vs. aggregate behavioral curves, operationalized engagement measures, and developed Synthetic Null Calibration to address false positives in per-user classification.

Result: Found systematic distortion in behavioral curves: individual users peak at ~11 exposures while aggregate curves peak at ~34 (3x gap) on Goodreads, and Amazon Electronics shows 5.3x distortion. MovieLens-25M confirmed survival bias (not aggregation per se) as the mechanism. Robust to category granularity and engagement operationalization. Synthetic Null Calibration addressed 32% false positive rate in per-user classification.

Conclusion: Aggregation introduces systematic distortion (Simpson's paradox) in behavioral curves driven by survival bias, not aggregation itself. This has critical implications for any domain where individual behavioral parameters are estimated from aggregate curves under differential attrition, requiring careful methodological approaches like Synthetic Null Calibration.

Abstract: Behavioral curve modeling -- fitting parametric functions to engagement-versus-exposure data -- is standard practice in recommendation, advertising, and clinical dosing. We show that aggregation introduces a systematic distortion: Simpson's paradox in behavioral curves. On Goodreads (3.3M users, 9 genres), individual users peak at n* approximately 11 exposures while the aggregate peaks at n* approximately 34 -- a 3x gap driven by survival bias. Amazon Electronics (18M reviews) shows a 5.3x distortion. MovieLens-25M (D approximately 1) serves as a negative control, confirming that survival bias -- not aggregation per se -- is the operative mechanism. The distortion is robust to category granularity, engagement operationalization, and classifier calibration. We develop Synthetic Null Calibration to address a 32% false positive rate in per-user classification. Our findings apply wherever individual behavioral parameters are estimated from aggregate curves under differential attrition.

</details>


### [141] [Efficient LLM Reasoning via Variational Posterior Guidance with Efficiency Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11019)
*Zizhao Chen,Yuying Li,Siting Lin,Lianxi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VPG-EA is a variational inference framework that improves reasoning efficiency in LLMs by transferring posterior-efficient patterns to prior policy, overcoming the sampling bottleneck in existing reinforcement learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models rely on chain-of-thought reasoning, but suffer from "overthinking" that degrades inference efficiency. Existing reinforcement learning methods for compressing reasoning chains face a sampling bottleneck where high-quality samples are extremely sparse in the exploration space.

Method: The authors formalize efficient reasoning as a variational inference problem and propose VPG-EA framework with parameter-shared dual-stream architecture to instantiate both posterior distribution and prior policy. The method filters pseudo-efficient paths via cross-view evaluation and unidirectionally transfers posterior's efficient patterns to prior policy through variational distillation.

Result: Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and 7B models show VPG-EA improves the comprehensive efficiency metric (epsilon cubed) by 8.73% and 12.37% respectively over the strongest baselines for each model size.

Conclusion: The proposed VPG-EA framework successfully addresses the sampling bottleneck in reasoning chain compression by leveraging variational inference principles and transferring efficient patterns from posterior to prior, significantly improving reasoning efficiency in language models.

Abstract: Although large language models rely on chain-of-thought for complex reasoning, the overthinking phenomenon severely degrades inference efficiency. Existing reinforcement learning methods compress reasoning chains by designing elaborate reward functions, which renders high-quality samples extremely sparse in the exploration space and creates a sampling bottleneck for the prior policy. Inspired by cognitive science, we theoretically prove that a posterior distribution guided by reference answers achieves higher expected utility than the prior distribution, thus capable of breaking through the sampling bottleneck of high-quality samples. However, the posterior distribution is unavailable during inference. To this end, we formalize efficient reasoning as a variational inference problem and introduce an efficiency-aware evidence lower bound as the theoretical foundation. Based on this, we propose the VPG-EA framework. It adopts a parameter-shared dual-stream architecture to instantiate both the posterior distribution and the prior policy; after filtering out pseudo-efficient paths via cross-view evaluation, it unidirectionally transfers the posterior's efficient patterns to the prior policy through variational distillation. Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and 7B scales demonstrate that VPG-EA improves the comprehensive efficiency metric epsilon cubed by 8.73% and 12.37% over the strongest baselines on each model size, respectively.

</details>


### [142] [Trust Region Inverse Reinforcement Learning: Explicit Dual Ascent using Local Policy Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11020)
*Anish Diwan,Davide Tateo,Christopher E. Mower,Haitham Bou-Ammar,Jan Peters,Oleg Arenz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRIRL bridges classical and adversarial IRL methods by enabling monotonic improvement without fully solving RL problems each iteration, using trust regions to find globally optimal policies for smaller reward updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical IRL requires solving full RL problems each iteration (computationally expensive), while adversarial methods avoid this cost but sacrifice stability and monotonic improvement. There's a need for a method that combines the monotonic improvement guarantee of classical IRL with the computational efficiency of adversarial approaches.

Method: The key insight is that a trust-region-optimal policy for a reward function update can be globally optimal for a smaller update in the same direction. This allows explicit optimization of the dual objective while only performing local search around the current policy, avoiding the need to fully solve RL problems at each iteration.

Result: TRIRL outperforms state-of-the-art imitation learning methods by 2.4x in aggregate inter-quartile mean across multiple challenging tasks. It also recovers reward functions that generalize to system dynamics shifts, and avoids the training instabilities of adversarial methods while maintaining monotonic performance improvement.

Conclusion: TRIRL successfully bridges classical and adversarial IRL approaches, combining the best of both worlds: monotonic improvement guarantees, computational efficiency, stability, and the ability to learn generalizable reward functions that can be globally optimized to match expert demonstrations.

Abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) is typically formulated as maximizing entropy subject to matching the distribution of expert trajectories. Classical (dual-ascent) IRL guarantees monotonic performance improvement but requires fully solving an RL problem each iteration to compute dual gradients. More recent adversarial methods avoid this cost at the expense of stability and monotonic dual improvement, by directly optimizing the primal problem and using a discriminator to provide rewards. In this work, we bridge the gap between these approaches by enabling monotonic improvement of the reward function and policy without having to fully solve an RL problem at every iteration. Our key theoretical insight is that a trust-region-optimal policy for a reward function update can be globally optimal for a smaller update in the same direction. This smaller update allows us to explicitly optimize the dual objective while only relying on a local search around the current policy. In doing so, our approach avoids the training instabilities of adversarial methods, offers monotonic performance improvement, and learns a reward function in the traditional sense of IRL--one that can be globally optimized to match expert demonstrations. Our proposed algorithm, Trust Region Inverse Reinforcement Learning (TRIRL), outperforms state-of-the-art imitation learning methods across multiple challenging tasks by a factor of 2.4x in terms of aggregate inter-quartile mean, while recovering reward functions that generalize to system dynamics shifts.

</details>


### [143] [A Switching System Theory of Q-Learning with Linear Function Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11021)
*Donghwan Lee,Han-Dong Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-learning with linear function approximation is analyzed as a switched system using joint spectral radius theory, connecting convergence to switched-system stability and extending to stochastic and Markovian settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous stability analysis of Q-learning with linear function approximation by interpreting it as a switched system, which allows for less conservative convergence certificates than traditional one-step norm bounds.

Method: Develops a switching-system interpretation of Q-learning with LFA based on joint spectral radius theory, deriving exact linear switched models for mean dynamics and extending to stochastic settings with i.i.d. and Markovian observations, including regularized variants.

Result: Establishes that convergence of Q-learning with LFA can be analyzed through stability of corresponding switched systems, with JSR-based certificates that capture products of switching modes and are less conservative than one-step norm bounds.

Conclusion: The framework unifies projected Bellman equations, finite-difference stochastic-policy switching, and switched-system stability in a single parameter-space formulation, providing a powerful theoretical tool for analyzing convergence of Q-learning with linear function approximation.

Abstract: This paper develops a switching-system interpretation of Q-learning with linear function approximation (LFA) based on the joint spectral radius (JSR). We derive an exact linear switched model for the mean dynamics and relate convergence to stability of the corresponding switched system. The same construction is then used for stochastic linear Q-learning with independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) observations and with Markovian observations. Although exact JSR computation is difficult in general, the certificate captures products of switching modes and can be less conservative than one-step norm bounds. The framework also yields a JSR-based view of regularized Q-learning with LFA. The resulting analysis connects projected Bellman equations, finite-difference stochastic-policy switching, and switched-system stability in a single parameter-space formulation.

</details>


### [144] [ASD-Bench: A Four-Axis Comprehensive Benchmark of AI Models for Autism Spectrum Disorder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11091)
*Shubhankit Singh,Hassan Shaikh,Kuldeep Raghuwanshi,Keshav Bulia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASD-Bench introduces a comprehensive tabular benchmark for autism spectrum disorder screening across three age cohorts, evaluating multiple model architectures on four axes and revealing age-specific diagnostic patterns and limitations of single-metric evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current automated ASD screening tools have significant limitations: they rely on single-architecture evaluations, focus only on axis-restricted assessments, primarily target adult cohorts, and fail to capture age-specific diagnostic patterns crucial for early intervention.

Method: Created ASD-Bench, a systematic tabular benchmark evaluating various ML approaches across three age cohorts (children 1-11, adolescents 12-16, adults 17-64) on four axes: predictive performance, calibration, interpretability, and adversarial robustness. Used a curated dataset of 4,068 AQ-10 records and evaluated classical models (XGBoost, AdaBoost, Random Forest, Logistic Regression), neural networks (MLP), deep tabular transformers (TabNet, TabTransformer, FT-Transformer), and TabPFN v2. Introduced the Heuristic Aggregate Penalty (HAP), a cost-sensitive metric that penalizes false negatives more heavily and incorporates cross-validation variance.

Result: Adult classification achieved high performance (10/17 models with perfect F1 and AUC), while adolescents were harder (F1 ceiling 0.837 vs. 0.915 for children). Feature importance shifted across cohorts: A9 (social motivation) dominated for children, A5 (pattern recognition) led for adolescents, and adults showed flatter importance profiles consistent with social masking. Found dissociation between accuracy and calibration - AdaBoost achieved perfect F1 but poor calibration (ECE=0.302). Provided cohort-specific deployment recommendations.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that single-metric evaluation is insufficient for clinical AI applications and reveals age-specific diagnostic patterns in ASD screening. Findings provide proof-of-concept evidence using questionnaire-derived labels rather than clinically validated diagnostics, highlighting the importance of multi-dimensional evaluation and age-specific considerations in automated ASD screening tools.

Abstract: Automated ASD screening tools remain limited by single-architecture evaluations, axis-restricted assessment, and near-exclusive focus on adult cohorts, obscuring age-specific diagnostic patterns critical for early intervention. We introduce ASD-Bench, a systematic tabular benchmark evaluating ML, deep learning, and foundation model configurations across three age cohorts (children 1-11 yr, adolescents 12-16 yr, adults 17-64 yr) on four axes: predictive performance, calibration, interpretability, and adversarial robustness. Applied to a curated v3 dataset of 4,068 AQ-10 records, our benchmark spans classical models (XGBoost, AdaBoost, Random Forest, Logistic Regression), neural networks (MLP), deep tabular transformers (TabNet, TabTransformer, FT-Transformer), and TabPFN v2. We introduce the Heuristic Aggregate Penalty (HAP): a cost-sensitive metric penalising false negatives more heavily and incorporating cross-validation variance for deployment stability. Adult classification yields high performance (10/17 models achieve perfect F1 and AUC), while adolescents present a harder task (F1 ceiling 0.837 vs. 0.915 for children). Feature hierarchies shift across cohorts: A9 (social motivation) dominates for children, A5 (pattern recognition) leads for adolescents, and adults exhibit a flatter importance profile consistent with developmental social masking. Accuracy and calibration are dissociated: AdaBoost achieves F1=1.000 on adults with ECE=0.302, confirming single-metric evaluation is insufficient for clinical AI. Cohort-specific deployment recommendations are provided. All findings should be interpreted as proof-of-concept evidence on questionnaire-derived labels rather than clinically validated diagnostic performance.

</details>


### [145] [Enabling Performant and Flexible Model-Internal Observability for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11093)
*Nengneng Yu,Sixian Xiong,Yibo Zhao,Wei Wang,Zaoxing Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DMI-Lib is a high-performance deep model inspection library that provides low-overhead access to model internal states by decoupling observability from the inference hot path using specialized GPU-CPU memory abstractions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern inference workloads increasingly need timely access to model internal states for debugging, monitoring, and analysis, but current methods either disrupt inference performance or don't provide sufficient observability capabilities.

Method: DMI-Lib introduces an asynchronous observability substrate with Ring^2 (a GPU-CPU memory abstraction for tensor capture/staging) and a policy-controlled host backend. It decouples observability from inference by placing observation points across internal signals while preserving serving optimizations and respecting GPU memory budgets.

Result: DMI-Lib achieves only 0.4%-6.8% overhead in offline batch inference and average 6% overhead in moderate online serving, reducing latency overhead by 2x-15x compared to existing baselines with similar observability features.

Conclusion: DMI-Lib successfully treats internal observability as a first-class systems primitive, enabling high-performance deep model inspection with minimal impact on inference performance, making it practical for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Today's inference-time workloads increasingly depend on timely access to a model's internal states. We present DMI-Lib, a high-speed deep model inspector that treats internal observability as a first-class systems primitive, decoupling it from the inference hot path via an asynchronous observability substrate built from Ring^2, a GPU-CPU memory abstraction for capturing and staging tensors, and a policy-controlled host backend that exports them. DMI-Lib enables the placement of observation points across a rich space of internal signals and diverse inference backends while preserving serving optimizations and adhering to tight GPU memory budgets. Our experiments demonstrate that DMI-Lib incurs only 0.4%--6.8% overhead in offline batch inference and an average of 6% in moderate online serving, reducing latency overhead by 2x-15x compared to existing baselines with similar observability features. DMI-Lib is open-sourced at https://github.com/ProjectDMX/DMI.

</details>


### [146] [Newton's Lantern: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Finetuning AC Power Flow Warm Start Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11102)
*Shourya Bose,Helgi Hilmarsson,Dhruv Suri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Newton's Lantern uses reinforcement learning to create warm starts that significantly reduce Newton-Raphson iterations for AC power flow, especially effective near voltage collapse where traditional supervised methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural warm start approaches for AC power flow using supervised learning fail near voltage collapse (saddle-node bifurcation), as the Newton-Raphson iteration count depends on error direction rather than magnitude, making conventional regression ineffective in heavily loaded cases.

Method: Proposes Newton's Lantern - a finetuning pipeline combining group relative policy optimization with a learned reward model trained on perturbations of base model predictions, using the actual Newton-Raphson iteration count as the supervisory signal rather than traditional regression loss.

Result: Across IEEE 118-bus, GOC 500-bus, and GOC 2000-bus benchmarks, Newton's Lantern is the only method that converges on every test snapshot while achieving the smallest mean iteration count, demonstrating superior performance near voltage collapse.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that reinforcement learning approaches like Newton's Lantern, which optimize directly for iteration count using perturbation-based training, overcome fundamental limitations of supervised methods for neural warm starts in AC power flow near voltage collapse conditions.

Abstract: Neural warm starts can sharply reduce the number of Newton-Raphson iterations required to solve the AC power flow problem, but existing supervised approaches generalize poorly on heavily loaded instances near voltage collapse. We prove a lower bound on the Newton-Raphson iteration count that depends on the direction of the warm start error rather than on its magnitude, and show as a corollary that the bound becomes vacuous as the smallest singular value of the power-flow Jacobian shrinks, identifying the failure mode of supervised regression near the saddle-node bifurcation. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce Newton's Lantern, a finetuning pipeline that combines group relative policy optimization with a learned reward model trained on perturbations of the base model's predictions, using the iteration count itself as the supervisory signal. Across IEEE 118-bus, GOC 500-bus, and GOC 2000-bus benchmarks, Newton's Lantern is the only method that converges on every test snapshot while attaining the smallest mean iteration count.

</details>


### [147] [Language Modeling with Hyperspherical Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11125)
*Justin Deschenaux,Caglar Gulcehre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S-FLM introduces a latent flow language model on the hypersphere that improves continuous flow language models for large-vocabulary reasoning by avoiding costly one-hot vector operations and providing better semantic structure through hyperspherical embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete diffusion models use parallel generation but rely on factorized distributions that are less expressive than autoregressive models. Flow Language Models (FLMs) avoid this but operate on one-hot vectors that scale with vocabulary size, making them costly. Additionally, one-hot embeddings lack meaningful distance metrics, making Gaussian noise semantically unclear for language unlike in images where it degrades structure progressively.

Method: Introduces S-FLM, a latent FLM in the hypersphere (S^{d-1}) that generates sequences by rotating vectors along a learned velocity field using cross-entropy loss. This avoids materializing one-hot vectors and provides meaningful geometric structure where rotations correspond to semantic changes.

Result: S-FLM substantially improves continuous flow language models on large-vocabulary reasoning tasks and closes the gap to masked diffusion models under standard-temperature sampling (T=1). However, a gap remains compared to optimized low-temperature decoding (T=0.1). Previous FLMs matched autoregressive models in Generative Perplexity but produced samples that weren't necessarily correct in verifiable domains like math and code, which S-FLM addresses.

Conclusion: S-FLM provides a more efficient and semantically meaningful approach to flow language modeling by operating in hyperspherical latent space, improving performance on reasoning tasks while reducing computational costs compared to one-hot based FLMs.

Abstract: Discrete Diffusion Language Models progressed rapidly as an alternative to autoregressive (AR) models, motivated by their parallel generation abilities. However, for tractability, discrete diffusion models sample from a factorized distribution, which is less expressive than AR. Recent Flow Language Models (FLMs) apply continuous flows to language, transporting noise to data with a deterministic ODE that avoids factorized sampling. FLMs operate on one-hot vectors whose dimension scales with the vocabulary size, making FLMs costly to train. Moreover, since all distinct one-hot embeddings are equidistant in $\ell_2$, adding Gaussian noise does not have a clear semantic interpretation (unlike images, where Gaussian noise progressively degrades structure). We introduce $\mathbb{S}$-FLM, a latent FLM in the hypersphere. $\mathbb{S}$-FLM generates sequences by rotating vectors in $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ along a velocity field learned with cross-entropy, avoiding the overhead of materializing one-hot vectors. Previous FLMs match AR in Generative Perplexity (Gen.\ PPL), but samples with high likelihood are not necessarily correct in verifiable domains such as math and code. $\mathbb{S}$-FLM substantially improves continuous flow language models on large-vocabulary reasoning and closes the gap to masked diffusion under standard-temperature sampling ($T=1$), while a gap remains under optimized low-temperature ($T=0.1$) decoding.

</details>


### [148] [HEPA: A Self-Supervised Horizon-Conditioned Event Predictive Architecture for Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11130)
*Jonas Petersen,Gian-Alessandro Lombardi,Riccardo Maggioni,Camilla Mazzoleni,Federico Martelli,Philipp Petersen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HEPA is a novel time-series event prediction model that uses horizon-conditioned predictive architecture with pretraining on unlabeled data and minimal labeled data fine-tuning, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critical events in multivariate time series (like turbine failures, cardiac arrhythmias) need accurate prediction, but labeled data is scarce because such events are rare and costly to annotate. Existing methods require extensive labeled data, which is problematic for rare events.

Method: Two key principles: 1) A causal Transformer encoder pretrained via Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) where a horizon-conditioned predictor learns to forecast future representations rather than values, forcing the encoder to capture predictable temporal dynamics from unlabeled data alone. 2) Freeze the encoder and finetune only the predictor toward target events, producing a monotonic survival cumulative distribution function (CDF) over horizons.

Result: HEPA handles water contamination, cyberattack detection, volatility regimes, and eight further event types across 11 domains, exceeding leading time-series architectures including PatchTST, iTransformer, MAE, and Chronos-2 on at least 10 of 14 benchmarks, with an order of magnitude fewer tuned parameters and, on lifecycle datasets, an order of magnitude less labeled data.

Conclusion: HEPA provides an effective solution for predicting rare critical events in multivariate time series by leveraging unlabeled data through representation learning and requiring minimal labeled data, outperforming current state-of-the-art methods across diverse applications.

Abstract: Critical events in multivariate time series, from turbine failures to cardiac arrhythmias, demand accurate prediction, yet labeled data is scarce because such events are rare and costly to annotate. We introduce HEPA (Horizon-conditioned Event Predictive Architecture), built on two key principles. First, a causal Transformer encoder is pretrained via a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA): a horizon-conditioned predictor learns to forecast future representations rather than future values, forcing the encoder to capture predictable temporal dynamics from unlabeled data alone. Second, we freeze the encoder and finetune only the predictor toward the target event, producing a monotonic survival cumulative distribution function (CDF) over horizons. With fixed architecture and optimiser hyperparameters across all benchmarks, HEPA handles water contamination, cyberattack detection, volatility regimes, and eight further event types across 11 domains, exceeding leading time-series architectures including PatchTST, iTransformer, MAE, and Chronos-2 on at least 10 of 14 benchmarks, with an order of magnitude fewer tuned parameters and, on lifecycle datasets, an order of magnitude less labeled data.

</details>


### [149] [Steerable Neural ODEs on Homogeneous Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11133)
*Emma Andersdotter,Daniel Persson,Fredrik Ohlsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Steerable neural ODEs extend manifold NODEs to homogeneous spaces, providing geometric framework for learning continuous-time equivariant dynamics of vector-valued features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing manifold neural ODEs (NODEs) need to be extended to handle features that transform under local symmetry groups on homogeneous spaces. There's a need for a geometric foundation to learn continuous-time equivariant dynamics of general vector-valued features on such spaces.

Method: Introduces steerable neural ODEs on homogeneous spaces M=G/H, interpreting features as sections of associated vector bundles. The evolution is described as parallel transport, resulting in coupled ODEs: flow equation on M and steering equation on features. The approach requires G-invariant vector field for flow and G-invariant connection for parallel transport.

Result: Show that steerable NODEs are G-equivariant when both the vector field generating flow and connection governing parallel transport are G-invariant. The framework incorporates existing NODE models and continuous normalizing flows on Lie groups.

Conclusion: Steerable neural ODEs provide a geometric framework for learning continuous-time equivariant dynamics of general vector-valued features on homogeneous spaces, unifying and extending existing approaches while maintaining mathematical rigor.

Abstract: We introduce steerable neural ordinary differential equations on homogeneous spaces $M=G/H$. These models constitute a novel geometric extension of manifold neural ordinary differential equations (NODEs) that transport associated feature vectors transforming under the local symmetry group $H$. We interpret features as sections of associated vector bundles over $M$, and describe their evolution as parallel transport. This results in a coupled system of ODEs consisting of a flow equation on $M$ and a steering equation acting on features. We show that steerable NODEs are $G$-equivariant whenever the vector field generating the flow and the connection governing parallel transport are both $G$-invariant. Furthermore, we demonstrate how steerable NODEs incorporate existing NODE models and continuous normalizing flows on Lie groups. Our framework provides the geometric foundation for learning continuous-time equivariant dynamics of general vector-valued features on homogeneous spaces.

</details>


### [150] [Spurious Correlation Learning in Preference Optimization: Mechanisms, Consequences, and Mitigation via Tie Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11134)
*Christian Moya,Alex Semendinger,Guang Lin,Elliott Thornley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Direct Preference Optimization induces spurious feature reliance; tie training with equal-utility pairs mitigates this vulnerability


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preference learning methods like DPO cause language models to rely on spurious correlations (sycophancy, length bias), which could lead to severe goal misgeneralization in future AI systems. Current approaches fail to address this fundamental vulnerability.

Method: Theoretical analysis of log-linear policies shows spurious learning occurs through mean spurious bias and causal-spurious correlation leakage. Proposes tie training - a data augmentation strategy using equal-utility preference pairs (ties) as regularization to reduce spurious learning without harming causal learning.

Result: Spurious feature reliance creates irreducible vulnerability to distribution shift (more data from same distribution doesn't help). Tie training selectively reduces spurious learning while maintaining causal learning. Validation shows theory holds for log-linear models and empirical evidence confirms mechanisms persist for neural networks and LLMs.

Conclusion: Standard preference learning has fundamental vulnerability to spurious correlations, but tie training provides provable mitigation. The solution is data-driven regularization using equal-utility pairs, which works across model types from log-linear to large language models.

Abstract: Preference learning methods such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) are known to induce reliance on spurious correlations, leading to sycophancy and length bias in today's language models and potentially severe goal misgeneralization in future systems. In this work, we provide a unified theoretical analysis of this phenomenon, characterizing the mechanisms of spurious learning, its consequences on deployment, and a provable mitigation strategy. Focusing on log-linear policies, we show that standard preference-learning objectives induce reliance on spurious features at the population level through two channels: mean spurious bias and causal--spurious correlation leakage. We then show that this reliance creates an irreducible vulnerability to distribution shift: more data from the same training distribution fails to reduce the model's dependence on spurious features. To address this, we propose tie training, a data augmentation strategy using ties (equal-utility preference pairs) to introduce data-driven regularization. We demonstrate that this approach selectively reduces spurious learning without degrading causal learning. Finally, we validate our theory on log-linear models and provide empirical evidence that both the spurious learning mechanisms and the benefits of tie training persist for neural networks and large language models.

</details>


### [151] [Rank Is Not Capacity: Spectral Occupancy for Latent Graph Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11142)
*Nikolaos Nakis,Panagiotis Promponas,Konstantinos Tsirkas,Katerina Mamali,Eftychia Makri,Leandros Tassiulas,Nicholas A. Christakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectra replaces fixed latent dimensions with spectrum-based capacity analysis, making model capacity a learned property rather than a brittle hyperparameter through spectral prefixes and realized dimension control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional graph representation learning treats latent dimension as a fixed hyperparameter that needs tuning via held-out performance, creating brittleness. Learned factors are only identifiable up to rotation and rescaling, making nominal rank unreliable for understanding model behavior and capacity.

Method: Proposes Spectral Prefix Extraction and Capacity-Targeted Representation Analysis (Spectra), which uses the spectrum of a learned positive semidefinite kernel as the unit of analysis. Normalizes eigenvalues to form a distribution on simplex, with Shannon effective rank serving as realized dimension. A single scalar parameter controls this realized dimension during training via bisection to target desired capacity within rank cap.

Result: Spectra successfully traces performance-capacity frontiers across diverse networks (collaboration, social, biological, infrastructure), making the accuracy vs. dimension trade-off visible. It performs competitively with strong link-prediction baselines, provides aligned lower-capacity views through spectral prefixes, and offers principled capacity control in overparameterized regimes.

Conclusion: Spectra transforms capacity from a brittle hyperparameter to a property of the fitted model, providing theoretical support through local regularity and monotonicity of realized-dimension profiles. This enables better understanding and control of model behavior across different capacity regimes.

Abstract: Graph representation learning has become a standard approach for analyzing networked data, with latent embeddings widely used for link prediction, community detection, and related tasks. Yet a basic design choice, the latent dimension, is still treated as a brittle hyperparameter, fixed before training and tuned by held-out performance. Learned factors are also identifiable only up to rotation and rescaling, so the nominal rank rarely coincides with the quantity that governs model behavior. We propose Spectral Prefix Extraction and Capacity-Targeted Representation Analysis (Spectra), which replaces rank as the unit of analysis with the spectrum of a learned positive semidefinite kernel, trace-normalized so that spectra are comparable across fits. The normalized eigenvalues form a distribution on the simplex, and their Shannon effective rank acts both as a summary of learned capacity and as a controllable training-time coordinate: a single scalar shapes this realized dimension during training, and bisection targets any desired value within the rank cap. To theoretically support that, we show local regularity and monotonicity of the realized-dimension profile. Across collaboration, social, biological, and infrastructure networks, Spectra traces performance--capacity frontiers that make the trade-off between predictive accuracy and realized dimension visible. It performs competitively with strong link-prediction baselines, yields aligned lower-capacity views of the same fitted model through spectral prefixes, and provides a principled handle on capacity in the overparameterized regime. Capacity thus becomes a property of the fitted model rather than a hyperparameter of the training.

</details>


### [152] [CORE: Cyclic Orthotope Relation Embedding for Knowledge Graph Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11159)
