*Yingqi Zeng,Luying Wang,Huiling Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORE is a novel knowledge graph completion model that embeds entities and relations on a boundary-less torus manifold to avoid issues with absolute boundary constraints in existing region-based models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Region-based embedding models for knowledge graph completion face a dilemma: with boundary constraints, they suffer from absolute boundary limitations during optimization; without constraints, relation regions expand indefinitely. There's a need to overcome these geometric limitations while maintaining the ability to capture complex logical patterns.

Method: CORE embeds entities and relations onto a boundary-less torus manifold, representing relations as cyclic orthotopes that can seamlessly wrap around spatial boundaries. This ensures smooth gradient conduction. The model also introduces adaptive width regularization to prevent unconditional region expansion.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows CORE can capture various complex relation patterns like subsumption and intersection. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate highly competitive performance, with significant improvements in link prediction accuracy in dense semantic environments.

Conclusion: CORE effectively addresses the boundary constraint problem in region-based KGC models by leveraging torus manifold embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art performance while maintaining theoretical soundness for capturing complex relational patterns.

Abstract: Knowledge graph completion (KGC) aims to automatically infer missing facts in multi-relational data by mapping entities and relations into continuous representation spaces. Recent region-based embedding models have shown great promise in capturing complex logical patterns by representing relations as geometric regions. However, these models inevitably suffer from absolute boundary constraints during optimization. Conversely, without such constraints, relation regions expand indefinitely. To address the limitation, we propose \textbf{CORE} (Cyclic Orthotope Relation Embedding), a novel KGC model that embeds entities and relations onto a boundary-less torus manifold.CORE represents relations as cyclic orthotopes on the torus manifold, allowing regions to seamlessly wrap around spatial boundaries to ensure smooth gradient conduction. Furthermore, an adaptive width regularization is introduced to prevent unconditional region expansion. Theoretical analysis proves that CORE can capture various complex relation patterns such as subsumption and intersection. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that CORE achieves highly competitive performance, significantly improving link prediction accuracy in dense semantic environments.

</details>


### [153] [Interpretability Can Be Actionable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11161)
*Hadas Orgad,Fazl Barez,Tal Haklay,Isabelle Lee,Marius Mosbach,Anja Reusch,Naomi Saphra,Byron Wallace,Sarah Wiegreffe,Eric Wong,Ian Tenney,Mor Geva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Interpretability research needs to focus on practical impact measured by actionability—how much insights enable real decisions and interventions—rather than just developing new methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite rapid growth in interpretability methods, there's growing concern that much of this work hasn't translated into practical impact, raising questions about its real-world relevance and utility.

Method: Proposes evaluation based on actionability along two dimensions (concreteness and validation), analyzes barriers to real-world impact, identifies five domains where interpretability offers unique leverage, and presents a framework with evaluation criteria aligned with practical outcomes.

Result: The paper establishes a framework for actionable interpretability with specific evaluation criteria focused on practical outcomes rather than just method development.

Conclusion: The central missing ingredient in interpretability research is not new methods, but better evaluation criteria centered on actionability, which should become a core objective while still allowing for exploratory research.

Abstract: Interpretability aims to explain the behavior of deep neural networks. Despite rapid growth, there is mounting concern that much of this work has not translated into practical impact, raising questions about its relevance and utility. This position paper argues that the central missing ingredient is not new methods, but evaluation criteria: interpretability should be evaluated by actionability--the extent to which insights enable concrete decisions and interventions beyond interpretability research itself. We define actionable interpretability along two dimensions--concreteness and validation--and analyze the barriers currently preventing real-world impact. To address these barriers, we identify five domains where interpretability offers unique leverage and present a framework for actionable interpretability with evaluation criteria aligned with practical outcomes. Our goal is not to downplay exploratory research, but to establish actionability as a core objective of interpretability research.

</details>


### [154] [COSMOS: Model-Agnostic Personalized Federated Learning with Clustered Server Models and Pseudo-Label-Only Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11165)
*Ben Rachmut,Luise Ge,William Yeoh,Ning Zhang,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COSMOS is a federated learning framework that enables server-side personalization using only pseudo-label communication, addressing both architectural and statistical heterogeneity with client clustering and knowledge distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning in heterogeneous environments is challenging because client models often differ in both architecture and data distribution. Existing approaches struggle to simultaneously handle architectural and statistical heterogeneity.

Method: COSMOS is model-agnostic and works as follows: 1) Clients train local models and predict on public data, 2) Server clusters clients by prediction similarity, 3) Server trains cluster-specific models for each group using its own compute, 4) Server distills resulting models back to clients.

Result: COSMOS consistently outperforms all model-agnostic FL baselines and remains competitive with state-of-the-art personalized FL methods across benchmarks. The theoretical analysis shows distillation from cluster models can yield exponential personalization risk contraction.

Conclusion: Personalized server-side learning with pseudo-labels is a promising paradigm for scalable and model-agnostic federated learning in highly heterogeneous environments.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) in heterogeneous environments remains challenging because client models often differ in both architecture and data distribution. While recent approaches attempt to address this challenge through client clustering and knowledge distillation, simultaneously handling architectural and statistical heterogeneity remains difficult. We introduce COSMOS, a model-agnostic framework that enables server-side personalization using only pseudo-label communication. Clients train local models and predict on the public data; the server clusters clients by prediction similarity, trains a cluster-specific model for each group using its own compute, and distills the resulting models back to clients. We provide the first theoretical analysis showing that distillation from the learned cluster models can yield exponential personalization risk contraction, going beyond the convergence-to-stationarity guarantees typically provided in model-agnostic FL. Experiments across benchmarks demonstrate that COSMOS consistently outperforms all model-agnostic FL baselines while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art personalized FL methods. More broadly, our results highlight personalized server-side learning with pseudo-labels as a promising paradigm for scalable and model-agnostic federated learning in highly heterogeneous environments.

</details>


### [155] [Unlearning with Asymmetric Sources: Improved Unlearning-Utility Trade-off with Public Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11170)
*Ahmed Mehdi Inane,Vincent Quirion,Gintare Karolina Dzugaite,Ioannis Mitliagkas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ALU uses public data to enable efficient machine unlearning with strong privacy guarantees, overcoming the noise-utility tradeoff that plagues current methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current noise-based certified machine unlearning methods face a fundamental tension: the noise needed for privacy certification destroys model utility, especially for large-scale deletion requests. While public data has been used in differential privacy to reduce privacy costs, its application to unlearning remains unexplored.

Method: The authors introduce Asymmetric Langevin Unlearning (ALU), a framework that injects public data to mitigate privacy costs. The method establishes a computational advantage over retraining and allows practitioners to trade off public data volume for reduced noise requirements.

Result: ALU achieves an O(1/n_pub²) suppression of unlearning cost, enabling mass unlearning of constant dataset fractions while maintaining high utility. Empirical evaluations show ALU effectively thwarts privacy attacks (including membership inference) and preserves utility under reasonable distribution shifts between public and private data.

Conclusion: ALU establishes a new paradigm for certified machine unlearning that uses public data as a control mechanism to overcome the noise-utility tradeoff, making practical unlearning at scale feasible even when public and private data distributions differ.

Abstract: Noise-based certified machine unlearning currently faces a hard ceiling: the noise magnitude required to certify unlearning typically destroys model utility, particularly for large-scale deletion requests.
  While leveraging public data is a standard technique in differential privacy to relax this tension, its role in unlearning remains unexplored. We address this gap by introducing Asymmetric Langevin Unlearning (ALU), a framework that uses public data to mitigate privacy costs. We prove that public data injection suppresses the unlearning cost by a factor of $O(1/n_{\mathrm{pub}}^2)$, guaranteeing a strict computational advantage over retraining. This establishes a new control mechanism: practitioners can mitigate the need for high noise-and the associated utility loss-by increasing the volume of public data. Crucially, we analyze the realistic setting of distribution mismatch, explicitly characterizing how shifts between public and private sources impact utility.
  We show that ALU enables mass unlearning of constant dataset fractions -- a regime where standard symmetric methods become impractical -- while maintaining high utility. Empirical evaluations using variational Rényi divergence and membership inference attacks confirm that ALU effectively thwarts privacy attacks while preserving utility under reasonable distribution shifts.

</details>


### [156] [Optimistic Dual Averaging Unifies Modern Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11172)
*Thomas Pethick,Wanyun Xie,Roman Machacek,Volkan Cevher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SODA generalizes Optimistic Dual Averaging, unifies several state-of-the-art optimizers under one framework, and provides a wrapper that eliminates weight decay tuning with a 1/k decay schedule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to provide a unified perspective on modern optimizers (Muon, Lion, AdEMAMix, NAdam) by showing they can all be viewed as optimistic instances of Optimistic Dual Averaging, and to eliminate the tedious hyperparameter tuning of weight decay.

Method: Introduces SODA (a generalization of Optimistic Dual Averaging) that provides a common framework for understanding various optimizers. Proposes a practical SODA wrapper for any base optimizer that uses a theoretically-grounded 1/k decay schedule for weight decay.

Result: Empirical results across various scales and training horizons show that SODA consistently improves performance without requiring additional hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: SODA provides a unified framework for understanding modern optimizers and offers a practical solution to eliminate weight decay tuning through a theoretically sound decay schedule, delivering consistent performance improvements across different scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce SODA, a generalization of Optimistic Dual Averaging, which provides a common perspective on state-of-the-art optimizers like Muon, Lion, AdEMAMix and NAdam, showing that they can all be viewed as optimistic instances of this framework. Based on this framing, we propose a practical SODA wrapper for any base optimizer that eliminates weight decay tuning through a theoretically-grounded $1/k$ decay schedule. Empirical results across various scales and training horizons show that SODA consistently improves performance without any additional hyperparameter tuning.

</details>


### [157] [Oversmoothing as Representation Degeneracy in Neural Sheaf Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11178)
*Arif Dönmez,Axel Mosig,Ellen Fritsche,Katharina Koch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a quiver-theoretic interpretation of Neural Sheaf Diffusion (NSD), showing that oversmoothing corresponds to representation degeneration, and introduces moment-map-inspired regularizers to stabilize learned sheaf geometries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand the representation-theoretic structure of the harmonic space in Neural Sheaf Diffusion, which remains largely implicit. The paper aims to provide an algebraic interpretation of oversmoothing phenomena in NSD and develop methods to prevent representation degeneration.

Method: The authors develop a quiver-theoretic interpretation by identifying cellular sheaves on graphs with representations of incidence quivers. They show how direct-sum decompositions induce harmonic space decompositions, connect sheaf diffusion to Geometric Invariant Theory principles, and introduce moment-map-inspired regularizers to bias restriction maps toward balanced geometries.

Result: The main results show that oversmoothing corresponds to representation degeneration, with learned sheaves collapsing toward low-complexity summands. The paper identifies a structural obstruction in equal-stalk architectures and shows non-uniform stalk dimensions remove this obstruction, making adaptive stability meaningful. Experiments on heterophilic benchmarks demonstrate these mechanisms.

Conclusion: The paper reframes oversmoothing as a degeneration phenomenon in the representation geometry underlying learned sheaf diffusion, providing both theoretical understanding and practical methods to stabilize NSD through quiver representation theory and Geometric Invariant Theory principles.

Abstract: Neural Sheaf Diffusion (NSD) generalizes diffusion-based Graph Neural Networks by replacing scalar graph Laplacians with sheaf Laplacians whose learned restriction maps define a task-adapted geometry. While the diffusion limit of NSD is known to be the space of global sections, the representation-theoretic structure of this harmonic space remains largely implicit. We develop a quiver-theoretic interpretation of NSD by identifying cellular sheaves on graphs with representations of the associated incidence quiver. Under this correspondence, learned sheaf geometries become points in a finite-dimensional representation space. We show that direct-sum decompositions of the underlying incidence-quiver representation induce decompositions of the harmonic space reached in the diffusion limit. This gives an algebraic interpretation of oversmoothing as representation degeneration: learned sheaves may collapse toward low-complexity summands whose global sections fail to preserve discriminative information. Building on this viewpoint, we connect sheaf diffusion to stability and moment-map principles from Geometric Invariant Theory. We introduce moment-map-inspired regularizers that bias restriction maps toward balanced representation geometries, and identify a structural obstruction in equal-stalk architectures: when $d_v = d_e$, admissibility for learnable stability parameters forces the trivial all-object summand onto a stability wall. Non-uniform stalk dimensions remove this obstruction, making adaptive stability meaningful. Experiments on heterophilic benchmarks are consistent with this mechanism: breaking stalk symmetry can reduce variance or improve validation behavior, and adaptive stability becomes more effective in selected rectangular settings. Overall, our framework reframes oversmoothing as a degeneration phenomenon in the representation geometry underlying learned sheaf diffusion.

</details>


### [158] [Muon is Not That Special: Random or Inverted Spectra Work Just as Well](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11181)
*Zakhar Shumaylov,Nathaël Da Costa,Peter Zaika,Bálint Mucsányi,Alex Massucco,Yoav Gelberg,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb,Yarin Gal,Philipp Hennig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper challenges the geometric narrative behind Muon optimizer's success, showing that precise geometry isn't key to performance through three contributions: 1) Freon optimizer family using Schatten norms/quasi-norms, 2) Kaon optimizer using random noise instead of geometry, and 3) identifying alignment and descent potential as true performance drivers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing geometric narrative justifying Muon optimizer's success, questioning whether precise geometric structure is actually the key factor affecting optimization performance rather than similarities with second-order methods and LMO theory.

Method: Three-part methodology: 1) Introduce Freon optimizer family based on Schatten norms/quasi-norms with QDWH-based iterative approximation; 2) Create Kaon optimizer that replaces singular values with random noise to test geometry's importance; 3) Analyze local quantities (alignment and descent potential) using stochastic random feature models to understand true performance drivers.

Result: Best Schatten parameters for GPT-2 lie in quasi-norm regime (no LMO representation); Kaon matches Muon's performance despite lacking coherent geometry; Performance driven by alignment and descent potential dynamics rather than global geometry; Muon succeeds by guaranteeing step-size optimality, not tracking ideal geometry.

Conclusion: Precise geometric structure is not the key factor in optimization performance; local dynamics of alignment and descent potential control performance, requiring step size tuning; Muon's success comes from step-size optimality, not geometric fidelity.

Abstract: The recent empirical success of the Muon optimizer has renewed interest in non-Euclidean optimization, typically justified by similarities with second-order methods, and linear minimization oracle (LMO) theory. In this paper, we challenge this geometric narrative through three contributions, demonstrating that precise geometric structure is not the key factor affecting optimization performance. First, we introduce Freon, a family of optimizers based on Schatten (quasi-)norms, powered by a novel, provably optimal QDWH-based iterative approximation. Freon naturally interpolates between SGD and Muon, while smoothly extrapolating into the quasi-norm regime. Empirically, the best-performing Schatten parameters for GPT-2 lie strictly within the quasi-norm regime, and thus cannot be represented by any unitarily invariant LMO. Second, noting that Freon performs well across a wide range of exponents, we introduce Kaon, an absurd optimizer that replaces singular values with random noise. Despite lacking any coherent geometric structure, Kaon matches Muon's performance and retains classical convergence guarantees, proving that strict adherence to a precise geometry is practically irrelevant. Third, having shown that geometry is not the primary driver of performance, we demonstrate it is instead controlled by two local quantities: alignment and descent potential. Ultimately, each optimizer must tune its step size around these two quantities. While their dynamics are difficult to predict a-priori, evaluating them within a stochastic random feature model yields a precise insight: Muon succeeds not by tracking an ideal global geometry, but by guaranteeing step-size optimality.

</details>


### [159] [CATS: Cascaded Adaptive Tree Speculation for Memory-Limited LLM Inference Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11186)
*Yuning Han,Yangchenchen Jin,Dylan Zhao,Jingwei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CATS is a self-speculative decoding framework for memory-constrained edge devices that enables cascaded verification and correction to maximize token acceptance rate and speedup while keeping peak memory equal to the target model alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods assume sufficient High-Bandwidth Memory to hold both target and draft models simultaneously, which fails on memory-constrained edge devices with limited DRAM. Auto-regressive decoding is memory-bound and bottlenecked by memory bandwidth rather than compute.

Method: CATS proposes a self-speculative decoding framework with cascaded verification and correction based on memory budget and parameter offloading patterns. It maximizes token acceptance rate and end-to-end speedup while keeping peak memory footprint equal to target model alone.

Result: CATS achieves up to 5.08x wall-clock speedup with no degradation in generation quality, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 1.45x under edge memory constraints across five benchmarks on real edge devices.

Conclusion: CATS effectively addresses the memory bottleneck for speculative decoding on edge devices by enabling efficient parallel verification while maintaining memory constraints, significantly improving throughput without compromising quality.

Abstract: Auto-regressive decoding in Large Language Models (LLMs) is inherently memory-bound: every generation step requires loading the model weights and intermediate results from memory (e.g., High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) for GPU servers), making throughput bottlenecked by memory bandwidth rather than compute. Speculative decoding addresses this by enabling parallel verification of multiple draft tokens, effectively amortizing the cost of each target-model call. However, existing speculative decoding methods are designed under the assumption that HBM is sufficiently large to hold both the target model and an auxiliary draft model simultaneously -- an assumption that breaks down on memory-constrained devices such as edge platforms with limited DRAM. We analyze the inference bottleneck in this memory-limited regime and propose CATS, a self-speculative decoding framework that conducts cascaded verification and correction based on the memory budget and parameter offloading patterns on memory-limited devices. This design maximizes token acceptance rate and end-to-end speedup while keeping the peak memory footprint on the device equal to that of the target model alone. We evaluate CATS on different models across five benchmarks on real edge devices. CATS can achieve a wall-clock speedup of up to 5.08x with no degradation in generation quality, outperforming the SOTA method by up to 1.45x under edge memory constraints.

</details>


### [160] [Deep Learning for Protein Complex Prediction and Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11189)
*Ziwei Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis uses deep learning to address protein complex modeling through domain-specific architectures that capture structural hierarchies and search algorithms to efficiently navigate sequence spaces for improved prediction and design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate modeling and design of protein complex structures is crucial for understanding cellular function and developing therapeutics, representing a central challenge in computational structural biology.

Method: The research employs deep learning approaches with two main components: 1) domain-specific architectures designed to capture the hierarchical nature of protein structures, and 2) search algorithms that efficiently explore vast protein sequence spaces to identify interacting homologs.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract, but the methods are aimed at improving complex structure prediction accuracy and enabling protein sequence design through the identification of interacting homologs.

Conclusion: The thesis demonstrates that combining specialized neural network architectures with efficient search algorithms provides a comprehensive deep learning framework for addressing fundamental challenges in protein complex modeling and design.

Abstract: Accurately modeling and designing protein complex structures is a central problem in computational structural biology, with broad implications for understanding cellular function and developing therapeutics. This thesis investigates two fundamental aspects of this problem using deep learning: domain-specific architectures that capture the hierarchical nature of protein structures, and search algorithms that efficiently navigate the vast sequence spaces of protein complexes to identify interacting homologs for improving complex structure prediction and to design protein sequences.

</details>


### [161] [Variational Linear Attention: Stable Associative Memory for Long-Context Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11196)
*Vishal Pandey,Gopal Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Variational Linear Attention (VLA) is a linear attention variant that solves progressive memory growth in standard linear attention by treating memory updates as online regularized least-squares problems with adaptive penalty matrices, achieving stable memory dynamics and improved associative recall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard linear attention reduces quadratic computational cost to linear but suffers from progressive memory growth in Frobenius norm, causing interference between stored associations over long sequences.

Method: VLA reframes memory updates as online regularized least-squares problems with adaptive penalty matrices maintained via Sherman-Morrison rank-1 formula. Normalizing write direction to unit length ensures recurrence Jacobian spectral norm equals 1.

Result: VLA reduces memory state Frobenius norm by 109× vs standard linear attention at T=1000, achieves near-perfect associative recall within per-head memory regime, maintains 62% accuracy at capacity boundary, and achieves 14× speedup with O(T) scaling.

Conclusion: VLA provides a theoretically sound and empirically effective solution to memory growth in linear attention, enabling stable associative memory with linear complexity while outperforming existing methods like DeltaNet and standard linear attention.

Abstract: Linear attention reduces the quadratic cost of softmax attention to $\mathcal{O}(T)$, but its memory state grows as $\mathcal{O}(T)$ in Frobenius norm, causing progressive interference between stored associations. We introduce \textbf{Variational Linear Attention} (VLA), which reframes the memory update as an online regularised least-squares problem with an adaptive penalty matrix maintained via the Sherman-Morrison rank-1 formula. We prove that normalising the write direction to unit length gives the recurrence Jacobian spectral norm exactly $1$ for all sequence lengths and head dimensions (Proposition 2), and that the state norm is self-limiting under bounded inputs (Proposition 1). Empirically, VLA reduces $\|S_t\|_F$ by $109\times$ relative to standard linear attention at $T{=}1{,}000$, achieves near-perfect exact-match accuracy on multi-query associative recall within the effective per-head memory regime ($n_\text{pairs} < d_h$), maintaining substantially higher retrieval performance than DeltaNet and standard linear attention under increasing memory load, and maintains 62\% accuracy at the per-head capacity boundary. A Triton-fused kernel achieves $14\times$ speedup over sequential Python and $\mathcal{O}(T)$ scaling, crossing below softmax attention latency at approximately 43\,000 tokens.

</details>


### [162] [FeatMap: Understanding image manipulation in the feature space and its implications for feature space geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11203)
*Elias B. Krey,Nils Neukirch,Nils Strodthoff*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep neural network feature spaces show surprisingly linear organization despite complex semantic manipulations, with simple linear models effectively mapping between original and manipulated features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the geometric structure of intermediate feature representations in deep neural networks, which remains poorly understood despite their importance for network expressivity and adaptability.

Method: Apply diverse input manipulations (geometric/photometric transformations, local masking, semantic edits via generative models), then learn mappings in feature space from original to manipulated features using different mapping types (linear/non-linear, local/global). Evaluate reconstruction quality and semantic content.

Result: Feasible to learn such mappings for all transformations; global transformer models perform best but shared linear models on single feature vectors work nearly as well, even for complex semantic manipulations. Linear transformations show consistent patterns (weight vs. bias dominance, effective rank) across layers.

Conclusion: Feature space appears organized in approximately linear structures. Generative image editing models enable deeper feature space understanding through input manipulation.

Abstract: Intermediate feature representations represent the backbone for the expressivity and adaptability of deep neural networks. However, their geometric structure remains poorly understood. In this submission, we provide indirect insights into this matter by applying a broad selection of manipulations in input space, ranging from geometric and photometric transformations to local masking and semantic manipulations using generative image editing models, and assess the feasibility of learning a mapping in the feature space, mapping from the original to the manipulated feature map. To this end, we devise different types of mappings, from linear to non-linear and local to global mappings and assess both the reconstruction quality of the mapping as well as the semantic content of the mapped representations. We demonstrate the feasibility of learning such mappings for all considered transformations. While global (transformer) models that operate on the full feature map often achieve best results, we show that the same can be achieved with a shared linear model operating on a single feature vector typically with very little degradation in reconstruction quality, even for highly non-trivial semantic manipulations. We analyze the corresponding mappings across different feature layers and characterize them according to dominance of weight vs. bias and the effective rank of the linear transformations. These results provide hints for the hypothesis that the feature space is to a first degree of approximation organized in linear structures. From a broader perspective, the study demonstrates that generative image editing models might open the door to a deeper understanding of the feature space through input manipulation.

</details>


### [163] [The Scaling Law of Evaluation Failure: Why Simple Averaging Collapses Under Data Sparsity and Item Difficulty Gaps, and How Item Response Theory Recovers Ground Truth Across Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11205)
*Jung Min Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Simple averaging in benchmark rankings fails when data is sparse and items vary in difficulty, but Item Response Theory (IRT) maintains accurate rankings across conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmark evaluation in AI and safety-critical domains relies heavily on simple averaging, but this approach may produce misleading rankings when evaluation matrices are sparse and items have varying difficulty levels.

Method: The paper uses controlled simulation experiments across four domains (NLP, clinical drug trials, autonomous vehicles, cybersecurity) and analyzes ranking accuracy using Spearman correlation. They compare simple averaging with a two-parameter logistic (2PL) Item Response Theory model, conducting a 150-condition grid sweep over sparsity and difficulty gap parameters.

Result: Simple averaging rankings degrade substantially under sparsity and difficulty heterogeneity (ρ drops from 1.000 at 100% coverage to 0.809 at 67% coverage), while IRT maintains ρ≥0.996 across all conditions. The failure surface shows strong interaction between sparsity and difficulty gap.

Conclusion: Simple averaging is inadequate for benchmark evaluation when faced with sparse data and varying difficulty. IRT provides robust rankings in such conditions, with important implications for Physical AI benchmarking where these conditions are common.

Abstract: Benchmark evaluation across AI and safety-critical domains overwhelmingly relies on simple averaging. We demonstrate that this practice produces substantially misleading rankings when two conditions co-occur: (1) the evaluation matrix is sparse and (2) items vary substantially in difficulty. Through controlled simulation experiments across four domains -- NLP (GLUE), clinical drug trials, autonomous vehicle safety, and cybersecurity -- we show that Spearman rank correlation $ρ$ between simple-average rankings and ground-truth rankings degrades from $ρ= 1.000$ at 100% coverage to $ρ= 0.809$ at 67% coverage with high difficulty heterogeneity (mean over 20 seeds). A standard two-parameter logistic (2PL) Item Response Theory (IRT) model maintains $ρ\geq 0.996$ across all conditions. A 150-condition grid sweep over sparsity $S \in [0, 0.70]$ and difficulty gap $D \in [0.5, 5.0]$ confirms that ranking error forms a failure surface with a strong $S \times D$ interaction ($γ_3 = +0.20$, $t = 13.05$), while IRT maintains $ρ\geq 0.993$ throughout. We discuss implications for Physical AI benchmarking, where evaluation matrices are often incomplete and difficulty gaps are extreme.

</details>


### [164] [Measuring Five-Nines Reliability: Sample-Efficient LLM Evaluation in Saturated Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11209)
*Eungyeup Kim,Chenchen Gu,Vashisth Tiwari,J. Zico Kolter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenge of evaluating extreme reliability (99.999% vs 99.9%) in LLMs by proposing an efficient sampling method using the cross-entropy method to concentrate on failure-prone inputs, revealing significant differences in failure rates among models with similar benchmark performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM benchmarks show near-perfect performance but fail to evaluate extreme reliability needed for real-world deployment where the difference between "five-nines" (99.999%) and "three-nines" (99.9%) reliability is critical. Standard Monte Carlo evaluation requires prohibitively large inference sizes to estimate rare failure probabilities with confidence.

Method: The paper observes that LLM failures exhibit systematic patterns, with a small subset of inputs accounting for most failures. Leveraging this, they propose learning a sampling distribution concentrated on failure-prone inputs using the cross-entropy method (CEM), enabling efficient estimation of extreme failure probabilities.

Result: The framework achieves up to 156.22x reduction in required inferences compared to naive uniform sampling when evaluated on three LLMs (Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct, gpt-oss-20b-low, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite) across parameterized GSM8K templates. Models with indistinguishable accuracy on standard benchmarks show substantial differences in estimated failure rates.

Conclusion: Reliability is a distinct and measurable axis of model quality beyond existing benchmarks. The proposed simple yet practical framework enables evaluation of extreme reliability in LLMs for reliability-sensitive applications, addressing an underexplored dimension of LLM evaluation.

Abstract: While existing benchmarks demonstrate the near-perfect performance of large language models (LLMs) on various tasks, this apparent saturation often obscures the need for rigorous evaluation of their reliability. In real-world deployment, however, achieving extremely high reliability (e.g., "five-nines" (99.999%) vs. "three-nines" (99.9%)) is fundamentally critical, as this gap results in an order-of-magnitude increase in failures, which is catastrophic in reliability-critical applications. Still, estimating such a rare failure probability with tight confidence bounds requires prohibitively large LLM inference sizes, making standard Monte Carlo evaluation infeasible under limited compute budgets. In this paper, we observe that LLM failures exhibit strong systematic patterns: across broad parameterized input spaces, a small subset of inputs disproportionately accounts for the majority of failures. Leveraging this observation, we propose to learn a sampling distribution concentrated on failure-prone inputs via the cross-entropy method (CEM). We evaluate our framework on three LLMs, Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct, gpt-oss-20b-low, and Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, across parameterized GSM8K templates and achieve up to 156.22x reduction in required inferences compared to naive uniform sampling. Our estimates reveal that models with indistinguishable accuracy on standard benchmarks can differ substantially in estimated failure rates, underscoring that reliability is a distinct and measurable axis of model quality. Our simple yet practical framework enables the evaluation of extreme reliability in LLMs, a distinct and underexplored dimension of evaluation beyond existing benchmarks, for their growing use in reliability-sensitive applications.

</details>


### [165] [Enforcing Constraints in Generative Sampling via Adaptive Correction Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11214)
*Noah Trupin,Yexiang Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive correction scheduling for constraint enforcement in generative sampling improves the cost-accuracy tradeoff by strategically allocating projection budget to steps with largest geometric mismatch, outperforming both terminal-only and stepwise projection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches to enforcing hard constraints in generative sampling use binary framing (terminal or stepwise projection), which overlooks how projection changes the distribution of states that future updates depend on. This can produce feasible samples that are inconsistent with intended sampling dynamics.

Method: Formalize constraint enforcement as a correction scheduling problem using one-step constraint defect as a local signal of geometric mismatch. Introduce adaptive correction scheduling - a state-dependent policy that allocates projection budget to steps that most strongly perturb the trajectory. Terminal and stepwise projection are limiting cases of this family.

Result: Across controlled manifold rollouts and a learned projected diffusion sampler, adaptive scheduling improves the cost-accuracy frontier at matched projection budgets. It recovers 71.2% of full stepwise benefit with 75% fewer corrections.

Conclusion: Constraint timing is a first-class design variable in generative sampling. Enforcing feasibility alone is insufficient to preserve intended constrained sampling dynamics. Adaptive correction scheduling offers a principled middle ground between terminal-only and stepwise projection approaches.

Abstract: Hard constraints in generative sampling are typically enforced by projection, applied either once at the end of sampling or after every update. This binary framing overlooks a fundamental issue: projection changes the distribution of states which future updates depend on. As a result, delayed projection can produce samples that are feasible but inconsistent with the intended sampling dynamics, even after final projection. We formalize constraint enforcement as a correction scheduling problem over the generative rollout. Using one-step constraint defect as a local signal of geometric mismatch, we introduce adaptive correction scheduling, a state-dependent policy that allocates projection budget to the steps that most strongly perturb the trajectory. Terminal and stepwise projection arise as limiting cases of this family. Across controlled manifold rollouts and a learned projected diffusion sampler, adaptive scheduling improves the cost-accuracy frontier at matched projection budgets, recovering 71.2% of full stepwise benefit with 75% fewer corrections. These results show that constraint timing is a first-class design variable in generative sampling, and that enforcing feasibility alone is insufficient to preserve the intended constrained sampling dynamics.

</details>


### [166] [Leveraging RAG for Training-Free Alignment of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11217)
*John T. Halloran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RAG-Pref is a training-free, retrieval-augmented alignment algorithm that combines online conditioning on preference pairs with offline alignment to significantly improve LLM refusal guardrails against agentic attacks while maintaining general alignment performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM alignment algorithms require heavy computational resources but are ineffective against recent agentic attacks. There's a need for improved refusal guardrails without drastically increasing computational overhead.

Method: RAG-Pref (Retrieval Augmented Generation for Preference alignment) - a simple RAG-based alignment algorithm that conditions on preferred and dispreferred samples during inference to leverage contrastive information. It's training-free (online), compatible with off-the-shelf packages.

Result: RAG-Pref combined with offline alignment achieves >3.7x average improvement in agentic attack refusals across 5 widely used LLMs, outperforming other online alignment algorithms (2.9x) and offline alignment alone (1.5x). It also maintains general human-preference alignment performance without significantly increasing computational requirements.

Conclusion: RAG-Pref provides an effective, computationally efficient solution for improving LLM refusal guardrails against agentic attacks while maintaining general alignment capabilities, outperforming existing online and offline alignment methods.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) alignment algorithms typically consist of post-training over preference pairs. While such algorithms are widely used to enable safety guardrails and align LLMs with general human preferences, we show that state-of-the-art alignment algorithms require significant computational resources while being far less capable of enabling refusal guardrails for recent agentic attacks. Thus, to improve refusal guardrails against such attacks without drastically increasing computational overhead, we introduce Retrieval Augmented Generation for Pref erence alignment (RAG-Pref), a simple RAG-based alignment algorithm which conditions on preferred and dispreferred samples to leverage contrastive information during inference. RAG-Pref is online (training-free), compatible with off-the-shelf packages, and, when combined with offline (training-based) alignment algorithms, enables more than an average 3.7 factor improvement in agentic attack refusals across five widely used LLMs, compared to 2.9 for other online alignment algorithms and 1.5 for offline alignment alone. We conclude by showing that, in stark contrast to other online alignment methods, RAG-Pref similarly increases performance on general human-preference alignment tasks and does not drastically increase overall computational requirements.

</details>


### [167] [ADMM-Q: An Improved Hessian-based Weight Quantizer for Post-Training Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11222)
*Ryan Lucas,Mehdi Makni,Xiang Meng,Adam Deng,Rahul Mazumder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADMM-Q is a novel weight quantization algorithm for LLMs that uses a combinatorial ADMM approach to minimize layer-wise reconstruction error while gradually enforcing quantization constraints, outperforming existing methods like GPTQ and RTN at aggressive quantization levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing weight quantization methods (GPTQ, RTN) suffer from poor model utility, especially at aggressive sub-4-bit quantization levels, limiting effective compression of large language models while maintaining performance.

Method: Proposes ADMM-Q based on a combinatorial variant of Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers that updates weights continuously to minimize layer-wise reconstruction error while gradually enforcing quantization constraints with convergence guarantees. Includes enhancements like penalty scheduling, preconditioning, and local search post-processing.

Result: ADMM-Q significantly improves perplexity scores across multiple quantization settings on Qwen3-8B: W3A16 (12.85→10.06), W4A8 SmoothQuant (9.29→8.68), and W2A4KV4 SpinQuant (66.11→19.42), demonstrating substantial performance gains over GPTQ.

Conclusion: ADMM-Q provides an effective, modular weight quantization solution that can be used as a drop-in replacement in existing quantization pipelines, offering superior performance at aggressive quantization levels while maintaining composability with other quantization techniques.

Abstract: Quantization is an effective strategy to reduce the storage and computation footprint of large language models (LLMs). Post-training quantization (PTQ) is a leading approach for compressing LLMs. Popular weight quantization procedures, including GPTQ and RTN, suffer in model utility, especially at aggressive quantization levels (sub-4-bit). We propose ADMM-Q, a novel weight quantization algorithm that considers the layer-wise quantization problem. Our algorithm is based on a combinatorial variant of the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Our operator-splitting procedure updates weights continuously to minimize the layer-wise reconstruction error, while gradually enforcing the quantization constraints with convergence guarantees. We propose additional algorithmic enhancements (e.g., penalty scheduling, preconditioning, and a local search post-processing step) to make ADMM-Q efficient at LLM scale. ADMM-Q is modular and can be used as a drop-in replacement for any weight quantizer within existing quantization pipelines: ADMM-Q is fully composable with existing techniques including range clipping, learned or random rotations, and activation scaling. Using ADMM-Q in place of GPTQ on Qwen3-8B, we decrease WikiText-2 perplexity in: (i) the W3A16 weight-only setting (12.85 $\rightarrow$ 10.06); (ii) the W4A8 SmoothQuant procedure (9.29 $\rightarrow$ 8.68); and (iii) the W2A4KV4 SpinQuant procedure (66.11 $\rightarrow$ 19.42).

</details>


### [168] [LiBaGS: Lightweight Boundary Gap Synthesis for Targeted Synthetic Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11231)
*Abhishek Moturu,Anna Goldenberg,Babak Taati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LiBaGS is a lightweight, generator-agnostic method for targeted synthetic data selection that scores samples based on decision-boundary proximity, uncertainty, real-data density, and support validity, using boundary-gap allocation and stopping rules to improve model accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic data is only useful when it fills missing parts of the training distribution relevant to the downstream task. Existing methods often add synthetic data indiscriminately or focus only on uncertainty, without considering whether samples remain on the real data manifold or target the most important decision-boundary regions.

Method: LiBaGS combines four scoring criteria: decision-boundary proximity, predictive uncertainty, real-data density, and support validity. It uses boundary-gap allocation to target sparse but realistic decision-boundary neighborhoods rather than simply adding more data. The method includes a marginal-value stopping rule to learn when enough synthetic samples have been added, assigns softer labels near ambiguous boundaries, and uses a diversity objective to avoid redundant near-duplicate selections.

Result: Experiments show LiBaGS improves accuracy over classical oversampling methods, hard augmentation techniques, uncertainty and density ablation methods, and targeted-generation selection criteria.

Conclusion: LiBaGS provides an effective generator-agnostic framework for targeted synthetic data selection that focuses on informative samples that remain on the real data manifold, resulting in better model performance compared to existing approaches.

Abstract: Synthetic data is useful only when the added samples fill missing parts of the training distribution that matter for the downstream task. We introduce LiBaGS, a lightweight, generator-agnostic method for targeted synthetic training data selection. LiBaGS scores candidate synthetic samples by combining decision-boundary proximity, predictive uncertainty, real-data density, and support validity, so that selected samples are both informative and likely to remain on the real data manifold. We then use a boundary-gap allocation rule that targets sparse but realistic decision-boundary neighborhoods, rather than simply adding more data or selecting only the most uncertain candidates. LiBaGS also learns when enough synthetic samples have been added through a marginal-value stopping rule, assigns softer labels near ambiguous boundaries, and uses a diversity objective to avoid redundant near-duplicate selections. Experiments show that LiBaGS improves accuracy over classical oversampling, hard augmentation, uncertainty and density ablations, and targeted-generation selection criteria.

</details>


### [169] [A Comparative Study of Model Selection Criteria for Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11233)
*Ali Soltani,Gabriel Kronberger,Fabricio Olivetti de Franca,Mattia Billa,Alessandro Lucantonio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic comparison shows MDL consistently produces lowest test error and shortest expressions in symbolic regression model selection, with MDL and BIC having highest probability of selecting ground-truth expressions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable automatic selection of symbolic regression solutions that generalize well remains an open issue despite modern GP systems generating Pareto optimal candidate solutions. Comprehensive comparisons of existing selection criteria across different data regimes are limited.

Method: Empirical comparison of widely used selection criteria (AIC, AICc, BIC, MDL, and Efron's bootstrap) on seven synthetic datasets with Gaussian noise. Candidate expressions were generated by perturbing ground-truth functions to assess generalization error and selection probability.

Result: MDL consistently identifies models with the lowest test error and shortest length across most datasets. MDL and BIC produced the highest probability of selecting ground-truth expressions. No single criterion dominates all results.

Conclusion: MDL emerges as a strong candidate for symbolic regression model selection due to its consistent performance in minimizing test error and model complexity, while MDL and BIC are most effective at identifying the true underlying expressions.

Abstract: Effective model selection is critical in symbolic regression (SR) to identify mathematical expressions that balance accuracy and complexity, and have low expected error on unseen data. Many modern implementations of genetic programming (GP) for SR generate a set of Pareto optimal candidate solutions, but reliable automatic selection of solutions that generalize well remains an open issue. Current literature offers various information-theoretic and Bayesian approaches, yet comprehensive comparisons of their performance across different data regimes are limited. This study presents a systematic empirical comparison of widely used selection criteria: the Akaike information criterion (AIC), the corrected AIC (AICc), the Bayesian information criterion (BIC), minimum description length (MDL), as well as Efron's bootstrap estimate for the in-sample prediction error on seven synthetic datasets with Gaussian noise. We rank candidate expressions generated by perturbing ground-truth functions to assess generalization error and selection probability of the ground-truth expression. Our findings reveal that MDL consistently identifies models with the lowest test error and the shortest length across most datasets. While no single criterion dominates all results, MDL and BIC produced the highest probability of selecting the ground-truth expressions.

</details>


### [170] [Internalizing Curriculum Judgment for LLM Reinforcement Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11235)
*Han Zheng,Yining Ma,Karthick Gunasekaran,Bharathan Balaji,Zheng Du,Shiv Vitaladevuni,Cathy Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: METIS is a novel framework for LLM reinforcement fine-tuning that internalizes curriculum judgment as a native capability through metacognitive self-judgment, using within-prompt reward variance to gauge prompt informativeness and dynamically allocate training resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM reinforcement fine-tuning methods externalize curriculum judgment via handcrafted heuristics or auxiliary models, which risks misalignment with the policy's training dynamics and lacks adaptability to the learning process.

Method: METIS internalizes curriculum judgment by predicting within-prompt reward variance (which gauges prompt informativeness) based on recent training outcomes as lightweight in-context examples. It uses intrinsic self-judgment to dynamically allocate training and closes the loop between judgment and optimization by jointly optimizing standard RFT rewards and a self-judgment reward.

Result: Across extensive discrete and continuous RFT benchmarks including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic function-calling, METIS consistently delivers superior performance while accelerating convergence by up to 67%.

Conclusion: METIS establishes a simple, closed-loop, and highly efficient curriculum internalization paradigm for LLM reinforcement fine-tuning that bypasses handcrafted heuristics and auxiliary models, enabling policies to learn what to learn next through metacognition.

Abstract: In LLM Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT), curriculum learning drives both efficiency and performance. Yet, current methods externalize curriculum judgment via handcrafted heuristics or auxiliary models, risking misalignment with the policy's training dynamics. In this paper, we introduce METIS (METacognitive Internalized Self-judgment), a novel framework that internalizes curriculum judgment as a native capability. Leveraging a critical observation that within-prompt reward variance effectively gauges prompt informativeness, METIS predicts this metric based on recent training outcomes as lightweight in-context learning examples. This intrinsic self-judgment then dynamically dictates the training allocation. Moreover, METIS closes the loop between judgment and optimization by jointly optimizing the standard RFT rewards and a self-judgment reward. This allows the policy to learn what to learn next, as a form of metacognition. Across extensive discrete and continuous RFT benchmarks from mathematical reasoning, code generation, to agentic function-calling, METIS consistently delivers superior performance while accelerating convergence by up to 67%. By bypassing handcrafted heuristics and auxiliary models, our work establishes a simple, closed-loop, and highly efficient curriculum internalization paradigm for LLM reinforcement fine-tuning.

</details>


### [171] [DeconDTN-Toolkit: A Library for Evaluation and Enhancement of Robustness to Provenance Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11237)
*Yongsen Tan,Zhecheng Sheng,Xiruo Ding,Serguei V. S. Pakhomov,Trevor Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers establish theoretical connection between provenance shift and invariant learning, create evaluation toolkit DeconDTN-Toolkit to simulate provenance shifts, and evaluate existing algorithms' vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provenance shift (where relationship between data source and label changes at deployment) is poorly understood and under-addressed in current distribution shift research, despite its practical importance.

Method: 1) Establish formal connection between provenance shift, counterfactual invariance, and invariant learning to derive robust learning objective. 2) Develop DeconDTN-Toolkit to simulate provenance shifts while maintaining existing benchmark protocols. 3) Introduce robust out-of-distribution performance indicator and evaluate existing algorithms.

Result: Reveal vulnerability of Empirical Risk Minimization under provenance shift, provide comprehensive evaluation of existing algorithms, and demonstrate both theoretical grounding and practical tools for addressing confounding by provenance.

Conclusion: The paper provides necessary theoretical foundation and practical toolkit (DeconDTN-Toolkit) to characterize and mitigate the problem of confounding by provenance shift, bridging an important gap in distribution shift research.

Abstract: Despite the burgeoning body of work on distribution shifts, provenance shift-where the relationship between data source and label changes at deployment-remains poorly understood and under-addressed. In this paper, we establish a formal connection between provenance shift, counterfactual invariance, and invariant learning to derive a learning objective for robustness. We then introduce \textsc{DeconDTN-Toolkit}, a specialized evaluation and remediation suite designed to simulate provenance shifts of varying degrees while maintaining the training protocol and the infrastructure of existing benchmarks. We reveal the vulnerability of Empirical Risk Minimization under provenance shift, introduce a robust out-of-distribution performance indicator, and conduct a comprehensive evaluation on existing algorithms. Our work provides both the theoretical grounding and the practical tools necessary to characterize the problem of confounding by provenance, and implementations of methods to mitigate it.

</details>


### [172] [Extending Kernel Trick to Influence Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11239)
*Zhenhuan Sun,Shahrokh Valaee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dual influence functions scale with dataset size instead of model size, offering computational efficiency for large models but limited to linearizable models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Original influence functions have computational complexity scaling with model size, making them infeasible for large models or when evaluating in parameter space is impractical.

Method: Developed a dual representation of influence functions that scales with dataset size rather than model size, applicable to linearizable models where model behavior can be approximated by linearizations throughout training.

Result: The dual representation provides an efficient alternative for estimating changes in parameters, model outputs, and loss due to data point removal when model size is large relative to dataset size, requiring materialization of a matrix sized by product of model output dimension and dataset size.

Conclusion: Dual influence functions offer computational advantages for large-scale models but are constrained to linearizable models and require potentially large matrix storage, providing a trade-off between computational complexity and applicability.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a dual representation of the influence functions, whose computational complexity scales with dataset size rather than model size. Both analytically and experimentally, we show that this representation can be an efficient alternative to the original influence functions for estimating changes in parameters, model outputs and loss due to data point removal, when model size is large relative to dataset size, or when evaluating the original influence functions in parameter space is infeasible. The dual representation, however, is limited to linearizable models, which are models whose behavior can be approximated by their linearizations throughout training, and requires materializing a matrix, whose size grows with the product of model output dimension and dataset size.

</details>


### [173] [Support-Proximity Augmented Diffusion Estimation for Offline Black-Box Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11246)
*Yonghan Yang,Ye Yuan,Zipeng Sun,Linfeng Du,Bowei He,Haolun Wu,Can Chen,Xue Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPADE is a novel framework for offline black-box optimization that uses enhanced diffusion modeling with calibrated estimation and support-proximity regularization to address out-of-distribution extrapolation challenges, outperforming existing methods on benchmark tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for offline black-box optimization face fundamental challenges: inverse methods struggle with ill-posed mapping of scores to designs, while forward methods lack distributional expressivity for effective uncertainty quantification. The core problem is the out-of-distribution extrapolation challenge when using only static datasets.

Method: SPADE reimagines forward surrogate modeling through conditional generative modeling. It uses a diffusion model to estimate p(y|x) with two key enhancements: 1) Calibrated Diffusion Estimation that enforces global consistency in statistical moments and pairwise rankings, and 2) Support-Proximity Regularization that implicitly internalizes data manifold constraints via kNN-based density estimation.

Result: Theoretically, the regularization is proved to be first-order equivalent to maximizing a Bayesian posterior with a valid design prior. Empirically, SPADE achieves state-of-the-art performance across Design-Bench tasks and an LLM data mixture optimization benchmark.

Conclusion: SPADE offers a promising new approach to offline black-box optimization by effectively combining the strengths of forward modeling with robust uncertainty quantification through diffusion models enhanced with calibration and regularization, overcoming limitations of both inverse and forward methods.

Abstract: Offline black-box optimization aims to discover novel designs with high property scores using only a static dataset, a task fundamentally challenged by the out-of-distribution (OOD) extrapolation problem. Existing approaches typically bifurcate into inverse methods, which struggle with the ill-posed nature of mapping scores to designs, and forward methods, which often lack the distributional expressivity to quantify uncertainty effectively. In this work, we propose SPADE (Support-Proximity Augmented Diffusion Estimation), a novel framework that reimagines forward surrogate modeling through the lens of conditional generative modeling. SPADE models the forward likelihood p(y|x) using a diffusion model, but with two critical enhancements to tailor it for optimization: (1) a Calibrated Diffusion Estimation module that enforces global consistency in statistical moments and pairwise rankings, and (2) a Support-Proximity Regularization mechanism that implicitly internalizes the data manifold constraint p(x) via kNN-based density estimation. Theoretically, we prove that our regularization is first-order equivalent to maximizing a Bayesian posterior with a valid design prior. Empirically, SPADE achieves state-of-the-art performance across Design-Bench tasks and an LLM data mixture optimization benchmark.

</details>


### [174] [A Proof-of-Concept Simulation-Driven Digital Twin Framework for Decision-Aware Diabetes Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11247)
*Zarrin Monirzadeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A proof-of-concept digital twin framework for diabetes modeling using clinical data and synthetic augmentation to generate interpretable simulated trajectories rather than clinically validated predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a foundation for simulation-driven digital twin systems in healthcare by focusing on generating interpretable simulated trajectories for diabetes management, addressing limitations of traditional predictive models that may lack explainability.

Method: Developed a digital twin framework using benchmark clinical data, synthetic temporal augmentation, and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) analysis. Evaluation was conducted using a public dataset combined with controlled synthetic scenarios to illustrate temporal behavior and intervention effects.

Result: The framework demonstrates feasibility of integrating prediction with counterfactual simulation for decision-aware analysis. Results illustrate the framework's capability to generate simulated trajectories showing temporal behavior and intervention effects, though not clinically validated.

Conclusion: This proof-of-concept work establishes a foundation for future research on simulation-driven digital twin systems in healthcare, emphasizing interpretable simulated trajectories rather than immediate clinical application, while demonstrating the potential for integrating prediction with counterfactual simulation for diabetes management.

Abstract: This paper presents a proof-of-concept digital twin framework for simulation-driven diabetes modeling using benchmark clinical data, synthetic temporal augmentation, and illustrative continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) analysis. Unlike traditional predictive models, the framework focuses on generating interpretable simulated trajectories rather than clinically validated outcomes. Evaluation is conducted using a public dataset combined with controlled synthetic scenarios to illustrate temporal behavior and intervention effects. Results illustrate the feasibility of integrating prediction with counterfactual simulation for decision-aware analysis. This work does not claim clinical readiness but provides a foundation for future research on simulation-driven digital twin systems in healthcare.

</details>


### [175] [Curriculum Learning-Guided Progressive Distillation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11260)
*Jincheng Cao,Fanzhi Zeng,Leqi Liu,Aryan Mokhtari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CLPD is a novel knowledge distillation framework that aligns data difficulty with teacher strength through explicit curriculum learning and progressive teacher scheduling to overcome limitations in traditional distillation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distillation approaches overlook two critical factors: learning order of training data and capacity mismatch between teacher and student models, which causes stronger teachers to sometimes produce worse students - a counter-intuitive phenomenon that limits distillation performance.

Method: Proposes Curriculum Learning-Guided Progressive Distillation (CLPD) - a unified framework with explicit curriculum organizing training examples from easy to hard, and implicit curriculum scheduling teachers of increasing capacity progressively. The framework is modular and integrates into standard distillation algorithms.

Result: Empirical results on reasoning benchmarks show CLPD consistently outperforms standard distillation, data ordering alone, and teacher scheduling alone across multiple settings, demonstrating the importance of joint consideration of data ordering and teacher capacity.

Conclusion: Jointly considering data ordering and teacher capacity is crucial for effectively distilling reasoning abilities into small language models, and CLPD provides an effective modular framework to achieve this alignment.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation is a key technique for transferring the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) into smaller, more efficient student models. Existing distillation approaches often overlook two critical factors: the learning order of training data and the capacity mismatch between teacher and student models. This oversight limits distillation performance, as manifested by the counter-intuitive phenomenon where stronger teachers fail to produce better students. In this work, we propose Curriculum Learning-Guided Progressive Distillation (CLPD), a unified framework that explicitly accounts for both factors by aligning data difficulty with teacher strength. CLPD constructs an explicit curriculum by organizing training examples from easy to hard, while simultaneously applying an implicit curriculum over supervision signals by progressively scheduling teachers of increasing capacity. Our framework is modular and can be integrated into standard distillation algorithms with minimal overhead. Empirical results on the reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that CLPD consistently outperforms standard distillation, data ordering alone, and teacher scheduling alone across multiple settings. These findings highlight the importance of jointly considering data ordering and teacher capacity when distilling reasoning abilities into small language models.

</details>


### [176] [Latent Chain-of-Thought Improves Structured-Data Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11262)
*Carson Dudley,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) with recurrent feedback tokens improves structured data (time-series/tabular) prediction by enabling multiple rounds of latent computation before final prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the success of chain-of-thought and test-time compute in enhancing language model reasoning, the paper explores applying similar latent CoT mechanisms with depth and looping to time-series and tabular data.

Method: Proposes a recurrent scheme where a structured-data transformer compresses query-position hidden states into feedback tokens after initial forward pass, appends them to input for reprocessing, enabling multiple latent computation rounds before prediction. Compares against: same-depth no-CoT baseline, deeper baseline matched to CoT effective depth, and looped transformer with weight-tied recurrence but no CoT tokens.

Result: Across 36 datasets: latent CoT improves over baseline on 8/9 time-series datasets (+10.99% avg gain) and 22/27 tabular datasets (+5.31% avg gain). CoT models perform best on average across both settings.

Conclusion: Chain-of-thought is a useful axis for scaling test-time compute for structured data, demonstrating that latent CoT mechanisms can significantly improve performance in time-series forecasting and tabular prediction tasks.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought and more broadly test-time compute are known to augment the expressive capabilities of language models and have led to major innovations in reasoning. Motivated by this success, this paper explores latent chain-of-thought as well as the impact of depth and looping for time-series and tabular data. We propose a recurrent scheme in which a structured-data transformer, after an initial forward pass, compresses its query-position hidden states into feedback tokens that are appended to the input and processed again, allowing multiple rounds of latent computation before prediction. We compare CoT models against a same-depth no-CoT baseline, a deeper baseline matched to the CoT model in effective depth, and a looped transformer with weight-tied recurrence but no additional chain-of-thought tokens. Across 36 datasets in time-series forecasting and tabular prediction, latent chain-of-thought improves over the baseline on 8/9 time-series datasets (+10.99\% average gain) and 22/27 tabular datasets (+5.31\% average gain). Across both settings, the CoT models perform the best on average. These results demonstrate that chain-of-thought is a useful axis for scaling test-time compute for structured data.

</details>


### [177] [Localization Boosting for Growth Markets: Mitigating Cross-Locale Behavioral Bias in Learning-to-Rank](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11272)
*Suryaa Veerabathiran Seran,Ashwin Naresh Kumar,Tracy Holloway King,Jing Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adobe Express uses a multi-objective ranking framework combining behavioral signals, VLM relevance labels, and locale-aware boosting to improve template ranking across international locales while preserving local content visibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adobe Express's international expansion faces content imbalance issues where US-centric content dominates due to disproportionate supply and interaction volume. This creates cross-locale exposure bias where US-popular templates are over-served in non-US locales, suppressing local content discoverability and degrading ranking quality in growth markets.

Method: The paper proposes a multi-objective learning-to-rank framework that combines three components: 1) behavioral supervision from user clicks, 2) vision-language model (VLM)-derived relevance labels as auxiliary semantic supervision, and 3) locale-aware boosting to address exposure bias. The approach disentangles exposure effects from semantic relevance to better serve diverse locales.

Result: Across five locales, the resulting model improves relevance while restoring stable localization. The method demonstrates that click-only training suppresses localization features, while adding VLM relevance labels improves semantic alignment but doesn't preserve local content visibility without locale-aware boosting.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates the importance of disentangling exposure effects from semantic supervision in international ranking systems. Successful localization requires combining behavioral signals, semantic relevance, and explicit locale-aware interventions to serve both relevant content and maintain local content visibility.

Abstract: Adobe Express is expanding internationally, but the US has a disproportionately large content supply and interaction volume. Learning-to-rank (LTR) models trained primarily on behavioral feedback inherit this imbalance: templates popular in US are over-served in non-US locales. This cross-locale exposure bias suppresses local content discoverability and degrades ranking quality in growth locales.
  We show that click-only training suppresses semantically informative localization features. Adding vision-language model (VLM) graded relevance labels as auxiliary supervision alongside clicks improves semantic alignment but does not preserve local content visibility. We propose a multi-objective framework combining behavioral supervision, VLM-derived relevance signals, and locale-aware boosting. Across five locales, the resulting model improves relevance while restoring stable localization, demonstrating the importance of disentangling exposure from semantic supervision.

</details>


### [178] [Beyond Similarity: Temporal Operator Attention for Time Series Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11287)
*Jevon Twitty,Vinh Pham,Nitiwith Rotchanarak,Viresh Pati,Yubin Kim,Shihao Yang,Jiecheng Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer attention's convex combination limitation in time-series forecasting is solved by augmenting it with learnable sequence-space operators, enabling signed mixing and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite high capacity, Transformers often underperform simple MLP/linear models in time-series forecasting. This paradox arises from attention's fundamental limitation: it forms outputs as convex combinations (simplex-constrained mixing), which cannot represent signed and oscillatory transformations needed for temporal signal processing and global temporal operators.

Method: Proposes Temporal Operator Attention (TOA), which augments standard attention with explicit, learnable sequence-space operators to enable direct signed mixing across time while preserving input-dependent adaptivity. Introduces Stochastic Operator Regularization - a high-variance dropout mechanism to stabilize training and prevent trivial memorization when using dense N×N operators.

Result: TOA consistently improves performance across forecasting, anomaly detection, and classification benchmarks when integrated into standard backbones like PatchTST and iTransformer. Shows particularly strong gains in reconstruction-heavy tasks, demonstrating explicit operator learning as effective for time-series modeling.

Conclusion: The simplex constraint in softmax attention creates a bottleneck for representing global temporal operators. Augmenting attention with explicit, learnable sequence-space operators addresses this limitation and is a key ingredient for effective time-series modeling, especially for operator-driven tasks.

Abstract: A persistent paradox in time-series forecasting is that structurally simple MLP and linear models often outperform high-capacity Transformers. We argue that this gap arises from a mismatch in the sequence-modeling primitive: while many time-series dynamics are governed by global temporal operators (e.g., filtering and harmonic structure), standard attention forms each output as a convex combination of inputs. This restricts its ability to represent signed and oscillatory transformations that are fundamental to temporal signal processing. We formalize this limitation as a simplex-constrained mixing bottleneck in softmax attention, which becomes especially restrictive for operator-driven time-series tasks. To address this, we propose $\textbf{Temporal Operator Attention (TOA)}$, a framework that augments attention with explicit, learnable sequence-space operators, enabling direct signed mixing across time while preserving input-dependent adaptivity. To make dense $N \times N$ operators practical, we introduce Stochastic Operator Regularization, a high-variance dropout mechanism that stabilizes training and prevents trivial memorization. Across forecasting, anomaly detection, and classification benchmarks, TOA consistently improves performance when integrated into standard backbones such as PatchTST and iTransformer, with particularly strong gains in reconstruction-heavy tasks. These results suggest that explicit operator learning is a key ingredient for effective time-series modeling.

</details>


### [179] [Quotient-Categorical Representations for Bellman-Compatible Average-Reward Distributional Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11289)
*Ege C. Kaya,Aliasghar Pourghani,Vijay Gupta,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A quotient-space formulation for average-reward distributional RL that addresses the translational ambiguity of bias functions by working in a quotient-categorical space where distributions are identified up to common translations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
