  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hierarchical federated learning methods lack mechanisms for jointly optimizing cluster assignment and client selection under data heterogeneity, limiting their effectiveness in edge computing environments with non-IID data distributions.

Method: Proposes Fed-BAC with additive cluster personalization and two-level bandit framework: (1) contextual bandits at cloud level learn server-to-cluster assignments, (2) Thompson Sampling at each edge server identifies high-contributing clients, and (3) additive decomposition enables knowledge sharing through globally aggregated networks while cluster-specific networks capture distribution variations.

Result: Fed-BAC achieves distributed accuracy gains up to +35.5pp over HierFAVG and +8.4pp over IFCA, converges 1.5-4.8× faster, requires only 80% client participation, improves cross-server fairness, and shows increased advantage with higher heterogeneity severity across CIFAR-10, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST benchmarks under Dirichlet non-IID partitioning.

Conclusion: Fed-BAC effectively addresses data heterogeneity in hierarchical federated learning through joint optimization of cluster assignment and client selection, demonstrating that additive cluster personalization becomes increasingly valuable as data distributions diverge, with benefits validated at scale.

Abstract: Hierarchical federated learning (HFL) leverages edge servers for partial aggregation in edge computing. Yet existing FL methods lack mechanisms for jointly optimizing cluster assignment and client selection under data heterogeneity. This paper proposes Fed-BAC, which integrates additive cluster personalization with a two-level bandit framework: contextual bandits at the cloud learn server-to-cluster assignments, while Thompson Sampling at each edge server identifies high-contributing clients. The additive decomposition enables the sharing of knowledge between groups through a globally aggregated network, while cluster-specific networks capture distribution variations. Across three classification benchmarks (CIFAR-10, SVHN, Fashion-MNIST) under moderate ($α= 0.5$) and severe ($α= 0.1$) Dirichlet non-IID partitioning, Fed-BAC achieves distributed accuracy gains of up to +35.5pp over HierFAVG and +8.4pp over IFCA, while requiring only 80% client participation, converging 1.5 to 4.8$\times$ faster depending on dataset and accuracy target, and improving cross-server fairness. These gains are further validated at 5$\times$ deployment scale on CIFAR-10. The advantage of Fed-BAC increases with heterogeneity severity, confirming that additive cluster personalization becomes increasingly valuable as data distributions diverge.

</details>


### [255] [More Edits, More Stable: Understanding the Lifelong Normalization in Sequential Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11836)
*Xin Ma,Wei Chen,Qi Liu,Derong Xu,Zhi Zheng,Tong Xu,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces StableEdit, a method for lifelong model editing in LLMs that uses Lifelong Normalization and a stability loop to prevent catastrophic forgetting and model collapse during continuous fact updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current lifelong model editing approaches suffer from catastrophic forgetting and model collapse when continuously updating evolving facts in LLMs, and while existing methods use Lifelong Normalization, its mechanism remains a "black box" that's poorly understood theoretically.

Method: The authors provide the first theoretical analysis of Lifelong Normalization, revealing a self-reinforcing stability loop. They prove that combined with ridge-regularized regression, LN yields orthogonal parameter updates with bounded norms. Based on these insights, they develop StableEdit which strengthens the stability loop via explicit warm-up and full whitening.

Result: Extensive experiments validate the theory and demonstrate competitive performance in lifelong model editing. StableEdit improves long-horizon stability at minimal overhead while mitigating forgetting and systemic collapse.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first theoretical understanding of Lifelong Normalization's role in lifelong model editing stability, leading to the practical StableEdit method that effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting and model collapse through its stability loop mechanism.

Abstract: Lifelong Model Editing aims to continuously update evolving facts in Large Language Models while preserving unrelated knowledge and general capabilities, yet it remains plagued by catastrophic forgetting and model collapse. Empirically, we find that recent editors resilient over long horizons share the same core strategy: Lifelong Normalization (LN), which normalizes value gradients using running statistics. Removing LN causes immediate performance collapse, and we observe a counter-intuitive positive cumulative effect where early edits can promote the success of future edits. Yet the mechanism of LN remains a "black box", leaving its precise role in lifelong stability poorly understood. In this work, we provide the first theoretical account of LN in the lifelong regime. Our analysis reveals a self-reinforcing stability loop and proves that, when combined with ridge-regularized regression, LN yields parameter updates with asymptotic orthogonality and bounded norms, directly mitigating forgetting and systemic collapse. Based on these insights, we derive StableEdit, which strengthens this stability loop via an explicit warm-up stage and full whitening, improving long-horizon stability at minimal overhead. Extensive experiments validate our theory and demonstrate competitive performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/MINE-USTC/StableEdit.

</details>


### [256] [Gradient Clipping Beyond Vector Norms: A Spectral Approach for Matrix-Valued Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11838)
*Alexander Yukhimchuk,Mladen Kolar,Martin Takáč,Sayantan Choudhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral clipping: A novel gradient clipping method that stabilizes neural network training by clipping only large singular values of layer-wise gradient matrices, preserving singular directions and offering efficient implementation via randomized truncated SVD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard gradient clipping treats all parameters as vectors, ignoring the matrix structure of modern neural network architectures. The authors observed empirically that data outliers often amplify only a small number of leading singular values in layer-wise gradient matrices while leaving the rest of the spectrum largely unchanged.

Method: 1. Proposed spectral clipping which stabilizes training by clamping singular values that exceed a threshold while preserving singular directions. 2. Introduced layer-wise adaptive thresholds based on moving averages or sliding-window quantiles of top singular values to minimize hyperparameter tuning. 3. Developed efficient implementations using randomized truncated SVD to clip only top r singular values, avoiding full decompositions for large layers.

Result: 1. Provided convergence analysis for non-convex optimization with spectrally clipped SGD, achieving optimal O(K^{(2-2α)/(3α-2)}) rate for heavy-tailed noise. 2. Demonstrated competitive performance across synthetic heavy-tailed settings and neural network training tasks. 3. The framework generalizes classical gradient norm clipping and can be easily integrated into existing optimizers.

Conclusion: Spectral clipping is an effective alternative to traditional gradient clipping that respects the matrix structure of neural networks, offering both theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency through adaptive thresholds and randomized SVD implementations.

Abstract: Gradient clipping is a standard safeguard for training neural networks under noisy, heavy-tailed stochastic gradients; yet, most clipping rules treat all parameters as vectors and ignore the matrix structure of modern architectures. We show empirically that data outliers often amplify only a small number of leading singular values in layer-wise gradient matrices, while the rest of the spectrum remains largely unchanged. Motivated by this phenomenon, we propose spectral clipping, which stabilizes training by clamping singular values that exceed a threshold while preserving the singular directions. This framework generalizes classical gradient norm clipping and can be easily integrated into existing optimizers. We provide a convergence analysis for non-convex optimization with spectrally clipped SGD, yielding the optimal $\mathcal{O}\left(K^{\frac{2 - 2α}{3α- 2}}\right)$ rate for heavy-tailed noise. To minimize hyperparameter tuning, we introduce layer-wise adaptive thresholds based on moving averages or sliding-window quantiles of the top singular values. Finally, we develop efficient implementations that clip only the top $r$ singular values via randomized truncated SVD, avoiding full decompositions for large layers. We demonstrate competitive performance across synthetic heavy-tailed settings and neural network training tasks.

</details>


### [257] [Martingale-Consistent Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11846)
*Moritz Gögl,Hanwen Xing,Christopher Yau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a martingale-consistent self-supervised learning framework that ensures predictions from coarse and refined views remain coherent under changing information, improving robustness and calibration in partial-observation scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised learning often operates under changing information conditions (shorter histories, missing features, partial observations), but standard SSL objectives don't enforce coherence between predictions from coarse and refined views, which should match in expectation as information is revealed.

Method: Proposes a martingale-consistent SSL framework with practical prediction-space and latent-space variants, using an unbiased two-sample Monte Carlo estimator based on stochastic refinement to enforce that coarse-view predictions match the average expected refined predictions.

Result: Evaluation on synthetic and real time-series, tabular, and image benchmarks under partial-observation regimes shows the framework improves robustness and calibration, yields more stable representations as information is revealed, and works in both semi-self-supervised and fully label-free settings.

Conclusion: Martingale consistency provides a principled way to enforce coherence between coarse and refined views in SSL, addressing limitations of standard invariance objectives by preventing systematic drift while allowing predictions to update naturally as information is revealed, leading to more robust models for partial-observation scenarios.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is often deployed under changing information, such as shorter histories, missing features, or partially observed images. In these settings, predictions from coarse and refined views should be coherent: before refinement, the coarse-view prediction should match the average prediction expected after refinement. Martingales formalize this coherence principle, but standard SSL objectives do not enforce it. Unlike invariance objectives that pull views together, martingale consistency constrains only the expected refined prediction, allowing predictions to update as information is revealed while preventing systematic drift. We introduce a martingale-consistent SSL framework that closes this gap, with practical prediction- and latent-space variants and an unbiased two-sample Monte Carlo estimator based on stochastic refinement. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and real time-series, tabular, and image benchmarks under partial-observation regimes, in both semi-self-supervised and fully label-free settings. Across these experiments, our framework improves robustness and calibration under partial observation, yielding more stable representations as information is revealed.

</details>


### [258] [GEAR: Granularity-Adaptive Advantage Reweighting for LLM Agents via Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11853)
*Sijia Li,Yuchen Huang,Zifan Liu,Yanping Li,Jingjing Fu,Li Zhao,Jiang Bian,Ling Zhang,Jun Zhang,Rui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEAR is an adaptive-granularity credit assignment framework that improves RL for LLM agents by using self-distillation to identify semantic deviation points and create adaptive credit regions for more effective policy updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL for LLM agents relies on outcome-level rewards with coarse supervision, making fine-grained credit assignment challenging. Obtaining reliable local credit and assigning it to the right parts of long-horizon trajectories remains an open problem.

Method: GEAR uses self-distillation to compare an on-policy student with a ground-truth-conditioned teacher to obtain reference-guided divergence signals. It identifies adaptive segment boundaries where divergence spikes at semantic deviations and modulates local advantage weights. The framework preserves token-level resolution where student remains aligned with teacher, but groups departures into adaptive segments with divergence-based advantage weighting.

Result: Experiments across eight mathematical reasoning and agentic tool-use benchmarks with Qwen3 4B and 8B models show GEAR consistently outperforms standard GRPO, self-distillation-only baselines, and token- or turn-level credit-assignment methods. Gains are especially strong on benchmarks with lower GRPO baseline accuracy, reaching up to around 20% improvement over GRPO.

Conclusion: GEAR's adaptive granularity approach effectively addresses the credit assignment challenge in long-horizon RL for LLM agents, demonstrating that adaptive reweighting schemes are particularly useful in more challenging long-horizon settings where standard methods struggle.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a widely used post-training approach for LLM agents, where training commonly relies on outcome-level rewards that provide only coarse supervision. While finer-grained credit assignment is promising for effective policy updates, obtaining reliable local credit and assigning it to the right parts of the long-horizon trajectory remains an open challenge. In this paper, we propose Granularity-adaptivE Advantage Reweighting (GEAR), an adaptive-granularity credit assignment framework that reshapes the trajectory-level GRPO advantage using token- and segment-level signals derived from self-distillation. GEAR compares an on-policy student with a ground-truth-conditioned teacher to obtain a reference-guided divergence signal for identifying adaptive segment boundaries and modulating local advantage weights. This divergence often spikes at the onset of a semantic deviation, while later tokens in the same autoregressive continuation may return to low divergence. GEAR therefore treats such spikes as anchors for adaptive credit regions: where the student remains aligned with the teacher, token-level resolution is preserved; where it departs, GEAR groups the corresponding continuation into an adaptive segment and uses the divergence at the departure point to modulate the segment' s advantage. Experiments across eight mathematical reasoning and agentic tool-use benchmarks with Qwen3 4B and 8B models show that GEAR consistently outperforms standard GRPO, self-distillation-only baselines, and token- or turn-level credit-assignment methods. The gains are especially strong on benchmarks with lower GRPO baseline accuracy, reaching up to around 20\% over GRPO, suggesting that the proposed adaptive reweighting scheme is especially useful in more challenging long-horizon settings.

</details>


### [259] [Improving the Performance and Learning Stability of Parallelizable RNNs Designed for Ultra-Low Power Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11855)
*Julien Brandoit,Arthur Fyon,Damien Ernst,Guillaume Drion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Cumulative Memory Recurrent Units (CMRU) that fix gradient flow issues in BMRU RNNs while preserving persistent memory, enabling better performance on long-range sequential tasks while maintaining hardware-friendly properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Transformers and parallelizable RNNs (like state-space models) dominate sequence learning, they struggle with long-term dependencies and trade power consumption for performance. The BMRU was designed for ultra-low power hardware but underperforms on complex tasks due to gradient blocking during state updates.

Method: The authors identify gradient blocking in BMRU state updates as the key limitation and propose a cumulative update formulation that restores gradient flow while preserving persistent memory. This creates skip-connections through time, leading to CMRU and its relaxed variant αCMRU.

Result: The cumulative formulation dramatically improves convergence stability and reduces initialization sensitivity. CMRU and αCMRU match or outperform Linear Recurrent Units (LRUs) and minimal Gated Recurrent Units (minGRUs) across diverse benchmarks at small model sizes, especially on tasks requiring discrete long-range retention.

Conclusion: CMRU maintains the hardware-friendly properties of BMRU (quantized states, persistent memory, noise-resilient dynamics essential for analog implementation) while fixing gradient issues, making it a promising approach for ultra-low power RNNs that can handle long-range dependencies effectively.

Abstract: Sequence learning is dominated by Transformers and parallelizable recurrent neural networks (RNNs) such as state-space models, yet learning long-term dependencies remains challenging, and state-of-the-art designs trade power consumption for performance. The Bistable Memory Recurrent Unit (BMRU) was introduced to enable hardware-software co-design of ultra-low power RNNs: quantized states with hysteresis provide persistent memory while mapping directly to analog primitives. However, BMRU performance lags behind parallelizable RNNs on complex sequential tasks. In this paper, we identify gradient blocking during state updates as a key limitation and propose a cumulative update formulation that restores gradient flow while preserving persistent memory, creating skip-connections through time. This leads to the Cumulative Memory Recurrent Unit (CMRU) and its relaxed variant, the $α$CMRU. Experiments show that the cumulative formulation dramatically improves convergence stability and reduces initialization sensitivity. The CMRU and $α$CMRU match or outperform Linear Recurrent Units (LRUs) and minimal Gated Recurrent Units (minGRUs) across diverse benchmarks at small model sizes, with particular advantages on tasks requiring discrete long-range retention, while the CMRU retains quantized states, persistent memory, and noise-resilient dynamics essential for analog implementation.

</details>


### [260] [Beyond Parameter Aggregation: Semantic Consensus for Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11857)
*Amr Abourayya,Jens Kleesiek,Michael Kamp*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated LLM fine-tuning through behavior consensus instead of parameter aggregation, enabling architecture-agnostic collaboration with dramatically reduced communication costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current federated fine-tuning approaches require transmitting large trainable weights, assume aligned architectures, and need white-box access to parameters. These assumptions become impractical as models grow larger and deployments become more heterogeneous.

Method: Clients fine-tune models locally on private data and exchange generated outputs on a shared public prompt set. The server maps outputs to semantic representation space, forms per-prompt semantic consensus, and returns pseudo-labels for further local fine-tuning.

Result: The approach matches strong federated fine-tuning baselines while reducing communication by orders of magnitude (e.g., 1006× for Llama3.1-405B), with reductions in runtime and energy consumption.

Conclusion: Behavior-level consensus provides a more appropriate abstraction for federated adaptation of generative foundation models than parameter aggregation, addressing practical constraints of large-scale heterogeneous deployments.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning of large language models is commonly formulated as a parameter aggregation problem. However, even parameter-efficient methods require transmitting large collections of trainable weights, assume aligned architectures, and rely on white-box access to model parameters. As model sizes continue to grow and deployments become increasingly heterogeneous, these assumptions become progressively misaligned with practical constraints. We consider an alternative formulation in which collaboration is mediated through model behavior rather than parameters. Clients fine-tune local models on private data and exchange generated outputs on a shared, public prompt set. The server maps these outputs into a semantic representation space, forms a per-prompt semantic consensus, and returns pseudo-labels for further local fine-tuning. This formulation fundamentally changes the communication scaling of federated LLM fine-tuning. The amount of information exchanged depends only on the public prompt budget and the size of the communicated behaviors, independent of model size. As a consequence, the protocol naturally accommodates heterogeneous architectures and applies directly to open-ended text generation. We present a theoretical analysis and empirical results demonstrating that this approach can match strong federated fine-tuning baselines while substantially reducing communication by orders of magnitude (e.g., analytically by a factor of $1006$ for Llama3.1-405B), as well as reductions in runtime and energy consumption. These results suggest that, for generative foundation models, behavior-level consensus provides a more appropriate abstraction for federated adaptation than parameter aggregation.

</details>


### [261] [Information theoretic underpinning of self-supervised learning by clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11870)
*Josef Kittler,Sara Atito,Muhammad Awais*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a theoretical foundation for self-supervised learning (SSL) by formulating SSL as K-L divergence optimization with constraints to prevent mode collapse, showing that common SSL heuristics like distillation and centering have theoretical underpinnings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is essential for building foundation AI models, but while there have been vigorous arguments and empirical research about SSL principles, there is a need to develop the theoretical underpinnings of SSL. The paper specifically focuses on providing theoretical foundations for deep clustering approaches in SSL.

Method: The authors formulate SSL as K-L (Kullback-Leibler) divergence optimization, similar to supervised learning. To prevent mode collapse, they impose an optimization constraint on the teacher distribution, which leads to normalization using inverse cluster priors. Using Jensen inequality, they show this normalization simplifies to the popular batch centering procedure.

Result: The theoretical model developed in the paper provides a mathematical foundation for common SSL practices. It shows that distillation and centering procedures, which are commonly used as heuristics in SSL, actually have solid theoretical underpinnings derived from the K-L divergence optimization framework with constraints to prevent mode collapse.

Conclusion: The paper successfully contributes to the theoretical development of SSL by providing a formal mathematical framework that not only explains why existing successful SSL methods work but also suggests directions for future theoretical investigations in self-supervised learning.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is recognized as an essential tool for building foundation models for Artificial Intelligence applications. The advances in SSL have been made thanks to vigorous arguments about the principles of SSL and through extensive empirical research. The aim of this paper is to contribute to the development of the underpinning theory of SSL, focusing on the deep clustering approach. By analogy to supervised learning, we formulate SSL as K-L divergence optimization.
  The mode collapse is prevented by imposing an optimisation constraint on the teacher distribution. This leads to normalization using inverse cluster priors. We show that using Jensen inequality this normalization simplifies to the popular batch centering procedure. Distillation and centering are common {heuristics-based} practices in SSL, {but our work underpins them theoretically.} The theoretical model developed not only supports specific existing successful SSL methods, but also suggests directions for future investigations.

</details>


### [262] [LOFT: Low-Rank Orthogonal Fine-Tuning via Task-Aware Support Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11872)
*Lanxin Zhao,Bamdev Mishra,Pratik Jawanpuria,Lequan Lin,Dai Shi,Junbin Gao,Andi Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOFT is a low-rank orthogonal fine-tuning framework that separates subspace selection from transformation design, enabling principled task-aware support selection for improved PEFT efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing orthogonal PEFT methods conflate two design choices: the adaptation subspace and the transformation within that subspace. This lack of separation prevents principled support selection and limits optimization of the efficiency-performance trade-off.

Method: LOFT frames orthogonal adaptation as multiplicative subspace rotation, providing a unified formulation that recovers existing methods. It introduces first-order analysis to show adaptation supports should be task-aware, and develops practical support selection strategies based on downstream training signals.

Result: LOFT recovers principal-subspace orthogonal adaptation while gradient-informed supports improve efficiency-performance trade-offs across language understanding, visual transfer, mathematical reasoning, and multilingual out-of-distribution adaptation tasks.

Conclusion: Principled support selection is crucial for improving orthogonal PEFT, and separating subspace selection from transformation design enables more efficient and effective parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Abstract: Orthogonal parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) adapts pretrained weights through structure-preserving multiplicative transformations, but existing methods often conflate two distinct design choices: the subspace in which adaptation occurs and the transformation applied within that subspace. This paper introduces LOFT, a low-rank orthogonal fine-tuning framework that explicitly separates these two components. By viewing orthogonal adaptation as a multiplicative subspace rotation, LOFT provides a unified formulation that recovers representative orthogonal PEFT methods, including coordinate-, butterfly-, Householder-, and principal-subspace-based variants. More importantly, this perspective exposes support selection as a central design axis rather than a byproduct of a particular parameterization. We develop a first-order analysis showing that useful adaptation supports should be informed by the downstream training signal, motivating practical task-aware support selection strategies. Across language understanding, visual transfer, mathematical reasoning, and multilingual out-of-distribution adaptation, LOFT recovers principal-subspace orthogonal adaptation while gradient-informed supports improve the efficiency-performance trade-off under matched parameter, memory, and compute budgets. These results suggest that principled support selection is an important direction for improving orthogonal PEFT.

</details>


### [263] [Sobolev Regularized MMD Gradient Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11884)
*Chenyang Tian,Bharath K. Sriperumbudur,Arthur Gretton,Zonghao Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sobolev-regularized MMD gradient flow improves convergence by adding gradient penalty to witness function, works for both generative modeling and sampling without isoperimetric assumptions, with proven global convergence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address limitations of MMD gradient flow: 1) non-convexity of MMD objective leading to poor convergence, 2) existing gradient flows are specialized to either generative modeling OR sampling but not both, 3) previous convergence analyses require restrictive isoperimetric assumptions on target distributions.

Method: Proposes Sobolev-regularized Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SrMMD) gradient flow, which adds a gradient penalty on the witness function to regularize the optimization. Uses kernel mean embeddings and Stein kernels for sampling applications. The method provides provable global convergence guarantees in both continuous and discrete time settings.

Result: Theoretical: Proves global convergence guarantees without needing isoperimetric assumptions on target distributions, only requiring a regularity condition on kernel mean embedding differences. Empirical: Demonstrates effectiveness on diverse tasks in both generative modeling and sampling applications, showing the flow's versatility.

Conclusion: SrMMD gradient flow is a unified framework that overcomes limitations of previous MMD-based methods by providing provable global convergence, working for both generative modeling and sampling tasks, and eliminating restrictive isoperimetric assumptions while maintaining empirical effectiveness.

Abstract: We propose Sobolev-regularized Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SrMMD) gradient flow, a regularized variant of maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) gradient flow based on a gradient penalty on the witness function. The proposed regularization mitigates the non-convexity of the MMD objective and yields provable \emph{global} convergence guarantees in MMD in both continuous and discrete time. A more surprising appeal is that our convergence analysis does not rely on isoperimetric assumptions on the target distribution. Instead, it is based on a regularity condition on the difference between kernel mean embeddings. A key highlight of the proposed flow is that it is applicable in both sampling (from an unnormalized target distribution) -- using Stein kernels -- and generative modeling settings, unlike previous works, where a gradient flow is suitable for only generative modeling or sampling but not both. The effectiveness of the proposed flow is empirically verified on a broad range of tasks in both generative modelling and sampling.

</details>


### [264] [Incentivizing Truthfulness and Collaborative Fairness in Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11889)
*Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,Jue Fan,Xiao Tian,Xinyi Xu,Patrick Jaillet,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First mechanism that ensures collaborative fairness (F) and incentivizes truthfulness (T) for data valuation in collaborative machine learning by combining semivalues with a truthful data valuation function using an unknown validation set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data valuation methods for collaborative ML incentivize data sharing but fail to verify or incentivize data truthfulness, allowing sources to manipulate their data (e.g., submit duplicated or noisy data) to artificially increase their valuations/rewards or prevent others from benefiting.

Method: Combines semivalues (e.g., Shapley value) for fairness with a truthful data valuation function (DVF) based on a validation set unknown to the sources. Introduces additional conditions to prove sources maximize expected data values by submitting datasets capturing true knowledge, even though semivalues are influenced by others' data.

Result: Theoretical mechanism provably ensures collaborative fairness (F) and incentivizes truthfulness (T) at equilibrium for Bayesian models. Validated on synthetic and real-world datasets. Also discusses implications and relaxations of F and T when mediator has limited budget or lacks validation set.

Conclusion: Presents first mechanism addressing both fairness and truthfulness in collaborative machine learning data valuation, overcoming limitations of existing methods that allow data manipulation while maintaining fairness guarantees.

Abstract: Collaborative machine learning involves training high-quality models using datasets from a number of sources. To incentivize sources to share data, existing data valuation methods fairly reward each source based on its data submitted as is. However, as these methods do not verify nor incentivize data truthfulness, the sources can manipulate their data (e.g., by submitting duplicated or noisy data) to artificially increase their valuations and rewards or prevent others from benefiting. This paper presents the first mechanism that provably ensures (F) collaborative fairness and incentivizes (T) truthfulness at equilibrium for Bayesian models. Our mechanism combines semivalues (e.g., Shapley value), which ensure fairness, and a truthful data valuation function (DVF) based on a validation set that is unknown to the sources. As semivalues are influenced by others' data, we introduce an additional condition to prove that a source can maximize its expected data values in coalitions and semivalues by submitting a dataset that captures its true knowledge. Additionally, we discuss the implications and suitable relaxations of (F) and (T) when the mediator has a limited budget for rewards or lacks a validation set. Our theoretical findings are validated on synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [265] [Procedural-skill SFT across capacity tiers: A W-Shaped pre-SFT Trajectory and Regime-Asymmetric Mechanism on 0.8B-4B Qwen3.5 Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11907)
*Igor Strozzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) shows uniform procedural skill improvement across Qwen3.5 model sizes (0.8B, 2B, 4B), with SFT helping most where models struggle with procedures. Earlier findings were artifacts of evaluation methodology mismatches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how SFT contributes to procedural skill acquisition across different model sizes, and to correct earlier mischaracterizations that resulted from evaluation methodology artifacts (format compliance issues and path mismatches).

Method: Used 353 demonstrations of (task + procedural-skill block, chain-of-thought, judge-pass) on Qwen3.5 models (0.8B, 2B, 4B) with Claude Haiku 4.5 as frontier reference. Evaluated on 200-task/40-skill holdout with matched-path LLM-only scoring, correcting format compliance artifacts. Conducted negative-iteration experiments and cross-family validation with GPT-5.4.

Result: SFT-attributable procedural improvement is roughly uniform across sizes: +0.070/+0.040/+0.075 for 0.8B/2B/4B. Post-SFT variation is dominated by pre-SFT base performance. SFT works hardest where base models struggle with procedures. Earlier "format-only at 0.8B" and "shrinking SFT at 4B" findings were path-mismatch artifacts.

Conclusion: SFT provides consistent procedural skill improvement across model sizes, with the most benefit where base models have procedural difficulties. Methodology matters - proper matched-path evaluation reveals uniform SFT contributions that were previously masked by evaluation artifacts.

Abstract: We measure procedural-skill SFT contribution across three Qwen3.5 dense scales (0.8B, 2B, 4B) on a 200-task / 40-skill holdout, with Claude Haiku 4.5 as a frontier reference. The corpus is 353 rows of (task + procedural-skill block, Opus chain-of-thought, judge-pass) demonstrations.
  \textbf{Main finding.} Under matched-path LLM-only scoring, the SFT-attributable procedural-$Δ$ lift is roughly uniform across sizes: $+0.070$ / $+0.040$ / $+0.075$ at 0.8B / 2B / 4B. Variation in post-SFT $Δ$ ($-0.005$, $+0.100$, $+0.065$) is dominated by a W-shaped pre-SFT base trajectory ($-0.075$, $+0.060$, $-0.010$, Haiku-4-5 at $+0.030$): the 5-step procedure hurts 0.8B and 4B, helps 2B, and helps frontier Haiku modestly. SFT works hardest in absolute terms where the base struggles with the procedure -- a regime-asymmetric pattern with a falsifiable prediction at 8B/14B.
  \textbf{Methodology.} (i) A bench format-compliance artifact: 83.5\% of the holdout uses a deterministic \texttt{ANSWER}-line extractor that under-counts free-form conclusions; an LLM-only re-judge reveals it was systematically biased against \CU. (ii) A negative-iteration sequence at 0.8B: five recipe variants cluster post-SFT \CU{} pass-rate within a 2\,pp band, constraining the absolute-pass-rate ceiling to base capacity rather than recipe.
  \textbf{Cross-family validation.} GPT-5.4 via OpenRouter on all 7 configurations (2800 paired episodes) agrees on the direction of every per-student finding: Cohen's $κ\geq 0.754$, agreement $\geq 93.25\%$.
  Earlier ``format-only at 0.8B'' and ``shrinking SFT at 4B'' framings were path-mismatch artifacts; this paper supersedes both (Appendix~\ref{sec:appendix-path}). Single-seed; threats in §\ref{sec:threats}.

</details>


### [266] [Delightful Gradients Accelerate Corner Escape](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11908)
*Jincheng Mei,Ian Osband*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Delightful Policy Gradient (DG) addresses slow convergence near sub-optimal policies by gating gradient terms with advantage and action surprisal, achieving faster escape from corners and maintaining O(1/t) convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard softmax policy gradient converges at O(1/t) rate but suffers from exponentially slow transient behavior near sub-optimal corners of the simplex due to self-trapping, where negative-advantage actions reinforce corner policies and can push optimal actions backward.

Method: Proposes Delightful Policy Gradient (DG) which modifies policy gradient by gating each term with the product of advantage and action surprisal. Analyzes the mechanism in K-armed bandits, showing the zero-temperature limit removes corner-trapping and provides logarithmic escape bounds. Proves structural insight that every action better than the corner action is an ally with non-negative contribution to escape.

Result: Proves DG removes corner-trapping mechanism near sub-optimal corners with logarithmic escape bounds in initial probability ratio. Shows harmful actions are polynomially suppressed at fixed temperatures. Demonstrates DG converges globally to optimal policy in bandits and tabular MDPs at O(1/t) rate. Presents counterexample showing failure under shared function approximation, but empirical results in MNIST contextual bandits show DG recovers faster from bad initializations than standard policy gradient.

Conclusion: DG addresses fundamental issues with standard policy gradient's slow convergence near sub-optimal policies, providing theoretical guarantees for faster escape and maintaining asymptotic convergence rates. While theoretical limitations exist under shared function approximation, practical performance suggests these limitations mark theoretical boundaries rather than practical prohibitions.

Abstract: Softmax policy gradient converges at $O(1/t)$, but its transient behavior near sub-optimal corners of the simplex can be exponentially slow. The bottleneck is self-trapping: negative-advantage actions reinforce the corner policy and can initially push the optimal action backward. We study \emph{Delightful Policy Gradient} (DG), which gates each policy-gradient term by the product of advantage and action surprisal. For $K$-armed bandits, we prove that the zero-temperature limit of DG removes this corner-trapping mechanism on a quantitative sector near any sub-optimal corner, yielding a first-exit escape bound logarithmic in the initial probability ratio. At every fixed temperature, the same local mechanism persists because harmful actions are polynomially suppressed as they become rare. A key structural insight is that every action better than the corner action is an \emph{ally}: its contribution to escape is non-negative. Combining corner instability with a monotonic value improvement identity, we prove that DG converges globally to the optimal policy in both bandits and tabular MDPs at an asymptotic $O(1/t)$ rate. We also show, via an exact counterexample, that this tabular mechanism can fail under shared function approximation. In MNIST contextual bandits with a shared-parameter neural network, DG nevertheless recovers from bad initializations faster than standard policy gradient, suggesting that the counterexample marks a boundary of the theory rather than a practical prohibition.

</details>


### [267] [Understanding Sample Efficiency in Predictive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11911)
*Gaspard Oliviers,Elene Lominadze,Rafal Bogacz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Predictive Coding demonstrates superior learning efficiency over Backpropagation, particularly in deep/narrow/pre-trained networks, explained through "target alignment" metric and validated theoretically/empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a theoretical understanding of why Predictive Coding (PC) shows better sample efficiency and effectiveness than Backpropagation (BP) in various contexts, which has been empirically observed but not well explained.

Method: Introduced "target alignment" metric measuring how network output changes align with prediction errors; derived analytical expressions for target alignment in Deep Linear Networks; validated both theoretically and empirically; studied full training trajectories for linear and non-linear models.

Result: PC demonstrates higher learning efficiency than BP, especially in deep, narrow, and pre-trained networks; derived exact conditions for optimal target alignment in PC; benefits persist in practice even when theoretical assumptions are violated.

Conclusion: This work provides mechanistic understanding of PC's superior learning efficiency over BP and offers guidance on optimal PC parameterization for effective learning.

Abstract: Predictive Coding (PC) is an influential account of cortical learning. Much of recent work has focused on comparing PC to Backpropagation (BP) to find whether PC offers any advantages. Small scale experiments show that PC enables learning that is more sample efficient and effective in many contexts, though a thorough theoretical understanding of the phenomena remains elusive. To address this, we quantify the efficiency of learning in BP and PC through a metric called ``target alignment'', which measures how closely the change in the output of the network is aligned to the output prediction error. We then derive and empirically validate analytical expressions for target alignment in Deep Linear Networks. We show that learning in PC is more efficient than BP, which is especially pronounced in deep, narrow and pre-trained networks. We also derive exact conditions for guaranteed optimal target alignment in PC and validate our findings through experiments. We study full training trajectories of linear and non-linear models, and find the predicted benefits of PC persist in practice even when some assumptions are violated. Overall, this work provides a mechanistic understanding of the higher learning efficiency observed for PC over BP in previous works, and can guide how PC should be parametrised to learn most effectively.

</details>


### [268] [STAGE: Tackling Semantic Drift in Multimodal Federated Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11919)
*Zekai Chen,Xun Wu,Xunkai Li,Yihan Sun,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STAGE is a protocol-first framework for multimodal federated graph learning that builds a shared semantic space by translating heterogeneous multimodal features into comparable representations, improving cross-client semantic calibration while reducing communication overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As graph data increasingly contain multimodal node attributes (text, images), multimodal federated graph learning (MM-FGL) has become important but challenging. The key problem is that clients from different modality domains lack a common semantic space - their local encoders produce inconsistent representations even for the same concept before collaboration begins.

Method: STAGE is a protocol-first framework that instead of relying on direct parameter averaging, builds a shared semantic space by first translating heterogeneous multimodal features into comparable representations, then regulates how these representations propagate over local graph structures.

Result: Extensive experiments on 8 multimodal-attributed graphs across 5 graph-centric and modality-centric tasks show that STAGE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing per-round communication payload.

Conclusion: STAGE effectively addresses the multimodal heterogeneity challenge in federated graph learning by improving cross-client semantic calibration and reducing the risk of inconsistency amplification during graph learning, making it a practical solution for collaborative training on multimodal graph data.

Abstract: Federated graph learning (FGL) enables collaborative training on graph data across multiple clients. As graph data increasingly contain multimodal node attributes such as text and images, multimodal federated graph learning (MM-FGL) has become an important yet substantially harder setting. The key challenge is that clients from different modality domains may not share a common semantic space: even for the same concept, their local encoders can produce inconsistent representations before collaboration begins. This makes direct parameter coordination unreliable and further causes two downstream problems: forcing heterogeneous client representations into a naively shared semantic space may create false semantic agreement, and graph message passing may amplify residual inconsistency across neighborhoods. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{STAGE}, a protocol-first framework for MM-FGL. Instead of relying on direct parameter averaging, STAGE builds a shared semantic space that first translates heterogeneous multimodal features into comparable representations and then regulates how these representations propagate over local graph structures. In this way, STAGE not only improves cross-client semantic calibration, but also reduces the risk of inconsistency amplification during graph learning. Extensive experiments on 8 multimodal-attributed graphs across 5 graph-centric and modality-centric tasks show that STAGE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing per-round communication payload.

</details>


### [269] [NOFE -- Neural Operator Function Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11970)
*Lars Uebbing,Harald L. Joakimsen,Siyan Chen,Georgios Leontidis,Kristoffer K. Wickstrøm,Michael C. Kampffmeyer,Sébastien Lefèvre,Arnt-Børre Salberg,Robert Jenssen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NOFE is a continuous dimensionality reduction method using neural operators to learn function-to-function mappings, outperforming discrete methods like PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP in preserving local structure and maintaining sampling independence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional dimensionality reduction methods treat data as discrete point clouds, ignoring the continuous domain structure inherent in many real-world processes (like climate data, physical systems). This limitation prevents them from handling variable sampling densities and maintaining consistency across domain patches.

Method: NOFE learns function-to-function mappings via a Graph Kernel Operator, enabling mesh-free evaluation at arbitrary query locations independent of input discretization. It's established as an approximation of sheaf-to-sheaf mappings, generalizing Sheaf Neural Networks to continuous domains.

Result: NOFE significantly outperforms PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP in local structure preservation (local Stress: 0.111 vs 0.398/0.773/0.791 on ERA5 climate dataset). It shows robust sampling independence, reducing Patch Stitching Error by 20× vs UMAP (59.0 vs 267.6), and maintains competitive global structure (Stress-1: 0.379 vs PCA's 0.268).

Conclusion: NOFE addresses key limitations of discrete dimensionality reduction methods by producing smooth, consistent embeddings that generalize across varying sample densities, making it suitable for continuous-domain applications like climate modeling and physical simulations.

Abstract: Most dimensionality reduction methods treat data as discrete point clouds, ignoring the continuous domain structure inherent to many real-world processes. To bridge this gap, we introduce Neural Operator Function Embedding (NOFE), a domain-aware framework for continuous dimensionality reduction. NOFE learns function-to-function mappings via a Graph Kernel Operator, enabling mesh-free evaluation at arbitrary query locations independent of input discretization. We establish NOFE as approximation of sheaf-to-sheaf mappings, generalizing Sheaf Neural Networks to continuous domains. We evaluate NOFE across different datasets, comparing it against PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP. Our results demonstrate that NOFE significantly outperforms baselines in local structure preservation, achieving a local Stress of 0.111 compared to 0.398 for PCA, 0.773 for t-SNE, and 0.791 for UMAP for the ERA5 climate reanalysis dataset. NOFE also exhibits robust sampling independence, reducing the Patch Stitching Error by up to $20.0\times$ relative to UMAP (59.0 vs. 267.6 under regional normalization) and ensuring consistency across disjoint domain patches. While maintaining competitive global structure preservation (Stress-1: 0.379 vs. PCA's 0.268), NOFE resolves fine-grained structures and produces smooth, consistent embeddings that generalize across varying sample densities, addressing key limitations of discrete reduction methods.

</details>


### [270] [Towards Order Fairness: Mitigating LLMs Order Sensitivity through Dual Group Advantage Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11974)
*Xu Chu,Guanyu Wang,Zhijie Tan,Xinrong Chen,Ziyu Li,Tong Mo,Weiping Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DGAO uses reinforcement learning to simultaneously improve LLM accuracy and order stability by balancing intra-group accuracy advantage and inter-group stability advantage, outperforming previous methods on multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer from order bias where their performance is affected by input arrangement order, limiting applications in in-context learning and RAG. Existing methods either increase inference overhead without fixing inherent bias or sacrifice accuracy while trying to mitigate order sensitivity.

Method: Proposes Dual Group Advantage Optimization (DGAO) using reinforcement learning to calculate and balance intra-group relative accuracy advantage and inter-group relative stability advantage. Rewards order-stable correct outputs and penalizes order-sensitive or incorrect responses. Also introduces new metrics: Consistency Rate and Overconfidence Rate.

Result: Extensive experiments show DGAO achieves superior order fairness while improving performance on RAG, mathematical reasoning, and classification tasks. First reinforcement learning approach to mitigate LLM order sensitivity.

Conclusion: DGAO successfully addresses LLM order bias by simultaneously improving accuracy and order stability through reinforcement learning, outperforming previous methods that suffered from trade-offs between stability and accuracy.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from order bias, where their performance is affected by the arrangement order of input elements. This unfairness limits the model's applications in scenarios such as in-context learning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Recent studies attempt to obtain optimal or suboptimal arrangements based on statistical results or using dataset-based search, but these methods increase inference overhead while leaving the model's inherent order bias unresolved. Other studies mitigate order sensitivity through supervised fine-tuning using augmented training sets with multiple order variants, but often at the cost of accuracy, trapping the model in consistent yet incorrect hallucinations. In this paper, we propose \textbf{D}ual \textbf{G}roup \textbf{A}dvantage \textbf{O}ptimization (\textbf{DGAO}), which aims to improve model accuracy and order stability simultaneously. DGAO calculates and balances intra-group relative accuracy advantage and inter-group relative stability advantage, rewarding the policy model for generating order-stable and correct outputs while penalizing order-sensitive or incorrect responses. This marks the first time reinforcement learning has been used to mitigate LLMs' order sensitivity. We also propose two new metrics, Consistency Rate and Overconfidence Rate, to reveal the pseudo-stability of previous methods and guide more comprehensive evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DGAO achieves superior order fairness while improving performance on RAG, mathematical reasoning, and classification tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Hyalinesky/DGAO.

</details>


### [271] [Stochastic Minimum-Cost Reach-Avoid Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11975)
*Jingduo Pan,Taoran Wu,Yiling Xue,Bai Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces reach-avoid probability certificates (RAPCs) and a contraction-based Bellman formulation to enable reinforcement learning agents to satisfy probabilistic reach-avoid constraints while minimizing costs in stochastic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safe and constrained reinforcement learning methods typically fail to jointly enforce probabilistic reach-avoid constraints and optimize cost in stochastic environments. There's a need for approaches that can handle both the probabilistic nature of reach-avoid specifications and cost optimization simultaneously.

Method: The authors introduce reach-avoid probability certificates (RAPCs) to identify states from which stochastic reach-avoid constraints are satisfiable. They then develop a contraction-based Bellman formulation that serves as a principled surrogate for integrating reach-avoid considerations into reinforcement learning, enabling cost optimization under probabilistic constraints.

Result: The proposed algorithms achieve almost sure convergence to locally optimal policies with respect to the resulting objective. Experiments in the MuJoCo simulator demonstrate improved cost performance and consistently higher reach-avoid satisfaction rates compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The RAPC framework and contraction-based Bellman formulation provide an effective solution for joint probabilistic constraint satisfaction and cost optimization in reinforcement learning, addressing a significant gap in existing safe RL methods for stochastic environments.

Abstract: We study stochastic minimum-cost reach-avoid reinforcement learning, where an agent must satisfy a reach-avoid specification with probability at least $p$ while minimizing expected cumulative costs in stochastic environments. Existing safe and constrained reinforcement learning methods typically fail to jointly enforce probabilistic reach-avoid constraints and optimize cost in the learning setting in stochastic environments. To address this challenge, we introduce reach-avoid probability certificates (RAPCs), which identify states from which stochastic reach-avoid constraints are satisfiable. Building on RAPCs, we develop a contraction-based Bellman formulation that serves as a principled surrogate for integrating reach-avoid considerations into reinforcement learning, enabling cost optimization under probabilistic constraints. We establish almost sure convergence of the proposed algorithms to locally optimal policies with respect to the resulting objective. Experiments in the MuJoCo simulator demonstrate improved cost performance and consistently higher reach-avoid satisfaction rates.

</details>


### [272] [QDSB: Quantized Diffusion Schrödinger Bridges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11983)
*Tobias Fuchs,Florian Kalinke,Nadja Klein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quantized Diffusion Schrödinger Bridges (QDSB) accelerate Schrödinger bridge training for unpaired distribution learning by using anchor quantization to reduce computational cost while maintaining sample quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning generative models from unpaired samples is important, but current Schrödinger bridge methods face computational bottlenecks. Simulation-free SBs avoid path simulation but require solving entropic optimal transport problems, which are computationally expensive and iterative minibatch solutions distort global transport geometry.

Method: QDSB computes the endpoint coupling on anchor-quantized endpoint distributions and lifts the resulting plan back to original data points through cell-wise sampling. This approach reduces computational complexity while maintaining transport stability.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows regularized optimal coupling is stable w.r.t. anchor quantization, with error controlled by anchor approximation quality. Real-world experiments show QDSB matches sample quality of baselines while requiring substantially less time.

Conclusion: QDSB provides an efficient alternative to existing Schrödinger bridge methods by using quantization techniques to reduce computational cost without sacrificing sample quality, making generative modeling from unpaired samples more practical.

Abstract: Learning generative models in settings where the source and target distributions are only specified through unpaired samples is gaining in importance. Here, one frequently-used model are Schrödinger bridges (SB), which represent the most likely evolution between both endpoint distributions. To accelerate training, simulation-free SBs avoid the path simulation of the original SB models. However, learning simulation-free SBs requires paired data; a coupling of the source and target samples is obtained as the solution of the entropic optimal transport (OT) problem. As obtaining the optimal global coupling is infeasible in many practical cases, the entropic OT problem is iteratively solved on minibatches instead. Still, the repeated cost remains substantial and the locality can distort the global transport geometry. We propose quantized diffusion Schrödinger bridges (QDSB), which compute the endpoint coupling on anchor-quantized endpoint distributions and lift the resulting plan back to original data points through cell-wise sampling. We show that the regularized optimal coupling is stable w.r.t. anchor quantization, with an error controlled by the quality of the anchor approximation. In real-world experiments, QDSB matches the sample quality of existing baselines, requiring substantially less time. Code and data are available at github.com/mathefuchs/qdsb.

</details>


### [273] [Split the Differences, Pool the Rest: Provably Efficient Multi-Objective Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12000)
*Ziyad Sheebaelhamd,Luca Viano,Volkan Cevher,Claire Vernade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-objective imitation learning algorithm (MA-BC) that partitions conflicting expert data while pooling non-conflicting data, achieving faster convergence to Pareto-optimal policies than independent expert approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard imitation learning approaches fail in multi-objective settings because naively aggregating conflicting expert trajectories from Pareto-optimal experts can produce dominated policies rather than recovering policies on the Pareto front.

Method: Propose Multi-Output Augmented Behavioral Cloning (MA-BC), which systematically partitions divergent expert data while pooling state-action pairs where no behavior conflict is observed among multiple Pareto-optimal experts in a MOMDP.

Result: Theoretically prove MA-BC converges to Pareto-optimal policies faster than independent expert approaches and establish minimax optimality. Empirically validate across discrete environments and extend to continuous LQR control task.

Conclusion: MA-BC provides a principled solution for multi-objective imitation learning by intelligently handling conflicting expert demonstrations, achieving optimal statistical rates and practical performance across various environments.

Abstract: This work investigates multi-objective imitation learning: the problem of recovering policies that lie on the Pareto front given demonstrations from multiple Pareto-optimal experts in a Multi-Objective Markov Decision Process (MOMDP). Standard imitation approaches are ill-equipped for this regime, as naively aggregating conflicting expert trajectories can result in dominated policies. To address this, we introduce Multi-Output Augmented Behavioral Cloning (MA-BC), an algorithm that systematically partitions divergent expert data while pooling state-action pairs where no behavior conflict is observed. Theoretically, we prove that MA-BC converges to Pareto-optimal policies at a faster statistical rate than any learner that considers each expert dataset independently. Furthermore, we establish a novel lower bound for multi-objective imitation learning, demonstrating that MA-BC is minimax optimal. Finally, we empirically validate our algorithm across diverse discrete environments and, guided by our theoretical insights, extend and evaluate MA-BC on a continuous Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) control task.

</details>


### [274] [Estimating Subgraph Importance with Structural Prior Domain Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12009)
*Changhyun Kim,Seunghwan An,Jong-June Jeon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method for estimating subgraph importance in pretrained GNNs using linear Group Lasso regression in embedding space, which leverages domain knowledge of graph substructures without needing specific GNN architecture details or ground-truth labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for effective subgraph importance estimation in pretrained Graph Neural Networks for graph-level tasks, especially methods that can work without access to specific GNN architecture details (output layer/readout function) and ground-truth labels.

Method: Formulates subgraph importance estimation as a linear Group Lasso regression problem in the GNN's embedding space, leveraging prior domain knowledge about graph substructures while remaining architecture-agnostic.

Result: Experiments on real-world graph datasets show the method consistently outperforms existing baselines in subgraph importance estimation, and it can be extended to identify important nodes within graphs.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides an effective, architecture-independent approach for estimating subgraph importance in pretrained GNNs, with demonstrated superiority over existing methods and potential for extension to node-level importance analysis.

Abstract: We propose a subgraph importance estimation method for pretrained Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on graph-level tasks, formulated as a linear Group Lasso regression problem in the embedding space. Our method effectively leverages prior domain knowledge of graph substructures, while remaining independent of the specific form of the output layer or readout function used in the GNN architecture, and it does not require access to ground-truth target labels. Experiments on real-world graph datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing baselines in subgraph importance estimation. Furthermore, we extend our method to identify important nodes within the graph.

</details>


### [275] [Limits of Learning Linear Dynamics from Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12010)
*Aybüke Ulusarslan,Niki Kilbertus,Nora Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: System identifiability in data-driven modeling: when full system identification fails, the restricted dynamics on reachable subspaces remain uniquely identifiable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many data-driven methods assume identifiability when learning governing dynamics from data, but when this assumption fails, estimated models yield spurious predictions and invalid mechanistic conclusions. Classical identifiability guarantees (controllability + persistent excitation) leave gaps about what happens when these conditions fail and which parts remain identifiable.

Method: Develop a geometric characterization of information limits imposed by experimental setups (initial state and control input), derive closed-form description of all consistent systems, and prove that restricted dynamics on reachable subspaces remain uniquely determined even without full system identifiability.

Result: The experimental setup dictates fundamental limits on recoverable information from observed trajectories. Even when full linear time-invariant (LTI) systems aren't identifiable, the restricted dynamics on the subspace reachable by the experiment remain uniquely determined.

Conclusion: Identifiability analysis must consider the specific experimental conditions, and partial identifiability of reachable subspace dynamics provides valuable information even when full system identification is impossible, offering more nuanced understanding than classical binary identifiability guarantees.

Abstract: Learning governing dynamics from data is a common goal across the sciences, yet it is only well-posed when the underlying mechanisms are identifiable. In practice, many data-driven methods implicitly assume identifiability; when this assumption fails, estimated models can yield spurious predictions and invalid mechanistic conclusions. Classical identifiability guarantees for controlled linear time-invariant (LTI) systems provide sufficient conditions -- controllability and persistent excitation -- but leave open whether identifiability holds when these conditions fail, and which parts of the system remain identifiable without full identifiability. We show that the experimental setup, i.e., the realized initial state and control input, dictates a fundamental limit on the information recoverable from the observed trajectory. We develop a geometric characterization of this limit and derive a closed-form description of all systems consistent with the experimental setup. Crucially, we prove that even when the full system is not identifiable, the restricted dynamics on the subspace reachable by the experiment remain uniquely determined.

</details>


### [276] [Efficient and Adaptive Human Activity Recognition via LLM Backbones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12019)
*Aleksandr Bredikhin,Philippe Lalanda,German Vega*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can serve as pretrained temporal backbones for sensor-based human activity recognition when adapted with parameter-efficient methods, achieving strong performance with minimal training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional Transformer-based HAR models require expensive task-specific training from scratch, have high data requirements, and struggle with domain shifts. There's a need for more efficient, adaptable approaches.

Method: Use pretrained LLMs as frozen temporal backbones, bridging sensor data to language space via structured convolutional projection, then adapt with parameter-efficient LoRA instead of full fine-tuning.

Result: Enables rapid convergence, strong data efficiency, robust cross-dataset transfer, especially in low-data/few-shot settings. Demonstrates complementary roles of convolutional frontends (local features) and LLMs (long-range dependencies).

Conclusion: LLMs provide practical, frugal, scalable foundations for adaptive HAR systems, opening new directions for reusing foundation models beyond their original language domains.

Abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) is a core task in pervasive computing systems, where models must operate under strict computational constraints while remaining robust to heterogeneous and evolving deployment conditions. Recent advances based on Transformer architectures have significantly improved recognition performance, but typically rely on task-specific models trained from scratch, resulting in high training cost, large data requirements, and limited adaptability to domain shifts. In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift that reuses large pretrained language models (LLMs) as generic temporal backbones for sensor-based HAR, instead of designing domain-specific Transformers. To bridge the modality gap between inertial time series and language models, we introduce a structured convolutional projection that maps multivariate accelerometer and gyroscope signals into the latent space of the LLM. The pretrained backbone is kept frozen and adapted using parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters and the overall training cost. Through extensive experiments on standard HAR benchmarks, we show that this approach enables rapid convergence, strong data efficiency, and robust cross-dataset transfer, particularly in low-data and few-shot settings. At the same time, our results highlight the complementary roles of convolutional frontends and LLMs, where local invariances are handled at the signal level while long-range temporal dependencies are captured by the pretrained backbone. Overall, this work demonstrates that LLMs can serve as a practical, frugal, and scalable foundation for adaptive HAR systems, opening new directions for reusing foundation models beyond their original language domain.

</details>


### [277] [Approximation Theory of Laplacian-Based Neural Operators for Reaction-Diffusion System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12025)
*Takashi Furuya,Ryo Ozawa,Jenn-Nan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural operators for Gierer-Meinhardt reaction-diffusion systems achieve polynomial parameter complexity growth with accuracy, overcoming curse of dimensionality through Laplacian spectral representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While neural operators have universal approximation results, specific approximation analysis for nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems remains limited. The paper addresses this gap by studying the Gierer-Meinhardt system as a prototypical model of nonlinear pattern formation.

Method: The study applies neural operators to learn the solution mapping from initial conditions to time-dependent solutions of generalized Gierer-Meinhardt systems. The approach exploits Laplacian spectral representation of the Green's function underlying the PDE, using eigenfunction-based neural operator architectures.

Result: The paper establishes explicit approximation error bounds in terms of network depth, width, and spectral rank. It shows that required parameter complexity grows at most polynomially with target accuracy, demonstrating that Laplacian eigenfunction-based architectures alleviate the curse of parametric complexity.

Conclusion: Laplacian eigenfunction-based neural operators provide efficient approximation for nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems with polynomial complexity scaling, overcoming limitations of generic operator learning. Numerical experiments on Gierer-Meinhardt systems support these theoretical findings.

Abstract: Neural operators provide a framework for learning solution operators of partial differential equations (PDEs), enabling efficient surrogate modeling for complex systems. While universal approximation results are now well understood, approximation analysis specific to nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems remains limited. In this paper, we study neural operators applied to the solution mapping from initial conditions to time-dependent solutions of a generalized Gierer-Meinhardt reaction-diffusion system, a prototypical model of nonlinear pattern formation. Our main results establish explicit approximation error bounds in terms of network depth, width, and spectral rank by exploiting the Laplacian spectral representation of the Green's function underlying the PDE. We show that the required parameter complexity grows at most polynomially with respect to the target accuracy, demonstrating that Laplacian eigenfunction-based neural operator architectures alleviate the curse of parametric complexity encountered in generic operator learning. Numerical experiments on the Gierer-Meinhardt system support the theoretical findings.

</details>


### [278] [Resilient Vision-Tabular Multimodal Learning under Modality Missingness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12031)
*Camillo Maria Caruso,Valerio Guarrasi,Paolo Soda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multimodal transformer framework for medical vision-tabular data that handles pervasive missing modalities without imputation, using masked self-attention and modality-dropout regularization for robust performance under partial data availability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world clinical settings rarely have complete modality availability, with frequent missing modalities and individual features. Most existing multimodal deep learning approaches assume complete modality availability, which doesn't reflect clinical reality.

Method: Proposes a multimodal transformer framework with three components: vision encoder, tabular encoder, and multimodal fusion encoder. Uses learnable modality tokens and intermediate fusion with masked self-attention that excludes missing tokens/modalities. Introduces modality-dropout regularization that stochastically removes available modalities during training.

Result: Evaluated on MIMIC-CXR dataset with structured clinical data from MIMIC-IV for multilabel classification of 14 diagnostic findings. Method consistently outperforms baselines across all missingness regimes, showing smoother performance degradation and improved robustness. Ablation studies show attention-level masking and intermediate fusion with joint fine-tuning are key.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles pervasive modality missingness in medical multimodal learning without relying on imputation, demonstrating robust performance across varying missingness scenarios through attention masking and regularization strategies.

Abstract: Multimodal deep learning has shown strong potential in medical applications by integrating heterogeneous data sources such as medical images and structured clinical variables. However, most existing approaches implicitly assume complete modality availability, an assumption that rarely holds in real-world clinical settings where entire modalities and individual features are frequently missing. In this work, we propose a multimodal transformer framework for joint vision-tabular learning explicitly designed to operate under pervasive modality missingness, without relying on imputation or heuristic model switching. The architecture integrates three components: a vision, a tabular, and a multimodal fusion encoder. Unimodal representations are weighted through learnable modality tokens and fused via intermediate fusion with masked self-attention, which excludes missing tokens and modalities from information aggregation and gradient propagation. To further enhance resilience, we introduce a modality-dropout regularization strategy that stochastically removes available modalities during training, encouraging the model to exploit complementary information under partial data availability. We evaluate our approach on the MIMIC-CXR dataset paired with structured clinical data from MIMIC-IV for multilabel classification of 14 diagnostic findings with incomplete annotations. Two parallel systematic stress-test protocols progressively increase training and inference missingness in each modality separately, spanning fully multimodal to fully unimodal scenarios. Across all missingness regimes, the proposed method consistently outperforms representative baselines, showing smoother performance degradation and improved robustness. Ablation studies further demonstrate that attention-level masking and intermediate fusion with joint fine-tuning are key to resilient multimodal inference.

</details>


### [279] [Scaling Laws and Tradeoffs in Recurrent Networks of Expressive Neurons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12049)
*Aaron Spieler,Georg Martius,Anna Levina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ELM Networks with complex neurons that can trade off between number of units, per-unit complexity, and connectivity under fixed parameter budgets, showing performance improves along all axes with optimal tradeoffs emerging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mainstream machine learning uses extremely simple units inherited from early neural networks, while cortical neurons are complex, multi-timescale processors shaped by evolution. The paper treats this as a normative architectural question: how should one allocate fixed parameter budgets between number of units, per-unit complexity, and connectivity?

Method: Introduces ELM Networks with Expressive Leaky Memory neurons that mirror cortical neuron functional components. The architecture allows independent adjustment of three dimensions: number of units N, per-unit effective complexity k_e, and per-unit connectivity k_c. Evaluates on SHD-Adding neuromorphic task and Enwik8 character-level language modeling.

Result: Performance improves monotonically along each axis individually. Under fixed budgets, clear non-trivial optimal tradeoffs emerge, with larger budgets favoring both more and more complex neurons. A closed-form information-theoretic model captures tradeoffs, attributing diminishing returns to per-neuron signal-to-noise saturation and across-neuron redundancy.

Conclusion: The simple-unit default in machine learning is not obviously optimal once the tradeoff surface is probed. The work offers a normative lens on cortex's reliance on complex spatio-temporal integrators and demonstrates stable training across orders of magnitude in scale.

Abstract: Cortical neurons are complex, multi-timescale processors wired into recurrent circuits, shaped by long evolutionary pressure under stringent biological constraints. Mainstream machine learning, by contrast, predominantly builds models from extremely simple units, a default inherited from early neural-network theory. We treat this as a normative architectural question. How should one split a fixed parameter budget $P$ between the number of units $N$, per-unit effective complexity $k_e$, and per-unit connectivity $k_c$? What controls the optimal allocation? This calls for a model in which per-unit complexity can be tuned independently of width and connectivity. Accordingly, we introduce the ELM Network, whose recurrent layer is built from Expressive Leaky Memory (ELM) neurons, chosen to mirror functional components of cortical neurons. The architecture allows for individually adjusting $N$, $k_e$, and $k_c$ and trains stably across orders of magnitude in scale.
