Motivation: Prior constrained sampling methods assume connected feasible sets, but real-world problems often involve disconnected components, making efficient sampling challenging. There's a need for methods that can handle unknown numbers of disconnected components defined by equality and inequality constraints.

Method: MASEM uses a resampling scheme to maximize the entropy of the empirical distribution based on k-nearest neighbor density estimation. It works on manifolds with disconnected components defined by smooth constraints and can be instantiated with multiple local samplers.

Result: MASEM decreases KL-divergence between empirical and target distributions exponentially in resampling steps in the mean field. It enables fast mixing across constrained sampling problems, improving over alternatives by an order of magnitude in Sinkhorn distance with competitive runtime.

Conclusion: MASEM provides an effective solution for constrained sampling on disconnected manifolds, offering theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements for applications in Bayesian optimization and robotics.

Abstract: Sampling from constrained distributions has a wide range of applications, including in Bayesian optimization and robotics. Prior work establishes convergence and feasibility guarantees for constrained sampling, but assumes that the feasible set is connected. However, in practice, the feasible set often decomposes into multiple disconnected components, which makes efficient sampling under constraints challenging. In this paper, we propose MAnifold Sampling via Entropy Maximization (MASEM) for sampling on a manifold with an unknown number of disconnected components, implicitly defined by smooth equality and inequality constraints. The presented method uses a resampling scheme to maximize the entropy of the empirical distribution based on k-nearest neighbor density estimation. We show that, in the mean field, MASEM decreases the KL-divergence between the empirical distribution and the maximum-entropy target exponentially in the number of resampling steps. We instantiate MASEM with multiple local samplers and demonstrate its versatility and efficiency on synthetic and robotics-based benchmarks. MASEM enables fast and scalable mixing across a range of constrained sampling problems, improving over alternatives by an order of magnitude in Sinkhorn distance with competitive runtime.

</details>


### [308] [Targeted Neuron Modulation via Contrastive Pair Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12290)
*Sam Herring,Jake Naviasky,Karan Malhotra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contrastive Neuron Attribution (CNA) identifies sparse neuron circuits (0.1% of MLP neurons) that distinguish harmful from benign prompts, enabling effective refusal behavior removal without output degradation, revealing alignment fine-tuning transforms pre-existing discrimination structures into targeted refusal gates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current steering methods for modifying language model refusal behavior operate on the residual stream and degrade output coherence at high intervention strengths, limiting practical use. There's poor understanding of the mechanisms behind instruction-tuned refusal behavior.

Method: Introduces Contrastive Neuron Attribution (CNA), which identifies the top 0.1% of MLP neurons whose activations most distinguish harmful from benign prompts using only forward passes (no gradients or auxiliary training). Tests CNA on instruct models by ablating discovered circuits and evaluating on jailbreak benchmarks.

Result: Ablating the CNA-discovered circuit reduces refusal rates by over 50% on standard jailbreak benchmarks while preserving fluency and non-degeneracy across all steering strengths. Base models contain similar late-layer discrimination structures, but steering these neurons only produces content shifts, not behavioral change.

Conclusion: Neuron-level intervention enables reliable behavioral steering without the quality tradeoffs of residual-stream methods. Alignment fine-tuning transforms pre-existing discrimination structure into a sparse, targetable refusal gate, suggesting that refusal behavior emerges from specific neuron circuits rather than distributed representations.

Abstract: Language models are instruction-tuned to refuse harmful requests, but the mechanisms underlying this behavior remain poorly understood. Popular steering methods operate on the residual stream and degrade output coherence at high intervention strengths, limiting their practical use. We introduce contrastive neuron attribution (CNA), which identifies the 0.1% of MLP neurons whose activations most distinguish harmful from benign prompts, requiring only forward passes with no gradients or auxiliary training. In instruct models, ablating the discovered circuit reduces refusal rates by over 50% on a standard jailbreak benchmark while preserving fluency and non-degeneracy across all steering strengths. Applying CNA to matched base and instruct models across Llama and Qwen architectures (from 1B to 72B parameters), we find that base models contain similar late-layer discrimination structures but steering these neurons produces only content shifts, not behavioral change. These results demonstrate that neuron-level intervention enables reliable behavioral steering without the quality tradeoffs of residual-stream methods. More broadly, our findings suggest that alignment fine-tuning transforms pre-existing discrimination structure into a sparse, targetable refusal gate.

</details>


### [309] [BSO: Safety Alignment Is Density Ratio Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12339)
*Tien-Phat Nguyen,Truong Nguyen,Thin Nguyen,Duy Minh Ho Nguyen,Ngoc-Thanh Dinh,Trung Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BSO is a single-stage method for safety alignment that reduces the problem to density ratio matching, requiring no auxiliary models and improving safety-helpfulness trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety alignment methods are complex (separate reward models, online RL, primal-dual updates) or use ad-hoc modifications to direct preference optimization that lack principled derivation.

Method: Show that likelihood ratio of optimal safe policy has closed-form decomposition to density ratio matching problem. Minimize Bregman divergences between data and model ratios yields Bregman Safety Optimization (BSO) - family of single-stage loss functions each induced by convex generator.

Result: BSO requires no auxiliary models, introduces only one hyperparameter beyond standard preference optimization, recovers existing safety-aware methods as special cases. Experiments show BSO consistently improves safety-helpfulness trade-off across benchmarks.

Conclusion: BSO provides general, simple, and principled approach to safety alignment that outperforms existing methods while being more elegant and efficient.

Abstract: Aligning language models for both helpfulness and safety typically requires complex pipelines-separate reward and cost models, online reinforcement learning, and primal-dual updates. Recent direct preference optimization approaches simplify training but incorporate safety through ad-hoc modifications such as multi-stage procedures or heuristic margin terms, lacking a principled derivation. We show that the likelihood ratio of the optimal safe policy admits a closed-form decomposition that reduces safety alignment to a density ratio matching problem. Minimizing Bregman divergences between the data and model ratios yields Bregman Safety Optimization (BSO), a family of single-stage loss functions, each induced by a convex generator, that provably recover the optimal safe policy. BSO is both general and simple: it requires no auxiliary models, introduces only one hyperparameter beyond standard preference optimization, and recovers existing safety-aware methods as special cases. Experiments across safety alignment benchmarks show that BSO consistently improves the safety-helpfulness trade-off.

</details>


### [310] [STRABLE: Benchmarking Tabular Machine Learning with Strings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12292)
*Gioia Blayer,Myung Jun Kim,Félix Lefebvre,Lennart Purucker,Alan Arazi,Eilam Shapira,Roi Reichart,Frank Hutter,Marine Le Morvan,David Holzmüller,Gaël Varoquaux*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STRABLE is a new benchmark of 108 real-world tables with string and numerical data, enabling the first large-scale study of tabular learning with strings. The study finds that for most categorical-dominant tables, advanced tabular learners with simple string embeddings work best, while LLM encoders become competitive for free-text-dominant tables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tables often contain both string and numerical entries, but current tabular learning research lacks proper benchmarking suites for such mixed data. It's unclear whether dedicated end-to-end learners are needed or if simple categorical encoding of strings suffices, and if encoded tables behave like numerical tabular data.

Method: Created STRABLE - a benchmarking corpus of 108 real-world tables with strings and numbers across diverse application fields. Conducted large-scale study evaluating 445 pipelines including end-to-end architectures and modular pipelines (string encoding → post-processing → tabular learner). Tested various LLM families and post-processing techniques.

Result: For most categorical-dominant tables in the wild, advanced tabular learners paired with simple string embeddings achieve good predictions at low computational cost. On free-text-dominant tables, large LLM encoders become competitive and their performance is sensitive to post-processing, showing differences across LLM families.

Conclusion: STRABLE establishes a foundation for research on tabular learning with strings, demonstrating that pipeline rankings from this benchmark generalize well. The study shows that simple approaches work well for categorical-dominant tables, while LLM approaches have potential for free-text-heavy cases, highlighting the need for continued research in this understudied area.

Abstract: Benchmarking tabular learning has revealed the benefit of dedicated architectures, pushing the state of the art. But real-world tables often contain string entries, beyond numbers, and these settings have been understudied due to a lack of a solid benchmarking suite. They lead to new research questions: Are dedicated learners needed, with end-to-end modeling of strings and numbers? Or does it suffice to encode strings as numbers, as with a categorical encoding? And if so, do the resulting tables resemble numerical tabular data, calling for the same learners? To enable these studies, we contribute STRABLE, a benchmarking corpus of 108 tables, all real-world learning problems with strings and numbers across diverse application fields. We run the first large-scale empirical study of tabular learning with strings, evaluating 445 pipelines. These pipelines span end-to-end architectures and modular pipelines, where strings are first encoded, then post-processed, and finally passed to a tabular learner. We find that, because most tables in the wild are categorical-dominant, advanced tabular learners paired with simple string embeddings achieve good predictions at low computational cost. On free-text-dominant tables, large LLM encoders become competitive. Their performance also appears sensitive to post-processing, with differences across LLM families. Finally, we show that STRABLE is a good set of tables to study "string tabular" learning as it leads to generalizable pipeline rankings that are close to the oracle rankings. We thus establish STRABLE as a foundation for research on tabular learning with strings, an important yet understudied area.

</details>


### [311] [A New Technique for AI Explainability using Feature Association Map](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12350)
*Sayantani Ghosh,Amit Kumar Das,Amlan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAMeX is a new XAI algorithm that uses graph-based Feature Association Maps to explain AI decisions, outperforming established methods like PFI and SHAP in feature importance analysis for classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of transparency in AI systems creates challenges for critical real-life applications, making it essential to explain AI decisions to build trust in these systems.

Method: The proposed FAMeX algorithm is based on a graph-theoretic formulation called Feature Association Map (FAM), which models feature associations as a foundation for explainability.

Result: Experiments with eight benchmark algorithms show that FAMeX outperforms competing XAI algorithms (Permutation Feature Importance and SHAP) in gauging feature importance for classification tasks.

Conclusion: FAMeX is a promising algorithm for explaining predictions from AI systems, demonstrating better performance than existing XAI methods in evaluating feature importance in classification contexts.

Abstract: Lack of transparency in AI systems poses challenges in critical real-life applications. It is important to be able to explain the decisions of an AI system to ensure trust on the system. Explainable AI (XAI) algorithms play a vital role in achieving this objective. In this paper, we are proposing a new algorithm for Explaining AI systems, FAMeX (Feature Association Map based eXplainability). The proposed algorithm is based on a graph-theoretic formulation of the feature set termed as Feature Association Map (FAM). The foundation of the modelling is based on association between features. The proposed FAMeX algorithm has been found to be better than the competing XAI algorithms - Permutation Feature Importance (PFI) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). Experiments conducted with eight benchmark algorithms show that FAMeX is able to gauge feature importance in the context of classification better than the competing algorithms. This definitely shows that FAMeX is a promising algorithm in explaining the predictions from an AI system

</details>


### [312] [Approximation of Maximally Monotone Operators : A Graph Convergence Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12301)
*Takashi Furuya,Yury Korolev,Takaharu Yaguchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new paradigm for approximating discontinuous or set-valued operators (like differential operators) using graph convergence instead of traditional uniform/Lp approximation, and proves that continuous neural networks can approximate maximally monotone operators while preserving structural properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many important operators (including differential operators) are discontinuous or set-valued and lie outside classical approximation frameworks, creating a need for new approximation methods that can handle such operators.

Method: The authors propose using graph convergence (Painlevé-Kuratowski convergence) instead of traditional approximation methods, focusing on maximally monotone operators. They show continuous encoder-decoder architectures can approximate such operators, and develop structure-preserving approximations using resolvent-based parameterizations that retain maximal monotonicity.

Result: The paper demonstrates that uniform and Lp approximation are fundamentally inadequate for discontinuous/set-valued operators, proves that any maximally monotone operator can be approximated by continuous encoder-decoder architectures via local graph convergence, and constructs structure-preserving approximations that retain maximal monotonicity.

Conclusion: A paradigm shift to graph convergence enables effective approximation of discontinuous and set-valued operators that traditional methods cannot handle, with practical neural network implementations that preserve important structural properties like maximal monotonicity.

Abstract: Operator learning has been highly successful for continuous mappings between infinite-dimensional spaces, such as PDE solution operators. However, many operators of interest-including differential operators-are discontinuous or set-valued, and lie outside classical approximation frameworks. We propose a paradigm shift by formulating approximation via graph convergence (Painlevé-Kuratowski convergence), which is well-suited for closed operators. We show that uniform and $L^p$ approximation are fundamentally inadequate in this setting. Focusing on maximally monotone operators, we prove that any such operator can be approximated in the sense of local graph convergence by continuous encoder-decoder architectures, and further construct structure-preserving approximations that retain maximal monotonicity via resolvent-based parameterizations.

</details>


### [313] [Agent-Based Post-Hoc Correction of Agricultural Yield Forecasts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12375)
*Matthew Beddows,Aiden Durrant,Georgios Leontidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM agents with agricultural domain knowledge tools can significantly improve crop yield forecasting by correcting existing model predictions, even with limited commercial farm data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Commercial soft fruit production lacks the extensive sensor networks, satellite imagery, and high-resolution meteorological data that state-of-the-art yield forecasting methods require, creating a gap between research methods and practical farm implementation.

Method: Proposed a structured LLM agent framework that performs post-hoc correction of existing model predictions. The framework encodes agricultural domain knowledge through specialized tools for phase detection, bias learning, and range validation.

Result: On proprietary strawberry yield data, LLM agent refinement of XGBoost reduced MAE by 20% and MASE by 56%. Similar improvements were seen across Moirai2 (MAE 24%, MASE 22%) and Random Forest (MAE 28%, MASE 66%) baselines. Llama 3.1 8B performed best, while LLaVA 13B showed inconsistent results.

Conclusion: LLM agents with domain-specific tools can effectively bridge the gap between research-grade forecasting methods and practical commercial farm applications by improving existing models with limited data, though model selection is crucial for consistent performance.

Abstract: Accurate crop yield forecasting in commercial soft fruit production is constrained by the data available in typical commercial farm records, which lack the sensor networks, satellite imagery, and high-resolution meteorological inputs that most state-of-the-art approaches assume. We propose a structured LLM agent framework that performs post-hoc correction of existing model predictions, encoding agricultural domain knowledge across tools for phase detection, bias learning, and range validation. Evaluated on a proprietary strawberry yield dataset and a public USDA corn harvest dataset, agent refinement of XGBoost reduced MAE by 20% and MASE by 56% on strawberry, with consistent improvements across Moirai2 (MAE 24%, MASE 22%) and Random Forest (MAE 28%, MASE 66%) baselines. Using Llama 3.1 8B as the agent produced the strongest corrections across all configurations; LLaVA 13B showed inconsistent gains, highlighting sensitivity to the choice of refinement model.

</details>


### [314] [Discrete Flow Matching for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12379)
*Fairoz Nower Khan,Nabuat Zaman Nahim,Peizhong Ju*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRIFT is an online fine-tuning method that updates offline-pretrained continuous-time Markov chain policies for discrete action RL using advantage-weighted discrete flow matching with path-space regularization and candidate-set approximation for large action spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 1) Most generative policy methods (diffusion/flow matching) are designed for continuous control, but many RL tasks have discrete action spaces. 2) Generative policies heavily rely on offline datasets, and offline-to-online RL is challenging because policies must improve from new interactions without losing useful behaviors learned from static data.

Method: DRIFT method features: 1) Online fine-tuning of offline-pretrained continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) policies using advantage-weighted discrete flow matching loss. 2) Path-space penalty that regularizes the full CTMC trajectory distribution (not just final actions) to preserve pretrained knowledge. 3) Candidate-set approximation for large discrete action spaces - updates actor over a small subset of actions sampled from reference-policy rollouts and uniform exploration.

Result: 1) Provides stable offline-to-online improvement across all tasks. 2) Achieves highest average score on Jericho with simple GRU encoder while outperforming methods using pretrained language models. 3) Path-space penalty remains bounded during fine-tuning. 4) CTMC generator adapts to shifted rewards faster than deterministic baselines. 5) Candidate-set error controlled by missing target probability mass, with generator error decreasing as candidate set covers more high-probability actions.

Conclusion: DRIFT successfully addresses the challenges of applying generative policies to discrete action RL and offline-to-online fine-tuning. The method combines CTMC policies with discrete flow matching, path-space regularization, and efficient candidate-set approximations to achieve stable improvement while preserving useful pretrained knowledge, with theoretical guarantees on error control.

Abstract: Many reinforcement learning (RL) tasks have discrete action spaces, but most generative policy methods based on diffusion and flow matching are designed for continuous control. Meanwhile, generative policies usually rely heavily on offline datasets and offline-to-online RL is itself challenging, as the policy must improve from new interaction without losing useful behavior learned from static data. To address those challenges, we introduce DRIFT, an online fine-tuning method that updates an offline pretrained continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) policy with an advantage-weighted discrete flow matching loss. To preserve useful pretrained knowledge, we add a path-space penalty that regularizes the full CTMC trajectory distribution, rather than only the final action distribution. For large discrete action spaces, we introduce a candidate-set approximation that updates the actor over a small subset of actions sampled from reference-policy rollouts and uniform exploration. Our theoretical analysis shows that the candidate-set error is controlled by missing target probability mass, and the induced CTMC generator error decreases as the candidate set covers more high-probability actions. Experiments on prevailing discrete action RL task show that our method provides stable offline-to-online improvement across all tasks, achieving the highest average score on Jericho with a simple GRU encoder while outperforming methods that use pretrained language models. Controlled experiments further confirm that the path-space penalty remains bounded during fine-tuning and that the CTMC generator adapts to shifted rewards faster than deterministic baselines. The candidate-set mechanism is supported by a stability analysis showing that the generator error decreases exponentially with candidate coverage.

</details>


### [315] [In-context learning to predict critical transitions in dynamical systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12308)
*Yunus Sevinchan,Juan Nathaniel,Kai Ueltzhöffer,Carla Roesch,Tobias Weber,Vaios Laschos,Hang Fan,Gregor Ramien,Johannes Haux,Pierre Gentine,Benjamin Herdeanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TipPFN uses in-context learning with a prior-data fitted network trained on synthetic bifurcation data to detect critical transitions, outperforming conventional methods in unseen regimes and real-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critical transitions (abrupt, irreversible changes in systems) are catastrophic but rarely observed, preventing development of reliable early warning systems. Conventional statistical indicators fail with limited data and correlated noise, and existing deep learning classifiers don't generalize beyond their training distribution.

Method: TipPFN is an in-context learning framework using a prior-data fitted network. It's trained on novel synthetic data generator based on canonical bifurcation scenarios coupled with diverse, randomized stochastic dynamics. The system flexibly handles contexts of various sizes, complexity and dimensionalities.

Result: Demonstrates robust, state-of-the-art early detection of critical transitions in previously unseen tipping regimes, sim-to-real examples, and real-world observations. Works effectively in both in-context learning and zero-shot settings.

Conclusion: TipPFN provides a promising approach for early warning of critical transitions that overcomes limitations of conventional methods and existing deep learning approaches, enabling reliable detection across diverse real-world systems.

Abstract: Critical transitions - abrupt, often irreversible changes in system dynamics - arise across human and natural systems, often with catastrophic consequences. Real-world observations of such shifts remain scarce, preventing the development of reliable early warning systems. Conventional statistical and spectral indicators, such as increasing variance, tend to fail under realistic conditions of limited data and correlated noise, whereas existing deep learning classifiers do not extrapolate beyond their training data distribution. In this work, we introduce TipPFN, an in-context learning (ICL) framework that uses a prior-data fitted network to infer a system's proximity to a critical transition. Trained on our novel synthetic data generator, which is based on canonical bifurcation scenarios coupled to diverse, randomized stochastic dynamics, TipPFN flexibly capitalizes on contexts of various sizes, complexity and dimensionalities. We demonstrate robust, state-of-the-art early detection of critical transitions in previously unseen tipping regimes, sim-to-real examples, and real-world observations in both ICL and zero-shot settings.

</details>


### [316] [Trust the Batch, On- or Off-Policy: Adaptive Policy Optimization for RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12380)
*Rasool Fakoor,Murdock Aubry,Nicholas Stranges,Alexander J. Smola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new reinforcement learning objective adaptively manages trust-region and off-policy concerns using policy-ratio statistics instead of fixed hyperparameters, improving robustness across diverse settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL is fragile due to policy-induced distribution shifts, especially problematic in large-model training where implementation discrepancies exist. Current methods use fixed hyperparameters that require retuning for different tasks/scales/mismatches.

Method: Proposes batch-adaptive objective replacing fixed clipping with normalized effective sample size of policy ratios. This statistic caps score-function weight and sets off-policy regularizer strength, automatically adapting based on ratio distribution.

Result: Method matches or exceeds tuned baselines across wide settings, introduces no new objective hyperparameters while removing existing ones, showing improved robustness without manual tuning.

Conclusion: Policy-ratio distribution statistics can effectively manage trust-region and off-policy concerns adaptively during training, eliminating fragility from fixed hyperparameters and improving RL robustness.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is structurally harder than supervised learning because the policy changes the data distribution it learns from. The resulting fragility is especially visible in large-model training, where the training and rollout systems differ in numerical precision, sampling, and other implementation details. Existing methods manage this fragility by adding hyper-parameters to the training objective, which makes the algorithm more sensitive to its configuration and requires retuning whenever the task, model scale, or distribution mismatch changes. This fragility traces to two concerns that current objectives entangle through hyper-parameters set before training begins: a trust-region concern, that updates should not move the policy too far from its current value, and an off-policy concern, that data from older or different behavior policies should influence the update only to the extent that it remains reliable. Neither concern is a constant to set in advance, and their severity is reflected in the policy-ratio distribution of the current batch. We present a simple yet effective batch-adaptive objective that replaces fixed clipping with the normalized effective sample size of the policy ratios. The same statistic caps the score-function weight and sets the strength of an off-policy regularizer, so the update stays close to the usual on-policy score-function update when ratios are nearly uniform, and tightens automatically when stale or mismatched data cause ratio concentration, while retaining a nonzero learning signal on high-ratio tokens. Experiments across a wide range of settings show that our method matches or exceeds tuned baselines, introducing no new objective hyper-parameters and removing several existing ones. The code is available at https://github.com/FeynRL-project/FeynRL.

</details>


### [317] [Detecting overfitting in Neural Networks during long-horizon grokking using Random Matrix Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12394)
*Hari K. Prakash,Charles H Martin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel Random Matrix Theory method detects overfitting in deep learning models by identifying "Correlation Traps" - large outliers in weight matrices that violate self-averaging, observable even without access to training or test data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overfitting is difficult to both prevent and detect in neural networks. Existing methods typically require access to training or test data to identify overfitting. The authors aim to develop a method that can detect overfitting onset using only the model weights themselves, providing a more fundamental and data-independent approach to overfitting detection.

Method: The method applies Random Matrix Theory by randomizing weight matrices element-wise, fitting the empirical spectral distribution with a Marchenko-Pastur distribution, and identifying large outliers ("Correlation Traps") that violate self-averaging properties. The approach works at the layer level without requiring access to training or test data. To distinguish between benign and harmful traps, they propose evaluating the JS divergence of output logits when passing random data through the trained model.

Result: During the "anti-grokking" phase (onset of overfitting where test accuracy decreases while training accuracy remains high), Correlation Traps form and grow in both number and scale. The method successfully identifies this phase as structurally distinct from pre-grokking. The approach also detects similar Correlation Traps in foundation-scale LLMs, suggesting these models may be experiencing harmful overfitting.

Conclusion: Correlation Traps provide a fundamental, data-independent indicator of overfitting onset in neural networks. The anti-grokking phase represents a distinct structural phase in training dynamics characterized by the emergence of these traps. The detection of traps in large-scale models suggests this phenomenon occurs at scale and could be used for model monitoring and early overfitting detection.

Abstract: Training Neural Networks (NNs) without overfitting is difficult; detecting that overfitting is difficult as well. We present a novel Random Matrix Theory method that detects the onset of overfitting in deep learning models without access to train or test data. For each model layer, we randomize each weight matrix element-wise, $\mathbf{W} \to \mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{rand}}$, fit the randomized empirical spectral distribution with a Marchenko-Pastur distribution, and identify large outliers that violate self-averaging. We call these outliers Correlation Traps. During the onset of overfitting, which we call the "anti-grokking" phase in long-horizon grokking, Correlation Traps form and grow in number and scale as test accuracy decreases while train accuracy remains high. Traps may be benign or may harm generalization; we provide an empirical approach to distinguish between them by passing random data through the trained model and evaluating the JS divergence of output logits. Our findings show that anti-grokking is an additional grokking phase with high train accuracy and decreasing test accuracy, structurally distinct from pre-grokking through its Correlation Traps. More broadly, we find that some foundation-scale LLMs exhibit the same Correlation Traps, indicating potentially harmful overfitting.

</details>


### [318] [Autoregressive Learning in Joint KL: Sharp Oracle Bounds and Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12316)
*Yunbei Xu,Yuzhe Yuan,Ruohan Zhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides a complete characterization of long-horizon error behavior in autoregressive sequence modeling under joint KL divergence, showing horizon-free approximation but Ω(H) estimation lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental problem of learning long sequences in autoregressive modeling under model misspecification, particularly focusing on how sequence horizon H affects approximation and estimation errors under joint Kullback-Leibler divergence.

Method: The authors establish matching upper and lower bounds through joint KL divergence analysis, comparing with Hellinger-based approaches, and develop a sharp joint-KL oracle theory that aligns training objectives, evaluation metrics, and approximation metrics.

Result: Key findings: 1) Joint KL admits a horizon-free approximation factor (contrasting with Ω(H) dependence for Hellinger-based methods), 2) Fundamental information-theoretic lower bound of Ω(H) for estimation that matches computationally efficient algorithms' O~(H) upper bounds, 3) These results align the log-loss training objective with sequence-level evaluation.

Conclusion: The work provides the first complete characterization of long-horizon error behavior under joint KL divergence, clarifies the landscape of autoregressive learning results by unifying objectives and metrics, and shows connections to policy learning regret bounds matching prior imitation learning rates.

Abstract: We study the fundamental and timely problem of learning long sequences in autoregressive modeling and next-token prediction under model misspecification, measured by the joint Kullback--Leibler (KL) divergence. Our goal is to characterize how the sequence horizon \(H\) affects both approximation and estimation errors in this joint-distribution, sequence-level regime. By establishing matching upper and lower bounds, we provide, to our knowledge, the first complete characterization of long-horizon error behavior under the natural joint KL objective, with improved rates and optimality justification relative to existing work. On the approximation side, we show that joint KL admits a horizon-free approximation factor, in sharp contrast to Hellinger-based analyses that exhibit an \(Ω(H)\) dependence for computationally efficient methods; this isolates the choice of divergence as the source of approximation amplification. On the estimation side, we prove a fundamental information-theoretic lower bound of order \(Ω(H)\) that holds for both decomposable policy classes and fully shared policies, matching the \(\widetilde O(H)\) upper bounds achieved by computationally efficient algorithms. Our analysis clarifies the landscape of recent autoregressive learning results by aligning the log-loss training objective, the sequence-level evaluation metric, and the approximation metric {\color{black}through a sharp joint-KL oracle theory}. We further show that these joint-KL guarantees imply policy learning regret bounds at rates matching prior imitation learning literature.

</details>


### [319] [OGLS-SD: On-Policy Self-Distillation with Outcome-Guided Logit Steering for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12400)
*Yuxiao Yang,Xiaoyun Wang,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPSD-MISMATCH addresses miscalibration in on-policy self-distillation by using verifiable outcomes to guide teacher logit calibration, improving reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies a mismatch problem in on-policy self-distillation where teacher responses can be shifted by reflection-induced bias and response templates, leading to miscalibrated token-level supervision that undermines the effectiveness of self-distillation.

Method: Proposes OPSD-MISMATCH, an outcome-guided logit-steering framework that leverages verifiable outcome rewards to contrast successful and failed on-policy trajectories and calibrate teacher logits, combining outcome-level correctness with dense token-level guidance.

Result: OPSD-MISMATCH stabilizes self-distillation and improves reasoning performance over standard OPSD and other variants across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness in addressing the identified miscalibration issues.

Conclusion: The proposed outcome-guided logit-steering approach effectively addresses miscalibration in on-policy self-distillation, leading to more stable and improved reasoning performance by properly aligning teacher and student responses using verifiable outcome rewards.

Abstract: We study {on-policy self-distillation} (OPSD), where a language model improves its reasoning ability by distilling privileged teacher distributions along its own on-policy trajectories. Despite the performance gains of OPSD, we identify a common but often overlooked mismatch between teacher and student responses: self-reflected teacher responses can be shifted by reflection-induced bias and response templates, leading to miscalibrated token-level supervision. To mitigate this issue, we propose \methodname, an outcome-guided logit-steering framework that leverages verifiable outcome rewards to contrast successful and failed on-policy trajectories and calibrate teacher logits. By combining outcome-level correctness with dense token-level guidance through logit steering, \methodname stabilizes self-distillation and improves reasoning performance over standard OPSD and other variants across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [320] [Grid Games: The Power of Multiple Grids for Quantizing Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12327)
*Vage Egiazarian,Erik Schultheis,Andrei Panferov,Earl Killian,Torsten Hoefler,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive 4-bit quantization formats that dynamically select among multiple floating-point grids per value group outperform fixed-grid approaches, showing consistent accuracy improvements in both post-training quantization and pre-training scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses limitations of existing 4-bit quantization formats (like NVFP4 and MXFP4) that use fixed floating-point grids for each group of values. The researchers investigate whether allowing dynamic selection among multiple available grids per group could improve quantization accuracy and flexibility.

Method: The authors formalize the power-of-two-grids (PO2) problem and study practical grid families including: PO2(NF4) (NF4 normal grid paired with learned grid), MPO2 (fully learned grid pair), PO2(Split87) (explicit-zero asymmetric grid), and SFP4 (TensorCore-implementable triple grid with NVFP4 variants). They provide theoretical analysis of PO2 grid benefits and conduct extensive experiments on post-training quantization of standard models and pre-training of Llama-like models.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows practical small-group formats benefit significantly from PO2 grids, with advantages diminishing for very large groups. Experimental results demonstrate that adaptive grids consistently improve accuracy versus single-grid FP4 formats under both weight-only and weight+activation quantization scenarios, across various grid implementations.

Conclusion: Adaptive grid selection for 4-bit quantization provides a promising direction for improving quantization efficiency and accuracy, offering consistent benefits over fixed-grid approaches while maintaining practical implementation feasibility through proposed grid families like SFP4 that leverage existing hardware capabilities.

Abstract: A major recent advance in quantization is given by microscaled 4-bit formats such as NVFP4 and MXFP4, quantizing values into small groups sharing a scale, assuming a fixed floating-point grid. In this paper, we study the following natural extension: assume that, for each group of values, we are free to select the "better" among two or more 4-bit grids marked by one or more bits in the scale value. We formalize the power-of-two-grids (PO2) problem, and provide theoretical results showing that practical small-group formats such as MXFP or NVFP can benefit significantly from PO2 grids, while the advantage vanishes for very large groups. On the practical side, we instantiate several grid families, including 1) PO2(NF4), which pairs the standard NF4 normal grid with a learned grid, 2) MPO2, a grid pair that is fully learned over real weights and activations, 3) PO2(Split87), an explicit-zero asymmetric grid and 4) SFP4, a TensorCore-implementable triple which pairs NVFP4 with two shifted variants. Results for post-training quantization of standard open models and pre-training of Llama-like models show that adaptive grids consistently improve accuracy vs single-grid FP4 under both weight-only and weight+activation. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/GridGames.

</details>


### [321] [Solve the Loop: Attractor Models for Language and Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12466)
*Jacob Fein-Ashley,Paria Rashidinejad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attractor Models use fixed-point solving for iterative refinement, offering constant training memory in depth and adaptive iterations, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both large-scale language modeling and small-scale reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent architectures like looped Transformers are unstable to train, costly to optimize and deploy, and constrained to small, fixed recurrence depths, limiting their scalability for iterative refinement approaches.

Method: Introduces Attractor Models where a backbone module proposes output embeddings, then an attractor module refines them by solving for fixed points using implicit differentiation, enabling constant training memory in depth and adaptive iteration selection based on convergence.

Result: Attractor Models outperform existing models in both large-scale language-model pretraining (46.6% perplexity improvement, 19.7% accuracy improvement) and reasoning with tiny models (91.4% Sudoku-Extreme accuracy, 93.1% Maze-Hard accuracy). A 770M model beats 1.3B Transformer trained on twice the tokens. Also demonstrates equilibrium internalization where solver can be removed at inference.

Conclusion: Attractor Models make iterative refinement scalable by transforming recurrence into a computation the model can learn to internalize, offering a practical path forward for stable, efficient recurrent architectures.

Abstract: Looped Transformers offer a promising alternative to purely feed-forward computation by iteratively refining latent representations, improving language modeling and reasoning. Yet recurrent architectures remain unstable to train, costly to optimize and deploy, and constrained to small, fixed recurrence depths. We introduce Attractor Models, in which a backbone module first proposes output embeddings, then an attractor module refines them by solving for the fixed point, with gradients obtained through implicit differentiation. Thus, training memory remains constant in effective depth, and iterations are chosen adaptively by convergence. Empirically, Attractor Models outperform existing models across two regimes, large-scale language-model pretraining and reasoning with tiny models. In language modeling, Attractor Models deliver a Pareto improvement over standard Transformers and stable looped models across sizes, improving perplexity by up to 46.6% and downstream accuracy by up to 19.7% while reducing training cost. Notably, a 770M Attractor Model outperforms a 1.3B Transformer trained on twice as many tokens. On challenging reasoning tasks, we show that our model with only 27M parameters and approximately 1000 examples achieves 91.4% accuracy on Sudoku-Extreme and 93.1% on Maze-Hard, scaling favorably where frontier models like Claude and GPT o3, fail completely, and specialized recursive reasoners collapse at larger sizes. Lastly, we show that Attractor Models exhibit a novel phenomenon, which we call equilibrium internalization: fixed-point training places the model's initial output embedding near equilibrium, allowing the solver to be removed at inference time with little degradation. Together, these results suggest that Attractor Models make iterative refinement scalable by turning recurrence into a computation the model can learn to internalize.

</details>


### [322] [KV-Fold: One-Step KV-Cache Recurrence for Long-Context Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12471)
*Alireza Nadali,Patrick Cooper,Ashutosh Trivedi,Alvaro Velasquez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV-Fold is a training-free method that enables long-context inference by treating KV cache as an accumulator in a left fold over sequence chunks, allowing transformers to process very long contexts without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional transformers struggle with long-context inference due to memory constraints. There's a need for methods that can handle long sequences without modifying or retraining models, while maintaining exact information retrieval over long distances.

Method: KV-Fold treats the key-value cache as an accumulator in a left fold operation over sequence chunks. At each step, the model processes a chunk conditioned on accumulated cache, appends new KV pairs, and passes the enlarged cache forward. This chunk-to-chunk recurrence reuses internal state across segments without model modification.

Result: The method achieves 100% exact-match retrieval on needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks across contexts from 16K to 128K tokens and chain depths up to 511 on Llama-3.1-8B, staying within single 40GB GPU memory limits. The induced recurrence is stable with per-step drift plateauing and insensitive to numerical precision changes.

Conclusion: Frozen pretrained transformers already support stable KV-cache recurrence, providing a practical route to long-context inference without architectural changes or training. KV-Fold maintains long-range retrieval while operating as tractable forward passes.

Abstract: We introduce KV-Fold, a simple, training-free long-context inference protocol that treats the key-value (KV) cache as the accumulator in a left fold over sequence chunks. At each step, the model processes the next chunk conditioned on the accumulated cache, appends the newly produced keys and values, and passes the enlarged cache forward; the same one-step update is applied repeatedly, analogous to foldl in functional programming. Building on the KV cache concatenation primitive introduced for latent multi-agent communication, we repurpose it as a chunk-to-chunk recurrence for long-context inference. When processing chunk t, the model attends to the KV cache carried from earlier chunks as a prefix, reusing its internal state across segments without modifying or retraining the model. Despite its simplicity, the induced recurrence is stable: per-step drift rises briefly and then saturates into a flat plateau that persists across deep chains. This plateau is insensitive to a 10,000x change in numerical precision, robust across chunk sizes, and consistent across model families. At the task level, KV-Fold preserves exact information over long distances. On a needle-in-a-haystack benchmark, it achieves 100% exact-match retrieval across 152 trials spanning contexts from 16K to 128K tokens and chain depths up to 511 on Llama-3.1-8B, while remaining within the memory limits of a single 40GB GPU. Compared to streaming methods, which trade fidelity for bounded memory, KV-Fold maintains long-range retrieval while operating as a sequence of tractable forward passes. Overall, our results show that frozen pretrained transformers already support a stable form of KV-cache recurrence, providing a practical route to long-context inference without architectural changes or training.

</details>


### [323] [Neural-Schwarz Tiling for Geometry-Universal PDE Solving at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12343)
*Paolo Secchi,Daniel S. Balint,Marco Maurizi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NEST introduces a local-to-global neural PDE solver framework that learns reusable local physical solvers on small voxel patches and composes them into global solutions through domain decomposition, enabling generalization across different geometries and scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learned PDE solvers use global-surrogate neural operators that require expensive problem-specific training and lack reusability across new domains, geometries, and boundary conditions, limiting large-scale deployment.

Method: NEST learns neural operators on minimal 3×3×3 voxel patches with diverse local geometries and boundary/interface data. At inference, domains are tiled into overlapping patches, the local solver is applied patchwise, and global consistency is enforced through iterative Schwarz coupling with partition-of-unity assembly.

Result: The framework demonstrates successful application to nonlinear static equilibrium in compressible neo-Hookean solids on large, geometrically complex 3D domains far beyond training patch scales, showing generalization across domain size, shape, and boundary conditions.

Conclusion: Local neural building blocks coupled through Schwarz iteration provide a reusable local-training pathway for scalable learned PDE solvers that generalize beyond monolithic neural models by shifting generalization to the combination of local physics learning and algorithmic global assembly.

Abstract: Most learned PDE solvers follow a global-surrogate paradigm: a neural operator is trained to map full problem descriptions to full solution fields for a prescribed distribution of geometries, boundary conditions, and coefficients. This has enabled fast inference within fixed problem families, but limits reuse across new domains and makes large-scale deployment dependent on expensive problem-specific data generation. We introduce $\textbf{NEST}$ ($\textbf{Ne}$ural-$\textbf{S}$chwarz $\textbf{T}$iling), a local-to-global framework that shifts learning from full-domain solution operators to reusable local physical solvers. The central premise is that, although global PDE solutions depend on geometry, scale, and boundary conditions, the physical response on small neighborhoods can be learned locally and composed into global solutions through classical domain decomposition. NEST learns a neural operator on minimal voxel patches ($3 \times 3 \times 3$) with diverse local geometries and boundary/interface data. At inference time, an unseen voxelized domain is tiled into overlapping patches, the learned local solver is applied patchwise, and global consistency is enforced through iterative Schwarz coupling with partition-of-unity assembly. In this way, generalization is shifted from a monolithic neural model to the combination of local physics learning and algorithmic global assembly. We instantiate NEST on nonlinear static equilibrium in compressible neo-Hookean solids and evaluate it on large, geometrically complex 3D domains far outside the scale of the training patches. Our results show that local neural building blocks, coupled through Schwarz iteration, offer a reusable local-training path toward scalable learned PDE solvers that generalize across domain size, shape, and boundary-condition configurations.

</details>


### [324] [Beyond GRPO and On-Policy Distillation: An Empirical Sparse-to-Dense Reward Principle for Language-Model Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12483)
*Yuanda Xu,Hejian Sang,Zhengze Zhou,Ran He,Zhipeng Wang,Alborz Geramifard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: When labeled verifiable training data is scarce, allocate it strategically: use sparse rewards on large teacher models for behavior discovery, then transfer discovered behavior as dense supervision to smaller student models, rather than applying sparse RL directly on students.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard practice of using scarce labeled verifiable data directly on the deployment model (e.g., running GRPO on student) is inefficient. Different reward-density regimes (sparse vs dense) should be matched to different model sizes and capabilities for optimal data allocation.

Method: Proposes a reward-density allocation principle: use sparse sequence-level rewards on the strongest (largest) models for exploration and behavior discovery, then transfer that behavior downstream as dense token-level supervision to smaller student models via a bridge approach (forward-KL warmup on teacher rollouts followed by OPD on student rollouts).

Result: On verifiable math tasks with Qwen3 and Llama models: RL-improved 8B teacher distilled via dense bridge outperforms direct GRPO on 1.7B student. Bridge approach consistently strongest on MATH before any student-side sparse RL and gives best pre-Stage-3 AIME endpoints. Bridge makes student-side sparse RL effective - GRPO lifts MATH from 75.4% to 78.5% after bridge, outperforming replay control by 2.8 points.

Conclusion: The optimal allocation principle: avoid using scarce labeled data on least prepared policies. Use sparse rewards for teacher-side discovery, dense transfer for student compression, and only apply student-side sparse rewards after the bridge. This reward-density allocation strategy improves efficiency of scarce verifiable training data.

Abstract: In settings where labeled verifiable training data is the binding constraint, each checked example should be allocated carefully. The standard practice is to use this data directly on the model that will be deployed, for example by running GRPO on the deployment student. We argue that this is often an inefficient allocation because it overlooks a reward-density principle: sparse sequence-level reward should train models where exploration is productive, while dense token-level teacher reward should be used where the aim is to compress behavior into a smaller model. In this view, GRPO-style sparse RL and OPD-style dense teacher supervision are not separate recipes; they are different reward-density regimes. The allocation rule is simple: use scarce labeled training data upstream on the strongest model that can turn it into reward-shaped behavior, then transfer that behavior downstream as dense supervision. We evaluate this rule on verifiable math with Qwen3 and Llama models. At fixed Qwen3-1.7B deployment-student size, an RL-improved 8B teacher distilled through the dense bridge outperforms direct GRPO on the same student, while transfer from the same teacher before RL underperforms. The bridge is important: a forward-KL warmup on teacher rollouts followed by OPD on student rollouts is consistently strongest on MATH before any post-bridge student-side sparse RL, and also gives the best pre-Stage~3 AIME endpoints for the canonical 8B/14B teachers. The bridge also makes later student-side sparse RL effective: GRPO that is weak on a cold student lifts MATH from $75.4\%$ to $78.5\%$ after the bridge and outperforms a matched replay control by $2.8$ points. The operational principal is to avoid using scarce labeled data on the least prepared policy: use sparse reward for teacher-side discovery, dense transfer for student compression, and student-side sparse reward only after the bridge.

</details>


### [325] [Learning, Fast and Slow: Towards LLMs That Adapt Continually](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12484)
*Rishabh Tiwari,Kusha Sareen,Lakshya A Agrawal,Joseph E. Gonzalez,Matei Zaharia,Kurt Keutzer,Inderjit S Dhillon,Rishabh Agarwal,Devvrit Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fast-Slow Training (FST) combines in-context learning (fast weights) with parameter updates (slow weights) for LLMs, achieving better sample efficiency and performance while reducing catastrophic forgetting compared to pure RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM training approaches face a dilemma: parameter updates (RL) cause catastrophic forgetting and loss of plasticity, while in-context learning can't match performance gains of parameter updates. There's no reason to restrict learning to only one method, and human learning operates at different time scales (System 1 vs 2).

Method: Introduces Fast-Slow Training framework with two components: slow weights (model parameters) for general reasoning, and fast weights (optimized context) that learn from textual feedback to absorb task-specific information. This combines in-context learning (fast) with parameter updates (slow).

Result: FST is 3x more sample-efficient than RL alone, reaches higher performance asymptotes, maintains 70% less KL divergence from base model, shows less catastrophic forgetting, preserves plasticity for subsequent tasks, and continues to learn in continual learning scenarios where RL stalls.

Conclusion: The Fast-Slow Training framework successfully addresses the limitations of pure RL training by combining fast context adaptation with slow parameter updates, enabling more efficient learning, reduced catastrophic forgetting, and better task adaptation while staying closer to the base LLM's general reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are trained for downstream tasks by updating their parameters (e.g., via RL). However, updating parameters forces them to absorb task-specific information, which can result in catastrophic forgetting and loss of plasticity. In contrast, in-context learning with fixed LLM parameters can cheaply and rapidly adapt to task-specific requirements (e.g., prompt optimization), but cannot by itself typically match the performance gains available through updating LLM parameters. There is no good reason for restricting learning to being in-context or in-weights. Moreover, humans also likely learn at different time scales (e.g., System 1 vs 2). To this end, we introduce a fast-slow learning framework for LLMs, with model parameters as "slow" weights and optimized context as "fast" weights. These fast "weights" can learn from textual feedback to absorb the task-specific information, while allowing slow weights to stay closer to the base model and persist general reasoning behaviors. Fast-Slow Training (FST) is up to 3x more sample-efficient than only slow learning (RL) across reasoning tasks, while consistently reaching a higher performance asymptote. Moreover, FST-trained models remain closer to the base LLM (up to 70% less KL divergence), resulting in less catastrophic forgetting than RL-training. This reduced drift also preserves plasticity: after training on one task, FST trained models adapt more effectively to a subsequent task than parameter-only trained models. In continual learning scenarios, where task domains change on the fly, FST continues to acquire each new task while parameter-only RL stalls.

</details>


### [326] [From Message-Passing to Linearized Graph Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12358)
*Joël Mathys,Basil Rohner,Saku Peltonen,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Linearized Graph Sequence Models, a framework that reframes graph message-passing as sequence modeling to simplify architectural design and separate computational depth from information propagation depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to bridge the gap between rapid advances in sequence modeling architectures (particularly in domains like language processing) and graph learning. While message-passing remains the backbone of graph learning, the paper aims to help graph learning benefit from modern sequence modeling advances.

Method: The method introduces Linearized Graph Sequence Models (LGSMs), which recast message-passing graph computation from the perspective of sequence modeling. The key innovation is systematically separating computational processing depth from information propagation depth, allowing graph architectural decisions to be treated as sequence modeling choices. The authors analyze what sequence properties make methods effective for learning and preserving graph inductive bias.

Result: The framework demonstrates improved performance on long-range information tasks in graphs. The authors validate their findings both empirically and theoretically, showing that recasting message-passing as sequence modeling enables better integration of modern sequence modeling techniques into graph learning.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled way to integrate modern sequence modeling advances into message-passing based graph learning. Beyond this, it demonstrates how separating processing and information depth can recast central architectural questions as input modeling choices, offering a simplified approach to designing graph learning architectures.

Abstract: Message-passing based approaches form the default backbone of most learning architectures on graph-structured data. However, the rapid progress of modern deep learning architectures in other domains, particularly sequence modeling, raises the question of how graph learning can benefit from these advances. We introduce Linearized Graph Sequence Models, a framework that recasts message-passing graph computation from the perspective of sequence modeling to simplify architectural choices. Our approach systematically separates the computational processing depth from the information propagation depth, allowing core graph architectural decisions to be treated as sequence modeling choices. Specifically, we analyze, both empirically and theoretically, what sequence properties make methods effective for learning and preserving the graph inductive bias. In particular, we validate our findings, demonstrating improved performance on long-range information tasks in graphs. Our findings provide a principled way to integrate modern sequence modeling advances into message-passing based graph learning. Beyond this, our work demonstrates how the separation of processing and information depth can recast central architectural questions as input modeling choices.

</details>


### [327] [MetaColloc: Optimization-Free PDE Solving via Meta-Learned Basis Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12368)
*Zichuan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaColloc: Optimization-free, data-free framework for solving PDEs using meta-trained neural basis functions assembled via linear least squares, achieving SOTA accuracy with massive speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional machine learning approaches for solving PDEs require slow, equation-specific optimization for each new PDE, creating a computational bottleneck that needs to be addressed.

Method: Meta-train a dual-branch neural network on diverse Gaussian Random Fields to create a universal dictionary of neural basis functions, then freeze the network and solve PDEs by assembling a collocation matrix and using linear least squares (or Newton-Raphson for non-linear PDEs).

Result: Massive improvements across six 2D and 3D PDEs: state-of-the-art accuracy on smooth and non-linear problems, with test-time computation reduced by several orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: MetaColloc successfully eliminates the optimization bottleneck in PDE solving while revealing important frequency-dependent behavior that informs future operator-aware meta-learning approaches.

Abstract: Solving partial differential equations (PDEs) with machine learning typically requires training a new neural network for every new equation. This optimization is slow. We introduce MetaColloc. It is an optimization-free and data-free framework that removes this bottleneck completely. We decouple basis discovery from the solving process. We meta-train a dual-branch neural network on diverse Gaussian Random Fields. This offline process creates a universal dictionary of neural basis functions. At test time, we freeze the network. We solve the PDE by assembling a collocation matrix. We find the solution through a single linear least squares step. For non-linear PDEs, we apply the Newton-Raphson method to achieve fast quadratic convergence. Our experiments across six 2D and 3D PDEs show massive improvements. MetaColloc reaches state-of-the-art accuracy on smooth and non-linear problems. It also reduces test-time computation by several orders of magnitude. Finally, we provide a detailed frequency sweep analysis. This analysis reveals a critical mismatch between function approximation and operator stability at extremely high frequencies. This profound finding opens a clear path toward future operator-aware meta-learning.

</details>


### [328] [Aligning Flow Map Policies with Optimal Q-Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12416)
*Christos Ziakas,Alessandra Russo,Avishek Joey Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow Map Policies enable fast action generation for RL by learning to take arbitrary jumps in generative policy dynamics, achieving SOTA offline-to-online RL performance with 21.3% improvement over previous methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative policies (diffusion/flow matching) are expressive for multimodal control but suffer from high inference latency due to many simulation steps, compounding across sequential decisions.

Method: Introduces flow map policies that learn arbitrary-size jumps across generative dynamics. Instantiates them for offline-to-online RL with trust-region optimization, deriving FMQ for adaptation and QGBS for inference-time refinement.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on 12 robotic manipulation/locomotion tasks from OGBench/RoboMimic, outperforming previous one-step policy MVP by 21.3% relative improvement in average success rate.

Conclusion: Flow map policies successfully address the latency-efficiency trade-off in generative policies, enabling fast action generation while maintaining expressivity, with strong empirical results in offline-to-online RL.

Abstract: Generative policies based on expressive model classes, such as diffusion and flow matching, are well-suited to complex control problems with highly multimodal action distributions. Their expressivity, however, comes at a significant inference cost: generating each action typically requires simulating many steps of the generative process, compounding latency across sequential decision-making rollouts. We introduce flow map policies, a novel class of generative policies designed for fast action generation by learning to take arbitrary-size jumps including one-step jumps-across the generative dynamics of existing flow-based policies. We instantiate flow map policies for offline-to-online reinforcement learning (RL) and formulate online adaptation as a trust-region optimization problem that improves the critic's Q-value while remaining close to the offline policy. We theoretically derive FLOW MAP Q-GUIDANCE (FMQ), a principled closed-form learning target that is optimal for adapting offline flow map policies under a critic-guided trust-region constraint. We further introduce Q-GUIDED BEAM SEARCH (QGBS), a stochastic flow-map sampler that combines renoising with beam search to enable iterative inference-time refinement. Across 12 challenging robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks from OGBench and RoboMimic, FMQ achieves state-of-the-art performance in offline-to-online RL, outperforming the previous one-step policy MVP by a relative improvement of 21.3% on the average success rate.

</details>


### [329] [Learning Minimally Rigid Graphs with High Realization Counts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12427)
*Oleksandr Slyvka,Jan Rubeš,Rodrigo Alves,Jan Legerský*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reinforcement learning approach using Graph Isomorphism Networks to construct minimally rigid graphs with maximum realizations via Henneberg moves, improving spherical realization bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finding graphs with exceptionally many realizations is an extremal problem in rigidity theory, but exhaustive search is infeasible due to super-exponential growth of candidate graphs and high computational cost of realization-count evaluation.

Method: Propose a reinforcement-learning approach that constructs minimally rigid graphs via 0- and 1-extensions (Henneberg moves). Optimize realization-count invariants using Deep Cross-Entropy Method with policy parameterized by Graph Isomorphism Network encoder and permutation-equivariant extension-level action head.

Result: Empirically matches known optima for planar realization counts and improves best known bounds for spherical realization counts, yielding new record graphs.

Conclusion: The reinforcement learning approach with GNN-based policy is effective for discovering extremal rigid graphs, advancing the search for graphs with maximum realizations in rigidity theory.

Abstract: For minimally rigid graphs, the same edge-length data can admit multiple realizations (up to translations and rotations). Finding graphs with exceptionally many realizations is an extremal problem in rigidity theory, but exhaustive search quickly becomes infeasible due to the super-exponential growth of the number of candidate graphs and the high cost of realization-count evaluation. We propose a reinforcement-learning approach that constructs minimally rigid graphs via 0- and 1-extensions, also known as Henneberg moves. We optimize realization-count invariants using the Deep Cross-Entropy Method with a policy parameterized by a Graph Isomorphism Network encoder and a permutation-equivariant extension-level action head. Empirically, our method matches the known optima for planar realization counts and improves the best known bounds for spherical realization counts, yielding new record graphs.

</details>


### [330] [Environment-Adaptive Preference Optimization for Wildfire Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12435)
*Enyi Jiang,Wu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EAPO is a framework for adapting wildfire prediction models to new environments with long-tailed distributions, using distribution-aligned datasets and hybrid fine-tuning with preference optimization to handle rare extreme events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wildfire prediction faces two major challenges: 1) rare extreme events create long-tailed distributions where standard learning objectives underemphasize the important minority class (fire), and 2) models trained on historical data often fail under distribution shifts when deployed in new environments with evolving conditions.

Method: Propose Environment-Adaptive Preference Optimization (EAPO): 1) For new input distribution, construct distribution-aligned datasets via k-nearest neighbor retrieval; 2) Perform hybrid fine-tuning on this local manifold combining supervised learning with preference optimization; 3) Specifically emphasize rare extreme events and refine decision boundaries while avoiding conflicting signals from heterogeneous training data.

Result: Evaluated on real-world wildfire prediction task with environmental shifts: EAPO achieves robust performance (ROC-AUC 0.7310) and improves detection in extreme regimes, demonstrating effectiveness in dynamic wildfire prediction systems.

Conclusion: EAPO successfully addresses the challenges of long-tailed distributions and distribution shifts in wildfire prediction by adaptively fine-tuning models to target environments, achieving robust performance and improved extreme event detection while handling environmental dynamics.

Abstract: Predicting rare extreme events such as wildfires from meteorological data requires models that remain reliable under evolving environmental conditions. This problem is inherently long-tailed: wildfire events are rare but high-impact, while most observations correspond to non-fire conditions, causing standard learning objectives to underemphasize the minority class (fire) that matters most. In addition, models trained on historical distributions often fail under distribution shifts, exhibiting degraded performance in new environments. To this end, we propose Environment-Adaptive Preference Optimization (EAPO), a framework that adapts prediction to the target environment with long-tail distribution. Given a new input distribution, we first construct distribution-aligned datasets via $k$-nearest neighbor retrieval. We then perform a hybrid fine-tuning procedure on this local manifold, combining supervised learning with preference optimization, as well as emphasizing on rare extreme events. EAPO refines decision boundaries while avoiding conflicting signals from heterogeneous training data. We evaluate EAPO on a real-world wildfire prediction task with environmental shifts. EAPO achieves robust performance (ROC-AUC 0.7310) and improves detection in extreme regimes, demonstrating its effectiveness in dynamic wildfire prediction systems.

</details>


### [331] [ORCE: Order-Aware Alignment of Verbalized Confidence in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12446)
*Chen Li,Xiaoling Hu,Songzhu Zheng,Jiawei Zhou,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A decoupled framework that separates answer generation from verbalized confidence estimation, using sampling-based correctness likelihood and rank-based reinforcement learning to improve confidence calibration without compromising answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often produce answers with high certainty even when incorrect, making reliable confidence estimation essential for real-world deployment. Existing verbalized confidence methods that jointly optimize answer and confidence generation can cause interference between confidence-alignment objectives and answer accuracy.

Method: A decoupled and order-aware framework that first generates an answer, then estimates confidence conditioned on the fixed question-answer pair. Uses a sampling-based surrogate from multiple model completions to estimate correctness likelihood, then optimizes rank-based reinforcement learning objectives to align confidence with correctness ranking.

Result: Experiments on reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks show improved calibration and failure prediction performance while largely preserving answer accuracy.

Conclusion: Verbalized confidence can be more reliably aligned by decoupling confidence estimation from answer generation and optimizing the relative ordering of confidence across responses, demonstrating that confidence optimization doesn't need to directly perturb answer generation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often produce answers with high certainty even when they are incorrect, making reliable confidence estimation essential for deployment in real-world scenarios. Verbalized confidence, where models explicitly state their confidence in natural language, provides a flexible and user-facing uncertainty signal that can be applied even when token logits are unavailable. However, existing verbalized-confidence methods often optimize answer generation and confidence generation jointly, which can cause confidence-alignment objectives to interfere with answer accuracy. In this work, we propose a decoupled and order-aware framework for verbalized confidence calibration. Our method first generates an answer and then estimates confidence conditioned on the fixed question--answer pair, allowing confidence optimization without directly perturbing the answer-generation process. To align confidence with correctness likelihood, we construct a sampling-based surrogate from multiple model completions and optimize rank-based reinforcement learning objectives that encourage responses with higher estimated correctness likelihood to receive higher verbalized confidence. Experiments on reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks show that our method improves calibration and failure prediction performance while largely preserving answer accuracy. These results demonstrate that verbalized confidence can be more reliably aligned by decoupling confidence estimation from answer generation and optimizing the relative ordering of confidence across responses.

</details>


### [332] [Multi-Stream LLMs: Unblocking Language Models with Parallel Streams of Thoughts, Inputs and Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12460)
*Guinan Su,Yanwu Yang,Xueyan Li,Jonas Geiping*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models can be improved by moving from single sequential message streams to multiple parallel computation streams, enabling simultaneous reading, thinking, and acting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI agents using chat models are bottlenecked by single sequential message streams, preventing them from acting while reading, reacting while writing, or thinking while acting, which limits their efficiency and capabilities.

Method: Switch from instruction-tuning for sequential message formats to instruction-tuning for multiple parallel streams of computation, splitting each role into separate streams. Each forward pass simultaneously reads from multiple input streams and generates tokens in multiple output streams with causal dependencies.

Result: This data-driven approach remedies usability limitations, improves model efficiency through parallelization, enhances security through better separation of concerns, and improves model monitorability.

Conclusion: Unblocking language models through parallel computation streams addresses fundamental limitations of current sequential chat models, enabling more capable and efficient autonomous agents.

Abstract: The continued improvements in language model capability have unlocked their widespread use as drivers of autonomous agents, for example in coding or computer use applications. However, the core of these systems has not changed much since early instruction-tuned models like ChatGPT. Even advanced AI agents function on message exchange formats, successively exchanging messages with users, systems, with itself (i.e. chain-of-thought) and tools in a single stream of computation. This bottleneck to a single stream in chat models leads to a number of limitations: the agent cannot act (generate output) while reading, and in reverse, cannot react to new information while writing. Similarly, the agent cannot act while thinking and cannot think while reading or acting on information.
