  In this work, we show that models can be unblocked by switching from instruction-tuning for sequential message formats to instruction-tuning for multiple, parallel streams of computation, splitting each role into a separate stream. Every forward pass of the language model then simultaneously reads from multiple input streams and generates tokens in multiple output streams, all of which causally depend on earlier timesteps. We argue that this data-driven change remedies a number of usability limitations as outlined above, improves model efficiency through parallelization, improves model security through better separation of concerns and can further improve model monitorability.

</details>


### [333] [Search Your Block Floating Point Scales!](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12464)
*Tanmaey Gupta,Hayden Prairie,Xiaoxia Wu,Reyna Abhyankar,Qingyang Wu,Austin Silveria,Pragaash Ponnusamy,Jue Wang,Ben Athiwaratkun,Leon Song,Tri Dao,Daniel Y. Fu,Chris De Sa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ScaleSearch is a novel method for selecting optimal scale factors in microscaling Block Floating Point (BFP) quantization formats by performing fine-grained searches to minimize quantization errors, improving existing quantization techniques and enabling near-lossless low-precision attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard BFP quantization uses fixed scales based on maximum magnitude in blocks, which can lead to suboptimal quantization errors. There's a need for better scale selection strategies to improve quantization accuracy for generative models while leveraging modern GPU accelerator support for microscaling BFP formats.

Method: ScaleSearch performs fine-grained scale factor selection using mantissa bits in microscaling formats to minimize quantization error. It integrates with existing quantization methods (Post Training Quantization and low-precision attention) and introduces ScaleSearchAttention, an accelerated NVFP4-based attention algorithm using adapted techniques for causal language modeling.

Result: ScaleSearch reduces quantization error by 27% for NVFP4 and improves language model PTQ by up to 15 points on MATH500 (Qwen3-8B). ScaleSearchAttention improves Wikitext-2 perplexity by up to 0.77 points for Llama 3.1 70B, closely matching baseline performance while providing quantization accuracy improvements.

Conclusion: The proposed ScaleSearch method effectively addresses suboptimal scale selection in BFP quantization, significantly reducing quantization errors and improving performance of existing quantization techniques for large language models, enabling more accurate low-precision inference on modern GPU hardware.

Abstract: Quantization has emerged as a standard technique for accelerating inference for generative models by enabling faster low-precision computations and reduced memory transfers. Recently, GPU accelerators have added first-class support for microscaling Block Floating Point (BFP) formats. Standard BFP algorithms use a fixed scale based on the maximum magnitude of the block. We observe that this scale choice can be suboptimal with respect to quantization errors. In this work, we propose ScaleSearch, an alternative strategy for selecting these scale factors: using a fine-grained search leveraging the mantissa bits in microscaling formats to minimize the quantization error for the given distribution. ScaleSearch can be integrated with existing quantization methods such as Post Training Quantization and low-precision attention, and is shown to improve their performance. Additionally, we introduce ScaleSearchAttention, an accelerated NVFP4-based attention algorithm, which uses ScaleSearch and adapted prior techniques to ensure near-0 performance loss for causal language modeling. Experiments show that ScaleSearch reduces quantization error by 27% for NVFP4 and improves language model PTQ by up to 15 points for MATH500 (Qwen3-8B), while ScaleSearchAttention improves Wikitext-2 PPL by upto 0.77 points for Llama 3.1 70B. The proposed methods closely match baseline performance while providing quantization accuracy improvements.

</details>


### [334] [High-arity Sample Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12465)
*Leonardo N. Coregliano,William Opich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper establishes a connection between high-arity sample compression schemes and PAC learnability in high-dimensional product spaces, showing that non-trivial compression implies learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent works have begun studying learning theory concepts for product spaces (high-arity learning theory), and there's a need to understand compression schemes in this high-dimensional context and their relationship to learnability.

Method: The paper considers a high-arity variant of sample compression schemes and proves theoretical connections between these compression schemes and PAC learnability.

Result: The main result shows that the existence of a high-arity sample compression scheme of non-trivial quality implies high-arity PAC learnability, establishing a fundamental connection in high-dimensional learning theory.

Conclusion: This work extends compression theory to high-arity settings and demonstrates that compression schemes remain a useful tool for establishing learnability even in complex product spaces, contributing to the emerging field of high-arity learning theory.

Abstract: Recently, a series of works have started studying variations of concepts from learning theory for product spaces, which can be collected under the name high-arity learning theory. In this work, we consider a high-arity variant of sample compression schemes and we prove that the existence of a high-arity sample compression scheme of non-trivial quality implies high-arity PAC learnability.

</details>


### [335] [Routers Learn the Geometry of Their Experts: Geometric Coupling in Sparse Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12476)
*Sagi Ahrac,Noya Hochwald,Mor Geva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reveals that Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) models exhibit geometric coupling between routers and experts, shows how auxiliary losses disrupt this coupling, and demonstrates that maintaining this coupling via parameter-free routing achieves better load balancing with minimal performance impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training SMoE models is challenging due to routing collapse onto few experts and the negative effects of auxiliary load-balancing losses on expert specialization. The authors aim to understand how routing decisions are mechanistically formed in SMoEs.

Method: 1) Theoretical analysis revealing geometric coupling between routers and corresponding experts through shared input-directed gradients; 2) Empirical validation on a 1B SMoE showing routing decisions are mirrored in expert activations; 3) Analysis of how auxiliary load-balancing affects this coupling; 4) Implementation of parameter-free online K-Means router using cosine similarity to maintain geometric coupling.

Result: 1) Demonstrated geometric coupling where higher router scores predict stronger expert neuron activations; 2) Showed auxiliary losses break this coupling by making router directions three times more similar; 3) Parameter-free router achieved lowest load imbalance with modest perplexity increase, outperforming auxiliary-loss and loss-free approaches.

Conclusion: Geometric coupling between routers and experts is central to effective routing in SMoEs, and maintaining this coupling through mechanisms like the online K-Means router enables better load balancing while preserving model performance, explaining how routers form assignment geometry that supports an effective division of labor.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) models enable scaling language models efficiently, but training them remains challenging, as routing can collapse onto few experts and auxiliary load-balancing losses can reduce specialization. Motivated by these hurdles, we study how routing decisions in SMoEs are formed mechanistically. First, we reveal a geometric coupling between routers and their corresponding experts. For a given token, the router weights for the selected expert and the expert weights processing it receive gradients along the same input direction, differing only in scalar coefficients. Thus, matched router--expert directions accumulate the same routed token history. This theoretical coupling also appears empirically in routing dynamics. In a $1$B SMoE trained from scratch, higher router scores predict stronger expert neuron activations, showing that routing decisions are mirrored inside the selected expert. Next, we analyze the effects of auxiliary load balancing on the router--expert geometric coupling, showing that such losses break this structure by spreading input-directed gradients across router weights, making distinct router directions nearly three times more similar to each other. Last, we demonstrate the centrality of geometric coupling for effective routing with a parameter-free online K-Means router, in which each expert maintains a running average of the hidden states routed to it and tokens are assigned based on cosine similarity. Compared with auxiliary-loss and loss-free balancing, this router achieves the lowest load imbalance with only a modest perplexity increase, indicating that geometric coupling captures a substantial part of what the router learns. Overall, our results explain how routers form assignment geometry that supports an effective division of labor.

</details>


### [336] [MEME: Multi-entity & Evolving Memory Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12477)
*Seokwon Jung,Alexander Rubinstein,Arnas Uselis,Sangdoo Yun,Seong Joon Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MEME is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM agents' memory capabilities across multiple sessions, focusing on multi-entity and evolving information scenarios that prior benchmarks didn't cover, revealing significant gaps in dependency reasoning performance across current systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents need to operate in persistent environments with information spanning multiple sessions, but existing benchmarks only evaluate single-entity updates, missing crucial aspects like multi-entity relationships and evolving information states.

Method: MEME defines six tasks covering the full space of multi-entity and evolving axes, including three novel tasks: Cascade and Absence (dependency reasoning) and Deletion (post-removal state). The benchmark evaluates six memory systems across three memory paradigms using 100 controlled episodes.

Result: All evaluated memory systems collapsed on dependency reasoning tasks (Cascade: 3%, Absence: 1% average accuracy) despite adequate static retrieval performance. Various improvements like prompt optimization, deeper retrieval, and better LLMs failed to close the gap. Only a file-based agent with Claude Opus 4.7 partially improved performance but at 70x baseline cost.

Conclusion: Current memory systems struggle significantly with dependency reasoning in persistent environments, and closing this performance gap requires impractical configurations, highlighting a critical research challenge for scalable LLM agent deployment in multi-session settings.

Abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly operate in persistent environments where they must store, update, and reason over information across many sessions. While prior benchmarks evaluate only single-entity updates, MEME defines six tasks spanning the full space defined by the multi-entity and evolving axes, including three not scored by prior work: Cascade and Absence (dependency reasoning) and Deletion (post-removal state). Evaluating six memory systems spanning three memory paradigms on 100 controlled episodes, we find that all systems collapse on dependency reasoning under the default configuration (Cascade: 3%, Absence: 1% in average accuracy) despite adequate static retrieval performance. Prompt optimization, deeper retrieval, reduced filler noise, and most stronger LLMs fail to close this gap. Only a file-based agent paired with Claude Opus 4.7 as its internal LLM partially closes the gap, but at ~70x the baseline cost, indicating closure currently depends on configurations that are not practical at scale. Code and data are available on the project page: https://seokwonjung-jay.github.io/meme-eval/.

</details>


### [337] [Pion: A Spectrum-Preserving Optimizer via Orthogonal Equivalence Transformation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12492)
*Kexuan Shi,Hanxuan Li,Zeju Qiu,Yandong Wen,Simon Buchholz,Weiyang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pion is a novel spectrum-preserving optimizer for LLM training that uses orthogonal transformations to update weight matrices while preserving their singular values, offering stable and competitive performance compared to standard optimizers like Adam.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing optimizers like Adam and Muon use additive updates that change the spectral properties of weight matrices. The authors aim to develop an optimizer that preserves singular values while still effectively updating model parameters, potentially offering more stable training dynamics for large language models.

Method: Pion updates weight matrices through left and right orthogonal transformations (orthogonal equivalence transformation) rather than additive updates. This approach preserves the singular values of weight matrices while allowing geometric modulation of the matrices. The authors derive the Pion update rule, systematically examine design choices, and analyze convergence behavior.

Result: Empirical results demonstrate that Pion offers stable and competitive performance compared to standard optimizers for both LLM pretraining and finetuning tasks. The optimizer successfully preserves spectral properties while enabling effective model updates.

Conclusion: Pion presents a promising alternative to existing optimizers by offering spectrum-preserving updates through orthogonal transformations. This approach provides stable training dynamics for LLMs while maintaining competitive performance across both pretraining and finetuning scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce Pion, a spectrum-preserving optimizer for large language model (LLM) training based on orthogonal equivalence transformation. Unlike additive optimizers such as Adam and Muon, Pion updates each weight matrix through left and right orthogonal transformations, preserving its singular values throughout training. This yields an optimization mechanism that modulates the geometry of weight matrices while keeping their spectral norm fixed. We derive the Pion update rule, systematically examine its design choices, and analyze its convergence behavior along with several key properties. Empirical results show that Pion offers a stable and competitive alternative to standard optimizers for both LLM pretraining and finetuning.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [338] [Control Charts for Multi-agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11135)
*Hayden Helm,Carey Priebe,Brandon Duderstadt*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces adaptive control charts for monitoring multi-agent systems, showing they're necessary for systems where agents can learn, but also vulnerable to slow-defecting adversarial agents, revealing a fundamental tradeoff between learning capability and security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for monitoring open-ended multi-agent systems are limited to qualitative inspection, which is insufficient as users increasingly deploy minimally restricted agents in complex environments. There's a need for automated monitoring tools that can handle adaptive, learning agents.

Method: The authors extend the process-theoretic notion of adaptive control charts from traditional statistical process control to multi-agent systems. They use simulation to test the approach and conduct both empirical demonstrations and theoretical analysis of the system's properties.

Result: Adaptive control charts are shown to be necessary for monitoring multi-agent systems where agents can learn from their environment. However, they are susceptible to adversarial agents that defect slowly over time. This reveals a fundamental tradeoff: either agents in a system cannot learn, or the system is vulnerable to adversaries.

Conclusion: There exists a fundamental tradeoff in multi-agent system control: system designers must choose between allowing agents to learn from their environment (making adaptive control charts necessary but vulnerable to slow-defecting adversaries) or restricting learning capabilities to maintain security. This highlights the inherent tension between adaptability and security in open-ended multi-agent systems.

Abstract: Generative agents have proven to be powerful assistants in a wide variety of contexts. Given this success, users are now deploying agents with minimal restrictions in open ended, multi-agent environments. Current methods for monitoring the dynamics of open-ended multi-agent systems are limited to qualitative inspection. In this paper, we extend the process-theoretic notion of adaptive control charts to multi-agent systems to enable automated monitoring. Using simulation, we demonstrate that adaptive control charts are necessary for monitoring multi-agent systems that can learn from their environment. We further demonstrate, both empirically and theoretically, that adaptive control charts are susceptible to adversarial agents that defect sufficiently slowly. These results illustrate a fundamental tradeoff in multi-agent system control: either agents in a system cannot learn or the system is susceptible to adversaries.

</details>


### [339] [Information and Contract Design for Repeated Interactions between Agents with Misaligned Incentives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11294)
*Nanda Kishore Sreenivas,Kate Larson*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A study of information asymmetry and incentive misalignment in multi-agent systems where a Sender with private information learns optimal communication strategies that Receivers act on, influenced by conflict levels and environmental information, with linear contracts enabling Senders to extract surplus at fairness costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how information asymmetries and misaligned incentives affect interactions between independent agents, specifically examining communication strategies when one agent holds vital private information but cannot act, while another must make decisions but depends on the first agent's information.

Method: Modeled an interaction between a Sender (holds private information, cannot act) and a Receiver (makes decisions, dependent on Sender's information). Introduced a mechanism allowing agents to form linear contracts with pricing for information, then analyzed how Senders learn optimal communication strategies.

Result: Senders learn optimal communication strategies that Receivers reliably act on. These strategies are highly sensitive to conflict levels in agents' rewards and the amount of environmental information Receivers can observe. Senders learn to use payment structures to improve their rewards, but this leads to fairness issues as Senders extract much of Receivers' surplus.

Conclusion: The study reveals important implications for fairness, contract design, and learning in multi-agent systems with information asymmetries. While linear contracts enable Senders to improve rewards through optimal communication strategies, they create fairness concerns as Senders exploit their informational advantage to extract surplus from Receivers.

Abstract: We study the consequences of information asymmetries and misaligned incentives in settings with multiple independent agents. We model an interaction between a Sender, who holds vital private information but cannot act, and a Receiver, who must make decisions but is dependent on the Sender's information. We find that the Sender learns an optimal communication strategy that the Receiver reliably acts on. Importantly, this strategy is highly sensitive to the degree of conflict in the agents' rewards and the amount of environmental information the Receiver can already observe. We introduce a mechanism allowing the agents to form linear contracts, where a price is established for the information. We demonstrate that the Sender learns to use these payment structures to improve its rewards, though this comes at a cost of "fairness" between agents as the Sender is able to extract much of the Receiver's surplus. This raises questions about fairness, contract design, and learning in the context of multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [340] [Predictive Maps of Multi-Agent Reasoning: A Successor-Representation Spectrum for LLM Communication Topologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11453)
*Ethan David James Park,Dalal Alharthi*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a structural diagnostic for multi-agent LLM communication graphs using the successor representation to predict failure modes, validated on a state-tracking task with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners lack pre-inference diagnostics for selecting optimal communication topologies (chain, star, mesh) in multi-agent LLM systems to predict which will amplify drift, converge to consensus, or remain robust under perturbation.

Method: Uses the successor representation M = (I - γP)^{-1} of row-stochastic communication operators and analyzes three spectral quantities: spectral radius ρ(M), spectral gap Δ(M), and condition number κ(M). Derives closed-form spectra for chain, star, and mesh topologies under row-stochastic normalization.

Result: Validated on 12-step structured state-tracking task with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct over 100 trials. Condition number perfectly predicts perturbation robustness (r_s = 1.0), spectral gap partially predicts consensus dynamics (r_s = 0.5), and spectral radius is perfectly inverted with respect to cumulative error (r_s = -1.0).

Conclusion: This represents a first step toward representational, drift-aware structural diagnostics for multi-agent LLM systems, sitting alongside classical spectral and consensus theory, with an affine-noise extension proposed to address limitations where linear spectra are blind to non-contracting bias drift.

Abstract: Practitioners deploying multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems must currently choose between communication topologies such as chain, star, mesh, and richer variants without any pre-inference diagnostic for which topology will amplify drift, converge to consensus, or remain robust under perturbation. Existing evaluation answers these questions only post hoc and only for the task measured. We introduce a structural diagnostic for multi-agent LLM communication graphs based on the successor representation $M = (I - γP)^{-1}$ of the row-stochastic communication operator, and we connect three of its spectral quantities, the spectral radius $ρ(M)$, the spectral gap $Δ(M)$, and the condition number $κ(M)$, to three distinct failure modes. We derive closed-form spectra for the chain, star, and mesh under row-stochastic normalization, and validate the predictions on a 12-step structured state-tracking task with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct over 100 independent trials. The condition number is a perfect rank-order predictor of empirical perturbation robustness ($r_s = 1.0$); the spectral gap partially predicts consensus dynamics ($r_s = 0.5$); and the spectral radius is perfectly \emph{inverted} with respect to cumulative error ($r_s = -1.0$). We trace this inversion to a regime in which linear spectra are blind to non-contracting bias drift, and we propose an affine-noise extension of the predictive map that recovers the empirical ordering. We read this as a first step toward representational, drift-aware structural diagnostics for multi-agent LLM systems, sitting alongside classical spectral and consensus theory.

</details>


### [341] [Distance-Constrained Unlabeled Multi-Agent Pathfinding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11503)
*Takahiro Suzuki,Yuma Tamura,Keisuke Okumura*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces Distance-r Independent Unlabeled Multi-Agent Pathfinding, a harder variant of MAPF with distance constraints, proves PSPACE-completeness for feasibility, and presents two algorithmic approaches that can handle hundreds of agents practically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address real-world multi-agent coordination challenges by extending classical Multi-Agent Pathfinding (MAPF) with distance constraints that require agents to maintain minimum pairwise distances. This models realistic scenarios where agents need physical spacing for safety or operational reasons, unlike standard MAPF which only prevents collisions.

Method: The authors use two complementary approaches: (1) reduction-based optimal algorithms with a feasibility-preserving compression procedure that transforms the problem while maintaining solution existence, and (2) a configuration generator-based search algorithm that explores possible agent configurations.

Result: The key theoretical result is that feasibility (whether a solution exists) for Distance-r Independent Unlabeled MAPF is PSPACE-complete, which contrasts with standard unlabeled MAPF where feasibility can be decided in polynomial time. Empirically, despite this hardness, the algorithms can handle hundreds of agents in practical timeframes.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a more realistic distance-constrained variant of MAPF, proves its computational hardness (PSPACE-completeness), and demonstrates practical algorithms that scale to hundreds of agents despite theoretical complexity, bridging theory and practice for real-world multi-agent coordination.

Abstract: We study a graph pathfinding problem Distance-$r$ Independent Unlabeled Multi-Agent Pathfinding, finding a set of collision-free paths between two sets where agents must stay at pairwise distance at least $r+1$ at all times. This additional constraint, generalizing collision modeling for classical MAPF, targets aspects of real-world multi-agent coordination. This additional distance constraint makes feasibility (i.e., whether a solution exists) PSPACE-complete, in contrast to standard (unlabeled) MAPF, where it can be decided in polynomial time. We address the challenge via two complementary approaches: (i) reduction-based optimal algorithms with a feasibility-preserving compression procedure, and (ii) a configuration generator-based search. Despite the hardness, empirical results show that our algorithm can handle hundreds of agents in a practical timeframe.

</details>


### [342] [GeomHerd: A Forward-looking Herding Quantification via Ricci Flow Geometry on Agent Interactive Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11645)
*Lake Yang,Junwei Su,Jingfeng Zeng,Wenhao Lu,Xingzhi Qian,Weitong Zhang,Chuan Wu,Dunhong Jin*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: GeomHerd is a forward-looking framework that detects financial herding by analyzing geometric properties of agent-interaction graphs, providing early warning signals before traditional price-correlation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing herding quantification methods rely on price-correlation statistics that inherently lag because they only detect coordination after it has already affected realized returns, making them ineffective for early detection of market fragility and systemic risk.

Method: Uses a heterogeneous LLM-driven multi-agent simulator with persona-conditioned LLMs as financial traders, analyzes interaction graphs via discrete Ollivier-Ricci curvature to capture structural topology of emerging coordination, and establishes theoretical mapping to classical CSAD herding statistic.

Result: GeomHerd detects herding significantly earlier than baselines: 272 steps before order-parameter onset, recalls 65% of critical trajectories 318 steps early, and precedes price-correlation methods by 40 steps; also shows vocabulary contraction during cascades and transfers to other domains.

Conclusion: GeomHerd provides a forward-looking geometric framework for early herding detection that bypasses observability lag of traditional methods, offering promising tools for anticipating market fragility and systemic risk through structural analysis of agent coordination.

Abstract: Herding -- where agents align their behaviors and act collectively -- is a central driver of market fragility and systemic risk. Existing approaches to quantify herding rely on price-correlation statistics, which inherently lag because they only detect coordination after it has already moved realised returns. We propose GeomHerd, a forward-looking geometric framework that bypasses this observability lag by quantifying coordination directly on upstream agent-interaction graphs. To generate these graphs, we treat a heterogeneous LLM-driven multi-agent simulator -- each financial trader instantiated by a persona-conditioned LLM call -- as a forecastable world, and evaluate the geometric pipeline on the Cividino--Sornette continuous-spin agent-based substrate as our headline financial testbed. By tracking the discrete Ollivier--Ricci curvature of these action graphs, GeomHerd captures the structural topology of emerging coordination. Theoretically, we establish a mean-field bridge mapping our graph-theoretic metric to CSAD, the classical macroscopic herding statistic, linking GeomHerd to downstream price-dispersion measurement. Empirically, GeomHerd anticipates herding long before aggregate market baselines: on the continuous-spin substrate, our primary detector fires a median of 272 steps before order-parameter onset; a contagion detector ($β_{-}$) recalls 65% of critical trajectories 318 steps early; and on co-firing trajectories the agent-graph signal precedes price-correlation-graph baselines by 40 steps. As a complementary indicator, the effective vocabulary of agent actions contracts during cascades. The geometric signature transfers out-of-domain to the Vicsek self-driven-particle model, and a curvature-conditioned forecasting head reduces cascade-window log-return MAE over detector-conditioned and price-only baselines.

</details>


### [343] [Events as Triggers for Behavioral Diversity in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12388)
*Hannes Büchi,Manon Flageat,Eduardo Sebastián,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A novel MARL framework that decouples agent identity from behavior using events as triggers for dynamic role switching, enabling agents to adopt diverse behaviors at specific moments through a behavior manifold and event-based hypernetwork.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MARL frameworks bind fixed behaviors to fixed agent identities, making them ineffective for tasks requiring agents to switch roles at specific moments. The authors argue that events (state changes inducing qualitative task changes) are needed to define behavioral transitions.

Method: Two key components: 1) Neural Manifold Diversity (NMD) - a formal distance metric for building an expressive behavior manifold that works with transient, agent-agnostic behaviors; 2) Event-based hypernetwork that generates LoRA modules over a shared team policy, enabling on-the-fly agent-policy reconfiguration in response to events.

Result: The framework outperforms established baselines across benchmarks, demonstrates zero-shot generalization, and is the only method that solves tasks requiring sequential behavior reassignment.

Conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the limitation of fixed agent-behavior binding in MARL by introducing an event-driven framework that enables dynamic role switching while ensuring diversity doesn't interfere with reward maximization by design.

Abstract: Effective multi-agent cooperation requires agents to adopt diverse behaviors as task conditions evolve-and to do so at the right moment. Yet, current Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) frameworks that facilitate this diversity are still limited by the fact that they bind fixed behaviors to fixed agent identities. Consequently, they are ill-equipped for tasks where agents need to take on different roles at very specific moments in time. We argue that, to define these behavioral transitions, the missing ingredient is events. Events are changes in the state of the system that induce qualitative changes in the task. Based on this view, we introduce a framework that decouples agent identity from behavior, capturing a continuous manifold from which agents instantiate their behaviors in response to events. This framework is based on two elements. First, to build an expressive behavior manifold, we introduce Neural Manifold Diversity (NMD), a formal distance metric that remains well-defined when behaviors are transient and agent-agnostic. Second, we use an event-based hypernetwork that generates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules over a shared team policy, enabling on-the-fly agent-policy reconfiguration in response to events. We prove that this construction ensures that diversity does not interfere with reward maximization by design. Empirical results demonstrate that our framework outperforms established baselines across benchmarks while exhibiting zero-shot generalization, and being the only method that solves tasks requiring sequential behavior reassignment.

</details>
